theory-1841.name: Convergent State-Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1841.description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not best explained as simply 'missing one rare kinase lesion.' Instead, many converge onto a limited number of recurrent biologic states that can be generated by different upstream mechanisms: cryptic RTK/RAS/RAF activation, derepression of RAS/PI3K by loss of negative regulators, angiogenic-stromal invasion programs, proliferation/checkpoint programs, lineage-state reprogramming, immune-structured states, and metabolism-centered states. In this theory, genomic negativity at the canonical panel level often masks biologic positivity at the pathway-state level; therefore, clinically useful classification should begin with state measurement and then resolve the causal mechanism within each state.
theory-1841.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Convergent State-Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not best explained as simply 'missing one rare kinase lesion.' Instead, many converge onto a limited number of recurrent biologic states that can be generated by different upstream mechanisms: cryptic RTK/RAS/RAF activation, derepression of RAS/PI3K by loss of negative regulators, angiogenic-stromal invasion programs, proliferation/checkpoint programs, lineage-state reprogramming, immune-structured states, and metabolism-centered states. In this theory, genomic negativity at the canonical panel level often masks biologic positivity at the pathway-state level; therefore, clinically useful classification should begin with state measurement and then resolve the causal mechanism within each state.

theory_statements.statement_name: Driver-negativity often reflects hidden pathway convergence rather than absence of oncogenic circuitry
theory_statements.theory_statement: In lung adenocarcinomas that are negative for canonical actionable RTK/RAS/RAF drivers by routine assays, oncogenic behavior is frequently maintained either by occult lesions missed by limited testing or by noncanonical mechanisms that converge onto active pathway states; therefore, clinical driver-negativity does not imply biologic pathway-negativity.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma designated driver-negative, oncogene-negative, pan-negative, triple-negative, EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative, EGFR/ALK-wild-type, or RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative by targeted clinical assays, panel testing, WES, or even WGS-based filtering. Most applicable to cohorts enriched for never-smokers, early-stage resected tumors, or clinically routine-testing-negative cases. Does not claim every driver-negative tumor has a single dominant addiction, only that many do, and that pathway activity often persists despite canonical genomic negativity.
special_cases: Some initially driver-negative tumors are technical false negatives caused by low tumor purity, low allelic fraction, incomplete primer coverage, limited panel scope, intratumoral heterogeneity, or insufficient fusion/splice detection.
A subset of truly unresolved tumors may be early evolutionary states, highly subclonal, or ecosystem-dominated, and may not yet show one dominant tumor-cell-intrinsic addiction.
Some tumors are genuinely driven by rare discrete lesions rather than broad state convergence; in such cases lesion-first and state-first classification will often agree.

supporting_evidence.text: Whole-genome refinement of RTK/RAS/RAF pathway-negative LUAD rescued hidden canonical pathway drivers in 28 of 85 WES-negative tumors, showing that many apparent negatives are not truly pathway-negative after deeper analysis.

The review of RPA(-)E versus RPA(-)G notes that WGS identified RTK/RAS/RAF alterations in 33% of WES-negative tumors, explicitly framing many WES-negative cases as technical or scope-related misses rather than true biologic negatives.

Comprehensive genomic profiling of routine-testing-negative LUAD detected genomic alterations in 94% of patients and actionable alterations in 31%, demonstrating that a large fraction of clinical triple-negative cases contain occult drivers missed by standard EGFR/KRAS/ALK testing.

Never-smoker LUAD initially labeled oncogene-negative by WES was often reclassified after deeper sequencing or orthogonal testing, recovering EGFR, ALK, ROS1, MET exon 14, ERBB2, KRAS, and AKT1 alterations; lower tumor cellularity strongly associated with these initially false-negative cases.
The low-cellularity explanation is directly supported by lower median predicted tumor cellularity in reclassified oncogene-negative samples versus tumors where drivers were initially identified.

The driver-negative definition paper on Korean LUAD found that RNA sequencing of initially negative tumors recovered novel kinase fusions and MET exon 14 skipping, reducing the truly unexplained fraction and emphasizing transcriptome-detectable hidden drivers.

The multiplex clinical genotyping study showed that 'no driver' in partially genotyped cohorts could combine true negatives with incompletely tested cases, underscoring that assay-defined negativity is partly methodological.

Proteogenomic LUAD studies explicitly state that a substantial proportion of tumors lack known or currently targetable mutations but still show strong pathway activation states detectable at the protein/phosphosite level.

Examples of protein-level hidden signaling include SHP2/PTPN11 phospho-activation in EGFR-mutant and ALK-fusion tumors without corresponding RNA/protein abundance change, SOS1 phosphosite activation in KRAS-mutant tumors, NRF2-axis activation in KEAP1-mutant tumors, ARHGEF5/Rho signaling in never-smokers, and neutrophil degranulation in STK11-mutant tumors detected at proteome but not RNA level.

Integrated proteogenomic clustering of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD resolved four biologically coherent subtypes—proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism—despite the absence of the dominant canonical drivers used clinically.

Oncogene-negative LUAD can form a distinct RAS/PI3K-active subtype through loss of negative regulators NF1, RASA1, and PTEN, with pERK and pAKT activation in mouse tumors and frequent pERK/pAKT positivity in human oncogene-negative tumors.

Pan-negative cohorts repeatedly yield rare but actionable hidden lesions including NRG1 fusions, NTRK1 rearrangements, MET exon 14 skipping, KIF5B-RET, KIF5B-RET-related RET fusions, EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 extracellular/transmembrane mutations, ARAF mutations, ERBB4 fusions, and BRAF fusions.

The hallmarks analysis explicitly noted that many LUADs lack an assigned hallmark alteration or a known driver mutation, motivating search for additional mechanisms such as epigenetic/RNA deregulation rather than accepting true driverlessness.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is established that some driver-negative LUADs harbor missed fusions, exon-skipping events, rare mutations, or noncanonical amplifications, and that proteogenomics can reveal pathway activity not obvious from DNA alone.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is the explicit unification of these findings into a single principle: clinically negative LUAD is often biologically positive at the pathway-state level, so pathway-state assignment should be treated as the primary explanatory unit rather than an optional add-on to lesion finding.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is broader than existing hidden-driver papers, which usually emphasize one lesion class or one subtype. It synthesizes discrete cryptic lesions, protein-state activation, and multi-omic subtype findings into one driver-negative interpretation framework.
references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [documents unresolved oncogene-negative fraction but does not formalize a state-first theory]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2021) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [shows hidden lesions and unresolved heterogeneity in RPA(-)G LUAD]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [shows pathway activity beyond genomics, but not a generalized driver-negative convergence theory]
Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker LUAD [closest direct precedent for subtype/state framing in canonical-driver-negative disease]

theory_statements.statement_name: A limited state-space explains much of driver-negative LUAD heterogeneity
theory_statements.theory_statement: A large share of biologically recurrent driver-negative LUADs can be categorized into a small number of partially overlapping state classes—RAS/PI3K-derepressed, angiogenic-stromal, proliferation/checkpoint, lineage-reprogrammed, immune-structured, and metabolism-rewired—and within each class, different genomic or non-genomic lesions are partially interchangeable if they generate the same systems-level phenotype.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to cohort-level stratification of driver-negative, oncogene-negative, or EGFR/ALK-wild-type lung adenocarcinoma using integrated transcriptomic, proteomic, phosphoproteomic, and microenvironmental data. Especially applicable to never-smoker LUAD and to canonical-driver-negative cohorts, but also informative for subsets embedded within broader LUAD cohorts.
special_cases: A single tumor may occupy more than one state simultaneously, for example an angiogenic-stromal state with coexisting immune exclusion or a lineage-reprogrammed state with ligand-fusion signaling.
Preinvasive lesions and early-stage tumors may sit in transitional states that map only partially onto invasive-state classes.
Highly unstable tumors may express mixed or drifting states rather than one stable category.

supporting_evidence.text: EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD reproducibly divided into proliferation-high, immune-high, angiogenesis-high, and metabolism-high groups, each with distinct molecular features, prognosis, and candidate vulnerabilities.

The proliferation-high group showed cell-cycle and DNA-replication programs, high CDK1/MKI67, TP53 enrichment, and suggested CDK/PLK/ATR vulnerabilities, exemplifying a proliferation/checkpoint state.
The immune-high group showed TLS markers, T/B-cell and NK-cell infiltration, checkpoint expression, and the best prognosis, exemplifying an immune-structured state.

The angiogenesis-high group showed LGALS3, FGF2, CXCL12, PDGFB, VEGF-related biology, TGF-beta/cell migration signatures, CAF-mediated immune exclusion, and poor prognosis, exemplifying an angiogenic-stromal state.

The metabolism-high group showed oxidative phosphorylation, lipid and carbon metabolism, ACAT1/CERS4/ACACA enrichment, and subtype-specific metabolic dependencies, exemplifying a metabolism-rewired state.
Oncogene-negative LUAD can form an explicit RAS/PI3K-active state driven by NF1, RASA1, and PTEN loss, with pERK and pAKT activation and sensitivity to SHP2 plus AKT inhibition.

A separate smoking-associated RasGAP-loss class was defined by concurrent RASA1 and NF1 inactivation, strong MAPK dependence, and trametinib sensitivity, supporting the idea that multiple lesion sets can converge onto related RAS/MAPK states.

Early never-smoker LUAD progression identified an AIS3 state enriched for tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs, followed by MIA invasive states with endothelial enrichment, supporting a recurrent angiogenic-stromal preinvasive to invasive continuum.

Lineage-reprogrammed states recur in multiple forms: NKX2-1-negative mucinous/gastric LUAD with HNF4A/FOXA1/2 reprogramming, and KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate/KAC states with KRAS-like transcriptional activation and poor prognosis.

Immune-state subtyping repeatedly split driver-negative or never-smoker LUAD into immune-hot versus immune-cold configurations, including 5-IRGP high/low risk groups and IM-1/IM-2/IM-3 never-smoker immune subtypes.

Cell-cycle and DNA-repair burden is a recurrent noncanonical theme in EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative and other driver-negative cohorts, including significant pathway enrichment for p53/ATM, checkpoint regulation, and DNA repair, plus recurrent ATM and CDKN2A/CDK4/MDM2 lesions.
EGFR-wild-type LUAD was stratified by a ferroptosis-inhibitor/high-fibroblast state (FeSig-high) versus FeSig-low, adding an additional metabolism/TME state in a canonical-driver-negative context.

Driver-negative LUAD focused on glycolytic reprogramming and the MIF-HIF-1alpha axis identified a glycolysis-high epithelial/AT2 state rather than a classic genomic lesion, reinforcing that some driver-negative classes are fundamentally pathway-state defined.

Transcriptomic subtype work in never-smoker LUAD identified a chaotic subtype with EMT/cell-adhesion/cell-movement programs but no enrichment of known drivers, providing another state-defined class beyond lesion status.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Several studies define individual subtype schemes—immune, proteogenomic, histologic, progression-based, ferroptosis-related, or lineage-based—and several hidden-driver studies define one mechanism at a time.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the abstraction that these apparently separate subtype systems largely collapse into a shared low-dimensional state-space, and that upstream lesions are often replaceable within each state class.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: No single cited study proposes a unified state architecture spanning hidden RTK lesions, RAS/PI3K derepression, angiogenic progression, lineage reprogramming, immune states, and metabolic states. The theory integrates them into one explanatory map.
references: Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker LUAD [closest direct precedent for four state classes in canonical-driver-negative disease]
Multiomics Analysis Reveals Molecular Changes during Early Progression of Precancerous Lesions to Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [supports angiogenic-stromal progression states]
Nkx2-1 lineage reprogramming studies [support lineage-state hidden biology]
An atlas of epithelial cell states and plasticity in lung adenocarcinoma [supports KAC/alveolar intermediate lineage-state hidden biology]
A novel signature based on immune-related gene pairs in driver-negative LUAD [supports immune-state stratification but not cross-state unification]

new_predictions_likely: If a large driver-negative LUAD cohort is profiled with matched DNA, RNA, proteomics, phosphoproteomics, and spatial microenvironment readouts, most tumors will reproducibly map onto a small number of pathway-state clusters even when the upstream lesions differ.

If treatment response is modeled using state-first labels such as RAS/PI3K-active, angiogenic-stromal, immune-high, proliferation/checkpoint, lineage-reprogrammed, and metabolism-rewired, prediction accuracy will exceed that obtained from the binary label 'driver-negative' alone.

If low-purity or panel-negative driver-negative tumors are re-analyzed by RNA fusion calling, WGS, and phosphoproteomics, the fraction of completely unexplained tumors will shrink, but the same recurrent state classes will remain.

If pERK/pAKT, angiogenesis/CAF score, NKX2-1/HNF4A lineage score, and immune-hot/cold state are measured prospectively in routine-testing-negative LUAD, these state markers will explain survival heterogeneity better than routine molecular negativity alone.
new_predictions_unknown: If state-first classification is applied prospectively in canonical-driver-negative LUAD, a compact panel of approximately 15-25 proteins/phosphoproteins plus one spatial stromal score may outperform large DNA panels for short-term therapy selection in the subset lacking actionable lesions.

If longitudinal sampling is performed under therapy, some driver-negative LUADs will transition along a finite state-transition graph—for example from proliferation/checkpoint to angiogenic-stromal or from lineage-reprogrammed to immune-excluded—rather than evolving in an unconstrained manner.

If single-cell multiome profiling is applied to the residual truly unresolved driver-negative tumors after deep genomic rescue, a new class may emerge in which no dominant tumor-cell-intrinsic state exists and progression is governed mainly by ecosystem architecture.
negative_experiments: Profile a large independent driver-negative LUAD cohort with integrated DNA/RNA/protein/phosphoprotein/spatial data; if tumors do not form reproducible recurrent state classes, the theory is weakened.
Test whether state-defined grouping predicts therapy response better than lesion-defined grouping in retrospective or prospective cohorts; if it does not, the state-first claim is weakened.

Examine whether different upstream lesions assigned to the same proposed state are functionally interchangeable with respect to downstream signaling and drug response; if they are not, the convergence model is weakened.
Assess whether residual driver-negative cases after deep genomic rescue remain enriched for recurring protein/TME states; if not, the theory overstates convergence.

unaccounted_for.text: Some studies imply that a substantial fraction of driver-negative disease is simply assay failure or low cellularity rather than distinct biology, so the balance between true convergence and technical miss is unresolved.
Copy-number-quiet piano never-smoker tumors may represent a stem-like low-driver state that does not map cleanly onto the main pathway-state classes proposed here.
Some progression studies emphasize non-genetic plasticity and histologic evolution without defining corresponding actionable pathway states, leaving some state boundaries underspecified.

conflicting_evidence.text: Multiple papers suggest that the strongest explanation for at least part of the driver-negative fraction is not convergence onto broad states but discovery of rare discrete lesions such as NRG1, NTRK1, RET, MET exon 14, ARAF, or ERBB4/BRAF fusions.

special_cases: In mucinous adenocarcinoma, ligand-fusion signaling and lineage reprogramming may coexist; neither lesion-first nor state-first classification alone is sufficient.
In very early lesions such as AAH/AIS, vascular and stromal states may appear before stable tumor-cell-intrinsic addictions.
In highly unstable tumors, the dominant phenotype may be emergent from many weak lesions rather than one clear hidden event.

references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [documents unresolved oncogene-negative fraction but not a cross-state convergence theory]
Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker LUAD [closest antecedent for state-based canonical-driver-negative subclassification]
Onc-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD studies [establish one pathway-state subtype but not a unified multi-state architecture]
Nkx2-1 reprogramming and KAC lineage studies [establish lineage-state hidden biology but not a general theory of driver-negative LUAD]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing literature supports hidden fusions, rare mutations, occult pathway activation, and several independent subtype frameworks within driver-negative or near-driver-negative LUAD.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory unifies those findings into one state-convergence framework and argues that a finite set of recurrent state classes is the primary explanatory structure for oncogene-negative LUAD.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because no cited paper explicitly integrates hidden genomic lesions, phosphoproteomic signaling, stromal angiogenesis, lineage reprogramming, immune structure, and metabolic rewiring into one driver-negative state architecture, the whole theory is best classified as new.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1842.name: Regulatory-Rewiring Theory of Hidden Driver Activation in Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1842.description: A major subset of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas is driven not by undiscovered hotspot mutations in the usual kinase genes, but by rewiring of signal delivery and signal interpretation. This rewiring occurs through four under-recognized mechanisms: ligand presentation and juxtacrine/autocrine activation, noncoding cis-regulatory activation via enhancer hijacking or super-enhancer/structural-variant overlap, exon-skipping or other RNA-processing changes that remove negative regulation, and phosphoproteomic activation of signaling nodes without strong DNA-level cues. The central idea is that many driver-negative tumors are 'wiring-positive': the cancer circuit is activated, but the activating lesion resides in regulatory architecture or post-genomic state rather than in a canonical coding driver hotspot.
theory-1842.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Regulatory-Rewiring Theory of Hidden Driver Activation in Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A major subset of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas is driven not by undiscovered hotspot mutations in the usual kinase genes, but by rewiring of signal delivery and signal interpretation. This rewiring occurs through four under-recognized mechanisms: ligand presentation and juxtacrine/autocrine activation, noncoding cis-regulatory activation via enhancer hijacking or super-enhancer/structural-variant overlap, exon-skipping or other RNA-processing changes that remove negative regulation, and phosphoproteomic activation of signaling nodes without strong DNA-level cues. The central idea is that many driver-negative tumors are 'wiring-positive': the cancer circuit is activated, but the activating lesion resides in regulatory architecture or post-genomic state rather than in a canonical coding driver hotspot.

theory_statements.statement_name: Ligand and splice rewiring can substitute for classical kinase-domain activation
theory_statements.theory_statement: In oncogene-negative LUAD, receptor pathway addiction can arise when regulatory alterations change ligand presentation or receptor turnover rather than directly mutating kinase domains; therefore, ligand-fusion events and splice-mediated loss of receptor negative regulation are mechanistically equivalent to classical activating kinase mutations within the same signaling axis.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to driver-negative or pan-negative lung adenocarcinoma subsets in which canonical EGFR/KRAS/ALK/ROS1/RET/BRAF/HER2/MET hotspot drivers are absent by routine testing. Most strongly supported in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, MET-altered NSCLC, and rare ERBB-pathway hidden-driver cases. Does not imply that every hidden driver is ligand- or splice-based.
special_cases: Some ligand-fusion tumors also show strong lineage-state features, so receptor activation and lineage reprogramming may be co-primary.
In MET exon 14 skipping, signaling remains ligand-dependent rather than strictly ligand-independent; the key lesion is loss of turnover control.

supporting_evidence.text: CD74-NRG1 was recurrent in pan-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, especially in female never-smokers, and functions as a membrane-tethered ligand-presenting alteration that activates ERBB2/ERBB3 with downstream AKT and S6K signaling.

Independent work in KRAS-negative/pan-negative IMA showed CD74-NRG1-conditioned media induced HER2, HER3, ERK, and AKT phosphorylation, and this signaling was suppressed by lapatinib or afatinib, confirming ligand-driven ERBB activation.
SLC3A2-NRG1 and other NRG1 fusion partners preserved the NRG1 EGF-like domain, indicating that partner interchangeability can preserve the same ligand-presentation mechanism.

NRG1 rearrangements with multiple 5-prime partners and NRG1 outlier expression were found in driver-unknown lung cancers, supporting the broader principle that ligand-driven ERBB signaling is a recurrent hidden-driver mode beyond one fusion partner or one histology.

MET exon 14 skipping removes the juxtamembrane Y1003 Cbl-binding site, reduces ubiquitination, delays receptor downregulation, and produces sustained HGF-dependent MET and MAPK signaling in tumors otherwise wild-type for KRAS, BRAF, EGFR, and HER2.

The MET exon 14-skipping discovery paper found the event concentrated in tumors lacking canonical point drivers or canonical fusions, emphasizing that altered receptor turnover can substitute for a classic oncogenic driver.

The comprehensive molecular profiling study highlighted MET exon 14 skipping and focal MET amplification as hidden RTK activation mechanisms in oncogene-negative LUAD, reinforcing the generality of receptor regulatory rewiring.

Rare EGFR extracellular-domain mutations in pan-negative LUAD and the EGFR M277E mutant produced ligand-independent receptor activation and sensitivity to EGFR-directed agents, illustrating that noncanonical receptor activation can occur outside standard kinase-domain hotspot logic.

Rare HER2 extracellular-domain and transmembrane-domain mutations in pan-negative LUAD were also found outside routine kinase-domain screening, again showing that receptor-axis activation can be hidden in noncanonical regulatory domains.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual ligand-fusion and exon-skipping mechanisms are well documented, particularly NRG1 fusions and MET exon 14 skipping.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the explicit abstraction that these should be grouped as one regulatory-rewiring class of hidden receptor activation, because both replace classical kinase mutation by altering how signaling is delivered or terminated.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is broader than any single paper's interpretation but still closely connected to existing mechanistic work on NRG1 and MET.
references: Fernandez-Cuesta et al. CD74-NRG1 studies [establish ligand-fusion ERBB activation in IMA]
Ma et al. and related MET exon 14 studies [establish splice-mediated MET activation via loss of receptor downregulation]
Oncogenic EGFR extracellular-domain mutation studies [show noncanonical receptor activation outside kinase hotspots]

theory_statements.statement_name: Noncoding and phosphoproteomic rewiring reveals a hidden driver class missed by mutation panels
theory_statements.theory_statement: When canonical coding drivers are absent, oncogenic dependency in LUAD can arise from cis-regulatory genome rewiring or from protein-state activation not predicted by RNA or DNA; therefore, enhancer architecture, structural variation, and phosphosite outliers should be treated as primary driver-discovery spaces rather than secondary annotations.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to non-CAGA LUAD, RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative LUAD, proteogenomic LUAD cohorts, and candidate hidden-driver subsets where outlier expression or phosphosite activation is present without a canonical coding explanation. Most relevant to tumors unresolved after conventional DNA-panel testing.
special_cases: The strongest direct cis-regulatory examples in the provided evidence are in LUAD for ERBB2 and in other cancers for IRS4/IGF2; cross-cancer generalization is supported mechanistically but not yet fully frequency-validated in LUAD.

Some phosphosite states occur downstream of known drivers rather than replacing them; those cases support the importance of protein-state measurement but not necessarily driver-negative biology specifically.

supporting_evidence.text: LUAD without clinically actionable genetic alterations showed super-enhancer and structural-variant overlap enriched in non-CAGA tumors, with outlier expression of cancer genes and recurrence risk associations, implying hidden driver activity in regulatory rather than coding space.

ERBB2/HER2 overexpression in non-CAGA LUAD was directly linked to super-enhancer formation plus structural rearrangement; CRISPR engineering of the inversion increased HER2 expression, and PDXs responded to poziotinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan but not afatinib.

The same paper treats wild-type ERBB2 activation by structural/epigenetic rewiring as a hidden driver-like event, explicitly showing that actionable pathway addiction can arise without canonical HER2 mutation.

RPA(-)G LUAD identified recurrent ILF2 promoter mutations within open chromatin, increased ILF2 expression, and correlation with higher structural-variant burden, nominating noncoding promoter mutation as a hidden driver mechanism in RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD.

The CESAM framework and its pan-cancer validations show that cis-expression structural alteration mapping can recover hidden regulatory drivers missed by coding-centric methods, providing a methodological precedent for hidden cis-drivers in lung cancer.

Proteogenomic LUAD identified phosphosite outliers that were not captured by RNA abundance, including SHP2 Y62 phosphorylation in EGFR-mutant tumors, ALK Y1507 in ALK fusions, SOS1 S1161 in KRAS-mutant tumors, KEAP1/NRF2 phosphosite activation, and neutrophil degranulation uniquely at the protein level in STK11-mutant tumors.

The proteogenomic study explicitly states that pathway activation in tumors lacking known targetable mutations may be detectable only at protein/phosphosite level, making phosphoproteomics a primary—not merely confirmatory—hidden-driver assay.

Outlier kinase expression without detectable rearrangement, such as ROS1 or FGFR3 overexpression in driver-unknown tumors, suggests that some hidden drivers act through abnormal expression states even when the structural cause remains unresolved.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Enhancer hijacking, structural-regulatory activation, and phosphoproteomic outlier signaling are known concepts, and ERBB2 super-enhancer activation in non-CAGA LUAD is directly shown in the evidence.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is to combine cis-regulatory rewiring and protein-state rewiring into one hidden-driver class for oncogene-negative LUAD and to argue that these spaces deserve first-line driver discovery status.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The ingredients are established, but the integrated driver-negative interpretation that treats regulatory architecture and phosphosite state as co-equal discovery domains is not standard in LUAD driver theory.
references: Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in LUAD without clinically actionable genetic alterations [direct LUAD precedent for cis-regulatory hidden-driver activation]
Pan-cancer analysis of somatic copy number alterations implicates IRS4 and IGF2 in enhancer hijacking [broad methodological precedent for hidden cis-drivers]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [direct precedent for phosphoproteomic hidden-state discovery]

new_predictions_likely: If canonical-driver-negative LUADs are profiled with matched RNA-seq, WGS, and H3K27ac/ATAC-based regulatory mapping, additional cases of wild-type oncogene activation by structural-regulatory rewiring will be found beyond ERBB2.

If driver-negative LUADs with strong pERK or pAKT but no canonical coding lesion are analyzed for promoter/enhancer structural abnormalities and exon-skipping events, a substantial fraction will reveal regulatory or RNA-processing hidden drivers.
If pan-negative mucinous LUAD is systematically screened for ligand-fusion biology rather than only kinase-fusion biology, the fraction of ERBB-axis-driven tumors will increase.
If phosphoproteomic outlier analysis is added to routine evaluation of sequencing-negative LUAD, clinically relevant target hypotheses will emerge in a subset that is invisible to DNA panels alone.
new_predictions_unknown: If a prospective treatment trial assigns sequencing-negative LUAD to therapy based primarily on phosphosite outliers and regulatory rewiring signatures rather than DNA mutations, objective response may exceed historical empiric therapy response rates.

If noncoding and phosphoproteomic hidden drivers are mapped together in single tumors, some cases may show regulatory rewiring upstream of the very phosphosite outlier that appears unexplained by DNA alone, linking chromatin and phosphoproteomic hidden drivers into one circuit.

If lineage-reprogrammed LUADs such as NKX2-1-low mucinous tumors are profiled for cis-regulatory rewiring, they may show recurrent enhancer-state dependencies that are lineage-specific and therapeutically targetable.
negative_experiments: Profile a broad canonical-driver-negative LUAD cohort with WGS, RNA-seq, and phosphoproteomics; if regulatory abnormalities and phosphosite outliers are rare and non-recurrent, the theory is weakened.
Test whether phosphosite outliers without canonical DNA lesions predict drug sensitivity better than chance; if not, phosphoproteomic rewiring may be epiphenomenal rather than driver-like.
Engineer candidate cis-regulatory or splice alterations into model systems; if they fail to reproduce signaling activation or growth phenotypes, the regulatory-rewiring interpretation is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: The strongest pan-cancer enhancer-hijacking examples in the evidence involve colorectal cancer and lung squamous carcinoma rather than LUAD, so the LUAD frequency of this mechanism beyond ERBB2 remains uncertain.
Some phosphoproteomic outliers cited in the evidence occur in canonical driver-positive tumors and may reflect downstream consequence rather than alternate driver mechanism.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several large driver-negative cohorts are best explained by rare coding lesions or tumor suppressor-loss states rather than by regulatory rewiring, implying that regulatory hidden drivers may be important but not dominant across all driver-negative LUAD.

special_cases: Mucinous adenocarcinoma is a privileged context for ligand-fusion hidden drivers and should be analyzed separately from nonmucinous driver-negative LUAD.
Cis-regulatory activation may be highly lineage-specific, so the relevant hidden driver can depend on cell-of-origin state.
Protein-state hidden drivers can coexist with coding hidden drivers; these are not mutually exclusive classes.

references: Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in LUAD without clinically actionable genetic alterations [direct LUAD regulatory-rewiring precedent]
Pan-cancer analysis of somatic copy number alterations implicates IRS4 and IGF2 in enhancer hijacking [general cis-regulatory hidden-driver framework]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [direct precedent for protein-state hidden dependencies]
CD74-NRG1 and MET exon 14 skipping studies [direct precedents for ligand and splice rewiring]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Regulatory activation, ligand fusions, exon skipping, and phosphoproteomic hidden signaling are individually established in cancer and partly in LUAD.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novelty is grouping them into a single 'regulatory rewiring' theory of driver-negative LUAD and elevating enhancer architecture and phosphosite state to primary driver-discovery status.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is built from existing mechanisms but combines them in a way not explicitly articulated in the provided LUAD literature, so it is somewhat related to existing theories rather than fully existing.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1843.name: Distributed Oncogenesis Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1843.description: Oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not a single disease class but an admixture of three mechanistic reservoirs: (A) occult canonical or near-canonical oncogenic lesions missed by limited assays or tumor purity constraints, (B) noncanonical activation of growth pathways through negative-regulator loss, splice alterations, noncoding regulatory rewiring, or proteomic/phosphoproteomic activation states, and (C) state-dominant tumors in which progression is driven more by lineage plasticity, stromal or immune ecosystem programs, angiogenesis, or non-genetic evolution than by a single dominant kinase lesion. The apparent persistence of a driver-negative fraction after broader sequencing reflects mixing of these reservoirs under one label. The theory predicts that assay choice, subtype classification, and therapy selection should be reservoir-specific rather than uniform.
theory-1843.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Distributed Oncogenesis Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not a single disease class but an admixture of three mechanistic reservoirs: (A) occult canonical or near-canonical oncogenic lesions missed by limited assays or tumor purity constraints, (B) noncanonical activation of growth pathways through negative-regulator loss, splice alterations, noncoding regulatory rewiring, or proteomic/phosphoproteomic activation states, and (C) state-dominant tumors in which progression is driven more by lineage plasticity, stromal or immune ecosystem programs, angiogenesis, or non-genetic evolution than by a single dominant kinase lesion. The apparent persistence of a driver-negative fraction after broader sequencing reflects mixing of these reservoirs under one label. The theory predicts that assay choice, subtype classification, and therapy selection should be reservoir-specific rather than uniform.

theory_statements.statement_name: Residual driver-negativity is a mixture phenomenon, not a single biological category
theory_statements.theory_statement: The residual driver-negative fraction in LUAD persists after progressively broader profiling because the label aggregates at least three distinct classes: occult canonical-like lesions detectable with deeper/broader genomic assays, noncanonical pathway activators that require integrative multi-omic readouts, and state-dominant tumors whose biology is organized primarily by lineage, immune, stromal, or spatial programs rather than a single canonical oncogene.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma cohorts called driver-negative, oncogene-negative, triple-negative, pan-negative, RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative, EGFR/ALK-wild-type, or similar after routine or study-specific molecular testing. Most applicable to resected or treatment-naive human LUAD cohorts, including never-smoker-enriched cohorts, but also to metastatic cohorts where assay incompleteness and tumor heterogeneity affect driver detection. Not intended for clearly oncogene-addicted EGFR-, ALK-, ROS1-, RET-, METex14-, KRAS-, BRAF-, ERBB2- or NTRK-driven tumors once those lesions are definitively identified.
special_cases: Percentages are not directly comparable across studies because negative definitions vary substantially, ranging from EGFR/KRAS/ALK-only exclusion to broader RTK/RAS/RAF/fusion/exon-skipping panels.
Some apparent driver-negative cases reflect incomplete testing or low tumor cellularity rather than distinct biology.

Advanced treated tumors may acquire reservoir-B or reservoir-C features secondarily, so untreated primary tumors and heavily treated metastatic tumors should be analyzed separately when testing the theory.

Never-smoker cohorts are more likely to over-represent fusion, ligand, lineage-state, and angiogenic/stromal mechanisms, whereas smoking-associated cohorts may over-represent tumor-suppressor-loss, CIN, APOBEC, and checkpoint-defective mechanisms.

supporting_evidence.text: Broad genomic profiling repeatedly reduces the apparent negative fraction by uncovering missed actionable lesions. In standard-testing-negative LUAD, CGP found genomic alterations in 94% of cases and guideline-defined actionable alterations in 31%, including ROS1/RET fusions, EGFR, KRAS, ERBB2, MET, BRAF, NF1, PI3K and cell-cycle lesions; ROS1-rearranged patients then showed objective clinical responses to ceritinib.

In never-smoker LUAD initially called oncogene-negative by WES, many cases were later reclassified by deeper sequencing or orthogonal clinical testing as harboring ALK or ROS1 fusions, EGFR, ERBB2, KRAS, MET exon 14, or AKT1 alterations; lower tumor cellularity was specifically associated with the initially negative calls.

Whole-genome reanalysis of RTK/RAS/RAF pathway-negative LUAD showed that a substantial fraction of WES-negative tumors actually carried cryptic pathway lesions detectable by WGS, reducing the truly negative subset and demonstrating reservoir A of occult canonical-like events.

Multiple studies directly discovered hidden canonical-like drivers in ostensibly negative tumors: KIF5B-RET in triple-negative LUAD, KIF5B-RET in additional fusion-negative cohorts, NTRK1 rearrangements in tumors lacking known oncogenic alterations, NRG1 fusions in pan-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, MET exon 14 skipping in tumors lacking common point drivers and canonical fusions, and rare noncanonical EGFR, HER2, ARAF and RIT1 activating mutations.

Driver-negative tumors also contain noncanonical pathway activators that are not simple missed canonical events: cooperative NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss creates an oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K subtype with pERK/pAKT activation and SHP2+AKT inhibitor sensitivity; concurrent RASA1/NF1 loss defines a smoking-associated MEK-sensitive NSCLC subset; KEAP1, STK11 and SMARCA4 focal deletions and ILF2 promoter mutations recur in RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative LUAD; ERBB2 can be activated by super-enhancer plus structural rearrangement without canonical ERBB2 mutation.

Proteogenomics reveals pathway states not captured by DNA or RNA alone, indicating a second noncanonical reservoir. Examples include SHP2/PTPN11 Y62 phosphorylation in EGFR-mutant tumors without corresponding RNA/protein increase, SOS1 S1161 phosphorylation in KRAS-mutant tumors, ARHGEF5 Y1370 phosphorylation and reduced SRGAP1 in never-smokers, KEAP1/NFE2L2 phosphosite activation, and an STK11-associated neutrophil degranulation program seen only at the protein level.

Several cohorts resolve ostensibly driver-negative disease into transcriptomic or proteogenomic states organized more by immunity, angiogenesis, metabolism, proliferation, ferroptosis resistance, or microenvironment than by hidden kinase lesions. EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD segregates into proliferation-high, immune-high, angiogenesis-high and metabolism-high subtypes; a 5-IRGP signature stratifies driver-negative LUAD by immune context and checkpoint expression; ferroptosis-related and FeSig signatures define EGFR-wild-type, fibroblast-rich, ferroptosis-resistant groups.

Early progression studies in never-smokers identify preinvasive and minimally invasive states whose key transition drivers are angiogenesis, endothelial tip-cell programs, matrix CAFs, and ecosystem shifts rather than new canonical oncogenes, indicating a third state-dominant reservoir. AIS3 is highlighted as the immediate pre-invasive angiogenic/CAF-rich state; MIA invasive regions show endothelial enrichment; EcoTyper assigns progression-associated ecotypes across lesions.

NKX2-1-low mucinous/gastric-like LUAD, KAC/alveolar-intermediate states, and non-genetic lepidic-to-solid progression illustrate that some tumors are better explained by lineage plasticity and spatial state evolution than by undiscovered canonical oncogenes. NKX2-1 loss unlocks a latent gastric program; KRT8+ alveolar intermediate cells and KRAS-signature-high KAC states precede or accompany transformation; histologic progression can occur without recurrent pattern-specific driver lesions.

Several studies explicitly note that many LUADs remain unexplained by known hallmark alterations or known driver genes, and propose additional hallmarks such as epigenetic/RNA deregulation or pathway-level hidden dependencies, supporting the claim that the negative category contains biologically distinct unresolved reservoirs rather than one missing gene list.

Some cohorts define driver-negative purely operationally and do not resolve hidden biology, reinforcing that technical and definitional heterogeneity itself contributes to the persistence of the label. Examples include the LCMC no-driver category, broad clinical driver-negative cohorts, and hallmarks-based unresolved tumors.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: The literature already shows that some apparently negative LUADs are explained by rare hidden drivers, some by pathway-state biology detectable only with multi-omics, and some by lineage or microenvironmental states. Individual studies, however, usually emphasize only one of these mechanisms at a time.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit claim that the persistent driver-negative fraction is durable because it is a mixed category composed of three separable reservoirs, and that the durability of the label is therefore expected rather than paradoxical.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is not a standard named theory in the cited literature; it is an integrative framework synthesizing multiple partially overlapping explanations into a single causal architecture for the residual negative fraction.
references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [documents hidden alterations in oncogene-negative LUAD but not a three-reservoir architecture]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2021) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [supports unresolved heterogeneity within pathway-negative LUAD]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [supports proteomic hidden-state biology but not reservoir mixing theory]
Kim et al. (2024) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [supports ecosystem/state subtypes within negative cohorts]
Lang et al. (2024) Multiomics Analysis Reveals Molecular Changes during Early Progression of Precancerous Lesions to Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [supports state-dominant progression biology]

theory_statements.statement_name: Reservoir membership predicts the correct discovery and validation stack
theory_statements.theory_statement: Each mechanistic reservoir has a distinct optimal validation stack: occult canonical-like lesions require deeper and broader DNA/RNA assays with attention to purity and fusion/splicing detection; noncanonical pathway activators require integrated DNA/RNA/protein/phosphoprotein and often chromatin or 3D-genome assays; state-dominant tumors require single-cell, spatial, and perturbational analysis of lineage, stromal, and immune circuits. Therefore, profiling technologies are not interchangeable in driver-negative LUAD.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to translational discovery design, biomarker development, and assay triage for unresolved or ostensibly driver-negative LUAD. Most relevant in fresh-frozen or well-preserved biospecimens where RNA, phosphoprotein, and spatial assays are feasible, but the prediction also applies to retrospective FFPE-driven workflows when assay ordering must be optimized. Not intended to replace standard targeted genotyping in clearly driver-positive disease.
special_cases: A single tumor may occupy more than one reservoir and therefore require more than one validation stack.

Rare kinase fusions with strong genomic evidence may not require full spatial or proteomic profiling for initial therapeutic assignment, though such profiling may still explain resistance or phenotype.

Fresh-tissue phosphoproteomics and spatial assays may be technically infeasible in many routine clinical samples, so some reservoir-B and reservoir-C cases will remain unresolved under FFPE-only workflows.

supporting_evidence.text: Deeper or broader genomic/transcriptomic assays were essential for solving reservoir-A cases. KIF5B-RET was found by whole-genome and transcriptome sequencing in triple-negative LUAD; NTRK1 fusions were detected by targeted NGS plus FISH and RT-PCR in tumors lacking known oncogenic alterations; MET exon 14 skipping required RNA/WGS-level interrogation; and broad CGP rescued many standard-testing-negative cases.

Noncanonical enhancer- or topology-driven drivers cannot be solved by standard DNA hotspot testing alone. ERBB2 activation in non-CAGA LUAD required WGS, H3K27Ac ChIP-seq, Hi-C, long-read sequencing, CRISPR rearrangement engineering, and PDX pharmacology; CESAM similarly used breakpoint-aware cis-expression mapping, TAD structure, and chromatin validation to identify enhancer hijacking in other cancers.

Proteogenomics reveals hidden pathway dependence missed by DNA/RNA. In treatment-naive LUAD, phosphoproteomics uncovered SHP2 Y62 activation in EGFR-mutant tumors, SOS1 phosphorylation in KRAS-mutant tumors, ARHGEF5 phosphorylation in never-smokers, KEAP1/NFE2L2 phosphosite changes, and a proteomics-only neutrophil degranulation program in STK11-mutant tumors. These states would not have been inferred reliably from RNA alone.

Integrated proteogenomic clustering specifically solved negative-cohort heterogeneity into proliferation-, immune-, angiogenesis- and metabolism-high subtypes in EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, and into STK11/FGL1/CPS1-defined immune-cold subtype 4 in NSCLC, demonstrating that reservoir-B and reservoir-C tumors require multi-omic state profiling rather than only mutation calling.

Single-cell and spatial profiling were necessary to identify state-dominant progression circuits: AIS3 required nonimmune-cell marker clustering, deconvolution, SCISSOR and spatial transcriptomics to reveal tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs; KAC/alveolar intermediate biology required scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, lineage tracing, and organoid perturbation; EcoTyper/ecotype analysis was needed to define progression ecosystems.

Lineage-state mechanisms required chromatin and transcription-factor occupancy assays, not just mutation profiling. NKX2-1-loss gastric differentiation was established using conditional genetics, microarray/qRT-PCR, ChIP-seq for NKX2-1 and Foxa1/2, histone-mark profiling, rescue experiments, and human tumor IHC.

Even when a study focused on a prognostic or biologic state rather than a hidden lesion, the decisive assay stack matched the proposed reservoir: driver-negative immune states were identified by transcriptomic IRGPs and immune deconvolution; FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD required single-cell interaction analysis plus functional knockdown and xenografts; glycolytic MIF-HIF-1alpha biology required bulk RNA, single-cell RNA, and cell perturbation experiments.

Direct methodological studies support assay specialization itself: CESAM was explicitly built to detect cis-regulatory hidden drivers using breakpoint, expression and TAD-aware integration, and the structural-variation/3D-genome review argues that many hidden oncogenic SVs require topology-aware interpretation rather than coding-focused sequencing.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Different studies already use different technologies to discover hidden lesions, phospho-state dependencies, or ecosystem states in LUAD. The literature also separately shows that no single platform is sufficient for all unresolved tumors.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit law-like claim that the correct assay stack can be predicted from the tumor's dominant reservoir, converting assay selection from an ad hoc methodological choice into a mechanistic consequence of tumor class.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a new translational synthesis rather than a pre-existing named theory. It generalizes from disparate studies to propose reservoir-specific technology triage as a testable principle.
references: Carrot-Zhang et al. (2021) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [shows WGS solves some but not all WES-negative cases]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [shows phosphoproteomics identifies hidden pathway states]

Kim et al. (2024) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [shows integrated proteogenomics resolves negative-cohort heterogeneity]

Cui et al. (2024) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [shows noncoding hidden drivers need chromatin and SV assays]

Lang et al. (2024) Multiomics Analysis Reveals Molecular Changes during Early Progression of Precancerous Lesions to Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [shows spatial/single-cell assays are required for state-dominant progression biology]

new_predictions_likely: In a prospective unresolved LUAD cohort, cases with mucinous histology, low purity, or discordant clinical suspicion for fusion-driven disease will disproportionately resolve with deep RNA sequencing and long-read or hybrid-capture fusion detection, consistent with reservoir A.

In unresolved LUADs with strong pERK, pAKT, pS6, SHP2, SOS1, NRF2, or neutrophil-degranulation protein signatures but no canonical lesion, reanalysis with WGS plus proteogenomics and chromatin-aware methods will be enriched for negative-regulator loss, regulatory SVs, enhancer rewiring, or phospho-state drivers, consistent with reservoir B.

Tumors that remain unresolved after broad DNA/RNA and phosphoproteomic profiling will cluster more strongly by spatial ecosystem features, lineage-state markers, or stromal architecture than by additional mutational burden, consistent with reservoir C.

A triage workflow using four initial features—tumor purity, mucinous/lineage pathology, phosphoprotein outliers, and spatial stromal complexity—will solve more hidden-driver cases per sample than a uniform panel-plus-WES workflow.
new_predictions_unknown: A low-cost preclassification model built from pathology, shallow RNA, selected phosphoproteins, and one spatial metric will be able to assign a dominant reservoir before full profiling and thereby improve discovery yield and trial enrollment efficiency.
Some reservoir-C tumors will fail lesion-matched targeted therapy even when a rare kinase lesion is found, because lineage or ecosystem circuitry dominates phenotype and resistance from baseline.

A subset of persistent unresolved cases will prove to be driven by transient or oscillatory signaling states detectable only in fresh-tissue phosphoproteomics or ex vivo perturbational phosphodynamics, not in static DNA/RNA assays.
Reservoir B and C may be partially convertible states: blocking one stromal or lineage circuit may unmask latent oncogene addiction in a subset of tumors.
negative_experiments: Apply a uniform maximal profiling stack (deep WGS, long-read fusion/splicing analysis, proteomics/phosphoproteomics, ChIP-based enhancer mapping, and spatial/single-cell profiling) to a large unresolved LUAD cohort. If almost all tumors fall into one common mechanism rather than multiple separable classes, the reservoir-mixing claim is weakened.

Test whether pre-specified features predict which assay stack solves a case. If purity, histology, phosphoprotein state, and spatial architecture fail to enrich for distinct solution modalities, the theory loses its translational specificity.
Examine whether tumors unresolved by DNA/RNA/protein profiling are truly enriched for lineage/ecosystem explanations. If they are not, reservoir C is overstated.

Compare outcomes of assay-triaged versus uniform workflows in a prospective study. If triage does not improve hidden-driver yield, time-to-solution, or subtype reproducibility, the theory's practical claim is undermined.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not yet specify stable proportions for reservoirs A, B, and C, because negative definitions, assay depth, specimen type, and smoking context differ too much across studies to estimate a universal mixture fraction.

Some studies define driver-negative only by absence of EGFR or a narrow panel rather than all actionable drivers, making it unclear whether their subtypes represent true residual-negative biology or incomplete exclusion of reservoir-A lesions.

conflicting_evidence.text: Broad genomic profiling and deeper sequencing can solve a substantial fraction of apparent negatives, suggesting that in some cohorts reservoir A may dominate and reducing the immediate need to invoke state-dominant biology for every unresolved case.

Some cohorts, especially never-smoker EGFR/ALK-wild-type cohorts, already classify tumors effectively by multi-omic states without directly demonstrating that those states are mutually exclusive from hidden canonical lesions, so the reservoir boundaries may be probabilistic rather than sharp.

special_cases: Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma is unusually enriched for ligand and kinase fusions such as NRG1, ERBB4, BRAF, and RET, so it disproportionately samples reservoir A plus lineage reservoir C.
EGFR-wild-type and never-smoker cohorts may contain APOBEC-high and ferroptosis-resistant subsets that blur the B/C boundary because pathway-state and mutagenic-process effects coexist.
TP53-, KEAP1-, STK11-, and CIN/APOBEC-dominant tumors may accumulate additional secondary drivers over time, complicating reservoir assignment in late-stage disease.
Some 'driver-negative' cohorts are only negative relative to local clinical practice rather than biologically exhaustive profiling.

references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [related; shows hidden genomic drivers and oncogene-negative fraction but not reservoir mixture theory]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2021) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [related; unresolved pathway-negative heterogeneity]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [related; proteomic hidden-state dependencies]
Kim et al. (2024) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [related; state-based negative-cohort subtyping]

Lang et al. (2024) Multiomics Analysis Reveals Molecular Changes during Early Progression of Precancerous Lesions to Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [related; microenvironmental and spatial progression states]

Xu et al. (2024) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [related; noncoding hidden-driver mechanism]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work separately supports missed rare drivers, noncanonical pathway activation, and lineage or microenvironmental state explanations for unresolved LUAD. Assay heterogeneity and persistent residual-negative fractions are also well documented.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel component is the explicit three-reservoir architecture and the linked claim that each reservoir predicts a different optimal discovery and validation technology stack.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No supplied paper frames the residual driver-negative category as a stable admixture of three causal reservoirs with assay-specific consequences. The theory is therefore a new synthesis built from existing partial mechanisms.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1844.name: Convergent Output Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1844.description: Most driver-negative LUADs converge onto a small number of phenotypic output states even when their initiating alterations differ. The dominant outputs are: (1) RTK/RAS/PI3K phospho-active growth signaling, (2) cell-cycle/checkpoint and DNA-repair addiction, (3) immune-evasive or stromal-excluded ecosystem states, and (4) lineage/metabolic reprogramming states such as mucinous-gastric, alveolar-intermediate, ferroptosis-resistant, or liver-like STK11/FGL1 programs. Hidden drivers matter clinically insofar as they generate one of these outputs. Therefore, subclassification of unresolved LUAD should prioritize measuring output states rather than only searching for ever-rarer initiating lesions.
theory-1844.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Convergent Output Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Most driver-negative LUADs converge onto a small number of phenotypic output states even when their initiating alterations differ. The dominant outputs are: (1) RTK/RAS/PI3K phospho-active growth signaling, (2) cell-cycle/checkpoint and DNA-repair addiction, (3) immune-evasive or stromal-excluded ecosystem states, and (4) lineage/metabolic reprogramming states such as mucinous-gastric, alveolar-intermediate, ferroptosis-resistant, or liver-like STK11/FGL1 programs. Hidden drivers matter clinically insofar as they generate one of these outputs. Therefore, subclassification of unresolved LUAD should prioritize measuring output states rather than only searching for ever-rarer initiating lesions.

theory_statements.statement_name: Diverse hidden lesions converge onto a limited set of output states
theory_statements.theory_statement: Despite genetic heterogeneity, driver-negative LUAD recurrently converges onto a small set of functional output states—phospho-active RTK/RAS/PI3K signaling, checkpoint/cell-cycle and DNA-repair dependency, immune-excluded or immune-hot stromal ecosystems, and lineage/metabolic reprogramming—and these outputs are more reproducible across cohorts than any single rare hidden lesion.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to unresolved or weakly explained LUAD after standard driver assessment, especially cohorts with multi-omic, transcriptomic, proteomic, spatial, or single-cell measurements. Most applicable to primary adenocarcinomas, including never-smoker and EGFR/ALK-wild-type cohorts, but also relevant to smoking-associated NSCLC when pathway output dominates over individual rare lesions. Not intended for tumors already cleanly explained by a single strong canonical oncogene with concordant downstream state.
special_cases: A tumor may occupy more than one output state simultaneously, for example RTK/RAS phospho-activation plus stromal exclusion or metabolic rewiring plus immune coldness.
Some outputs are assay-dependent; phospho-active states require fresh or high-quality proteomic material, whereas ecosystem and lineage states can often be seen in RNA or spatial data.
High-TMB and highly unstable tumors may show noisy initiating lesions, making output-state measurement especially advantageous.

supporting_evidence.text: A phospho-active RAS/PI3K output state recurs in ostensibly negative tumors. Oncogene-negative LUAD with NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss shows pERK and pAKT positivity in mouse and human tumors; SHP2 and AKT inhibition suppresses the state. Concurrent RASA1/NF1 loss in smoking-associated NSCLC similarly creates MAPK-dominant dependence and trametinib sensitivity.

Independent hidden-driver lesions also converge on the same growth-signaling outputs: CD74-NRG1 and SLC3A2-NRG1 activate HER2/HER3-ERK-AKT signaling; EZR-ERBB4 activates ERBB4-ERK; TRIM24-BRAF activates ERK; NTRK1 fusions activate MAPK and AKT; MET exon 14 skipping produces sustained ligand-dependent MAPK signaling; EGFR and HER2 extracellular or transmembrane mutations, ARAF and RIT1 mutations all signal through MAPK and/or PI3K pathways.

Proteogenomics repeatedly identifies pathway output states more clearly than initiating lesions. SHP2 Y62, SOS1 S1161, NRF2 phosphosites, ALK Y1507, ARHGEF5 Y1370, and proteomics-only neutrophil degranulation all represent output states linked to but not reducible to DNA mutation status.

A second recurrent output state is checkpoint/cell-cycle and DNA-repair dependency. Driver-negative EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative LUAD is enriched for cell-cycle and DNA-repair pathway mutations; ATP/ATM/p53 axis disruption is recurrent; TP53-mutant or checkpoint-defective proteogenomic states show mismatch-repair and DNA-damage-response upregulation; CDKN2A loss with CDK4/MDM2 gain defines a hidden cell-cycle subset; proliferative proteogenomic subtype P is driven by CDK/PLK/ATR/E2F activity.

A third recurrent output state is immune and stromal ecosystem organization. Driver-negative LUAD can be partitioned into immune-high, angiogenesis-high, and fibroblast-rich states; STK11/FGL1 subtype 4 is immune-cold; high-risk IRGP and FerRLSig states show checkpoint-high immunosuppression; angiogenesis-high never-smoker LUAD shows CAF-mediated CD8 exclusion and proangiogenic ligand expression.

A fourth recurrent output state is lineage or metabolic reprogramming. NKX2-1-low tumors adopt a gastric/mucinous program; KAC and KRAS-signature-high states reflect alveolar-intermediate transformation; ferroptosis-resistant EGFR-wild-type tumors are fibroblast-rich and FeSig-high; STK11/HNF1A/FGL1/CPS1 tumors adopt a liver-like metabolic program; metabolism-high negative-cohort subtype M shows oxidative phosphorylation/lipid signatures.

Several studies show that transcriptomic or proteogenomic subtypes are clinically more stable than individual rare alterations. Never-smoker LUAD can be organized into steady, proliferative, and chaotic states; into IM-1/2/3 immune subtypes; or into P/I/A/M proteogenomic subtypes, each with prognostic and therapeutic implications independent of a single rare lesion.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Many studies already classify LUAD by immune state, proteogenomic subtype, or lineage program, and many others describe hidden lesions converging on MAPK or PI3K. The idea of pathway convergence itself is also familiar in cancer biology.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the claim that output-state convergence is the main organizing principle of driver-negative LUAD and is more reproducible and therapeutically relevant than the initiating hidden lesion class.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory is a new integration of pathway-state, ecosystem-state, and lineage-state observations into a common output-centric framework for unresolved LUAD.
references: Wilkerson et al. (2012) Lung adenocarcinoma subtypes [related transcriptomic subtype tradition]
Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [related pathway convergence logic]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [related output-state evidence]
Kim et al. (2024) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [related state-subtype evidence]

theory_statements.statement_name: Therapy should be matched to output state before hidden lesion identity is complete
theory_statements.theory_statement: In driver-negative LUAD, therapeutic prioritization should be guided first by measurable output state—growth-signal phospho-activation, checkpoint/cell-cycle addiction, immune/stromal ecosystem state, or lineage/metabolic reprogramming—because these states predict vulnerability more directly than the exact hidden lesion and often remain targetable even when the initiating lesion is unknown or undruggable.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to biomarker-guided therapeutic hypothesis generation in unresolved LUAD and adjacent NSCLC contexts where canonical actionable drivers are absent or insufficient to explain phenotype. Best suited to preclinical-to-translational settings and not yet a validated standard-of-care rule.
special_cases: When a directly druggable kinase fusion or canonical lesion is identified, lesion-matched therapy still has priority unless output-state data argue strongly for immediate combination therapy or likely resistance.
Immune output states may not translate cleanly across histologies or treatment lines, especially in previously treated tumors.
Output-state-directed treatment may fail if the state is reactive rather than causal.

supporting_evidence.text: Growth-signal output states show actionable vulnerability even when hidden lesions differ. Oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K tumors respond to SHP2 plus AKT inhibition; RASA1/NF1-deficient NSCLC is trametinib-sensitive; NTRK1 fusions are sensitive to TRKA inhibitors; NRG1/ERBB4/BRAF fusion-driven IMA is suppressed by HER-family, RAF, or MEK inhibitors; ARAF/RAF1 hotspot mutants are sorafenib- and trametinib-sensitive.

Cell-cycle/checkpoint output states imply therapeutic direction even without a single hidden lesion. Driver-negative cell-cycle subsets are specifically proposed for CDK4/6, ATR, PLK, or MDM2 targeting; TP53-mutant DNA-repair states suggest PARP or EZH2 logic; proliferative subtype P is linked to CDK/PLK/ATR dependencies.

Immune and stromal output states also imply therapy classes. The immune-high subtype suggests checkpoint or cytokine-axis targeting; high-risk IRGP and FerRLSig groups show higher PD-1/PD-L1/CTLA4 and are hypothesized to be more immunotherapy-responsive; angiogenesis-high subtype suggests anti-angiogenic and stromal-targeting approaches; STK11/FGL1/LAG3 states suggest mTOR plus LAG3/FGL1-directed combinations.

Lineage/metabolic output states similarly generate therapy hypotheses. FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type tumors are selectively vulnerable to 6-MP in vitro and in vivo; MIF-HIF-1alpha-high driver-negative tumors are sensitive to MIF inhibition in cell systems; KAC/KRAS-state organoids are suppressed by KRAS G12D inhibition; metabolism-high subtype M shows ACACA dependency and clorsulon candidacy.

Several studies explicitly show that prognostic or therapeutic stratification improves when subtypes or output states are used instead of relying solely on genomic lesion identity: the 60-gene classifier recovers the aggressive chaotic subtype; immune subtypes refine ICB prediction; S3/PI-like states predict ICI response better than checkpoint markers alone in some cohorts.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: State-based therapeutic reasoning already exists in immuno-oncology, proteogenomics, and synthetic-lethality studies, and many supplied papers propose vulnerabilities for particular subtypes.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit rule that output-state assignment should precede complete hidden-lesion resolution in therapeutic prioritization for driver-negative LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This extends existing subtype and pathway-vulnerability observations into a broader output-first therapeutic principle for unresolved LUAD.
references: Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [related output-state vulnerability mapping]
Kim et al. (2024) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [related subtype-specific vulnerability proposals]
Cui et al. (2023) Ferroptosis-related gene signature for predicting prognosis and identifying potential therapeutic drug in EGFR wild-type lung adenocarcinoma [related output-state therapy logic]

new_predictions_likely: Across independent unresolved LUAD cohorts, clustering on phosphoprotein, immune/stromal, and lineage/metabolic output features will be more reproducible than clustering on rare hidden genomic lesions alone.
Response to pathway-targeted perturbation in organoids or ex vivo slices will correlate more strongly with output-state assignment than with the presence of any one rare hidden lesion.
Tumors with immune-cold STK11/FGL1 or angiogenesis-high/CAF-rich outputs will show inferior monotherapy ICI response even when TMB or a rare targetable lesion is present.

A subset of genomically heterogeneous mucinous or EGFR-wild-type tumors will share convergent ERK/AKT or metabolic output states and therefore share drug sensitivity despite different initiating lesions.
new_predictions_unknown: An output-state atlas of unresolved LUAD built from joint phosphoproteomics, spatial transcriptomics, and perturbation will reveal a small number of recurrent therapeutic states that cut across smoking status, histology, and hidden-lesion identity.
Some tumors currently considered resistant to targeted therapy because of absent actionable lesions will respond if assigned by output-state biomarkers alone, without complete genomic explanation.
Transition between output states may explain early resistance more often than acquisition of a new genomic driver in a subset of driver-negative LUAD.
negative_experiments: Test whether output-state clustering is actually more reproducible across cohorts than lesion-based clustering. If not, the theory's central organizing principle is weakened.

Directly compare whether ex vivo drug response correlates better with hidden lesion identity or with output-state assignment. If lesion identity consistently outperforms output states, the theory is weakened.
Evaluate whether tumors assigned to the same output state but with different hidden lesions truly share vulnerabilities. If they do not, convergence is overstated.
If spatial and proteomic outputs are mostly reactive consequences rather than stable tumor-intrinsic states, output-first therapy should fail prospectively.

unaccounted_for.text: Some clinically observed responses are still clearly lesion-matched, such as ROS1- and ARAF-driven cases, and these can dominate over broader output-state logic in practice.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies emphasize that broader genomic testing alone identifies enough hidden canonical drivers to justify lesion-first management, potentially reducing the need for output-first classification in a sizable fraction of cases.

special_cases: Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma may need parallel lesion- and output-based classification because hidden fusions and lineage output states are both highly informative.
Output states may be stage-specific; early AIS3/MIA angiogenic outputs are not necessarily interchangeable with late metastatic angiogenic states.
EGFR-mutant or KRAS-mutant comparators can share output states with driver-negative tumors, so output-state assignment is not equivalent to driver-negativity.

references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [related pathway-convergence framework]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [related output-state therapeutic logic]
Kim et al. (2024) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [related state-first subtype logic]
He et al. (2023) A prognostic signature for lung adenocarcinoma in people who have never smoked [related transcriptomic state stratification]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: There is substantial prior work on LUAD subtypes, pathway convergence, and state-based therapeutic vulnerability. Multiple papers already show that immune, angiogenic, proliferative, or lineage states have prognostic and therapeutic relevance.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The theory's novel step is to treat these outputs as the main common language of driver-negative LUAD and to place lesion identity in a secondary role once an output state is known.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory extends existing subtype and pathway-convergence ideas into a stronger output-first organizing principle specifically for unresolved LUAD, so it is somewhat related to existing frameworks but still novel in formulation.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1845.name: State-Space Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1845.description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly driverless and are not explained by one hidden rare kinase alone.
