Liu (2021) Genomic and evolutionary classification of lung cancer in never smokers [piano tumors and hidden stemness-related drivers]

theory_statements.statement_name: Residual unresolved tumors are enriched for noncoding and protein-state drivers
theory_statements.theory_statement: After broad DNA/RNA rescue of occult canonical and noncanonical coding drivers, the remaining unresolved oncogene-negative LUADs are enriched for mechanisms best seen in chromatin-state and protein-state space: cis-regulatory structural rewiring, promoter/TSS lesions, methylation-driven expression changes, and phospho-proteomic signaling outliers. Therefore, the unresolved tail of driver-negative LUAD should shrink more by adding chromatin and protein-state assays than by simply enlarging hotspot DNA panels.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to residual unresolved LUADs after targeted panel, WES/WGS, fusion, and splice-aware analyses; especially relevant to non-CAGA LUAD, WGS-characterized RPA(-)G cases, and integrated proteogenomic cohorts where RNA abundance alone failed to explain tumor-driving signaling states.
special_cases: Some proteomic outliers are downstream consequences of already-known genomic drivers rather than independent primary drivers.
Many noncoding or epigenetic candidates remain hypothesis-generating unless orthogonally validated by chromatin conformation, CRISPR perturbation, or matched functional assays.
Certain protein-state outliers may mark vulnerability rather than initiation.

supporting_evidence.text: Non-CAGA LUADs yielded ERBB2 overexpression driven by super-enhancer formation plus structural rearrangement, discovered only through integrated WGS, H3K27Ac ChIP-seq, Hi-C, long-read sequencing, and CRISPR recreation. Routine genomic screening had classified these tumors as lacking clinically actionable alterations.

The same super-enhancer/structural-variant framework nominated additional candidate regulatory drivers such as FRS2 and CAV2, implying that residual non-CAGA cases harbor lineage-restricted transcriptional outlier mechanisms beyond canonical coding events.

In WGS-characterized RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD, recurrent ILF2 promoter mutations were associated with higher ILF2 expression, while promoter/TSS deletions in KEAP1 and STK11 and other focal noncoding deletions reduced suppressor expression; these lesions lie outside standard coding-exon assays.

The same WGS-negative-residual cohort contained complex structural classes such as double minutes and tyfonas enriched for expression outlier genes, indicating that residual driver activity can be carried by noncanonical amplification topology rather than standard focal-amplification calls alone.

Proteogenomic analyses identified signaling mechanisms not captured by RNA alone, including ARHGEF5 Y1370 phosphorylation outliers with SRGAP1 downregulation in never-smoker and ALK-fusion tumors, explicitly described as a mechanism that would be missed by RNA-centric assays.

KEAP1 G511V had little explanatory power at the RNA level but showed strong phosphoproteomic consequences and structural plausibility for disrupting the KEAP1-NFE2L2 interaction, illustrating a functionally important driver state visible mainly in protein/phosphosite space.

Integrated methylation-RNA-protein analyses found 120 methylation-driven cis effects, including CLDN18, ANK1, PTPRCAP, GSTP1, MGA, and TBX3, establishing that epigenetic cis regulation can create driver-like expression states invisible to mutation-only pipelines.

Proteogenomic analyses also found copy-number driven trans-effects and CMAP-linked downstream programs, nominating genes such as RALA, BZW2, and CTSB whose functional impact becomes evident in proteome space rather than by local DNA/RNA abnormality alone.

Whole-genome/proteogenomic studies repeatedly noted that many informative events are not captured by RNA abundance alone, that WGS alone is insufficient to define functional super-enhancer/SV interplay, and that chromatin/protein integration is required to convert residual unexplained cases into mechanistic hypotheses.

Logical induction from the evidence above: once coding rescue saturates, the residual explanatory burden shifts toward topologic cis regulation and post-transcriptional/post-translational signaling states, so larger DNA hotspot panels should produce diminishing returns relative to chromatin and phosphoproteomic profiling.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Enhancer hijacking, promoter mutation, methylation-driven cis regulation, and proteomic signaling outliers are recognized mechanisms in cancer and have been documented in lung and pan-cancer settings.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel inference is that these mechanisms form the dominant residual explanatory reservoir specifically after broad canonical DNA/RNA rescue has already been attempted in driver-negative LUAD, and that this should determine assay prioritization.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is not a new molecular mechanism per se; it is a new cross-modal prioritization rule for the unresolved subset, built by synthesis across regulatory-genomic and proteogenomic studies.
references: Kim (2023) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [direct hidden regulatory-driver example]
Suzawa (2021) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [noncoding promoter lesions in unresolved tumors]
Gillette (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [protein/phosphosite-space hidden mechanisms]

Wang (2023) Proteogenomic analysis of lung adenocarcinoma reveals tumor heterogeneity, survival determinants, and therapeutically relevant pathways [methylation cis-effects and WGS/proteogenomic residual mechanisms]

new_predictions_likely: If a contemporary 'driver-negative' LUAD cohort is re-profiled with a fixed multimodal workflow of deep DNA panel plus RNA fusion/splice sequencing plus WGS-level copy-number/SV calling plus phosphoproteomics, the largest incremental diagnostic yield over standard practice will come first from structural/splice rescue and then from regulatory/protein-state rescue in the residual cases.

Residual unresolved tumors after DNA/RNA rescue will be enriched for low-purity samples, mucinous or lineage-shifted histology, and discordance between RNA and protein/phosphosite activity, rather than for additional undiscovered hotspot SNVs.

Never-smoker driver-negative LUADs will contain a higher fraction of structural, ligand-fusion, and lineage-state driver classes, whereas smoker-associated driver-negative tumors will more often resolve into suppressor-loss or complex-SV/amplification states.

Tumors with no canonical driver but strong pERK and pAKT by IHC/proteomics will disproportionately harbor combined negative-regulator loss, especially NF1/RASA1/PTEN alterations or PTEN protein loss without genomic PTEN mutation.

A substantial minority of tumors labeled negative by panel sequencing but showing outlier receptor abundance will prove to carry either noncanonical domain mutations, splicing events, or cis-regulatory structural lesions rather than broad unexplained signaling noise.
new_predictions_unknown: A nontrivial subset of currently unresolved driver-negative LUAD will be explained by enhancer-topology lesions affecting lineage-restricted receptor-adaptor genes such as FRS2 or CAV2, but only in specific histologic or lineage contexts.

Some apparently driver-negative tumors with high APOBEC activity will prove to be maintained by distributed subclonal pathway lesions rather than a single dominant clonal oncogene, creating a true 'poly-driver' state.

A residual genomically quiet piano-like subgroup will depend on stemness regulators or chromatin-state circuits and will be more vulnerable to differentiation or chromatin-remodeling therapy than to kinase inhibition.

Phosphoproteomic outlier states in unresolved tumors will define therapeutically reproducible 'drivers without recurrent mutations', enabling treatment selection by signaling-state class rather than lesion identity.
A subset of driver-negative mucinous tumors will be found to use ligand-presenting or juxtacrine fusion logic beyond NRG1, involving other membrane-tethered growth-factor families.
negative_experiments: Profile a large independent driver-negative LUAD cohort with comprehensive DNA, RNA, copy-number, methylation, chromatin, and proteomics; if the majority of cases still do not resolve into assay-missed canonical, noncanonical coding, structural/splice/ligand, or pathway-equivalent/protein-state classes, the theory is weakened.

Test whether broader assay breadth meaningfully increases driver yield beyond standard panel testing; if rescue rates are negligible in well-powered cohorts with adequate purity, the first statement is false.

Compare pathway activation in unresolved tumors to combined suppressor-loss and protein-loss states; if pERK/pAKT activation does not track with negative-regulator loss or protein-level loss, the pathway-equivalent component is weakened.

Add chromatin and phosphoproteomic assays to residual unresolved tumors after deep DNA/RNA profiling; if these assays do not enrich for explanatory lesions or vulnerabilities compared with further hotspot-panel expansion, the third statement is weakened.

In low-purity tumors reclassified as negative by routine assays, repeat profiling with high-sensitivity orthogonal methods; if low purity does not materially affect rescue probability, the assay-relative-negativity claim is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not fully explain why some recurrent amplification loci such as 14q13.3/NKX2-1-region events fail functional sufficiency tests in certain mouse models, beyond placing them in the category of context-dependent or cooperative lesions.

The theory does not specifically explain prognostic-only transcriptomic or radiomic studies of driver-negative LUAD that did not perform molecular rescue analyses and therefore remain biology-rich but mechanism-poor.
Some studies define driver-negative disease only by EGFR or EGFR/ALK wild-type status, making them too heterogeneous to map cleanly onto the four proposed classes.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies explicitly remained unable to identify a hidden genomic driver and instead emphasized transcriptomic, microenvironmental, or cell-state features, implying that broad genomics alone is insufficient for a meaningful subset.
Expanded assays can also nominate recurrent but weakly validated candidates such as AUTS2 or PDE4D deletion, showing that broader discovery increases ambiguity as well as rescue.

Some broad copy-number gains, such as low-level 4q12 gain, did not convincingly behave as primary drivers or predict TKI sensitivity, cautioning against over-interpreting every noncanonical lesion as explanatory.

special_cases: Fusion-driven invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma is a particularly enriched special case, especially for NRG1, RET, ERBB4, and BRAF fusion classes.
Low-purity tumors can mimic biological driver-negativity by technical undercalling, especially for KRAS exon 1, fusions, and exon-skipping events.
KRAS-mutant tumors with hidden suppressor loss are not strictly oncogene-negative, but they are mechanistically informative analogs for pathway-equivalent noncanonical driver states.
Histologic clues such as mucinous cribriform pattern can function as auxiliary assays that enrich for hidden fusion drivers.

references: Shin (2015) Genomic Profiling of Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [occult canonical driver rescue in never-smoker LUAD]
Suzawa (2021) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [WGS-resolved hidden-driver framework]

Kim (2023) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [regulatory-driver extension]
Li (2021) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [pathway-equivalent noncanonical state]
Ahn (2018) Concurrent RASA1/NF1 loss in NSCLC [dual RasGAP-loss subset]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing literature already supports occult fusions/splice variants, rare noncanonical coding drivers, regulatory rewiring, and some suppressor-loss mechanisms in LUAD. Several studies also recognize that driver-negative status depends strongly on assay breadth.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory's novelty is the integrated architecture: it partitions oncogene-negative LUAD into assay-relative rescue classes plus residual regulatory/protein-state classes, and proposes an explicit hierarchy for explaining unresolved tumors.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The constituent mechanisms are established separately, but the unified framework and prioritization of residual explanatory space are a cross-study synthesis rather than a standard pre-existing theory.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1850.name: Ligand-and-Lineage Substitution Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1850.description: A biologically distinct subset of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is driven neither by classic kinase-domain activation nor by generic tumor-suppressor loss, but by substitution of the normal alveolar epithelial control system with alternative signaling/logistic programs. Two recurrent substitutions appear in the evidence: ligand substitution, where membrane-tethered or fusion-derived ligands such as NRG1 activate ERBB2/ERBB3 without a receptor mutation, and lineage substitution, where loss or reduction of NKX2-1/TTF1 and related lineage control permits gastric/GI-like or stem-like cell states that can serve as the organizing biology of the tumor. This theory proposes that a meaningful fraction of unresolved driver-negative LUAD is maintained by 'who supplies the signal' and 'what lineage state interprets it', not just by which kinase is mutated.
theory-1850.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Ligand-and-Lineage Substitution Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A biologically distinct subset of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is driven neither by classic kinase-domain activation nor by generic tumor-suppressor loss, but by substitution of the normal alveolar epithelial control system with alternative signaling/logistic programs. Two recurrent substitutions appear in the evidence: ligand substitution, where membrane-tethered or fusion-derived ligands such as NRG1 activate ERBB2/ERBB3 without a receptor mutation, and lineage substitution, where loss or reduction of NKX2-1/TTF1 and related lineage control permits gastric/GI-like or stem-like cell states that can serve as the organizing biology of the tumor. This theory proposes that a meaningful fraction of unresolved driver-negative LUAD is maintained by 'who supplies the signal' and 'what lineage state interprets it', not just by which kinase is mutated.

theory_statements.statement_name: Ligand-presenting fusions define a non-kinase driver class
theory_statements.theory_statement: In a subset of oncogene-negative LUAD, the primary transforming lesion is not mutation of a receptor kinase but creation of an aberrant ligand-presentation module, most clearly via NRG1 fusions that preserve the EGF-like domain and drive ERBB2/ERBB3 signaling. Such tumors are therefore better modeled as ligand-driven receptor activation states than as receptor-mutant cancers.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma subsets initially negative for canonical driver panels, especially invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma and never-smoker tumors, in which NRG1 fusion discovery was performed by RNA-seq, RT-PCR, FISH, or expanded targeted fusion testing. Most directly supported for CD74-NRG1 and SLC3A2-NRG1; conceptually extends to other ligand-like or receptor-context-dependent fusions in which the fusion product activates intact receptor machinery.
special_cases: The theory is best established for NRG1 fusions and should not be generalized to all non-kinase fusions without mechanistic testing.
The signaling phenotype still depends on recipient-cell expression of ERBB2 and ERBB3; the ligand fusion is not fully autonomous from receptor context.
Some NRG1-aberrant cases show outlier expression without a detected fusion, so ligand substitution may occur through additional mechanisms not yet resolved.

supporting_evidence.text: CD74-NRG1 was discovered in never-smoker pan-negative LUAD and shown to present the NRG1 EGF-like domain extracellularly, activate ERBB2/ERBB3 and PI3K-AKT signaling, and increase anchorage-independent growth.

In KRAS-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, CD74-NRG1 was recurrent and activated HER2/HER3, ERK, and AKT; the signaling and soft-agar phenotype were suppressed by lapatinib and afatinib, supporting a ligand-driven rather than receptor-mutant model.
SLC3A2-NRG1 was found in the same KRAS-negative/pan-negative IMA setting and was interpreted as an analogous NRG1 ligand fusion preserving the EGF-like domain and activating HER-family signaling.

Transcriptome meta-analysis found multiple NRG1 rearrangements and NRG1 outlier-expression cases concentrated in driver-unknown lung cancers, indicating that ligand-based oncogenesis generalizes beyond one fusion partner and can exist as either fusion or regulatory overexpression.
All available CD74-NRG1-positive tumors were invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas in never-smoker women, implying a lineage-selective ecological niche for ligand-substitution drivers.

Logical induction from the evidence above: because the transforming entity is a membrane-tethered or fusion-derived ligand acting through intact ERBB receptors, these cancers constitute a driver class orthogonal to kinase-domain mutation and should be classified by ligand logic rather than by receptor genotype alone.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: NRG1 fusions as oncogenic drivers in invasive mucinous lung adenocarcinoma are already established, and their ERBB2/ERBB3-dependent mechanism is documented.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is elevating these events into a broader mechanistic category of ligand-presenting driver architecture that can organize a subtype of oncogene-negative LUAD distinct from kinase-mutant disease.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The underlying discoveries are existing, but the subtype-level abstraction as 'ligand substitution' is a new synthesis that may guide classification and therapy design.
references: Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusions in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [foundational ligand-fusion mechanism]
Nakaoku (2014) NRG1 fusions in KRAS-negative IMA [recurrence and HER2/HER3 dependence]
Dhanasekaran (2014) Transcriptome Meta-Analysis of Lung Cancer Reveals Recurrent Aberrations in NRG1 and Hippo Pathway Genes [broader NRG1 rearrangement class]

theory_statements.statement_name: Loss of pulmonary lineage control creates alternative interpretable driver states
theory_statements.theory_statement: When pulmonary lineage regulators such as NKX2-1/TTF1 are reduced or lost, LUAD can enter alternative lineage states—mucinous gastric-like, GI-like, or stem-like—that reorganize which signaling inputs become oncogenic and may themselves function as driver-equivalent states. In these tumors, lineage-state transition is not merely a downstream phenotype but part of the causal architecture of tumor maintenance.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to mucinous/gastric-like LUAD, NKX2-1-low or NKX2-1-negative tumors, KRAS-associated mucinous adenocarcinoma models, low-TTF1 KRAS subgroups, and genomically quiet never-smoker piano-like tumors with stemness features. Includes both mouse mechanistic data and human correlative pathology/transcriptomic data.
special_cases: NKX2-1 effects are context dependent: it suppresses KRAS-driven mucinous tumorigenesis but can support other lung epithelial states and is amplified in some LUADs.
Lineage-state change alone is not always sufficient for transformation; cooperating oncogenic or suppressor-loss events may still be required.
Not every NKX2-1-low tumor is driver-negative; the theory concerns lineage-state substitution within the driver-negative or noncanonical subset.

supporting_evidence.text: KrasG12D plus Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induced invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma of the lung, whereas KrasG12D alone did not generate the same phenotype, showing that reducing pulmonary lineage control changes what oncogenic program the tissue can sustain.

Loss of Nkx2-1 revealed a latent gastric differentiation program in mouse lung adenocarcinoma and adult pulmonary epithelium, including Hnf4a, Pdx1, Ctse, Gkn1, and mucinous markers; human NKX2-1-negative mucinous adenocarcinomas showed corresponding gastric markers.

Nkx2-1 loss globally redistributed Foxa1/2 binding from pulmonary loci to de novo gastric/GI loci, providing a direct chromatin mechanism by which lineage substitution rewires the tumor's regulatory interpretation of signaling.
Combined loss of Nkx2-1 and Hnf4a de-repressed Hmga2, linking lineage-state escape to an embryonal and more primitive tumor program.

KRAS-mutant KC tumors had low NKX2-1/TTF1, frequent CDKN2A/CDKN2B loss, mucinous/GI-like differentiation markers, and a distinct proteomic state, indicating that low pulmonary-lineage fidelity defines a coherent biologic subtype rather than a passive consequence.

Never-smoker piano tumors were genomically quiet yet enriched for stemness/development features and lacked clear recurrent canonical drivers, supporting the idea that alternative cell-state control can substitute for canonical oncogene logic in some tumors.

Logical induction from the evidence above: if reduction of pulmonary lineage control repeatedly changes transcriptional targets, histology, and the set of usable oncogenic inputs, then lineage-state transition must be treated as part of the driver architecture rather than as mere downstream differentiation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: NKX2-1 loss and mucinous/gastric differentiation in KRAS-driven lung adenocarcinoma are established, as are low-TTF1 subgroups and stemness-like genomically quiet tumors in never-smokers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit driver-theoretic claim that lineage substitution itself is a causal organizing state for a subset of oncogene-negative LUAD and should be classified alongside genomic lesion classes.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This reframes several known lineage and differentiation findings as one subtype-defining theory of noncanonical oncogenesis rather than isolated pathology observations.
references: Maeda (2012) KrasG12D and Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induce mucinous adenocarcinoma of the lung [lineage-factor cooperation]
Snyder (2013) Hidden gastric differentiation program revealed by Nkx2-1 loss [lineage substitution mechanism]
Skoulidis (2015) KRAS-mutant LUAD KC cluster with low TTF1 [human lineage-state subtype]
Liu (2021) Genomic and evolutionary classification of lung cancer in never smokers [piano/stemness context]

new_predictions_likely: Among unresolved driver-negative mucinous LUADs, the highest-yield additional assay after DNA/RNA sequencing will be phospho-ERBB2/ERBB3 IHC or phosphoproteomics, which will enrich for ligand-substitution tumors even when the fusion partner is initially unknown.
NRG1-fusion-positive and other ligand-substitution tumors will show stronger dependence on receptor-context proteins such as ERBB2, ERBB3, and downstream PI3K-AKT than on MAPK-only blockade.

Driver-negative LUADs with low NKX2-1/TTF1 and gastric-marker expression will be enriched for mucinous histology, altered enhancer occupancy, and noncanonical structural or regulatory events rather than classic kinase-domain mutations.

In tumors with low NKX2-1/TTF1 but no identified canonical driver, chromatin assays will reveal redistributed FOXA-family occupancy and lineage-specific enhancer activation resembling the gastric-like mouse models.
new_predictions_unknown: Additional non-NRG1 ligand-presenting fusions in oncogene-negative LUAD will be found that act through intact receptor complexes, creating a broader family of ligand-substitution drivers.
Forced restoration of pulmonary lineage control in a subset of NKX2-1-low driver-negative LUAD models will collapse tumor fitness even without directly inhibiting the nominal signaling pathway.

Lineage-substituted tumors will prove selectively vulnerable to therapies that interrupt receptor-context interpretation, such as HER-family dimerization blockade combined with chromatin-state modulation.

Some piano-like driver-negative tumors will not require a stable clonal genomic driver at all, but instead depend on self-reinforcing lineage-state circuits that can be reset by differentiation therapy.
negative_experiments: If broad testing of unresolved mucinous/never-smoker driver-negative LUAD fails to reveal enrichment of NRG1-like ligand-presenting mechanisms or ERBB2/ERBB3 activation, the ligand-substitution part of the theory is weakened.

If NKX2-1-low or gastric-like driver-negative tumors do not show reproducible lineage-specific chromatin rewiring or signaling-context changes compared with NKX2-1-high tumors, the lineage-substitution component is weakened.

If receptor-context blockade is not more effective than generic pathway inhibition in ligand-substitution tumors, the claim that these are mechanistically distinct from receptor-mutant cancers is weakened.
If restoring pulmonary lineage programs in appropriate model systems fails to alter fitness or signaling interpretation, the theory overstates the causal role of lineage state.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not directly explain suppressor-loss-driven noncanonical states such as NF1/RASA1/PTEN-driven oncogene-negative LUAD, which may belong to a different biological subclass.
It does not directly address broad occult canonical rescue cases where the main issue is assay failure rather than alternative ligand or lineage logic.

conflicting_evidence.text: NKX2-1 can also behave as an amplified lineage-survival oncogene in a subset of LUAD, indicating that loss of lineage control is not a universal noncanonical route and that NKX2-1 biology is strongly context dependent.
Some mucinous or lineage-shifted tumors are still explained by conventional fusions or KRAS mutations, meaning lineage state alone is not sufficient to define a separate driver class in every case.

special_cases: Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma is the clearest human special case for this theory.
NKX2-1 effects are bidirectional and oncogenic context dependent; this theory concerns the NKX2-1-low or lineage-displaced branch, not all NKX2-1 biology.
Ligand substitution and lineage substitution may co-occur, particularly in mucinous/never-smoker tumors where the receptive receptor context is preserved.

references: Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusions in lung adenocarcinoma [ligand-fusion mechanism in IMA]
Nakaoku (2014) Recurrent NRG1 fusions in KRAS-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [HER2/HER3 ligand-substitution biology]
Maeda (2012) KrasG12D and Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induce mucinous adenocarcinoma of the lung [lineage-factor cooperation]
Snyder (2013) Hidden gastric differentiation program revealed by Nkx2-1 loss [gastric lineage substitution]
Skoulidis (2015) KRAS-mutant LUAD subtypes [low-TTF1 mucinous/GI-like state]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Ligand-fusion drivers such as CD74-NRG1 and lineage-factor mechanisms centered on NKX2-1 are already known in mucinous or lineage-shifted lung adenocarcinoma.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel feature is combining them into one theory of signal substitution and lineage substitution as a coherent alternative to kinase-mutation-centric oncogenesis in a subset of driver-negative LUAD.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is built from known mechanisms but unifies them into a broader explanatory architecture for unresolved oncogene-negative disease, so it is best classified as somewhat related to existing work rather than wholly new.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1851.name: Lineage-Context Hidden Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1851.description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly lacking drivers; instead, their dominant oncogenic logic is shifted from canonical intracellular RTK/RAS/RAF coding activation toward lineage-conditioned driver mechanisms. In this theory, alveolar-to-mucinous/gastric lineage reprogramming, low NKX2-1/TTF1 state, and enhancer-topology rewiring create a permissive context in which ligand fusions, receptor-context signaling, and regulatory structural events become the main hidden drivers. Histology and lineage state are therefore not merely phenotypes of the tumor, but predictors of the class of driver lesion most likely to be present.
theory-1851.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Lineage-Context Hidden Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly lacking drivers; instead, their dominant oncogenic logic is shifted from canonical intracellular RTK/RAS/RAF coding activation toward lineage-conditioned driver mechanisms. In this theory, alveolar-to-mucinous/gastric lineage reprogramming, low NKX2-1/TTF1 state, and enhancer-topology rewiring create a permissive context in which ligand fusions, receptor-context signaling, and regulatory structural events become the main hidden drivers. Histology and lineage state are therefore not merely phenotypes of the tumor, but predictors of the class of driver lesion most likely to be present.

theory_statements.statement_name: Lineage-shifted LUAD redistributes hidden-driver architecture toward ligand and regulatory drivers
theory_statements.theory_statement: In lung adenocarcinomas that enter a mucinous, gastric-like, or NKX2-1-low/TTF1-low lineage state, the probability that the dominant hidden driver is a ligand-presenting fusion, HER-family-context activation event, or enhancer/topology-mediated regulatory lesion is increased relative to the probability of a classical intracellular kinase hotspot mutation; conversely, TTF1-high conventional tumors more often follow canonical mutation-defined driver routes.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, KRAS-wild-type or pan-negative mucinous LUAD, NKX2-1-low/NKX2-1-negative or TTF1-low lineage-shifted adenocarcinoma, and never-smoker-enriched LUAD subsets with gastric-lineage features. It is not intended as a statement about all LUAD, and it does not claim that mucinous histology excludes canonical drivers.
special_cases: KRAS mutations remain a major alternative driver route in mucinous LUAD and can produce similar mucinous histology without ligand or regulatory hidden drivers.
Some lineage-shifted tumors may be driven primarily by tumor suppressor loss or cooperative suppressor-loss states rather than by ligand fusions or enhancer-topology events.
The statement is strongest for invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma and weaker for nonmucinous TTF1-low tumors.

supporting_evidence.text: CD74-NRG1 fusions were repeatedly found in pan-negative, KRAS-negative, never-smoker invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas and were strongly enriched in women who never smoked, directly linking mucinous lineage to hidden ligand-fusion drivers.
SLC3A2-NRG1 was also found in KRAS-negative/pan-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, extending the mucinous hidden-driver class beyond CD74-NRG1.

Other noncanonical fusions in KRAS-negative IMA included EZR-ERBB4, TRIM24-BRAF, and KIAA1468-RET, showing that mucinous/pan-negative IMA is broadly fusion-rich rather than restricted to one single driver gene.

The same IMA study explicitly described 30 of 34 KRAS-negative IMA cases as pan-negative after routine driver testing, and RNA-seq then recovered multiple novel fusions, showing that this histology is particularly enriched for assay-missed structural drivers.

Human NKX2-1-negative mucinous lung adenocarcinomas were CTSE-positive and often GKN1-positive, while NKX2-1-positive tumors lacked this gastric program, indicating a reproducible lineage-shifted state linked to mucinous biology.

In mouse lung adenocarcinoma, loss of Nkx2-1 unveiled a latent gastric differentiation program involving Hnf4a, Pdx1, Ctse, Gkn1 and mucinous features, and Foxa1/2 were redistributed from pulmonary loci to gastric/GI loci, providing a mechanistic basis for lineage switching that parallels human mucinous LUAD.
KrasG12D plus Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induced mucinous adenocarcinoma in mice, establishing that lineage-state perturbation itself can redirect tumor phenotype and driver logic.

The KRAS-mutant KC subgroup was TTF1-low, mucinous/GI-like, enriched for CDKN2A/CDKN2B loss, and biologically distinct from other KRAS subgroups, reinforcing that lineage-state switching defines a specific biology beyond canonical kinase genotype.

In non-CAGA LUAD lacking clinically actionable canonical drivers, ERBB2 overexpression was produced by super-enhancer formation plus structural rearrangement near HNF1β, indicating that lineage-linked regulatory topology can substitute for classical coding oncogene activation in this broader lineage-context class.

This law/statement was logically inferred through induction from the repeated co-occurrence of mucinous/NKX2-1-low lineage states with NRG1 fusions, other noncanonical fusions, and regulatory structural ERBB2 activation, together with the direct mechanistic mouse evidence for lineage switching.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: NRG1 fusions in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, NKX2-1 loss-driven gastric differentiation, and ERBB2 regulatory structural activation in non-CAGA LUAD are each established observations. Mucinous LUAD is also known to differ biologically from conventional LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel synthesis is the law-like claim that lineage state itself predicts hidden-driver class: mucinous/NKX2-1-low/gastric-shifted tumors preferentially recruit ligand-fusion and regulatory-topology drivers rather than standard kinase hotspots.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement integrates several previously separate findings into a predictive subtype principle that links histologic and lineage state to driver architecture. Because that generalization is not itself directly established in the cited studies, it is best classified as new.
references: Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusion in lung adenocarcinoma [foundational ligand-fusion precedent in mucinous LUAD]
Nakaoku (2014) recurrent novel fusion genes in KRAS-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [fusion-rich mucinous subset]
Snyder (2013) latent gastric differentiation program after Nkx2-1 loss [lineage-switching precedent]
Kim (2024) ERBB2 super-enhancer/structural abnormality in non-CAGA LUAD [regulatory-topology precedent]

theory_statements.statement_name: Ligand-presenting fusion architecture can be a primary driver without receptor mutation
theory_statements.theory_statement: A structural lesion that recontextualizes an extracellular ligand domain into persistent membrane-tethered, juxtacrine, autocrine, or proteolytically releasable presentation can function as the primary oncogenic driver of LUAD even when the cognate receptor is wild type; in such tumors, oncogenic addiction resides in ligand-presentation architecture plus receptor-context competence rather than in receptor kinase mutation.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies most directly to NRG1-fusion-positive lung adenocarcinoma, especially invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma and pan-negative/never-smoker tumors expressing ERBB2 and ERBB3. By extension it is intended for analogous ligand-presenting rearrangements, not for all fusions or all receptor-driven tumors.
special_cases: The fusion requires a permissive receptor context; cells lacking sufficient ERBB2/ERBB3 expression may not be transformed by NRG1 ligand presentation to the same extent.
Some assays may capture the fusion transcript but not the full signaling dependence if receptor phosphorylation is not measured.
Not all extracellular-ligand fusions will necessarily be equivalent to NRG1 fusions.

supporting_evidence.text: CD74-NRG1 was discovered in pan-negative never-smoker LUAD and shown to fuse CD74 to the NRG1 EGF-like domain, creating membrane-associated ligand presentation rather than a mutated receptor kinase.

The mechanistic papers showed that CD74-NRG1 activated ERBB2/ERBB3, increased p-AKT and p-S6K, and enhanced anchorage-independent growth, directly demonstrating that the oncogenic lesion resides in ligand architecture and receptor-context signaling.

p-ERBB3 staining was present in all five CD74-NRG1-positive tumors and rare in unselected adenocarcinomas, indicating that receptor-context activation is a specific consequence of the fusion driver rather than a generic feature of LUAD.
Conditioned media from CD74-NRG1-expressing cells activated HER2/HER3, ERK, and AKT in reporter cells, supporting a juxtacrine/paracrine or cleavable ligand mechanism rather than receptor mutation.

CD74-NRG1-driven signaling and anchorage-independent growth were suppressed by lapatinib and afatinib, confirming dependence on the receptor complex activated by the fusion ligand rather than on a mutant ligand kinase.
SLC3A2-NRG1 was found in the same KRAS-negative IMA context, indicating that ligand-domain preservation and receptor-context activation generalize beyond one NRG1 fusion partner.

The broader transcriptome meta-analysis identified multiple NRG1 rearrangements (CD74-NRG1, RBPMS-NRG1, WRN-NRG1, SDC4-NRG1, RAB2IL1-NRG1) and NRG1 outlier expression enriched in driver-unknown tumors, all preserving the NRG1 EGF domain, showing that the operative unit is ligand presentation rather than one unique fusion partner.

This law/statement was logically inferred through induction from the repeated observation that NRG1-fusion tumors lack canonical receptor mutations yet show selective ERBB2/ERBB3 activation and HER-family inhibitor sensitivity.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: NRG1 fusions as ligand-driven oncogenic events and their dependence on HER2/HER3 signaling are already established. These studies already show that the fusion can activate receptor signaling without receptor mutation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The new element is the abstraction of these observations into a general driver law: oncogenic addiction can reside in ligand-presentation architecture itself, defining a separate hidden-driver class for LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Because the statement is a higher-order theoretical generalization of existing NRG1-fusion work rather than a brand-new molecular mechanism, it is somewhat related to existing theory rather than wholly new.
references: Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusion [direct molecular precedent]
Nakaoku (2014) NRG1 fusions in KRAS-negative IMA [HER2/HER3-dependent ligand-fusion precedent]
Shin et al. transcriptome meta-analysis of lung cancer [broader NRG1 rearrangement class in driver-unknown tumors]

theory_statements.statement_name: Structural variants become oncogenic in driver-negative LUAD only when matched to a compatible active enhancer state
theory_statements.theory_statement: In a subset of oncogene-negative LUAD, a structural rearrangement acquires driver status only when it intersects a compatible lineage-active enhancer or super-enhancer landscape; therefore, breakpoint structure alone is insufficient to explain oncogenicity, and oncogenic output depends jointly on 3D genome rewiring and cell-state-specific regulatory activity.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies primarily to non-CAGA LUAD and other driver-negative tumors with transcriptional outlier behavior lacking canonical actionable mutations, especially ERBB2-centered super-enhancer/structural events. It is not meant as a universal rule for all amplifications or all structural variants.
special_cases: A structural variant may be silent if the rearranged chromatin neighborhood lacks active enhancer capacity in that cellular lineage.
A super-enhancer alone may not be sufficient if no structural event creates the relevant new contact architecture.
Some copy-number gains can act primarily by dosage without requiring enhancer-topology rewiring.

supporting_evidence.text: In LUAD without clinically actionable genetic alterations, ERBB2 overexpression was traced to super-enhancer formation together with structural abnormalities involving ERBB2 and HNF1β, revealing a hidden regulatory-structural driver missed by routine WES/RNA-seq driver calls.

The specific HNF1β-ERBB2 inversion/rearrangement brought the loci into close spatial proximity and was resolved only by integrating WGS, H3K27Ac ChIP-seq, Hi-C, and long-read sequencing, showing that breakpoint detection alone was insufficient to explain the driver.

CRISPR recreation of the rearrangement increased HER2 expression only when double-strand breaks were introduced at both ERBB2 and HNF1β loci; one-sided cleavage did not reproduce the effect, demonstrating a joint requirement for structural rewiring rather than isolated local damage.

The study explicitly concluded that WGS alone is insufficient to determine super-enhancer-related structural activation and that genomics and epigenomics must be integrated, directly supporting the proposition that enhancer state and structure jointly define driver status.

The pan-cancer CESAM framework recovered cis-regulatory structural drivers such as IGF2 and IRS4 enhancer hijacking, showing that structural variants can be reinterpreted as regulatory drivers only when matched to expression and chromatin-domain context.

The review on enhancer hijacking, neo-TADs, CTCF-boundary disruption, and ecDNA emphasizes that many oncogenic SVs operate through noncoding regulatory rewiring and are missed by exome-focused assays, providing strong conceptual support for the same driver principle in unresolved LUAD.

This law/statement was logically inferred through abduction from the ERBB2 LUAD case and induction from pan-cancer enhancer-hijacking studies showing that recurrent structural events only become oncogenic when they create productive enhancer-promoter or domain-level regulatory contacts.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Enhancer hijacking, neo-TAD formation, and cis-regulatory structural activation are established concepts in cancer biology, and ERBB2 regulatory structural activation has been shown in non-CAGA LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is the LUAD-specific law that the oncogenicity of many hidden structural lesions is conditional on enhancer compatibility with lineage state, making cell identity a determinant of whether an SV is a driver.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This extends existing enhancer-hijacking theory into a more specific predictive framework for driver-negative LUAD, so it is new but clearly rooted in closely related prior mechanisms.
references: Kim (2024) ERBB2 super-enhancer structural abnormality in LUAD [direct LUAD precedent]
Weischenfeldt (2017) cis-regulatory driver discovery by CESAM [closely related enhancer-hijacking framework]
Pan-cancer IGF2 tandem-duplication enhancer hijacking studies [de novo contact-domain precedent]
Pan-cancer review of oncogenic structural variants [general enhancer-topology and ecDNA framework]

new_predictions_likely: In independent driver-negative LUAD cohorts, RNA fusion sequencing plus phospho-ERBB3 IHC will outperform expanded DNA hotspot panels for identifying clinically actionable drivers in mucinous or NKX2-1-low tumors.

Among KRAS-wild-type invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas that are negative on standard DNA panels, NRG1-family fusions or other extracellular-ligand/context fusions will be significantly enriched compared with nonmucinous TTF1-high tumors.

Driver-negative LUADs with gastric-lineage markers such as HNF4A, CTSE, GKN1, or PDX1 will show a higher prevalence of regulatory structural lesions or noncanonical fusions than lineage-conventional LUADs.

Non-CAGA LUADs with ERBB2 or other receptor outlier expression but no coding mutation will frequently show focal H3K27Ac super-enhancer gain plus local structural breakpoints on WGS/long-read follow-up.
new_predictions_unknown: A broader class of membrane-tethered or protease-releasable ligand fusions beyond NRG1 will be found in driver-negative LUAD, especially in mucinous or NKX2-1-low disease, and these tumors will respond to receptor-complex blockade rather than to fusion-partner-directed therapy.
Restoring an NKX2-1-like lineage state in NRG1-fusion or ERBB2-regulatory-driver models will reduce receptor-context addiction even if the underlying structural lesion remains present.

Single-cell multiomic profiling will show that in mucinous driver-negative LUAD the lineage-switched transcriptional state and the structural hidden driver are usually co-resident in the same dominant clone rather than segregated across separate subclones.

Some unresolved TTF1-low nonmucinous LUADs will be driven by cryptic enhancer-hijacking events affecting lineage-compatible receptors or adapter genes that are currently below standard short-read detection thresholds.
negative_experiments: If large independent cohorts show that mucinous/NKX2-1-low driver-negative LUAD is not enriched for ligand fusions or regulatory structural lesions relative to TTF1-high tumors, the first statement would be weakened.
If CD74-NRG1 or analogous ligand-fusion models remain fully transforming after ERBB2/ERBB3 suppression, the ligand-presentation law would be seriously challenged.
If recreating ERBB2 rearrangements in cells lacking the relevant enhancer landscape still produces strong HER2 overexpression, the enhancer-compatibility law would be weakened.
If phosphoproteomic readouts show no systematic receptor-context activation in fusion-positive mucinous tumors, the receptor-addiction component of the theory would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not explain many nonmucinous oncogene-negative tumors apparently driven by cooperative suppressor-loss states involving NF1, RASA1, and PTEN with RAS/PI3K activation.

It does not directly explain APOBEC-high tumors or nitro-PAH/nitrosamine-signature-defined never-smoker tumors where the dominant biology may be mutagenic-process-driven rather than lineage/ligand-driven.
It does not explain genomically quiet piano tumors in never-smokers that lack obvious known drivers and may instead involve stemness-related or quiescence-exit mechanisms.

conflicting_evidence.text: KRAS explains a large fraction of mucinous adenocarcinomas, so mucinous histology is not specific for ligand or regulatory hidden drivers.

Amplification of the 14q13.3 NKX2-1 locus and coexpression of NKX2-1 with NKX2-8 or PAX9 were insufficient to induce tumors in vivo in one mouse study, showing that lineage-factor perturbation is not by itself always a sufficient driver.
Some oncogene-negative or TTF1-low tumors are defined by suppressor-loss, cell-cycle, or proteomic immune states rather than ligand or enhancer-topology drivers, limiting the breadth of the theory.

special_cases: This theory is strongest for invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, never-smoker-enriched tumors, NKX2-1-low/TTF1-low tumors, and non-CAGA LUAD with expression outliers.
Canonical KRAS-mucinous tumors are a major alternative route and should be treated as a boundary condition rather than a contradiction.
The theory concerns hidden-driver architecture, not all progression or resistance mechanisms in LUAD.

references: Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusion in lung adenocarcinoma [gene-specific precedent for ligand-driven mucinous LUAD]
Nakaoku (2014) recurrent novel fusion genes in KRAS-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [fusion-rich mucinous hidden-driver precedent]
Snyder (2013) latent gastric differentiation program after Nkx2-1 loss [lineage-switching precedent]
Kim (2024) ERBB2 overexpression by super-enhancer formation with genomic structural abnormalities in non-CAGA LUAD [regulatory topology precedent]
Weischenfeldt et al. CESAM / enhancer hijacking analyses [closely related cis-regulatory driver framework]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Separate prior work already supports NRG1 ligand fusions in mucinous LUAD, NKX2-1-loss gastric lineage switching, and ERBB2 super-enhancer/structural activation in non-CAGA LUAD. Enhancer hijacking and lineage-specific regulatory drivers are also established concepts more broadly in cancer.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: What is new is the unifying theory that lineage state itself predicts hidden-driver class in oncogene-negative LUAD, specifically linking mucinous/NKX2-1-low states to ligand-fusion and enhancer-topology driver architectures.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No cited study explicitly formulates a general theory that histologic/lineage identity is a driver-class selector across hidden LUAD mechanisms. The present theory is therefore new, though built from several related mechanistic precedents.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1852.name: Multi-Layer Hidden Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1852.description: Oncogene-negative LUAD is not one disease but a mixture of mechanistically distinct driver classes distributed across assay layers. One class consists of canonical drivers missed for technical reasons; another consists of rare coding drivers outside routine panels; a third consists of structural/splice drivers requiring fusion- or RNA-aware assays; and a fourth consists of noncoding, copy-number, regulatory, proteomic, or phospho-signaling drivers that may show little or no explanatory signal at the DNA hotspot level. The apparent 30% 'driver-negative' fraction therefore reflects both assay incompleteness and genuine biological diversification beyond the RTK/RAS/RAF hotspot model.
theory-1852.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Multi-Layer Hidden Driver Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Oncogene-negative LUAD is not one disease but a mixture of mechanistically distinct driver classes distributed across assay layers. One class consists of canonical drivers missed for technical reasons; another consists of rare coding drivers outside routine panels; a third consists of structural/splice drivers requiring fusion- or RNA-aware assays; and a fourth consists of noncoding, copy-number, regulatory, proteomic, or phospho-signaling drivers that may show little or no explanatory signal at the DNA hotspot level. The apparent 30% 'driver-negative' fraction therefore reflects both assay incompleteness and genuine biological diversification beyond the RTK/RAS/RAF hotspot model.

theory_statements.statement_name: Apparent driver negativity decomposes into assay-missed canonical, rare coding, structural/splice, and noncoding/proteomic driver classes
theory_statements.theory_statement: A lung adenocarcinoma called oncogene-negative after routine testing can belong to at least four mechanistic classes: (1) canonical drivers missed because of assay coverage, purity, or sampling; (2) rare coding drivers outside the standard panel; (3) structural or splice-altering drivers requiring RNA-, FISH-, or WGS-aware detection; and (4) regulatory/protein-state drivers whose oncogenic effect is not visible in hotspot DNA calls. Therefore, driver negativity should be treated as a provisional assay-state rather than a biologically uniform category.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma cohorts initially defined as EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative, RTK/RAS/RAF-negative, or panel-negative by targeted genotyping, WES, or limited fusion testing. It is most applicable to cohorts where broader orthogonal profiling was subsequently performed.
special_cases: A residual subset may remain unexplained even after broad profiling and may represent truly unresolved biology rather than assay failure.
Some tumors contain more than one detectable driver-class lesion or a canonical driver plus a cooperative suppressor-loss state.
The relative sizes of the four classes depend on the starting assay.

supporting_evidence.text: In never-smoker LUAD initially termed oncogene-negative by WES, many tumors were later rescued by clinical sequencing, FISH, or deeper WES: ALK fusions, ROS1 fusion, EGFR mutations, ERBB2 mutations, KRAS mutation, and MET exon 14 skipping were all recovered from the initial negative set.

Whole-genome reanalysis of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD rescued hidden canonical pathway lesions including KRAS hotspot mutations, focal NF1/RASA1 deletions, and EGFR BFBC amplification with overexpression, showing a large assay-missed canonical class.

CGP of tumors previously negative by conventional EGFR/KRAS/ALK testing recovered additional canonical or actionable drivers including EGFR variants not covered by prior primer sets, KRAS variants outside prior coverage, ROS1 fusions, MET alterations, PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway lesions, and cell-cycle lesions.

Rare coding drivers outside standard panels were found in pan-negative LUAD, including ARAF codon 214 variants, RAF1 hotspot variants, AKT1 E17K, EGFR ECD mutations, HER2 non-kinase mutations, RIT1 mutations, and probable rare EGFR kinase-domain variants such as A955R.

Structural and splice drivers were repeatedly recovered only after broader assays: ALK fusions, ROS1 fusions, RET fusions, NTRK1 fusions, MET exon 14 skipping, EGFR exon 25-26 deletion, and multiple novel kinase fusions were not captured by standard hotspot-centric testing.

Regulatory and noncoding hidden drivers were uncovered by WGS- or epigenome-aware analyses, including ILF2 promoter mutations, KEAP1 promoter/TSS deletion, STK11 promoter/TSS deletion, ERBB2 super-enhancer structural activation, and recurrent noncoding surfactant-gene regulatory mutations.

Proteomic and phosphoproteomic profiling uncovered hidden functional drivers not evident at the RNA level alone, including ARHGEF5 phospho-activation, KEAP1 G511V/NRF2 activation visible mainly in protein/phosphosite space, and many CNA trans-effects or phosphokinase outliers without corresponding DNA/RNA explanatory lesions.

The transcriptome meta-analysis of driver-unknown lung cancers identified NRG1 rearrangements, Hippo-pathway fusions, ROS1/FGFR3 expression outliers, and MET exon 14 skipping in tumors previously lacking known drivers, directly supporting a mixed multi-layer hidden-driver structure.

This law/statement was logically inferred through induction from multiple cohorts showing that the same initial 'negative' label fractured into distinct rescued classes depending on which additional assay layer was applied.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already known that some driver-negative LUADs harbor missed fusions, splice events, rare mutations, or copy-number alterations. Many individual studies document one hidden-driver class at a time.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the explicit decomposition of oncogene-negative LUAD into a layered taxonomy of hidden-driver classes tied to assay modality, treating negativity as an assay-state rather than a single biologic subtype.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Because the evidence exists piecemeal but not as a unified framework organizing the hidden-driver problem across assay layers, this theory is new but closely connected to prior findings.
references: Paik et al. multiplex driver testing in lung adenocarcinoma [panel-defined negative cohort precedent]
Genomic profiling of never-smoker LUAD studies [occult canonical and structural rescue precedent]
Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [WGS rescue precedent]
Proteogenomic LUAD studies [protein-state hidden-driver precedent]

theory_statements.statement_name: Structural and splicing assays deliver the highest marginal gain once hotspot DNA testing is exhausted
theory_statements.theory_statement: After routine hotspot mutation testing is negative, the next largest yield in hidden-driver discovery comes from assays sensitive to rearrangement, exon-skipping, and structural copy-number architecture—specifically RNA fusion calling, FISH, splice-aware analysis, and WGS—because these modalities recover both canonical and noncanonical drivers that are disproportionately missed by exon-focused testing.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to panel-negative or WES-negative LUAD, especially never-smoker and low-purity cohorts, and to tumors lacking EGFR/KRAS/ALK-class lesions on first-pass testing. It is less directly applicable when broad RNA/WGS profiling was already part of the initial workflow.
special_cases: If the initial assay already includes deep RNA fusion analysis and structural variant calling, the marginal value of additional FISH/WGS may be smaller.
Very low-expression fusions or very low-purity samples may still escape RNA-based detection and require orthogonal DNA or FISH assays.
Some residual cases will require noncoding or proteomic analyses rather than more structural testing.

supporting_evidence.text: In the never-smoker WES-negative cohort, clinical FISH recovered ALK and ROS1 fusions and deeper WES recovered MET exon 14 skipping, demonstrating that structural/splicing assays add substantial yield after standard exome-based negativity.

Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD identified hidden EGFR BFBC amplification, NF1 and RASA1 focal deletions, KEAP1 and STK11 promoter/TSS deletions, double minutes, tyfonas, and BFBC events that were inaccessible to prior WES/RNA-seq driver calls.

Deep transcriptome sequencing of EGFR/KRAS/EML4-ALK-screen-negative tumors recovered multiple fusion kinases and MET exon 14 skipping, and the study explicitly estimated that around 10% of drivers were transcriptional variants detectable by transcriptome sequencing.

Novel and noncanonical RET, ALK, ROS1, and NTRK1 fusions were identified only by transcriptome sequencing, targeted intron capture, RT-PCR, or FISH in tumors that had previously lacked known drivers on routine testing.
MET exon 14 skipping repeatedly emerged as a hidden driver class requiring RNA or splice-aware detection and was missed by initial standard testing in several studies.
The EGFR exon 25-26 deletion was found only through whole-genome structural analysis, illustrating that even EGFR can be hidden in structural form after routine mutation testing is exhausted.

This law/statement was logically inferred through induction from the repeated pattern that orthogonal fusion-, splice-, and WGS-based structural assays, rather than simply larger hotspot panels, most often rescued first-pass negative tumors.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: The importance of fusion-aware testing and MET exon 14 detection is well established, and many studies already show that WES or hotspot panels miss structural drivers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is the comparative law about testing order and marginal yield: once hotspot DNA is negative, structural/splice-aware assays are predicted to outperform further hotspot expansion as the next discovery step in many LUAD cohorts.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a practical theoretical synthesis of existing data rather than a wholly new molecular mechanism, so it is somewhat related to existing work but still framed here as a broader discovery principle.
references: Paik et al. multiplexed driver testing [limited hotspot panel precedent]
Comprehensive RNA-seq studies in screening-negative LUAD [fusion/splice rescue precedent]
WGS reanalysis of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [structural rescue precedent]
NTRK1, RET, ALK, ROS1 fusion studies [orthogonal structural-assay precedent]

theory_statements.statement_name: A residual biologic subset is driver-negative not because of missing sequence lesions alone, but because the causal state is regulatory, suppressor-combinatorial, or proteomic
theory_statements.theory_statement: After canonical, rare coding, and structural/splice rescues are removed, the remaining unresolved oncogene-negative LUADs are enriched for mechanisms in which no single activating proto-oncogene event is sufficient or visible: combinatorial suppressor loss activating RAS/PI3K, promoter/enhancer lesions, methylation-driven cis effects, protein-state or phospho-state outliers, and stemness/lineage-state programs. In these tumors, the driver is a systems-state rather than a single hotspot lesion.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to the unresolved remainder after broad genomic rescue in oncogene-negative LUAD, including non-CAGA tumors, oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K tumors, proteogenomically profiled never-smoker LUAD, and genomically quiet piano-like tumors. It does not claim that all residual cases share one exact mechanism.
special_cases: Some residual tumors may still harbor undiscovered structural or noncoding events rather than true systems-state drivers.
The statement encompasses several sub-mechanisms and should not be interpreted as a single homogeneous subtype.
Some systems-state drivers may coexist with weak or partial canonical drivers.

supporting_evidence.text: Combinatorial inactivation of NF1, RASA1, and PTEN in mice generated oncogene-negative LUAD with activated RAS and PI3K pathways nearly as potently as KrasG12D, and human oncogene-negative tumors frequently showed pERK/pAKT activation even without complete explanatory genomic lesions.

Among human oncogene-negative tumors without genomic PTEN mutation, PTEN protein loss was common by IHC, showing that non-genomic or epigenetic suppressor inactivation can be a hidden driver mechanism invisible to DNA mutation testing.

Proteogenomic studies identified ARHGEF5 phospho-activation with SRGAP1 downregulation, KEAP1 G511V/NRF2 activation visible mainly in phosphoproteomics, neutrophil-degranulation immune-cold STK11 states, and many phosphokinase outliers lacking matching CNA/RNA explanations.

The methylation-cis-effect analysis identified 120 methylation-driven cis-regulatory events affecting genes such as CLDN18, ANK1, PTPRCAP, MGA, and TBX3, which would not be discovered by mutation-only pipelines.
In WGS-negative RTK/RAS/RAF LUAD, recurrent promoter mutations such as ILF2 and promoter/TSS deletions in KEAP1 and STK11 implicated noncoding regulatory lesions in the residual unexplained class.

The never-smoker piano subtype was genomically quiet, often lacked known recurrent drivers, showed long telomeres and stemness-like features, and led the authors to hypothesize hidden stemness/quiescence-exit drivers such as NOTCH1- or ARID1A-like programs.

The MIF-HIF-1α axis in driver-gene-negative LUAD and transcriptomic immune/risk signatures in driver-negative cohorts show that some negative tumors are best captured by metabolic or regulatory states rather than by newly discovered canonical lesions.

This law/statement was logically inferred through abduction from the observation that even after broad structural and coding rescue, several studies still report biologically coherent but lesion-poor residual subsets characterized by pathway activity, proteomic states, or stemness rather than single dominant sequence events.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual studies already show cooperative suppressor-loss, methylation-driven cis regulation, phosphoproteomic hidden signaling, and genomically quiet never-smoker subsets. Residual unexplained driver-negative tumors are widely recognized.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit claim that the residual driver-negative fraction should be conceptualized as a systems-state driver class—regulatory, combinatorial, or proteomic—rather than simply as the residue of missed single-gene events.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthetic theory integrating several mechanistically different but conceptually similar observations into one explanatory framework for the residual unresolved category. It is new as an overarching theory, though built from known components.
references: Combinatorial suppressor-loss models of oncogene-negative LUAD [pathway-state precedent]
Proteogenomic LUAD studies [protein-state hidden-driver precedent]
WGS noncoding driver analyses in RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [regulatory-state precedent]
Genomic/evolutionary classification of never-smoker lung cancer [genomically quiet stemness-like precedent]

new_predictions_likely: In a prospective driver-negative LUAD workflow, adding RNA fusion/splice analysis and WGS-based structural reconstruction after a negative hotspot panel will rescue more cases than simply expanding the hotspot panel to additional rare coding genes.

Tumors remaining negative after broad structural rescue will show enrichment for pathway-state abnormalities such as PTEN protein loss, pERK/pAKT activation, or phosphoproteomic outliers even when no single activating coding event is identified.
Low-purity and low-cellularity samples will be overrepresented among first-pass driver-negative tumors that are later rescued as harboring canonical drivers.
Integrated methylation-plus-proteomics profiling of residual driver-negative LUAD will identify a recurrent subset with cis-regulatory silencing or activation not apparent from DNA mutation calls.
new_predictions_unknown: A classifier combining assay history, tumor purity, histology, RNA fusion features, and phosphoproteomics will partition driver-negative LUAD into the four hidden-driver classes with clinically meaningful therapeutic prediction.

Some currently unresolved driver-negative tumors will prove to depend on noncoding regulatory lesions that are clonally stable but invisible to short-read pipelines, and long-read plus chromatin-conformation assays will reveal them.

Residual systems-state driver-negative LUADs will respond better to pathway-combination therapy selected by phosphoproteomics (for example SHP2 plus AKT, or NRF2-context therapies) than to any genotype-matched monotherapy.

A measurable proportion of 'driver-negative' LUADs will have no single essential lesion even after complete genomic characterization and will instead require state-collapse therapies that alter lineage or transcriptional circuitry.
negative_experiments: If broad orthogonal profiling does not divide driver-negative LUAD into distinct rescued classes but instead reveals one dominant hidden mechanism, the first statement would be weakened.
If expanded hotspot panels consistently rescue more negative tumors than fusion/splice/structural assays after first-pass negativity, the second statement would be weakened.

If residual negative tumors show no enrichment for proteomic, phospho-signaling, methylation, or suppressor-combinatorial states compared with driver-positive tumors, the third statement would be weakened.
If low-purity samples are not overrepresented among first-pass negative tumors later rescued as canonical-driver-positive, the assay-missed canonical component would be less important than proposed.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory is broad and does not by itself predict which specific hidden-driver class a given tumor will belong to without additional subtype information such as histology, smoking status, or proteomic state.
Some studies defining driver-negative cohorts did not perform comprehensive enough follow-up to determine which residual cases were truly unexplained versus simply under-profiled.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies remained unable to identify hidden drivers even after broader profiling, indicating that not all apparent negativity is cleanly decomposed by current assay layers.

Some candidate rare genes or alterations such as ASEF, AUTS2, and PDE4D lacked strong replication or functional validation, cautioning against over-assigning residual negativity to newly observed but weakly supported events.

special_cases: The class proportions will differ by cohort: never-smoker cohorts will enrich structural and rare coding hidden drivers, while smoking-related cohorts may enrich suppressor-loss and high-passenger contexts.
Cohorts defined only by limited targeted testing will contain more assay-missed canonical drivers than cohorts initially profiled by WES/WGS/RNA-seq.
This theory describes the hidden-driver landscape as a classification framework; it is intentionally broader than any one molecular pathway.

references: Paik (2011) multiplexed assays of oncogenic drivers in lung cancers [panel-defined negative-state precedent]
Genomic profiling of never-smoker LUAD [occult canonical and structural rescue precedent]
Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [noncoding and structural hidden-driver precedent]
Proteogenomic characterization of LUAD [protein-state hidden-driver precedent]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing studies show many individual reasons why LUAD can appear driver-negative, including missed fusions, rare coding variants, structural events, and noncoding or proteomic mechanisms. These findings are mostly presented separately within assay-specific studies.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the unified multi-layer theory that classifies apparent driver negativity into assay-linked mechanistic classes and explicitly treats the remaining unresolved fraction as a systems-state category rather than as a single undefined residual.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No cited study provides a full layered taxonomy of hidden-driver classes spanning technical misses, rare coding variants, structural/splice lesions, and systems-state drivers. The present framework is therefore new, though strongly grounded in prior component findings.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1853.name: Hidden-Driver Convergence Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1853.description: A large fraction of apparently oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not driverless; they are produced by convergent activation of a limited set of signaling states through lesion classes that standard hotspot panels under-detect. These hidden classes include rare noncanonical coding variants, structural fusions, exon-skipping events, focal or complex amplifications, noncoding/promoter lesions, and regulatory or proteomic activation states. The practical implication is that driver-negative LUAD should be reclassified by mechanism of pathway activation rather than by absence of canonical mutations.
theory-1853.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Hidden-Driver Convergence Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A large fraction of apparently oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not driverless; they are produced by convergent activation of a limited set of signaling states through lesion classes that standard hotspot panels under-detect. These hidden classes include rare noncanonical coding variants, structural fusions, exon-skipping events, focal or complex amplifications, noncoding/promoter lesions, and regulatory or proteomic activation states. The practical implication is that driver-negative LUAD should be reclassified by mechanism of pathway activation rather than by absence of canonical mutations.

theory_statements.statement_name: Driver negativity is often an assay-defined artifact rather than a biological absence of oncogenic control
theory_statements.theory_statement: In lung adenocarcinoma, especially in never-smokers and tumors initially negative on limited RTK/RAS/RAF testing, a substantial subset of 'driver-negative' cases are reclassified as driver-positive when broader assays interrogate structural variation, splicing, noncanonical exons/domains, copy-number architecture, and RNA/protein consequences; therefore, the observed driver-negative state is frequently produced by incomplete observability of driver classes rather than true absence of dominant oncogenic lesions.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to primary lung adenocarcinoma cohorts initially classified as negative by hotspot genotyping, limited clinical panels, standard WES, or mutation-centric workflows, particularly never-smoker cohorts and studies that subsequently added RNA-seq, WGS, FISH, fusion assays, deep sequencing, or proteogenomics. It does not claim that all driver-negative tumors are assay artifacts; some remain unexplained after broad profiling.
special_cases: A residual subset remains unresolved even after WGS/RNA/proteomics, so the law is probabilistic rather than universal.
Low tumor purity/cellularity can itself be a major cause of false-negative calls even when the correct assay class is used.
Some studies define driver negativity differently, so rescue rates are assay-definition dependent.

supporting_evidence.text: In an institutional never-smoker LUAD cohort, 29 of 84 tumors were initially oncogene-negative by WES, but 9 were reclassified by clinical sequencing/FISH and 2 more by deep WES, showing that many initial negatives were missed canonical or splice/structural drivers.

WGS re-analysis of tumors lacking RTK/RAS/RAF alterations by WES/RNA-seq reclassified 20 of 85 cases as pathway-altered, including KRAS hotspot mutations, NF1/RASA1 deletions, and EGFR amplification, demonstrating substantial under-calling by earlier workflows.

Whole-transcriptome sequencing of tumors initially negative for EGFR/KRAS mutations and EML4-ALK by FISH uncovered novel kinase fusions and MET exon 14 skipping, directly showing that screening-negative tumors can harbor expressed structural or splicing drivers.

CD74-NRG1 fusions were discovered in pan-negative never-smoker lung adenocarcinomas and in KRAS-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma only after RNA-seq/RT-PCR/FISH workflows beyond routine driver testing.
NTRK1 fusions such as MPRIP-NTRK1 and CD74-NTRK1 were found specifically in tumors without known oncogenic alterations after targeted fusion-aware testing, and were transforming and drug-sensitive.
RET and ROS1 fusions, including novel partners and variants, were uncovered in tumors that lacked canonical known drivers or were initially negative on narrower testing.

Rare noncanonical coding drivers outside standard assay scope were identified in pan-negative tumors, including EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 non-kinase mutations, ARAF codon 214 variants, AKT1 E17K, EGFR A955R, and RIT1 mutations.
MET exon 14 skipping repeatedly emerged as a hidden driver class detectable only by splice-aware or deeper testing, including RNA-seq, integrated DNA/RNA profiling, or ultra-deep WES.
Broad CGP of tumors negative for EGFR/KRAS/EML4-ALK by standard testing recovered EGFR and KRAS mutations missed because the earlier kits did not cover those variants or lacked sensitivity.

The broad 10-gene driver-negative category in multiplexed clinical testing was explicitly acknowledged to miss untested genes, non-hotspot variants, and unassayed fusions, meaning the category itself contains a built-in hidden-driver reservoir.

Integrated WGS plus H3K27Ac ChIP-seq, Hi-C, and long-read sequencing uncovered ERBB2 overexpression driven by super-enhancer formation and structural abnormality in non-CAGA LUAD, illustrating a driver class invisible to routine mutation-centric testing.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is well established that some lung adenocarcinomas classified as driver-negative by limited assays are later found to harbor fusions, exon-skipping events, amplifications, or rare coding drivers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the unifying law that driver negativity in LUAD is best treated as a problem of hidden driver observability across assay layers, and that diverse rescue events converge on a small number of signaling-state archetypes rather than representing unrelated exceptions.
