novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The component observations are existing, but the synthesis into a general observability/convergence framework for oncogene-negative LUAD is a broader, more integrative reinterpretation.
references: Li et al. Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [direct evidence that WGS rescues hidden pathway drivers]
Vazquez et al. Genomic Profiling of Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [direct evidence that many WES-negative tumors are rescued by broader testing]
Takeuchi et al. transcriptome-based kinase fusion discovery in driver-negative LUAD [direct precedent for RNA-based rescue of hidden fusions]
Fernandez-Cuesta et al. CD74-NRG1 fusion in pan-negative LUAD [direct precedent for ligand-fusion rescue]
Vaishnavi et al. Oncogenic and drug sensitive NTRK1 rearrangements in lung cancer [direct precedent for hidden fusion rescue]

theory_statements.statement_name: Hidden drivers organize into recurrent mechanistic subtypes rather than an undifferentiated residual class
theory_statements.theory_statement: After broad molecular profiling, oncogene-negative LUAD segregates into recurring mechanistic subtypes defined by the mode of pathway activation: kinase-fusion driven, ligand-fusion/ERBB-context driven, RasGAP/PI3K suppressor-collapse driven, enhancer or super-enhancer rewiring driven, complex amplification/ecDNA or rearrangement driven, and proteomic or phospho-regulatory state-driven tumors. These subtypes are partially predictable from histology, smoking status, lineage state, and phosphoproteomic context, and each implies a distinct therapeutic logic.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma after exclusion or separate labeling of standard canonical drivers and after use of broad assays that can detect structural, regulatory, and protein-state abnormalities. Most strongly supported in never-smoker LUAD, invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, and WGS/proteogenomic cohorts. It is a subtype theory, not a claim that every tumor maps cleanly to one pure class.
special_cases: Some tumors harbor mixed mechanisms or unresolved biology despite broad profiling.
Histology is informative but not deterministic; for example mucinous morphology enriches NRG1 and some kinase fusions but is not exclusive to them.
Certain suppressor-loss states may be more accurately defined by pathway activation than by any single genomic event.

supporting_evidence.text: Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma forms a distinct hidden-driver-rich subtype in which KRAS-negative cases are frequently driven by NRG1 fusions, ERBB4 fusion, BRAF fusion, or RET fusion.

Ligand-fusion tumors such as CD74-NRG1 are mechanistically distinct from kinase-fusion tumors: they activate ERBB2/ERBB3 through membrane-tethered or cleaved ligand presentation rather than direct kinase-domain fusion.

ALK-fusion LUAD shows a distinct phosphoproteomic state with outlier ALK mRNA/protein, phospho-ALK Y1507, partner phosphosites, and downstream ARHGEF5 phosphorylation, supporting a specific kinase-fusion subtype.
A RasGAP/PI3K suppressor-collapse subtype is supported by oncogene-negative LUAD with NF1, RASA1, and PTEN loss, strong pERK/pAKT, and selective vulnerability to SHP2 plus AKT blockade.
A regulatory rewiring subtype is exemplified by non-CAGA LUAD with ERBB2 overexpression caused by super-enhancer formation and structural rearrangement involving HNF1B-ERBB2 proximity.

A complex-structural-amplification subtype is supported by WGS-negative RTK/RAS/RAF cases enriched for double minutes, breakage-fusion-bridge cycles, and tyfonas, often containing expression outlier genes and rescuing events such as EGFR amplification.

A noncoding/regulatory lesion subtype is supported by recurrent ILF2 promoter mutations associated with increased expression and by promoter/TSS deletions of KEAP1 and STK11, none of which are visible in coding-only analyses.

A proteomic/phospho-state-driven subtype is supported by signaling outliers such as ARHGEF5 phospho-activation, KEAP1-NRF2 functional activation visible at phosphoprotein level, and STK11-mutant neutrophil-degranulation immune-cold phenotypes not evident from RNA alone.

Expression- and lineage-state-based cohorts identify additional biologic subtypes among EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD and among never-smoker tumors (piano/steady/proliferative/chaotic), indicating that some driver-negative states reflect distinct biology rather than pure technical false negatives.

Histologic patterns such as mucinous cribriform architecture enriched for kinase fusions, and mucinous invasive morphology enriched for NRG1 fusions, imply partial phenotype-level predictability of subtype membership.

Broad genomic surveys identify recurrent noncanonical subtype-specific drivers such as RIT1 mutations enriched exclusively in oncogene-negative tumors, further arguing against a single undifferentiated residual class.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Multiple individual subtype mechanisms already exist in the literature: kinase fusions, NRG1-driven mucinous tumors, NF1/RASA1/PTEN suppressor-loss states, and ERBB2 enhancer rewiring.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the unified subtype taxonomy spanning genomic, regulatory, and proteomic hidden-driver modes within oncogene-negative LUAD, with the claim that therapeutic strategy should follow activation mechanism rather than residual-category membership.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis across disparate existing findings into a broader classification theory; the pieces are known, but the integrative categorization is not standard.
references: Fernandez-Cuesta et al. CD74-NRG1 fusion in lung adenocarcinoma [ligand-fusion subtype precedent]
Nakaoku et al. recurrent fusions in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [mucinous hidden-driver subtype precedent]
Li et al. combinatorial tumor suppressor inactivation in oncogene-negative LUAD [suppressor-collapse subtype precedent]
Mechanism of ERBB2 overexpression by super-enhancer formation with genomic structural abnormalities [regulatory rewiring subtype precedent]
CPTAC LUAD proteogenomic characterization [proteomic hidden-state precedent]

new_predictions_likely: If all clinically driver-negative LUADs undergo joint DNA/RNA fusion calling, splice-aware analysis, copy-number architecture reconstruction, and phosphoproteomics, the residual truly unexplained fraction will fall substantially below panel-defined driver-negative rates.
Invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas that are KRAS-negative will be enriched for ligand-fusion or noncanonical kinase-fusion mechanisms relative to non-mucinous driver-negative LUAD.

Tumors with strong pathway phospho-activation but no coding driver will disproportionately harbor noncoding/promoter lesions, focal suppressor TSS deletions, or enhancer/super-enhancer rewiring on WGS plus chromatin profiling.

ALK/ROS1/RET/NTRK fusion-positive hidden-driver tumors will show cleaner mutual exclusivity with other major drivers than regulatory-rewiring or suppressor-collapse tumors, because the former more often act as single dominant kinase engines.
new_predictions_unknown: A clinically useful hidden-driver classifier built from histology plus a low-plex phosphoprotein panel plus shallow WGS breakpoint features will predict the mechanistic subtype of driver-negative LUAD without full proteogenomics.

Some currently unresolved driver-negative LUADs will prove to be microbiome- or ligand-conditioned tumors in which no genomic alteration is necessary for pathway addiction, but the state will still map onto one of the same phospho-signaling archetypes.

A subset of driver-negative LUADs with no DNA/RNA lesion but stable phospho-ERBB3 or phospho-SHP2 activation will be maintained by extracellular ligand traps or juxtacrine signaling from stromal compartments rather than tumor-intrinsic genomic events.

Long-read plus Hi-C profiling of residual unexplained cases will reveal a recurrent class of TAD-boundary or ecDNA-mediated enhancer rewiring around noncanonical LUAD genes not yet recognized as drivers.
negative_experiments: If comprehensive multi-omic testing fails to reduce the residual driver-negative fraction compared with hotspot/WES-only workflows, the observability-artifact component of the theory is weakened.
If hidden drivers discovered by broad profiling do not cluster into reproducible mechanistic subtypes with distinct pathway outputs, the subtype-taxonomy component is weakened.
If histology, smoking status, and phosphoproteomic context show no enrichment for specific hidden-driver classes, the phenotype-predictability claim is weakened.
If most rescued lesions prove to be biologically incidental and not therapeutically or functionally dominant, the convergence component is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Some cohorts explicitly report no specific rescued hidden driver and instead identify transcriptomic or prognostic states without mechanistic resolution, indicating that current data still leave a residual unexplained compartment.
Some spatially heterogeneous tumors may appear driver-negative because of sampling rather than assay class alone; this is related but not fully resolved by the present theory.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some studies of driver-negative or wild-type cohorts do not report successful rescue of a hidden genomic driver despite broad profiling, suggesting a fraction may represent genuinely different biology or unresolved non-genomic mechanisms rather than missed alterations.

special_cases: Best applied after recording the exact assay definition of 'driver-negative'; panel-negative, WES-negative, and WGS/RNA-negative tumors are biologically different populations.
The theory includes non-genomic/proteomic driver states, so not every subtype is reducible to a single DNA lesion.
The theory is strongest for adenocarcinoma; some supporting examples come from squamous or pan-cancer studies and are used as mechanistic analogies rather than direct prevalence estimates.

references: Li et al. Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [hidden-driver rescue by WGS]
Vazquez et al. Genomic Profiling of Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [reclassification of WES-negative tumors]
Nakaoku et al. recurrent fusions in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [mucinous hidden-driver taxonomy]
Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities [regulatory-driver precedent]
CPTAC LUAD proteogenomic studies [protein-state hidden-driver precedent]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: There is already substantial evidence that many panel- or WES-negative LUADs harbor hidden fusions, splice events, amplifications, rare mutations, or regulatory drivers, and several subtype-specific mechanisms have been individually described.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novelty is the integrated theory that these events should be organized into a small number of recurring mechanistic subtypes, each reflecting a distinct observability failure mode and therapeutic logic.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is built from existing discoveries but recombines them into a higher-level explanatory taxonomy of oncogene-negative LUAD, so it is broader than any single existing paper but not wholly de novo.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1854.name: Lineage-State and Regulatory-Rewiring Theory of Residual Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1854.description: The residual fraction of lung adenocarcinomas that remain unexplained after broad genomic testing are driven less by undiscovered classic kinase lesions and more by lineage-state transitions coupled to regulatory rewiring. In this view, loss or redirection of pulmonary lineage programs, gain of gastric/stem-like programs, enhancer/TAD reorganization, and ligand-competent receptor context create oncogenic states that may be detectable primarily through chromatin, transcriptomic, or proteomic assays.
theory-1854.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Lineage-State and Regulatory-Rewiring Theory of Residual Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: The residual fraction of lung adenocarcinomas that remain unexplained after broad genomic testing are driven less by undiscovered classic kinase lesions and more by lineage-state transitions coupled to regulatory rewiring. In this view, loss or redirection of pulmonary lineage programs, gain of gastric/stem-like programs, enhancer/TAD reorganization, and ligand-competent receptor context create oncogenic states that may be detectable primarily through chromatin, transcriptomic, or proteomic assays.

theory_statements.statement_name: A subset of driver-negative LUAD is governed by lineage-state switches rather than classical mutation-centric oncogenes
theory_statements.theory_statement: When no canonical or hidden kinase driver is detectable, some lung adenocarcinomas are maintained by altered lineage identity—especially loss of NKX2-1-driven pulmonary identity, gain of gastric or stem-like programs, and activation of alternative transcription-factor circuits such as HNF4A/FOXA relocalization—such that tumor progression is encoded in cell-state regulation more than in a single dominant coding lesion.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to mucinous and NKX2-1-low/negative adenocarcinoma states, never-smoker 'piano' tumors with sparse canonical drivers, KRAS-associated low-TTF1 subgroups, and related mouse lineage-switch models. It is not intended for clearly kinase-fusion-driven tumors where lineage features may be secondary.
special_cases: Some lineage-state tumors still carry a canonical genomic driver such as KRAS; the law concerns what governs phenotype and maintenance, not only initiation.
Acute lineage-factor loss may be insufficient to reproduce the full advanced state; additional genetic or epigenetic events may be required.
Human support is strongest in mucinous/NKX2-1-low contexts and weaker in genomically quiet non-mucinous residual cases.

supporting_evidence.text: NKX2-1 loss unmasks a latent gastric differentiation program in mouse and human lung adenocarcinoma, with expression of HNF4A, PDX1, CTSE, GKN1, and mucinous markers, indicating that altered lineage identity can be a central tumor-state determinant.

Foxa1/2 binding is globally redistributed after Nkx2-1 loss, with loss of pulmonary occupancy and gain at gastric/GI loci, directly showing regulatory rewiring of lineage circuitry rather than simple mutation-driven pathway activation.

Combined KrasG12D and Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induces mucinous adenocarcinoma, whereas the same lineage factor can suppress Kras-driven tumorigenesis, demonstrating that lineage state can control the oncogenic phenotype.

KRAS-mutant KC tumors show low TTF1/NKX2-1, frequent CDKN2A/B loss, and gastrointestinal-like or mucinous differentiation programs, supporting a lineage-defined subtype whose biology is not reducible to canonical oncogene status alone.

Piano tumors in never-smokers are genomically quiet, stemness-enriched, and often lack known recurrent drivers or fusions, implying a driver mode linked to quiescence exit and stem-like state rather than obvious canonical oncogenic lesions.

The 'chaotic' never-smoker LUAD subtype lacks significant enrichment of known driver mutations or fusions yet shows strong EMT/mesenchymal and fibroblast-rich biology, supporting the possibility of biologic states not reducible to known genomic drivers.

Non-genetic evolution across histologic progression showed no recurrent pattern-specific genomic lesions, with progression instead associated with transcriptional and epigenetic reprogramming, consistent with cell-state-driven evolution.

MIF-HIF1A and ferroptosis/fibroblast-related studies of EGFR-wild-type or driver-negative LUAD identify metabolic and microenvironment-linked states without recovering a specific hidden genomic driver, consistent with the existence of non-mutation-centric tumor states.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Lineage plasticity, NKX2-1 loss, mucinous transdifferentiation, and stem-like quiet tumors are already known in lung adenocarcinoma and in mouse models.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that these observations together explain a meaningful subset of the residual driver-negative compartment as lineage-state disease, not merely as incompletely sequenced kinase disease.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The underlying biology exists in prior work, but treating residual driver-negative LUAD as a lineage-state category rather than only a search space for more hidden mutations is a broader synthesis.
references: Snyder et al. NKX2-1 loss unmasks gastric differentiation in lung adenocarcinoma [direct lineage-switch precedent]
Winslow-related NKX2-1/KRAS mucinous adenocarcinoma studies [direct mucinous lineage-state precedent]
Genomic and evolutionary classification of lung cancer in never smokers [piano stemness/quiet-tumor precedent]
Proteogenomic studies of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD [state-based subtype precedent]

theory_statements.statement_name: Regulatory architecture defects are the likely genomic substrate of many residual cases
theory_statements.theory_statement: In the subset of tumors not explained by coding drivers, recurrent disease may arise from dysregulated regulatory architecture—super-enhancer formation, enhancer hijacking, promoter/TSS loss or mutation, TAD-boundary disruption, ecDNA/double-minute amplification, or methylation-driven cis effects—so that oncogenic output is encoded by abnormal enhancer-promoter topology or cis-regulation rather than coding-sequence change.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LUADs subjected to WGS, epigenomic profiling, noncoding mutation analysis, copy-number architecture reconstruction, and/or methylation-proteogenomic integration, especially non-CAGA tumors and WGS-defined RTK/RAS/RAF-negative cases. Some mechanistic exemplars come from other cancers as analogies where direct LUAD evidence is still limited.
special_cases: Not every noncoding or structural event is a driver; functional coupling to expression or protein-state change is required.
Some cited enhancer-hijacking and neo-TAD mechanisms are strongest in pan-cancer analogs rather than direct LUAD proof.
High-level amplicons can combine dosage and regulatory rewiring, blurring subtype boundaries.

supporting_evidence.text: ERBB2 overexpression in non-CAGA LUAD was mechanistically traced to super-enhancer formation plus genomic structural abnormality, validated by Hi-C, PacBio, and CRISPR recreation, directly showing regulatory architecture as a hidden driver substrate.

ILF2 promoter mutations in WGS-defined RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD occurred in recurrent open chromatin and were associated with higher ILF2 expression, supporting noncoding promoter mutation as a hidden regulatory driver class.

Focal promoter/TSS deletions of KEAP1 and STK11 were uncovered only by WGS in tumors remaining negative after standard pathway-driver analyses, indicating regulatory-region loss as a hidden suppressor-inactivation mechanism.

Significant structural-variant breakpoints at the STK11 locus and recurrent noncoding regulatory elements affecting surfactant-gene expression show that WGS uncovers recurrent non-exomic driver-like architecture missed by exome-centric analyses.

Methylation-driven cis-effects involving CLDN18, ANK1, PTPRCAP, MGA, and other loci demonstrate that epigenetic cis-regulation can generate coherent RNA/protein/phosphoprotein alterations invisible to mutation-only pipelines.

Double minutes, BFBC events, and tyfonas were common complex SV classes in WGS-negative RTK/RAS/RAF LUAD and frequently contained expression outlier genes, implying that structural architecture itself is a major hidden-driver substrate.

The review and CESAM framework identify enhancer hijacking, CTCF-boundary disruption, and neo-TAD formation as systematic hidden-driver mechanisms missed by coding-focused assays, with IRS4 and IGF2 as validated pan-cancer examples relevant by analogy to LUAD.
ecDNA and double-minute concepts provide an additional architecture-driven explanation for high-level oncogene expression not reducible to simple focal amplification on standard assays.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Super-enhancer rewiring, promoter lesions, methylation cis-effects, ecDNA, and enhancer hijacking are all known mechanisms in cancer, and some have already been shown in LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is to propose that these architecture-level lesions form the default explanatory substrate for a major portion of the residual post-genomic driver-negative compartment in LUAD, linking unresolved biology to assay class rather than to ever-rarer coding drivers alone.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative extrapolation from direct LUAD evidence and strong pan-cancer mechanistic analogies; it is not wholly new mechanistically, but it reorients the search strategy for unresolved oncogene-negative LUAD.
references: Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by super-enhancer formation with genomic structural abnormalities [direct LUAD regulatory-driver precedent]
Pan-cancer analysis of somatic copy number alterations implicates IRS4 and IGF2 in enhancer hijacking [regulatory architecture precedent]
Reviews of structural variation in cancer and ecDNA biology [broader mechanistic precedent]

new_predictions_likely: Residual driver-negative LUADs that remain unexplained after DNA/RNA driver calling will be enriched for NKX2-1-low, HNF4A-high, or stemness-like transcriptional states compared with rescued fusion-driven tumors.
In unresolved mucinous or low-TTF1 tumors, ATAC-seq/H3K27Ac profiling will reveal gained enhancer activity at gastric or ERBB-context loci more often than new coding drivers.
Adding methylation arrays and promoter/TSS-focused WGS analysis to unresolved cases will recurrently identify suppressor inactivation not evident from exon sequencing alone.
Tumors in the residual unexplained compartment will show greater subtype stability by transcriptomic/epigenomic state than by specific coding mutation pattern across regions of the same tumor.
new_predictions_unknown: A subset of piano or chaotic never-smoker LUADs will prove to be maintained by reversible enhancer-state programs, making them unusually sensitive to chromatin-directed therapies rather than kinase inhibitors.

Longitudinal sampling will show some driver-negative LUADs transitioning between pulmonary and gastric/stem-like states under therapy without acquiring a new dominant coding driver, implying resistance through lineage plasticity alone.

In unresolved NKX2-1-low tumors, 3D genome mapping will reveal recurrent but noncoding pulmonary-to-gastric boundary rewiring events analogous to neo-TAD formation, centered on lineage genes rather than classic oncogenes.
A stromal-ligand-plus-lineage-state mechanism may explain a subset of ERBB3-phosphorylated but genomically quiet tumors, creating dependence on microenvironmental ligand circuits.
negative_experiments: If residual driver-negative LUADs do not show enrichment for lineage-state changes, enhancer rewiring, promoter lesions, or methylation cis-effects compared with rescued hidden-driver tumors, the theory is weakened.
If NKX2-1-low or piano tumors are explained at high frequency by newly found classic kinase lesions once sequencing improves, the lineage-state emphasis is weakened.
If chromatin and methylation profiling of unresolved cases rarely yields expression-coupled regulatory abnormalities, the regulatory-architecture substrate claim is weakened.
If mucinous and low-TTF1 phenotypes are not associated with reproducible transcription-factor rewiring or enhancer changes, the lineage-regulation mechanism is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Clearly transforming hidden drivers such as ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1, and ARAF variants are better explained by classical oncogenic activation than by lineage-state or regulatory-architecture theories.

conflicting_evidence.text: Many ostensibly driver-negative tumors are in fact rescued by broader genetic assays, meaning lineage-state explanations should not be invoked before fusion/splice/rare-mutation and structural rescue is exhausted.

special_cases: This theory is most useful for the residual post-rescue compartment, not the full panel-defined driver-negative category.
Cross-cancer examples of enhancer hijacking and neo-TADs are used as mechanistic templates, so direct LUAD validation remains necessary in many cases.
Lineage-state and regulatory-architecture mechanisms may coexist with weak genomic drivers rather than replace them.

references: Snyder/Winslow-related NKX2-1 lineage-switch studies [direct lineage-state precedent]
Genomic and evolutionary classification of lung cancer in never smokers [piano/stemness precedent]
Mechanism of ERBB2 overexpression by super-enhancer formation in non-CAGA LUAD [direct regulatory-architecture precedent]
Pan-cancer enhancer hijacking and neo-TAD literature [mechanistic analogs]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: There is strong prior evidence for NKX2-1-mediated lineage plasticity, stem-like quiet tumors, and regulatory rewiring mechanisms such as super-enhancer formation and enhancer hijacking.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The new contribution is the proposal that these mechanisms jointly explain much of the residual driver-negative LUAD compartment that remains after standard hidden-driver rescue, shifting the field's null hypothesis from 'rarer kinase lesion' to 'state and architecture pathology.'
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is grounded in existing observations but synthesizes them into a new research program for unresolved driver-negative LUAD, so it is adjacent to existing work rather than identical to it.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1855.name: Hidden-Driver Compression Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1855.description: A large fraction of supposedly oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not biologically driverless; they are assay-compressed into a negative bin because their drivers lie outside standard hotspot-panel assumptions. These tumors are enriched for four hidden driver classes: structural kinase/ligand fusions, noncanonical coding variants outside usual hotspots/domains, regulatory or noncoding structural alterations, and pathway activation visible only at protein/phosphosite level. The practical implication is that driver-negative LUAD should be reclassified by driver class and evidence layer rather than by absence of panel-detected mutations.
theory-1855.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Hidden-Driver Compression Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A large fraction of supposedly oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not biologically driverless; they are assay-compressed into a negative bin because their drivers lie outside standard hotspot-panel assumptions. These tumors are enriched for four hidden driver classes: structural kinase/ligand fusions, noncanonical coding variants outside usual hotspots/domains, regulatory or noncoding structural alterations, and pathway activation visible only at protein/phosphosite level. The practical implication is that driver-negative LUAD should be reclassified by driver class and evidence layer rather than by absence of panel-detected mutations.

theory_statements.statement_name: Most driver-negative LUADs are hidden-driver positive under broader assays
theory_statements.theory_statement: When lung adenocarcinomas initially called driver-negative are reassessed with fusion-aware, splice-aware, copy-number-aware, and whole-genome or proteogenomic methods, a substantial subset is reclassified as containing oncogenic events; therefore, 'driver-negative' is often an assay-state rather than a biological state.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma cohorts initially classified as lacking canonical RTK/RAS/RAF pathway drivers by limited hotspot assays, standard WES, or mutation-centric workflows; especially relevant in never-smoker LUAD and in cohorts where DNA-only testing predominated. It does not imply all residual negative tumors are explained by current methods.
special_cases: A residual subset remains unexplained even after broad profiling, implying genuinely unresolved biology rather than mere assay failure.
The rescue fraction depends strongly on assay breadth, tumor purity, RNA quality, and whether structural and noncoding events are assayed.
Panel-defined 'no driver' cohorts that incompletely tested all genes mix true negatives with untested cases.

supporting_evidence.text: In never-smoker LUAD, 29 of 84 tumors were initially oncogene-negative by WES, but clinical sequencing/FISH reclassified 9 and deep WES rescued 2 MET exon 14 cases, increasing driver detection in the institutional cohort to nearly 81%.

Whole-genome reanalysis of 85 LUADs initially lacking RTK/RAS/RAF pathway alterations by WES/RNA-seq reclassified a substantial fraction, including missed KRAS hotspot mutations, NF1 and RASA1 focal deletions, and EGFR amplification driven by breakage-fusion-bridge cycles.

In a transcriptome-sequenced screening-negative LUAD cohort, deeper RNA analysis uncovered novel kinase fusions and MET exon 14 skipping after prior EGFR/KRAS Sanger and EML4-ALK FISH negativity, showing that many apparent negatives reflect assay design limitations.

Targeted comprehensive genomic profiling of EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative LUAD found missed canonical and noncanonical alterations including EGFR, KRAS, ROS1, MET, NF1, CDKN2A/CDK4/MDM2, and PI3K pathway lesions, directly demonstrating under-calling by standard testing.

In pan-negative Chinese LUAD, expanded testing beyond the canonical panel uncovered EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 non-kinase mutations, ARAF mutation, and CD74-NRG1 fusions, showing that hidden drivers include noncanonical domains and untested genes.

In pan-negative LUAD, EGFR extracellular-domain sequencing uncovered multiple ECD variants including the oncogenic M277E event, establishing a hidden driver class outside routine EGFR kinase-domain testing.

Integrated non-CAGA LUAD profiling showed that tumors lacking clinically actionable alterations by WES/RNA-seq could harbor regulatory structural drivers such as ERBB2 super-enhancer formation with structural rearrangement, a class missed by routine genomic testing alone.

The existence of broad low-level gains such as 4q12 without demonstrated driver behavior shows that broader testing also detects noncausal alterations; therefore reclassification requires functional or pathway corroboration, not detection alone.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is well established that some driver-negative LUADs are explained by missed fusions, splice events, or rare mutations when broader profiling is performed.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit unification of these findings into an assay-compression principle: 'driver-negative' is often a misclassification produced by modality bias, and hidden drivers can be organized into recurring evidence layers rather than gene lists.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis across many studies rather than a single pre-existing named theory. The component observations are known, but the compression framework is a new integrative generalization for LUAD.
references: Vasconcelos et al. / TCGA-style whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [assay rescue of missed structural/coding drivers; basis for assay-compression concept]
Imielinski et al. transcriptome sequencing of screening-negative LUAD [hidden fusions and MET exon 14 skipping discovered after targeted negative workup]
Seo et al. comprehensive genomic profiling after standard negative testing [missed canonical and noncanonical actionable events]
Suzuki et al. / Wang et al. NRG1 and other noncanonical fusion discoveries in pan-negative mucinous LUAD [structural hidden-driver class]

theory_statements.statement_name: Residual unresolved LUADs separate into pathway-state subtypes rather than a single null class
theory_statements.theory_statement: Among LUADs that remain negative after broad genomic testing, the residual tumors are better explained as biologically distinct pathway-state subtypes: (i) dual RAS/PI3K negative-regulator loss states, (ii) lineage-state or stemness-dominant low-SCNA 'quiet' states, (iii) regulatory/chromatin-rewired transcriptional outlier states, and (iv) protein-state driven signaling outliers not inferable from RNA alone.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to the subset of LUADs that remain unexplained or only partially explained after canonical driver analysis and, ideally, after expanded genomic testing. Particularly relevant to never-smoker, non-CAGA, and whole-genome-profiled cohorts. It is not intended for canonical EGFR/KRAS/ALK-driven tumors unless discussing co-driver biology.
special_cases: Some tumors will span more than one subtype, such as a quiet genomic state plus stemness, or regulatory activation plus protein-state dependency.
These subtype boundaries are probabilistic and cohort-dependent because different studies used different assay layers.
Mucinous ligand-fusion tumors form a distinct specific subtype and are handled separately in a more specific theory.

supporting_evidence.text: Oncogene-negative LUAD can be recreated by combined loss of Nf1, Rasa1, and Pten, and many human oncogene-negative tumors showed moderate-to-strong activation of both RAS and PI3K pathways despite lacking canonical oncogene mutations; PTEN protein loss was often present without genomic PTEN mutation.

The piano subtype in never-smoker lung cancer is genomically quiet, stemness-associated, highly heterogeneous, and can lack known driver mutations or fusions, implying a real biology of low-SCNA/low-driver-state tumors rather than only assay failure.

Non-CAGA LUADs can harbor regulatory outlier states produced by super-enhancer and structural-variant overlap, especially ERBB2-centered rearrangement-associated overexpression, indicating a chromatin-rewired subtype.

Proteogenomic analyses identified signaling states not apparent from RNA alone, including ARHGEF5 phosphorylation outliers in never-smoker/ALK-fusion enriched contexts and KEAP1/NFE2L2 functional activation visible primarily at protein/phosphosite level.

Methylation-driven cis effects and CNA trans-effects identified expression/protein outlier programs such as CLDN18, ANK1, PTPRCAP, RALA, BZW2, and CTSB, supporting epigenetic and network-level activation states that are not captured by canonical mutation calls.

Never-smoker and East Asian proteogenomic studies identified APOBEC-high and carcinogen-signature-defined subgroups with distinct kinase and DNA repair signaling, indicating that some tumors are better classified by mutagenic process and proteomic state than by a single driver lesion.

Transcriptomic studies in never-smoker LUAD identified subtypes such as 'chaotic' that lacked enrichment for known driver mutations or fusions yet showed aggressive stromal/EMT biology, supporting a residual state-based rather than gene-based taxonomy.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Multiple studies already suggest that residual driver-negative LUADs can involve suppressor-loss signaling, stemness, regulatory rewiring, or proteomic signaling outliers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit four-way subdivision of unresolved LUAD into pathway-state classes that cut across assay modality and propose a practical taxonomy for residual negatives.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory is an integrative abstraction of heterogeneous observations across mouse functional models, WGS, epigenomics, and proteomics; the subtype categories themselves are newly synthesized.
references: Li et al. combinatorial tumor suppressor inactivation in oncogene-negative LUAD [RAS/PI3K suppressor-loss state]
de Bruin / never-smoker WGS classification studies [piano quiet-state biology]
Yoon et al. non-CAGA super-enhancer/SV LUAD [regulatory rewiring state]
CPTAC proteogenomic LUAD studies [protein-state and phosphosite-defined biology beyond RNA]

new_predictions_likely: If a prospective LUAD cohort classified as driver-negative by panel sequencing is reprofiled with paired WGS plus RNA-seq plus phosphoproteomics, at least three separable residual groups should emerge: structural-rescued, suppressor-loss RAS/PI3K-active, and regulatory/protein-state outlier tumors.

In tumors with no canonical genomic driver but strong pERK and pAKT staining, PTEN protein loss or NF1/RASA1 functional deficiency will be more common than in genomically similar but signaling-low tumors.

In non-CAGA or unexplained LUADs, transcriptional outlier genes linked to H3K27Ac peaks and SV breakpoints will show stronger therapeutic relevance than rare coding mutations of uncertain significance.
Across multiple institutions, the fraction of 'truly unresolved' LUAD will inversely correlate with assay breadth, especially the inclusion of RNA fusion calling and WGS-level structural analysis.
new_predictions_unknown: A substantial subset of piano-like, genomically quiet tumors will prove to be driven by noncoding chromatin topology lesions or stem-cell-state epigenetic circuits rather than by undetected coding drivers.

Proteome-only outlier states such as ARHGEF5 phospho-activation or KEAP1-NRF2 pathway activation will define therapeutically targetable subgroups even when neither DNA nor RNA profiling identifies a convincing lesion.
Some tumors now classified as regulatory outliers will be driven by ecDNA or enhancer-hijacked mini-amplicons that are only recoverable by long-read or optical mapping approaches.
Mutational-process subtyping, especially APOBEC-high versus carcinogen-like versus quiet/stemness states, may outperform single-driver assignment for prognosis in unresolved never-smoker LUAD.
negative_experiments: If broad multi-omic reanalysis of a carefully selected driver-negative LUAD cohort yields very few new drivers and no coherent biological subgrouping, the assay-compression framework would be weakened.
If PTEN protein loss, NF1/RASA1 dysfunction, or pathway activation are not enriched among unresolved oncogene-negative LUADs, the suppressor-loss subtype claim would be weakened.

If regulatory outlier genes linked to SE/SV or methylation states fail to show reproducible expression/protein activation or therapeutic dependence, the regulatory-state arm of the theory would be weakened.
If proteomic/phosphoproteomic states closely mirror RNA abundance in unresolved LUAD, the claim that protein-state biology forms a distinct hidden-driver layer would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Some studies explicitly failed to find recurrent pattern-specific genomic lesions and instead favored non-genetic progression mechanisms, which this theory accommodates only broadly rather than mechanistically.

Several cohorts reported no specific hidden genomic driver discovery despite extensive profiling, indicating that residual biology may sometimes be dominated by evolution, sampling, or non-driver programs rather than discreet hidden lesions.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some apparently negative tumors remain negative even after broad testing, implying that not all driver-negative LUAD is an assay artifact.
Certain recurrent copy-number alterations such as broad low-level 4q12 gain were detected but not shown to be functional drivers, cautioning against overcalling every newly found lesion as causal.

special_cases: Best applied after orthogonal rescue testing has already excluded obvious missed canonical drivers.
Requires careful separation of causal lesions from passenger complexity in smoking-associated tumors.
Protein-state and regulatory-state subtypes are more likely to be cohort- and platform-sensitive than classic fusion-driven subtypes.

references: Imielinski et al. transcriptome sequencing of driver-screen-negative LUAD [hidden fusion and splice drivers; related to assay-miss theory]
TCGA/CPTAC whole-genome and proteogenomic LUAD studies [show residual biology beyond canonical drivers; related subtype evidence]
Li et al. combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors in oncogene-negative LUAD [functional RAS/PI3K suppressor-loss state]
Never-smoker genomic classification studies defining piano subtype [quiet/stemness residual subtype evidence]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Assay-missed fusions, splice events, rare coding mutations, and suppressor-loss pathway activation in driver-negative LUAD are all individually documented.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The theory's novelty is to treat residual negative LUAD as a structured space of assay-rescued and pathway-state subtypes rather than a miscellaneous remainder bin.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The component facts are established, but the cross-modality taxonomy and assay-compression framing are newly synthesized from the provided evidence.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1856.name: Driver-Substitution Theory Across LUAD and Other Epithelia
theory-1856.description: Oncogene-negative LUAD is not primarily explained by one undiscovered recurrent gene, but by substitution of canonical kinase-driver logic with alternative activating architectures that are recurrent across cancers: ligand fusions, rare RAF/RAS-family regulatory-site mutations, exon-skipping or domain-skipping RTKs, enhancer/SV rewiring, and co-loss of redundant tumor suppressors. The cross-cancer parallel is that when one usual route to MAPK/RTK addiction is absent, tumors repeatedly substitute another architecture that preserves pathway output while evading standard assays.
theory-1856.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Driver-Substitution Theory Across LUAD and Other Epithelia
components.theory_description: Oncogene-negative LUAD is not primarily explained by one undiscovered recurrent gene, but by substitution of canonical kinase-driver logic with alternative activating architectures that are recurrent across cancers: ligand fusions, rare RAF/RAS-family regulatory-site mutations, exon-skipping or domain-skipping RTKs, enhancer/SV rewiring, and co-loss of redundant tumor suppressors. The cross-cancer parallel is that when one usual route to MAPK/RTK addiction is absent, tumors repeatedly substitute another architecture that preserves pathway output while evading standard assays.

theory_statements.statement_name: Pathway output is preserved by architecture substitution
theory_statements.theory_statement: When common LUAD drivers in EGFR/KRAS/ALK-class loci are absent, tumors frequently substitute alternative activating architectures that preserve RTK/MAPK/PI3K output: noncanonical extracellular or regulatory-domain mutations, kinase or ligand fusions, exon-skipping events, or rare paralogous hotspot mutations in pathway regulators.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma and closely related epithelial tumors where pathway addiction is commonly mediated through RTK/RAS/RAF signaling, especially in cohorts preselected as canonical-driver negative. It is a comparative theory and not limited to one histology, though the main target is LUAD.
special_cases: Some substitutions activate lineage or chromatin programs rather than direct RTK/MAPK signaling.
Not every alternative architecture is equally transforming; some events remain candidates without full functional proof.
A fraction of tumors may instead be driven by tumor suppressor co-loss rather than a substituted positive oncogene.

supporting_evidence.text: Rare ARAF codon 214 and paralogous RAF1 codon 257/259 mutations were identified in otherwise driver-sparse LUAD, transformed epithelial cells, activated MEK/ERK signaling, and were drug sensitive, establishing a noncanonical RAF substitution route.

RIT1 switch II-domain mutations occurred specifically in oncogene-negative LUAD, were mutually exclusive with canonical drivers, and activated MAPK and PI3K signaling with clear transforming activity.

MET exon 14 skipping represents a splice-architecture substitute for canonical RTK mutation, discovered in screening-negative LUAD and mechanistically linked to loss of the Y1003/CBL regulatory site and receptor stabilization.

EGFR extracellular-domain mutations in pan-negative LUAD, especially M277E and other ECD variants, show that activation can substitute outside the canonical kinase domain while preserving EGFR dependency.

Ligand and kinase fusions such as CD74-NRG1, SLC3A2-NRG1, EZR-ERBB4, TRIM24-BRAF, KIAA1468-RET, NTRK1 fusions, ROS1 fusions, and KIF5B-RET repeatedly explain tumors previously lacking canonical point drivers.

Regulatory architecture substitution also occurs through promoter or enhancer rewiring, such as ERBB2 super-enhancer/SV activation in LUAD and analogous pan-cancer enhancer hijacking examples like IGF2 and IRS4.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Each alternative architecture class is known individually in cancer biology and many have already been reported in LUAD or related epithelial cancers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit substitution principle: these lesions are not a miscellaneous collection but recurrent architectural solutions that preserve common pathway output when standard driver routes are absent.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory synthesizes disparate rare-event literatures into a cross-architecture law of driver substitution, which is broader than existing gene-specific frameworks.
references: Berger et al. ARAF/RAF1 hotspot studies [noncanonical RAF architecture substitution]
Berger et al. RIT1 oncogenic mutations in oncogene-negative LUAD [RAS-family substitution route]
MET exon 14 skipping studies [splicing-based RTK substitution]
NRG1, RET, ROS1, NTRK1 fusion studies [structural substitution routes]
CESAM / enhancer hijacking papers [regulatory substitution route]

theory_statements.statement_name: Cross-cancer parallels reveal recurrent hidden-driver motifs that LUAD should inherit
theory_statements.theory_statement: Rare driver architectures discovered in other cancers predict hidden-driver classes in LUAD, because the same mechanistic motifs recur across tissue types: extracellular-domain receptor activation, tandem-duplication or atypical ALK architecture, enhancer hijacking, noncoding promoter mutations, and kinase truncation/fusion activation.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to hypothesis generation for unresolved lung adenocarcinoma by analogy to mechanistically related epithelial cancers and other solid tumors. It is intended for discovery prioritization, not immediate clinical assumption without validation in LUAD.
special_cases: Cross-cancer transfer works best for motif-level mechanisms, not for exact gene frequencies.
Tissue-specific enhancer context and lineage factors can prevent a motif from being active in LUAD even if it is oncogenic elsewhere.
Some examples cited from glioma or colorectal cancer are mechanistic analogies, not direct LUAD observations.

supporting_evidence.text: Glioma-associated EGFR extracellular-domain mutations and EGFRvIII demonstrate that receptor activation can occur through ectodomain structural perturbation and free-cysteine-mediated dimerization, paralleling noncanonical EGFR ECD mutations already found in pan-negative LUAD.

Atypical ALK architecture such as C2orf44-ALK in colorectal cancer can evade standard FISH or RT-PCR, directly supporting the idea that structurally unusual ALK-class events may still hide in LUAD if only canonical assays are used.

Pan-cancer enhancer hijacking mechanisms involving IGF2 and IRS4 show that structural copy-number events can act through 3D regulatory rewiring rather than dosage alone, providing a mechanistic template for unresolved LUAD regulatory drivers.

Pan-cancer kinase fusion surveys identified novel BRAF, RET, FGFR3, PKN1, FGR, PIK3CA, and TERT fusion architectures beyond classical fusions, indicating that rare architecture classes recur across epithelial cancers and should be anticipated in unresolved LUAD.

FGFR2/FGFR3 mutation work in lung squamous carcinoma shows that noncanonical extracellular and kinase-domain changes outside standard LUAD panels can still be transforming and drug-sensitive, supporting parallel search strategies in unresolved LUAD.

Noncoding promoter and TSS deletions such as KEAP1 and STK11, and promoter mutations such as ILF2, found in WGS-negative LUAD support the broader cross-cancer lesson that regulatory noncoding lesions are underappreciated driver motifs.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Cross-cancer analogy is commonly used in oncology, and the cited mechanisms are already established in their source tumor types.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit proposal that unresolved LUAD discovery should be motif-driven, using recurrent architectural mechanisms from other cancers as the search prior rather than focusing only on more genes within the same canonical pathway list.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a new heuristic theory for LUAD hidden-driver discovery built from established cross-cancer mechanisms, so it is new in formulation though not in ingredients.
references: Brennan et al. / Lee et al. EGFR ectodomain and EGFRvIII studies in glioma [mechanistic motif of extracellular activation]
Frampton et al. atypical ALK/RET fusions in colorectal and lung cancers [assay-evasive structural motif]
Weischenfeldt et al. / CESAM enhancer hijacking studies [3D regulatory motif]
Stransky et al. pan-cancer kinase fusion landscape [recurrent rare architecture motifs]

new_predictions_likely: Systematic reanalysis of unresolved LUAD using motif-based filters will discover additional events in already implicated genes but unusual architectures, such as extracellular-domain receptor mutations, exon-skipping lesions, or promoter/enhancer rearrangements, more often than discovering a single new highly recurrent hotspot gene.

Residual driver-negative LUADs enriched for MAPK signaling but lacking canonical drivers will show overrepresentation of noncanonical RAF/RAS-family lesions or structural BRAF-class events when long-read or RNA-based assays are added.
Rare ligand fusions and noncanonical receptor-domain mutations will be disproportionately enriched in never-smoker and mucinous/lineage-skewed LUAD relative to smoking-high, high-TMB tumors.
Using cross-cancer motif priors will improve validation yield of candidate LUAD hidden drivers compared with agnostic gene-frequency ranking alone.
new_predictions_unknown: Some unresolved LUADs will harbor atypical ALK/ROS1/RET/NTRK rearrangements analogous to C2orf44-ALK that are negative by standard fusion assays but positive by long-read or transcript-assembly methods.
A subset of piano or regulatory-state LUADs will be driven by enhancer hijacking of lineage survival genes other than ERBB2, potentially including NKX2-1-adjacent or surfactant-lineage loci.
Noncanonical extracellular-domain mutations in ERBB3, ERBB4, FGFR, or MET will emerge as a recurrent but tiny hidden-driver class in pan-negative LUAD once full ectodomain sequencing becomes routine.
Cross-cancer promoter-fusion architectures that overexpress intact coding oncogenes, analogous to TBL1XR1-PIK3CA or WASF2-FGR, will be found in a small number of currently unexplained LUADs.
negative_experiments: If motif-driven reanalysis of unresolved LUAD fails to enrich for unusual architecture classes relative to unselected tumors, the substitution framework would be weakened.

If cross-cancer motifs such as enhancer hijacking, ectodomain activation, and promoter fusion do not produce any additional validated LUAD discoveries beyond already known examples, the predictive value of the cross-cancer analogy would be weakened.

If newly found rare architecture events in unresolved LUAD systematically fail functional validation or target dependency testing, the theory would overestimate the causal importance of architectural substitution.
If exact gene recurrence, rather than architecture class, proves to be the dominant successful discovery prior in unresolved LUAD, the motif-first component would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not by itself explain purely lineage-state or stemness-driven quiet tumors without an obvious substituted activating architecture.
It does not fully account for protein-state-only dependencies such as ARHGEF5 phospho-activation that may occur without a clear genomic substitute architecture.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some candidate loci such as 14q13.3 amplification components or broad 4q12 gain do not function as simple substituted single drivers, indicating that not every unusual architecture restores pathway output in a clinically actionable way.
Certain studies found no specific rescued driver and emphasized non-genetic progression, suggesting that architecture substitution is not the sole explanation for all unresolved tumors.

special_cases: The theory is strongest for hypothesis prioritization and assay design, not immediate proof of causality.
Functional validation remains essential because many rare architectures are passengers or context-dependent.
Tissue context matters: a motif can be transferable even when the specific gene is not.

references: Berger et al. Oncogenic RIT1 mutations in lung adenocarcinoma [one substitute architecture class]
Frampton et al. KIF5B-RET and atypical ALK architectures [structural substitute classes]
Stransky et al. The landscape of kinase fusions in cancer [pan-cancer architecture catalog]
CESAM / enhancer hijacking studies [regulatory substitute classes]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Specific substitution architectures have been individually described across LUAD and other tumor types.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The theory's novelty is the architecture-substitution law and the proposal to use cross-cancer mechanistic motifs as the main prior for discovering hidden LUAD drivers.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No single established theory appears to unify these observations in LUAD. This is a new integrative framework built from recurring mechanistic motifs already known in the literature.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1857.name: Convergent Noncanonical Driver Architecture in Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1857.description: A large fraction of ostensibly oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not biologically driverless. They arise through several noncanonical architectures that converge on a limited set of actionable output states: hidden kinase activation, wild-type oncogene overexpression by structural/epigenomic rewiring or copy gain, and combinatorial tumor suppressor loss that recreates mitogenic signaling without a canonical hotspot mutation. The practical implication is that 'driver-negative' LUAD should be reclassified by signaling-output architecture rather than treated as a single residual category.
theory-1857.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Convergent Noncanonical Driver Architecture in Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A large fraction of ostensibly oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not biologically driverless. They arise through several noncanonical architectures that converge on a limited set of actionable output states: hidden kinase activation, wild-type oncogene overexpression by structural/epigenomic rewiring or copy gain, and combinatorial tumor suppressor loss that recreates mitogenic signaling without a canonical hotspot mutation. The practical implication is that 'driver-negative' LUAD should be reclassified by signaling-output architecture rather than treated as a single residual category.

law.theory_statement: When lung adenocarcinomas are classified as driver-negative using limited hotspot panels, incomplete fusion testing, or exome-only workflows, broader orthogonal profiling systematically converts a substantial subset into driver-positive cases by revealing hidden fusions, noncanonical coding mutations, exon-skipping events, or cryptic copy-number/structural lesions.
law.domain_scope: Applies to clinically or genomically classified oncogene-negative/driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas, especially cohorts defined by EGFR/KRAS/ALK-centered testing, fixed actionable panels, or WES without comprehensive WGS plus RNA-seq/fusion analysis. It is strongest in never-smoker or clinically oncogene-enriched populations and weaker in cohorts already deeply profiled by WGS, RNA-seq, and structural-variant-aware methods.
special_cases: The reclassification fraction depends strongly on tumor purity, allele fraction, RNA quality, structural-variant coverage, and whether amplifications are counted only when accompanied by overexpression.
Residual WGS-negative cases remain after deep profiling, so this law does not imply that all driver-negative LUADs are assay artifacts.
The law is strongest for tumors initially screened only for canonical drivers and may be much weaker in cohorts already assessed by broad hybrid-capture DNA plus RNA fusion testing.

supporting_evidence.text: In never-smoker LUAD initially called oncogene-negative by WES, 29 of 84 tumors (35%) were negative at first pass, but after deeper sequencing and orthogonal clinical testing only 16 of 84 (19%) remained unresolved; lower tumor cellularity characterized initially negative tumors.

In residual RTK/RAS/RAF-negative TCGA LUAD, 85 tumors initially classified as negative by WES/RNA-seq were reanalyzed by WGS and 28 of 85 were reassigned as pathway-positive, showing that about one-third of apparent negatives were false negatives or undercalled canonical-pathway lesions.

The review-level synthesis of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD states that 20-30% of WES-negative LUADs become positive after WGS, with missed KRAS mutations, RTK amplifications, RASA1/NF1 deletions, and NRG1 fusions accounting for many reclassifications.

In a clinical 'triple-negative' EGFR/KRAS/ALK cohort of 51 LUAD patients, comprehensive genomic profiling identified genomic alterations in 94% and actionable alterations in 31%, including occult EGFR and KRAS mutations plus ROS1, RET, MET, and ERBB2 alterations.

Within that same standard-test-negative cohort, 8 of 51 cases had EGFR mutations and 8 of 51 had KRAS mutations newly discovered by CGP, indicating that many tumors labeled negative by routine assays were actually canonical-driver-positive.

The same CGP-negative cohort contained 7 ROS1-rearranged cases discovered after negative conventional testing, and these patients subsequently showed strong response to ceritinib, confirming that at least some 'driver-negative' calls were missed actionable fusions.

KIF5B-RET was discovered in a clinically triple-marker-negative metastatic LUAD case and then recurrently identified in additional triple-negative and double-negative primary LUADs, demonstrating that tumors initially considered negative for common drivers can harbor hidden kinase fusions.

NTRK1 rearrangements were found in lung adenocarcinomas lacking known oncogenic alterations by standard clinical assays, with 2 of 36 negative cases discovered by targeted NGS and one more by subsequent FISH screening.

CD74-NRG1 fusions were discovered in pan-negative never-smoker LUAD/IMA cohorts after exclusion of EGFR, KRAS, BRAF, ERBB2, ALK, ROS1, and RET alterations, showing that some pan-negative tumors conceal rare recurrent ligand fusions.

In East Asian never-smoker LUAD, 22 of 202 tumors remained pan-negative after sequential EGFR/HER2/KRAS/BRAF/ALK/ROS1 testing, and the authors explicitly proposed further whole-exome discovery because most known-driver-negative tumors likely still harbor undiscovered events rather than representing a homogeneous null class.

RNA-seq discovery in Korean LUAD identified MET exon 14 skipping and multiple novel tyrosine kinase fusions in tumors lacking canonical point drivers and canonical transforming fusions, illustrating that transcript-level events explain part of the residual negative set.

The broad NSCLC fusion meta-analysis estimated that NRG1 perturbations, Hippo-pathway fusions, NF1 fusions, and MET exon 14 skipping together explain about 16% of driver-unknown lung cancers, further supporting systematic depletion of the 'unknown' category by noncanonical transcriptomic events.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is established that broader DNA/RNA profiling reveals occult fusions, exon-skipping events, and rare mutations in some lung adenocarcinomas initially called driver-negative by limited clinical testing. Multiple individual studies document this reclassification phenomenon.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the explicit law-like claim that driver-negative LUAD is often a measurement-defined transient state with a predictable depletion dynamic under assay expansion, rather than a stable biological class.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The component observations are existing, but the compressed general rule across panel-negative, WES-negative, and fusion-undercalled cohorts is a higher-level synthesis rather than a standard named theory.
references: Carrot-Zhang (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [direct evidence that WGS rescues many WES-negative cases]
Kohno (2012) KIF5B-RET fusion in lung adenocarcinoma [occult fusion in triple-negative LUAD]
Vaishnavi (2013) Oncogenic and drug sensitive NTRK1 rearrangements in lung cancer [hidden kinase fusions in standard-negative disease]
Park/related CGP-negative studies (as represented here by the triple-negative cohort) [clinical reclassification of standard-test-negative LUAD]

law.theory_statement: Distinct noncanonical lesion classes in oncogene-negative LUAD converge onto a small number of recurrent output states: (i) RTK/RAS/RAF-MAPK activation by rare mutations, fusions, exon skipping, or dosage gain; (ii) dual RAS plus PI3K activation by combined loss of negative regulators; (iii) lineage-survival transcriptional dependence; and (iv) enhancer- or dosage-driven overexpression of wild-type oncogenes.
law.domain_scope: Applies to human oncogene-negative LUAD and validated mouse/cell-line models intended to represent this disease, including residual RTK/RAS/RAF-negative subsets, EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, pan-negative mucinous LUAD, and oncogene-negative mouse models driven by tumor suppressor loss. It does not claim that every driver-negative tumor maps uniquely to one state, only that these are recurrent attractors.
special_cases: Some tumors occupy mixed states, especially when structural activation, lineage state, and microenvironmental signaling coexist.
Phosphoproteomic or transcriptomic outputs may partly reflect stromal or immune contributions rather than purely tumor-intrinsic biology.
Residual WGS-negative tumors may remain outside these classes due to currently unmeasured noncoding, epigenetic, or post-translational mechanisms.

supporting_evidence.text: The oncogene-negative LUAD mouse model demonstrated that concurrent Nf1, Rasa1, and Pten loss creates a robust oncogene-negative subtype with pERK and pAKT activation, directly validating dual RAS plus PI3K activation as one recurrent output state.

Oncogene-negative human LUAD frequently still showed moderate-to-strong RAS and PI3K pathway activation, and >60% had alteration in either pathway with 22% altered in both, indicating that many 'negative' tumors converge on pathway output despite lacking canonical proto-oncogene lesions.

Rare alternative drivers including RIT1 switch-II mutations, RAF-family CR2 hotspot mutations, MET exon 14 skipping, RET fusions, NTRK1 fusions, EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, and NRG1 fusions all converge on MAPK/ERBB/RTK signaling activation despite heterogeneous lesion class.

Residual oncogene-negative LUADs in TCGA were enriched for focal amplifications near MET, ERBB2, FGFR1/WHSC1L1, PDGFRA/KIT/KDR, and MAPK1, showing that copy-number-driven pathway dosage is another recurrent route to the same signaling outputs.

NKX2-1 amplification defines a lineage-survival subtype in LUAD in which a wild-type lineage transcription factor becomes the core growth dependency, validating lineage-survival dependence as a noncanonical driver architecture.

Non-CAGA LUAD shows ERBB2 overexpression driven by super-enhancer formation and structural rearrangement near HNF1B rather than canonical ERBB2 mutation or classic focal amplification, validating wild-type oncogene activation by enhancer/3D-genome rewiring.

The 23-gene SE-SV outlier set in non-CAGA LUAD, including ERBB2, EGFR, CDK4, MDM2, FRS2, CAV2, FGF3, FGF4, and FGF19, supports a recurrent architecture in which structural plus enhancer dysregulation activates wild-type genes as driver-like outliers.

RIT1 mutations, NF1 mutations, and RASA1/ARHGAP35 loss were all enriched in oncogene-negative LUAD across large cohorts, showing that noncanonical activation often occurs through pathway-regulator perturbation rather than receptor mutation alone.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Separate studies already describe hidden kinase lesions, pathway-negative-regulator loss, lineage-survival oncogenes, and enhancer-hijacking in LUAD. These mechanisms are individually established or strongly suggested.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the proposal that these apparently disparate lesions are best collapsed into a small set of recurrent output states that supersede the undifferentiated label of driver-negative LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative synthesis theory: each component exists in the literature, but the compression into four recurring driver architectures is not a standard explicit framework in LUAD.
references: Carrot-Zhang (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [heterogeneous residual mechanisms]
Campbell/TCGA LUAD oncogene-negative studies [NF1/RIT1/MET/ERBB2 alternatives]
Tanaka/related non-CAGA ERBB2 super-enhancer work [wild-type oncogene activation by enhancer-SV architecture]
Onc-negative RAS/PI3K mouse work [combinatorial tumor suppressor convergence]

law.theory_statement: Within ostensibly driver-negative LUAD, recurrence and poor outcome concentrate in tumors that exhibit noncanonical activation architectures or aggressive state programs—such as enhancer/SV-driven outlier expression, proliferative or angiogenic proteogenomic states, or non-genetic solid-like progression states—rather than in mutation absence alone.
law.domain_scope: Applies mainly to resected or early-stage LUAD cohorts where recurrence-free or overall survival was related to noncanonical genomic, transcriptomic, radiomic, or proteogenomic features. It is not intended for heavily pretreated metastatic cohorts where treatment history dominates natural history.
special_cases: Some biologically potent noncanonical drivers may not be prognostic if completely resected or if they primarily influence drug sensitivity rather than intrinsic recurrence risk.
This law pertains to risk concentration after subclassification of driver-negative tumors, not to the binary fact of being driver-negative.
State-defined risk can coexist with canonical oncogene drivers, so the law is strongest in canonical-driver-depleted cohorts but not exclusive to them.

supporting_evidence.text: In non-CAGA LUAD, outlier expression of a 23-gene SE-SV-associated set was linked to significantly worse recurrence-free survival, and FGF3/FGF4/FGF19 outliers were particularly associated with recurrence risk.

In never-smoker NS-LUAD, the chaotic subtype had no significantly enriched canonical driver mutations or fusions yet showed the worst prognosis, including strong adverse effects even in stage I disease.

In EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, the proliferation-high and angiogenesis-high proteogenomic subtypes had worse outcomes than immune-high or metabolism groups despite not being defined by a single dominant actionable oncogene.

The histologic lepidic-to-solid transition model showed that solid-like L2S-high states carry poorer prognosis even though no recurrent pattern-specific genetic lesion was identified, demonstrating that aggressive progression can be state-driven rather than mutation-defined.

A driver gene-negative radiomics-defined LUAD cohort separated into high- and low-risk groups, with the high-risk group showing substantially worse survival and enrichment of immune-related pathways, indicating that biologically meaningful risk heterogeneity exists within the canonical driver-negative label.

FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD had poorer overall survival and a fibroblast-rich, ferroptosis-resistant microenvironment, showing that adverse prognosis in canonical-driver-depleted disease can concentrate in state-defined metabolic/TME programs.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Aggressive transcriptomic or microenvironmental states and structural-expression outlier programs are already known to correlate with poor prognosis in LUAD and other cancers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel point is the explicit claim that prognosis in driver-negative LUAD is better explained by noncanonical architecture/state classes than by the residual label of 'no driver found' itself.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis of existing prognostic observations into a mechanism-oriented rule specific to the driver-negative problem rather than a wholly new underlying biology.
references: Non-CAGA LUAD super-enhancer studies [recurrence linked to expression outliers]
Never-smoker NS-LUAD subtype studies [chaotic subtype prognosis]
Tavernari/related non-genetic progression and L2S studies [state-driven progression and poor outcome]

law.theory_statement: Therapeutic vulnerabilities in oncogene-negative LUAD align more reliably with the activated axis of dependence than with the nominal lesion class: kinase-output tumors respond to matched kinase/MAPK blockade, dual RAS-PI3K tumors to vertical pathway combinations, enhancer-overexpression tumors to target engagement against the overexpressed wild-type receptor, and microenvironmental/metabolic state tumors to state-directed interventions.
law.domain_scope: Applies to preclinical and limited clinical evidence in oncogene-negative or canonical-driver-depleted LUAD and closely related NSCLC subsets. It is strongest where the causal axis has functional validation and weaker where evidence is only correlative or prognostic.
special_cases: Some lesion classes remain prognostic rather than therapeutically validated, so axis assignment may overcall actionability in under-tested subtypes.
Drug response can be modified by lineage state or co-mutations even when the same axis is active.
In some settings, the nominal lesion and the dependency axis coincide; the law matters most when they do not, such as wild-type receptor overexpression or combined suppressor loss.

supporting_evidence.text: NTRK1 fusions were transforming and sensitive to TRKA-active inhibitors, including ARRY-470 and CEP-701, showing that hidden kinase-output tumors can be treated by pathway-matched kinase blockade.

RAF-family CR2 hotspot mutations enhanced MEK/ERK signaling and were inhibited by sorafenib and trametinib; the index ARAF S214C patient had a prolonged sorafenib response, validating axis-directed therapy for rare noncanonical MAPK lesions.

RET fusions such as KIF5B-RET transformed model cells and were inhibited by RET-active multikinase inhibitors or vandetanib in preclinical systems, supporting treatment by the activated kinase axis rather than by the initial negative clinical label.

NRG1 fusions activated HER2/HER3 signaling and this output was suppressed by lapatinib and afatinib, showing that ligand-fusion tumors should be treated through their downstream receptor axis rather than as a miscellaneous fusion class.

EZR-ERBB4 and TRIM24-BRAF fusion models were inhibited by HER-family or RAF/MEK-directed inhibitors respectively, reinforcing that noncanonical fusions are therapeutically organized by activated output pathway.

The oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K mouse and cell-line models responded to SHP2 inhibition, and combined SHP2 plus AKT inhibition produced the strongest anti-tumor effects, directly supporting vertical pathway therapy for dual-output suppressor-loss tumors.

ERBB2 overexpression driven by super-enhancer/structural rearrangement in non-CAGA LUAD responded in PDX models to poziotinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan despite lack of canonical ERBB2 mutation, validating target engagement against a structurally overexpressed wild-type receptor.

FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD responded preferentially to 6-mercaptopurine, with tumor suppression and reduced fibroblast infiltration in sensitive xenografts, supporting state-directed therapy for a metabolic/fibroblast-defined rather than mutation-defined subgroup.

RASA1/NF1 co-inactivated NSCLC showed marked trametinib sensitivity and additional sensitivity to combination strategies, consistent with axis-based treatment of RAS-hyperactivated suppressor-loss disease.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Precision oncology already matches kinase fusions or rare kinase mutations to inhibitors, and vertical pathway combinations are established preclinical logic in RAS/PI3K signaling.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the explicit rule that driver-negative LUAD should be therapeutically assigned by inferred dependency axis reconstructed from noncanonical architecture or state, not by the residual category of 'no driver found'.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory statement is a translational synthesis of multiple lesion-specific studies rather than a de novo mechanism, but the axis-first therapeutic framing is broader than standard lesion-by-lesion treatment discussions.
references: Basket-trial precision oncology literature [lesion-matched therapy principle]
SHP2/AKT combination studies in RAS-PI3K activated tumors [vertical pathway targeting]
Enhancer-hijacking / wild-type oncogene overexpression studies [non-mutation target engagement]

new_predictions_likely: If a rigorously defined modern driver-negative LUAD cohort is profiled by joint WGS, long-read SV calling, RNA-seq, phosphoproteomics, and H3K27ac profiling, more than half of currently unresolved cases will segregate into one of four output classes: kinase-output, dual RAS/PI3K, lineage-survival, or enhancer-overexpression.
Among tumors with high phospho-ERK plus high phospho-AKT and evidence of impaired NF1/RASA1/PTEN axis integrity, SHP2 plus AKT inhibition will outperform either monotherapy in organoids or PDXs.
A subset of tumors with very high ERBB2, EGFR, MET, or FGFR protein but no coding activation will show local structural rewiring or focal enhancer gain rather than random overexpression.
Recurrence after resection in canonical-driver-depleted LUAD will be predicted better by architecture/state class assignment than by the binary label of driver-negative alone.
new_predictions_unknown: Some residual WGS-negative LUADs will be driven mainly by allele-specific promoter/enhancer dysfunction in lineage or stress-response genes and will only be detectable by single-cell multiome or allele-resolved epigenomics.

A fraction of unresolved driver-negative LUADs will prove to be maintained by ecDNA-mediated amplification of wild-type receptors or transcription factors without stable chromosomal amplification, creating sensitivity to transcriptional hub disruption.

Some driver-negative LUADs will be sustained by paracrine ligand circuits from fibroblasts or immune cells that maintain RTK phosphorylation without tumor-intrinsic kinase lesions; interrupting the ligand source will outperform tumor-cell-targeted kinase inhibition in those cases.
