There exists a cryptic subset of LUAD in which the decisive driver state is detectable only at phosphosite level, with little or no corresponding DNA, RNA, or total-protein abnormality.
negative_experiments: Profile a modern driver-negative LUAD cohort with comprehensive WGS, RNA-seq, long-read SV, phosphoproteomics, and H3K27ac; if most tumors still fail to map into recurrent output classes and show no convergence in signaling state, the theory is weakened.

Compare therapy prediction by lesion label versus output-axis label in preclinical models and retrospective clinical cohorts; if axis-based assignment does not improve response prediction, the therapeutic-axis statement is weakened.

In enhancer-overexpression candidates, disrupt the predicted enhancer-rewired contacts by CRISPRi or boundary restoration; if oncogene expression and tumor fitness do not decrease, the enhancer-overexpression branch is weakened.
In dual RAS/PI3K candidates, restore RASA1/NF1/PTEN function or suppress both outputs; if growth is not dependent on combined pathway signaling, the convergence claim is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not fully capture fibroblast-rich ferroptosis-resistant EGFR-wild-type LUAD where the dominant organizing biology may be TME- and ferroptosis-state-driven rather than one of the four principal genomic architectures.

The theory only partially explains precursor-state findings such as AIS3 tip-endothelial/mCAF programs and KAC transitional alveolar states, which may act before stable noncanonical driver architecture is established.

The theory does not resolve whether mutational-process-defined subsets such as SBS40a-dominant or aristolochic-acid-associated never-smoker LUAD are purely etiologic states or true mechanistic driver classes.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies argue that progression and spatial heterogeneity in LUAD can be driven primarily by non-genetic transcriptional-state change and microenvironmental reprogramming rather than hidden genomic driver architecture, implying that not all unresolved tumors are architecture-first diseases.

Even after WGS, residual RPA(-)G tumors lacked a recurrent substitute driver and remained heterogeneous, which argues that convergence may be weaker and less discretely classifiable than the theory proposes.

special_cases: Mucinous adenocarcinomas should often be analyzed separately because they are strongly enriched for NRG1, ERBB4, BRAF, RET, and NKX2-1-lineage mechanisms.
Never-smoker cohorts likely overrepresent fusion, enhancer-overexpression, APOBEC-linked, and lineage-state mechanisms relative to smoking-associated cohorts.
Some apparently driver-negative tumors are assay-false-negatives rather than a biologically distinct class.
Some architecture classes overlap; for example, an enhancer-overexpression tumor can also be lineage-dependent or TME-conditioned.

references: Carrot-Zhang (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [closest direct antecedent; residual RPA-negative heterogeneity]
Campbell/TCGA LUAD oncogene-negative analyses [NF1/RIT1/MET/ERBB2 alternative drivers in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Tanaka et al. non-CAGA ERBB2 super-enhancer study [wild-type oncogene activation by structural epigenomics]
Onc-negative RAS/PI3K mouse work [combinatorial tumor suppressor convergence model]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work already shows that many driver-negative LUADs harbor hidden fusions, rare mutations, pathway-regulator losses, or wild-type oncogene activation by copy-number or structural/epigenomic mechanisms.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory unifies those findings into a single architecture-to-output framework and argues that diagnostic and therapeutic classification should be rebuilt around recurrent dependency axes rather than the residual label of driver-negative disease.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The component mechanisms are established, but the theory-level compression into four recurrent architectures with a common therapeutic logic is a broader synthesis than any single cited study.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1858.name: Driver-Negative LUAD as State-Specific Ecologies Rather Than Hidden Single Lesions
theory-1858.description: A substantial subset of driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas is best explained not by a single missed oncogene, but by stable tumor-cell-state and ecosystem programs. In this view, some tumors are maintained by lineage plasticity, immune ecology, fibroblast-dependent ferroptosis resistance, angiogenic stroma, or non-genetic histologic progression states. Genomic lesions may still be present, but the dominant explanatory and therapeutic unit is the ecological state rather than the discrete mutation.
theory-1858.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Driver-Negative LUAD as State-Specific Ecologies Rather Than Hidden Single Lesions
components.theory_description: A substantial subset of driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas is best explained not by a single missed oncogene, but by stable tumor-cell-state and ecosystem programs. In this view, some tumors are maintained by lineage plasticity, immune ecology, fibroblast-dependent ferroptosis resistance, angiogenic stroma, or non-genetic histologic progression states. Genomic lesions may still be present, but the dominant explanatory and therapeutic unit is the ecological state rather than the discrete mutation.

law.theory_statement: In a substantial subset of canonical-driver-depleted LUAD, prognosis and biological behavior are explained more strongly by stable transcriptional/proteomic state programs than by the presence or absence of an identifiable single oncogenic lesion.
law.domain_scope: Applies to resected or treatment-naive LUAD cohorts in which transcriptomic, proteomic, or spatial-state subtypes were defined, especially never-smoker, EGFR/ALK-wild-type, EGFR-wild-type, or histology-progressing cohorts. It does not claim that lesions are irrelevant, only that state can become the dominant explanatory variable.
special_cases: Some state programs are still rooted in a canonical oncogene but remain better predicted by the downstream ecological state than by mutation alone.
In deeply metastatic or therapy-exposed tumors, treatment history may reshape state structure and reduce the explanatory strength of baseline state assignments.
This law is less applicable to rare, strongly kinase-addicted tumors such as NTRK1- or RET-fusion cases where the lesion remains dominant.

supporting_evidence.text: In EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, integrated transcriptome/proteome profiling resolved four subtypes—proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism—with distinct prognosis and vulnerabilities, despite no single alternative driver explaining the entire cohort.

In never-smoker NS-LUAD, the chaotic, proliferative, and steady subtypes predicted survival and immune behavior; the chaotic subtype had poor outcome without significant enrichment of canonical driver mutations or fusions.

In EGFR-wild-type LUAD, the FeSig-high versus FeSig-low split predicted survival, fibroblast content, ferroptosis resistance, and drug sensitivity, indicating a strong biology organized by metabolic/TME state rather than by a canonical oncogene label.

The lepidic-to-solid transition model found that histologic progression and poor prognosis were associated with transcriptional and spatial-state reprogramming rather than recurrent pattern-specific genetic lesions.

Review-level synthesis explicitly states that LUAD progression and spatial heterogeneity can be driven primarily by non-genetic transcriptional state shifts and TME reprogramming rather than acquisition of new driver mutations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: State-based and microenvironment-based LUAD classification already exists, especially for proteogenomic subtyping, ferroptosis states, and histologic progression programs.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The new step is to treat these state programs as a primary explanatory theory for a major subset of driver-negative LUAD, rather than as secondary correlates layered on top of a hidden lesion model.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement is closely connected to existing subtype literature but repositions that literature as an alternative explanation for the driver-negative problem.
references: Proteogenomic characterization of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD [four-state subtype framework]
FeSig EGFRWT LUAD studies [ferroptosis/TME state model]
L2S/non-genetic progression studies [histologic state progression without recurrent new driver lesions]

law.theory_statement: A recurrent EGFR-wild-type/driver-poor LUAD ecology is organized by fibroblast-rich stroma, intercellular ligand communication, and ferroptosis resistance genes; in this ecology, stromal support and ferroptosis suppression jointly function as the effective tumor-maintaining mechanism.
law.domain_scope: Applies to EGFR-wild-type LUAD and especially FeSig-high tumors in bulk and single-cell analyses, with strongest evidence in cohorts analyzed for fibroblast infiltration, ligand-receptor interaction, and ferroptosis-gene expression. It is not yet established as universal across all driver-negative LUAD.
special_cases: The ecology may coexist with other oncogenic lesions; EGFR-wild-type is not equivalent to fully driver-negative.
The specific mediating ligand pathways remain partly inferred and not all cohorts validated the same ligand-target signature.
Fibroblast abundance may be necessary but not sufficient; the tumor cell must also express a ferroptosis-resistant program.

supporting_evidence.text: FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD had worse survival and was defined by elevated DDIT4, NOX4, and GCLC, each of which promoted survival/migration and reduced ferroptosis sensitivity in cell assays.

Across multiple datasets, fibroblast abundance correlated positively with FeSig, fibroblast marker genes increased with FeSig, and histology plus alpha-SMA staining confirmed more fibroblasts in FeSig-high tumors.

Single-cell analysis showed a higher fibroblast fraction in FeSig-high tumors and stronger fibroblast-epithelial communication by CellChat, supporting fibroblasts as active organizers rather than passive bystanders.

NicheNet/CellChat analysis implicated IGF-binding-related signaling as a candidate fibroblast-to-tumor communication axis, and the derived communication score correlated negatively with ferroptosis score in several cohorts.

6-mercaptopurine preferentially suppressed FeSig-high A549 xenografts and reduced fibroblast infiltration, consistent with the idea that this ecological state is therapeutically targetable at the level of state support rather than classical mutation targeting.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Ferroptosis resistance, CAF-rich microenvironments, and stromal protection are established cancer concepts, and the FeSig paper directly links them in EGFR-wild-type LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel synthesis is to elevate this from a prognostic signature into a mechanistic ecological subtype of driver-poor LUAD in which fibroblasts plus ferroptosis suppression together act as the effective driver system.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The ingredients are largely existing, but the ecological-driver framing is more synthetic and explanatory than the original biomarker-oriented presentation.
references: EGFR-wild-type ferroptosis signature work [direct basis for the fibroblast-ferroptosis ecology]
CAF-mediated immune exclusion and stromal support literature [general ecological precedent]

law.theory_statement: Some driver-negative-appearing LUADs originate from or are maintained by transitional lineage states—rather than by a dominant canonical oncogene—with KRT8-positive alveolar intermediates, NKX2-1-loss gastric-like states, and quiescence-exit/stemness programs serving as recurrent examples.
law.domain_scope: Applies to early-stage, never-smoker, mucinous, or lineage-reprogrammed LUAD contexts where single-cell, spatial, or lineage-factor evidence is available. It is strongest for early progression and mucinous/gastric-like biology, and weaker for advanced metastatic disease.
special_cases: These transitional states may later acquire canonical or noncanonical genomic drivers; the law concerns initiation/maintenance state, not exclusivity of mutation.
Not all KRT8-positive or NKX2-1-low states are obligatorily tumorigenic.
The exact causal sequence among injury, cell-state change, and mutation acquisition remains unresolved in several models.

supporting_evidence.text: KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate cells (KACs) were enriched near tumors, correlated with KRAS-like programs, were less differentiated, increased along normal-AAH-LUAD progression, and were associated with poorer survival, supporting a transitional precursor-like state linked to tumorigenesis.

NKX2-1-negative LUAD adopted a gastric differentiation program in mouse and human mucinous tumors, indicating that lineage transcription factor loss can generate a tumor-maintaining identity shift rather than simply removing a marker.

The piano subtype in never-smoker lung cancer was genomically quiet, long-telomere, stemness-like, and often lacked known recurrent drivers; the authors explicitly hypothesized quiescence exit through ARID1A, NOTCH1, RET, KRAS, UBA1, or related regulators.

Review and precursor-state studies argue that some progression states in LUAD are non-genetic and plastic, supporting the concept that lineage state itself can be a principal explanatory variable in lesions that seem genomically underdriven.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Transitional alveolar states, NKX2-1-loss mucinous reprogramming, and stemness/quiescence hypotheses already exist in LUAD and precursor-lesion studies.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel move is to connect them into a unified lineage-plasticity explanation for a subset of driver-negative-appearing LUADs, especially those with low genomic burden or ambiguous canonical drivers.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis across several lineage-focused studies rather than a brand-new mechanism, but it creates a more coherent explanatory theory for genomically quiet disease.
references: KAC transitional-state studies [AT2-to-tumor intermediary model]
NKX2-1-loss/gastric differentiation studies [lineage reprogramming in mucinous LUAD]
LCINS piano/quiescence hypothesis work [genomically quiet stemness-like subtype]

law.theory_statement: In early or canonical-driver-depleted LUAD, angiogenic and immune-remodeling ecosystems can act as progression engines independently of new recurrent driver mutation acquisition; tip endothelial cells, mCAFs, and immune-excluded solid-pattern niches are recurrent manifestations of this principle.
law.domain_scope: Applies primarily to precursor lesions, minimally invasive disease, and early-stage resected LUAD analyzed by bulk, single-cell, or spatial methods. It is a progression law, not necessarily an initiation law for all advanced tumors.
special_cases: These ecosystems may cooperate with canonical oncogenes rather than replace them entirely.
Most evidence is spatial and associative rather than perturbational, so causal assignment remains provisional.
The law is strongest in early progression states and may not extrapolate linearly to metastatic disease.

supporting_evidence.text: AIS3 was associated with tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs, with endothelial and stromal activity rising in the late AIS state and spatial enrichment of endothelial cells in invasive regions, supporting an angiogenic-stromal progression engine.

MIA paired noninvasive and invasive analyses showed that endothelial-rich invasive programs emerge without needing a new recurrent mutational explanation, indicating progression through stromal/vascular remodeling.

Solid-pattern/L2S-high LUAD had immune exclusion, peripheral immune localization, and suppressive Treg/checkpoint signatures in tumor cores, indicating that ecological remodeling can define the aggressive state even when no pattern-specific recurrent genetic lesion is found.
The general review-level synthesis states that LUAD spatial heterogeneity and progression can be driven primarily by TME reprogramming rather than new driver mutations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Angiogenesis, CAFs, and immune-excluded niches are established components of LUAD progression biology, and the cited papers directly describe these patterns.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel synthesis is to treat these ecosystems as progression engines that can substitute for recurrent new mutation acquisition in explaining driver-negative or genomically quiet progression.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative reframing of existing spatial and precursor-lesion findings into a progression-focused theory for driver-poor LUAD.
references: AIS3/tip EC/mCAF progression work [angiogenic-stromal transition model]
L2S and spatial immune-exclusion studies [non-genetic progression states]
Review synthesis on non-genetic LUAD progression [TME reprogramming as progression force]

new_predictions_likely: In EGFR-wild-type or canonical-driver-depleted LUAD, fibroblast-rich tumors with high DDIT4/NOX4/GCLC will be more sensitive to combined ferroptosis induction plus CAF-disrupting therapy than to either alone.
Single-cell multiome profiling of genomically quiet 'piano-like' or chaotic never-smoker LUAD will reveal recurrent lineage-transition programs even when no shared canonical driver lesion is found.
Early-stage L2S-high or AIS3-like lesions will show greater benefit from anti-angiogenic or vessel-normalization interventions than L2S-low/lepidic lesions.
KAC-high premalignant or tumor-adjacent lung tissue will carry higher short-term risk of LUAD emergence than KAC-low tissue independent of smoking history.
new_predictions_unknown: A subset of driver-negative LUAD will depend on reversible ecologic circuits that disappear ex vivo, causing many current organoid models to miss the true driver state unless stromal and immune compartments are retained.
There exists a lineage-plasticity subtype in which restoring NKX2-1-like pulmonary identity will suppress tumor fitness more effectively than direct pathway inhibition.
Some piano/quiet tumors will prove to arise from stem-cell niche dysregulation rather than tumor-cell-autonomous mutation burden, making local niche-targeted prevention feasible.

Solid immune-excluded progression states may be inducible without new genomic change by biomechanical or vascular cues alone, implying that some recurrence could be prevented by early ecological intervention.
negative_experiments: If fibroblast depletion or blockade of candidate fibroblast-tumor ligand axes does not change ferroptosis sensitivity in FeSig-high models, the fibroblast-ferroptosis ecology branch is weakened.
If KAC-high or NKX2-1-low transitional states fail to predict progression or dependency after controlling for mutation burden and histology, the lineage-plasticity theory is weakened.
If anti-angiogenic or vessel-normalization interventions do not preferentially affect AIS3/MIA endothelial-rich lesions in relevant models, the microenvironmental progression law is weakened.
If integrated state classifiers do not outperform comprehensive genomic profiling in prognosis or response prediction for canonical-driver-depleted cohorts, the state-dominance law is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Rare but clearly targetable kinase-fusion cases such as RET-, NTRK1-, or NRG1-driven tumors are better explained by lesion-dominant models than by state-dominant ecology.
Residual RPA(-)G tumors with ILF2 promoter mutation, KEAP1/STK11 deletions, or complex structural variants may require a genomic architecture explanation rather than a purely ecological one.

conflicting_evidence.text: Many apparently driver-negative tumors are reclassified as driver-positive after broader profiling, which limits how often a pure state-first explanation can be the primary answer.
Some cohorts show recurrent hidden genomic lesions such as RIT1, NF1, MET, ERBB2, or RET in oncogene-negative disease, indicating that mutation architecture often remains central.

special_cases: Mucinous LUAD is a special case in which lineage state and rare fusions often coexist, making lesion-versus-state separation difficult.
Never-smoker cohorts may show stronger lineage and ecological organization than smoking-associated cohorts with high genomic burden.
Early progression lesions and advanced metastatic tumors may obey different balances between state-dominant and lesion-dominant control.

references: Kim et al. proteogenomic characterization of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD [four-state model in canonical-driver-depleted disease]
FeSig EGFRWT LUAD study [fibroblast-rich ferroptosis-resistant state]
Tavernari/L2S-related progression studies [non-genetic progression states]
KAC and NKX2-1 lineage studies [lineage-plasticity mechanisms]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work already supports ecological, lineage, and non-genetic state explanations for subsets of LUAD, particularly in never-smoker, EGFR/ALK-wild-type, ferroptosis-related, and histologic progression contexts.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory unifies those strands into an explicit alternative to the hidden-lesion model, proposing that many driver-negative LUADs are best understood as stable ecological states whose effective drivers are stromal, metabolic, immune, or lineage programs.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The pieces are established, but their assembly into a coherent state-dominant theory of driver-negative LUAD is more synthetic than standard lesion-centered interpretations.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1859.name: Residual Ecosystem-Driven LUAD
theory-1859.description: After exhaustive subtraction of hidden canonical and rare genomic drivers, a substantial residual fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is best explained as an ecosystem-maintained disease state rather than a single-lesion disease. In these tumors, stable combinations of lineage plasticity, stromal/vascular remodeling, immune architecture, and metabolic stress programs create self-reinforcing attractors that substitute for classical kinase addiction and determine prognosis and therapy response.
theory-1859.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Residual Ecosystem-Driven LUAD
components.theory_description: After exhaustive subtraction of hidden canonical and rare genomic drivers, a substantial residual fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is best explained as an ecosystem-maintained disease state rather than a single-lesion disease. In these tumors, stable combinations of lineage plasticity, stromal/vascular remodeling, immune architecture, and metabolic stress programs create self-reinforcing attractors that substitute for classical kinase addiction and determine prognosis and therapy response.

law.theory_statement: In residual driver-negative LUAD that remains unresolved after broad DNA/RNA profiling, tumor aggressiveness, recurrence risk, and progression are more consistently organized by transcriptional state and microenvironmental architecture than by recurrent shared genomic lesions.
law.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma cohorts explicitly or approximately depleted of canonical drivers, especially residual groups after comprehensive sequencing, never-smoker EGFR/ALK-wild-type cohorts, early-stage progression cohorts, and histologic-pattern/spatially profiled LUAD. Does not apply to tumors ultimately explained by clear kinase fusions, hotspot mutations, or focal oncogene activation after deeper profiling.
special_cases: This law excludes tumors reclassified by deeper profiling as RET, NTRK1, NRG1, MET exon 14, EGFR ECD, RAF-family, RIT1, ERBB2, or other rare genomic driver-positive cases.
Some state programs may still be initiated or weakly constrained by noncanonical genomic events, so the law concerns dominant explanatory structure, not absolute genomic absence.
The law is strongest for residual unresolved subsets, not for all LUAD.

supporting_evidence.text: A multi-omics LUAD progression framework concluded that progression and spatial heterogeneity are driven primarily by non-genetic plastic transcriptional state shifts and tumor microenvironment reprogramming rather than acquisition of new driver mutations.

Histologic progression from lepidic to solid was not explained by recurrent pattern-specific mutations, copy-number events, or fusions; instead, progression followed a transcriptional and methylation continuum captured by the L2S signature and associated with prognosis across more than 2,000 LUADs.

In never-smoker NS-LUAD, the chaotic subtype had no significantly enriched canonical driver mutations or fusions, yet showed the worst prognosis, strong EMT/cell-movement programs, mixed lineage, and abundant CAFs, indicating that poor-outcome biology can be organized without recurrent canonical genomic enrichment.

EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD segregated into four proteogenomic states (proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, metabolism) with distinct outcomes, pathways, and vulnerabilities, reinforcing that residual non-EGFR/ALK disease is heterogeneous and state-structured rather than a single hidden-driver entity.

FeSig-defined EGFR-wild-type LUAD was stratified by ferroptosis-related state, fibroblast-rich microenvironment, and ligand-receptor communication rather than by a newly identified oncogenic lesion, yet these states strongly associated with outcome and therapy sensitivity.

The immune-risk study of driver-gene-negative LUAD did not identify a new genomic driver but still stratified prognosis by immune transcriptional state, checkpoint expression, and immune-cell composition, indicating that clinically meaningful biology persists even when canonical drivers are absent.

Residual RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative LUAD after WGS (RPA(-)G) was heterogeneous and lacked a single recurrent replacement driver; instead, it showed high TMB, TP53/KEAP1/SMARCA4 enrichment, focal deletions, noncoding changes, and complex SVs, supporting the idea that the unresolved compartment is not organized by one recurrent lesion class.

The hallmarks analysis of LUAD reported that only 47% of tumors had a known driver mutation for sustaining proliferative signaling and only 55% when amplifications were added, with many tumors lacking hallmark-linked genomic explanations, motivating non-genetic or unrecognized-state explanations.

This law was logically inferred by induction from the convergence of state-defined prognostic subtypes, non-genetic progression studies, and the failure of residual WGS-negative cohorts to yield a single recurrent replacement oncogene.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Non-genetic plasticity, immune/stromal remodeling, and transcriptomic progression states are established in LUAD and other cancers. Several cited studies already show that progression or prognosis can be governed by state variables independent of canonical driver identity.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is elevating these observations into a general explanatory law for the residual, comprehensively profiled driver-negative LUAD compartment: after genomic subtraction, state architecture becomes the dominant causal framework rather than an unresolved search for one more recurrent kinase lesion.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This law is grounded in existing literature but synthesizes multiple otherwise separate observations into a single high-level theory specifically for residual driver-negative LUAD.
references: Tavernari et al. (year not provided) LUAD progression studies [non-genetic progression and TME reprogramming as precedent for state-first progression]
L2S / lepidic-to-solid progression study (year not provided) [epigenetic-transcriptional progression continuum in LUAD]
Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [state-defined residual disease heterogeneity]
A prognostic signature for lung adenocarcinoma in people who have never smoked [chaotic subtype as driver-poor but aggressive state]

law.theory_statement: A subset of residual driver-negative or driver-negative-appearing LUAD originates from transitional or lineage-reprogrammed epithelial states that are not necessarily sufficient oncogenic lesions themselves but create high-plasticity, high-tolerance cellular substrates on which invasion, persistence, and later stabilization occur.
law.domain_scope: Applies to early-stage LUAD, never-smoker LUAD, precursor-to-invasive transitions, and lineage-reprogrammed or transitional epithelial contexts such as KRT8-positive alveolar intermediates, AIS3/MIA transitions, and NKX2-1-loss-associated mucinous/gastric programs. Not intended for clearly mutation-addicted kinase-fusion tumors.
special_cases: The transitional state may be obligatory in some developmental trajectories and optional in others.
In some tumors, lineage states may be consequences of genomic lesions such as KRAS or NKX2-1 loss rather than fully prior conditions.
Some transitional states may be field effects in adjacent lung rather than autonomous malignant clones.

supporting_evidence.text: KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate cells (KACs) were tumor-proximal, less differentiated, enriched in tumor-adjacent normal regions, associated with KRAS signatures and poor survival, detected before overt tumors in carcinogen-exposed mice, and lineage-linked to tumors, supporting a transitional cell state permissive for tumorigenesis.

AIS3 represented a late preinvasive state associated with non-pGGO radiology, tip endothelial cells, matrix CAFs, angiogenesis, EMT, and proposed transition toward invasion, indicating that preinvasive progression is organized by a transitional ecosystem rather than a newly shown driver mutation.

MIA_nonInv and paired MIA_Inv subtypes showed that noninvasive transcriptomic states already encode invasive-state organization and endothelial/stromal shifts, suggesting progression can be prefigured by state transitions before frank invasion.

NKX2-1 loss in Kras-driven mouse lung tumors induced gastric-lineage/mucinous reprogramming rather than a simple proliferative phenotype; human NKX2-1-negative mucinous LUAD expressed gastric markers, showing that lineage-state conversion can define oncogenic behavior.

The piano subtype of never-smoker lung cancer was genomically quiet, stemness-like, long-telomere, high-ITH, and often lacked recurrent canonical drivers; the authors explicitly hypothesized quiescence-exit biology involving ARID1A/NOTCH1-like regulators as a mechanism for tumors with no detected known drivers.

The quiescence-exit hypothesis proposed that mutations in ARID1A, NOTCH1, or similar genes could drive tumors lacking known cancer-driver mutations or fusions by promoting exit from quiescence and increasing intratumor heterogeneity.

The MIF+AT2 single-cell population in driver gene-negative LUAD showed elevated glycolysis and malignancy-associated features, implying that a specific altered epithelial state exists within this clinically driver-negative setting.

This law was logically inferred through abduction from the coexistence of preinvasive transitional states, tumor-adjacent KAC field states, and lineage-reprogrammed mucinous states that repeatedly precede or define malignant behavior without requiring one shared recurrent genomic driver.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Transitional alveolar states, precursor-state biology, NKX2-1-linked lineage plasticity, and quiescence/stemness hypotheses already exist in lung cancer literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is to unify these as a general law for residual driver-negative LUAD: transitional lineage states are not merely byproducts but causal tumor-permissive substrates that can substitute for single-lesion explanations in unresolved cases.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative causal strengthening of existing observations rather than a wholly unprecedented claim.
references: KAC study (year not provided) [AT2-to-tumor intermediate-state model]
Multiomics Analysis Reveals Molecular Changes during Early Progression of Precancerous Lesions to Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [AIS3/MIA transitional-state progression]
NKX2-1-negative mucinous LUAD lineage studies [gastric reprogramming as lineage-defined tumor state]
LCINS piano subtype analysis [quiescence-exit hypothesis]

law.theory_statement: Once residual driver-negative LUAD enters a high-risk state, stromal fibroblasts, tip endothelial programs, suppressive immune topology, and ligand-mediated intercellular signaling stabilize that state and often become more predictive of outcome and therapy response than any single tumor-cell mutation.
law.domain_scope: Applies to aggressive residual noncanonical LUAD states with strong stromal, vascular, or immune architecture, including FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type tumors, chaotic never-smoker LUAD, AIS3/MIA transition states, and solid/L2S-high lesions. Not intended for purely cell-autonomous fusion-driven tumors with weak stromal dependence.
special_cases: Immune-hot subtypes may still benefit from immunotherapy despite high-risk state features.
Different high-risk tumors may be locked by different dominant axes: CAF-IGF signaling, endothelial/tip-EC programs, or immune exclusion.
Some stromal signatures may partly reflect reactive response rather than causal maintenance.

supporting_evidence.text: FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD showed abundant fibroblasts, stronger fibroblast-epithelial communication, a negative correlation between communication score and ferroptosis score, higher IGF-binding pathway activity, and worse survival; 6-MP reduced both tumor growth and alpha-SMA-positive fibroblast infiltration, indicating a stromal maintenance axis.

Single-cell and bulk analyses showed fibroblasts correlated positively with FeSig across multiple datasets, while fibroblast-rich FeSig-high tumors had nest-like CAF barriers and stronger ligand-receptor communication.

AIS3 and invasive MIA regions were enriched for tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs, and spatial transcriptomics confirmed endothelial enrichment in invasive regions, supporting an angiogenic-stromal lock-in mechanism before invasion.

Solid histologic LUAD regions showed immune exclusion with immune cells concentrated at the periphery and residual core infiltrates enriched for suppressive markers such as FOXP3, CD25, Tim3, CTLA4, VISTA, and ICOS, indicating that aggressive state can be maintained by microenvironmental architecture.

The chaotic never-smoker LUAD subtype had high CAF abundance, EMT/cell-movement programs, and poor prognosis without significantly enriched canonical drivers, consistent with microenvironment-dependent state maintenance.

The angiogenesis-high EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD subtype was enriched for CAF and angiogenic ligands such as FGF2, CXCL12, PDGFB, and LGALS3, reinforcing that a stromal-vascular program can define a noncanonical disease state.

The STK11-mutant immune-cold subtype showed low immune infiltration and a neutrophil-degranulation proteomic program, providing another example of a state stabilized by immune-microenvironment configuration rather than a single druggable oncogene.

This law was logically inferred from the repeated association between fibroblast/endothelial/immune topology and prognosis across multiple noncanonical LUAD cohorts, plus the therapeutic reversibility of at least one fibroblast-rich state.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: CAF-rich, angiogenic, and immune-excluded ecosystems are well documented in LUAD and other cancers, and stromal communication has established prognostic value.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that in residual driver-negative LUAD these ecosystems are not ancillary context but the primary maintenance circuitry of the disease state.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory intensifies and unifies existing microenvironment literature into a driver-negative-specific maintenance law.
references: FeSig EGFR-wild-type LUAD study [fibroblast-ferroptosis resistance state]
AIS3 / preinvasive LUAD angiogenesis studies [tip EC and mCAF progression ecology]
L2S / solid-region spatial immunology study [immune-excluded architecture in aggressive LUAD]

law.theory_statement: In residual driver-negative LUAD, the most productive therapies will preferentially target state-maintenance circuitry—metabolic stress adaptation, ferroptosis resistance, stromal ligands, angiogenic support, lineage-state regulators, or immune topology—rather than relying exclusively on continued search for a single hidden kinase lesion.
law.domain_scope: Applies after broad genomic, fusion, copy-number, and splice-event profiling has failed to reveal a clear tractable genomic driver. Best suited to residual unresolved tumors displaying reproducible state signatures or ecosystem-defined subtypes.
special_cases: Comprehensive profiling should precede state-directed therapy, because many apparently driver-negative tumors are later reclassified as rare genomic-driver-positive.
Some state vulnerabilities may require combinations with chemotherapy, immunotherapy, or pathway inhibitors.
This law does not imply kinase discovery should stop; it implies therapy should branch once residual status is confirmed.

supporting_evidence.text: 6-MP showed preferential efficacy in FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD, with stronger activity in A549 than H3122 and reduction of fibroblast infiltration in vivo, demonstrating that a state-defined metabolic/stromal program can be therapeutically actionable without a new canonical driver lesion.

In driver gene-negative LUAD, MIF-HIF-1alpha activation increased glycolysis and proliferation in AT2 cells, while the MIF inhibitor ISO-1 reversed these effects, showing that a metabolic-state node can be directly perturbed in a canonical-driver-negative context.

EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD subtypes mapped to subtype-specific dependencies (CDK9, TRAF2, GRB2, ACACA) and PRISM-aligned candidate drugs, supporting the principle that state-defined vulnerabilities can be mapped independently of canonical driver identity.

The immune-risk signature in driver-negative LUAD separated tumors by checkpoint expression and predicted therapy-response differences, implying that treatment allocation can be guided by state composition even when no new oncogene is identified.

The radiomics nomogram in driver-negative LUAD linked low-risk tumors to metabolic pathways and high-risk tumors to immune-related pathways, suggesting orthogonal non-genomic treatment axes within the driver-negative clinical group.

TTF1-negative LUAD and STK11/FGL1-rich subtype analyses suggested sensitivity to DNA-repair targeting, mTOR inhibition, docetaxel, or LAG3/FGL1-axis intervention based on proteogenomic state rather than canonical driver mutation.
This law was logically inferred through induction from multiple examples where residual or noncanonical LUAD subtypes yielded actionable dependencies without discovery of a unifying hidden oncogene.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: State-directed therapy, dependency mapping, and subtype-specific vulnerabilities are established concepts in oncology, including lung cancer.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is recasting these as the default translational strategy for the residual comprehensively profiled driver-negative LUAD compartment, rather than as optional adjuncts after failed driver hunting.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement is translationally stronger than the existing literature but remains well anchored in published dependency and response observations.
references: FeSig EGFR-wild-type LUAD study [6-MP as state-directed therapy]
Driver gene-negative LUAD MIF-HIF-1alpha study [metabolic-state perturbation]
Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [subtype dependency mapping]

new_predictions_likely: If residual driver-negative LUADs are clustered jointly by spatial transcriptomics, phosphoproteomics, and cell-cell communication scores, subtype reproducibility will exceed clustering by private DNA lesions alone.

In residual driver-negative LUAD organoid-fibroblast cocultures, perturbing CAF-epithelial ligand axes or ferroptosis-resistance programs will reduce growth more often than broad RTK inhibitor screening.

Adjacent histologically normal lung from residual driver-negative cases will more often contain KAC-like, AIS3-like, or lineage-reprogrammed epithelial states than adjacent lung from clear fusion-driven tumors.

In residual driver-negative resected tumors, prognosis will correlate more strongly with fibroblast abundance, endothelial-tip scores, immune-exclusion topology, or ferroptosis-resistance signatures than with total mutation burden alone.
new_predictions_unknown: Forced restoration of pulmonary lineage stability, such as reactivation of NKX2-1-like differentiation programs in selected residual tumors, will collapse some driver-negative states even without targeting a conventional oncogene.

A subset of residual driver-negative LUAD will depend on airway microbiome-derived inflammatory metabolites that reinforce MIF-HIF-1alpha or fibroblast-IGF signaling; antibiotic or microbiome-modulating intervention will selectively alter state stability.

Longitudinal ctDNA methylation plus circulating protein measurements will reveal preinvasive tipping points corresponding to state transitions (for example KAC-like to invasive or fibroblast-rich lock-in) before radiographic invasion becomes apparent.

Residual driver-negative LUADs will show mechanically dependent survival such that removal of alveolar stromal geometry or matrix stiffness cues causes disproportionate collapse ex vivo relative to clear oncogene-driven comparators.
negative_experiments: If residual comprehensively profiled driver-negative LUADs cluster more reproducibly by recurrent genomic lesions than by ecosystem/state variables, the central state-dominance law is weakened.

If perturbation of MIF, ferroptosis genes, CAF ligands, IGF-related communication, endothelial programs, or lineage-state regulators has minimal effect across patient-derived residual driver-negative models, the state-maintenance claim is weakened.

If tumor-adjacent normal tissue from residual driver-negative LUAD lacks transitional epithelial states more often than matched oncogene-driven tumors, the lineage-permissive initiation law is weakened.

If stromal depletion or ecosystem perturbation is generally less effective than empiric kinase inhibition in residual driver-negative models despite absent genomic drivers, the residual state-therapy law is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not explain tumors that only appear driver-negative because limited assays missed rare but real genomic drivers such as NTRK1 fusions, KIF5B-RET, EGFR ECD mutations, ARAF/RAF1 hotspot mutations, RIT1 mutations, or hidden EGFR/KRAS mutations.

The theory only partly accounts for tumors driven by clearly genomic wild-type gene activation through enhancer hijacking or structural rearrangement, such as ERBB2 SE-SV overexpression or other noncoding architecture-first mechanisms.
It does not naturally subsume genetically explicit tumor-suppressor convergence models such as NF1/RASA1/PTEN-driven oncogene-negative LUAD, which are more pathway-genetic than state-ecologic.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies show that many apparently driver-negative tumors are reclassified as genomically driver-positive with deeper profiling, implying that the true ecosystem-dominant residual may be smaller than crude panel-negative estimates suggest.

Large never-smoker and East Asian cohorts still show EGFR or other canonical drivers dominating much of the population, limiting the scope of any state-first explanation to the residual unresolved subset rather than the broader disease.

special_cases: This theory is only intended for the residual unresolved compartment after broad genomic subtraction.
Lineage-dominant residual tumors and microenvironment-dominant residual tumors may require distinct submodels even if both belong to the same ecosystem framework.
The theory is compatible with weak or private genomic lesions acting as initial triggers so long as maintenance is predominantly state-based.

references: Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [related subtype/ecosystem framework in residual non-EGFR/ALK disease]
A prognostic signature for lung adenocarcinoma in people who have never smoked [related transcriptomic-state stratification in never-smoker LUAD]
Multiomics Analysis Reveals Molecular Changes during Early Progression of Precancerous Lesions to Lung Adenocarcinoma in Never-Smokers [related non-genetic progression-state framework]
Tavernari-related non-genetic LUAD progression work summarized in review [closest precedent for non-genetic progression as primary mechanism]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Prior literature already shows non-genetic progression, stromal remodeling, immune topology, and lineage plasticity in LUAD, and several studies define state-based subgroups in genomically restricted cohorts.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel step is to synthesize these into a unified general theory that the residual, comprehensively profiled driver-negative LUAD compartment is fundamentally ecosystem-maintained and should be explained and treated as a state disease rather than primarily as an undiscovered-oncogene disease.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The theory extends and integrates existing observations rather than inventing a wholly disconnected framework, but its explicit application to residual driver-negative LUAD as a distinct explanatory category is new.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1860.name: Distributed Weak-Driver Convergence in Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1860.description: A large fraction of driver-negative LUAD is not truly oncogene-null; instead it is driven by distributed subthreshold lesions that converge on signaling output without creating a classic single dominant driver. These lesions include RasGAP loss, weak RTK copy gain, wild-type lineage factor amplification, enhancer hijacking, noncoding promoter mutations, exon skipping, and phosphoproteomic activation. The clinically important unit is therefore pathway-output state, not lesion class.
theory-1860.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Distributed Weak-Driver Convergence in Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A large fraction of driver-negative LUAD is not truly oncogene-null; instead it is driven by distributed subthreshold lesions that converge on signaling output without creating a classic single dominant driver. These lesions include RasGAP loss, weak RTK copy gain, wild-type lineage factor amplification, enhancer hijacking, noncoding promoter mutations, exon skipping, and phosphoproteomic activation. The clinically important unit is therefore pathway-output state, not lesion class.

law.theory_statement: In driver-negative LUAD, many tumors achieve oncogenic sufficiency through heterogeneous, individually rare lesions that converge on a limited set of outputs—RTK/RAS/MAPK activation, PI3K-AKT-mTOR activation, lineage-survival enforcement, or cell-cycle amplification—without any one substitute lesion recurring at high frequency.
law.domain_scope: Applies to panel-negative or WES-negative LUAD cohorts later reassessed by WGS, fusion analysis, copy-number profiling, noncoding analysis, or phosphoproteomics. Especially relevant to RPA(-), oncogene-negative, and non-CAGA cohorts.
special_cases: Some cohorts still contain hidden canonical drivers rather than true weak-driver convergence.
Certain histology-linked subgroups such as NRG1-fusion IMA or RET/NTRK fusion disease are better viewed as rare strong drivers, not distributed weak drivers.
The law concerns pathway sufficiency through convergence, not equal functional importance of every lesion.

supporting_evidence.text: Whole-genome analysis of RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative LUAD rescued one-third of WES-negative cases by finding missed KRAS mutations, ARAF/EGFR/MAPK1/SOS1 amplifications, RASA1/NF1 deletions, and NRG1 alterations, demonstrating that apparent driver-negativity often resolves into diverse low-frequency convergent lesions.

Distinct-pattern analysis of oncogene-negative LUAD identified enrichment of rare alterations in SOS1, RASA1, VAV1, ARHGAP35 and focal amplifications near FGFR1/WHSC1L1, PDGFRA/KIT/KDR, and MAPK1, again indicating convergence on signaling through heterogeneous rare events.

Comprehensive molecular profiling of LUAD showed that oncogene-negative tumors were enriched for MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, NF1 mutation, and RIT1 mutation, increasing evidence of RTK/RAS/RAF activation from 62% to 76% without a single dominant replacement lesion.

Residual RPA(-)G tumors contained recurrent STK11 and KEAP1 deletions, ILF2 promoter mutations, amplifications of NKX2-1, KAT6A, CCNE1, MDM2, MYC, MCL1, and MYCL, and many complex amplicon architectures, but no single shared substitute driver, directly supporting distributed convergence without high recurrence.

The non-CAGA LUAD super-enhancer/SV study identified many outlier-overexpressed genes (ERBB2, EGFR, CDK4, MDM2, FRS2, CAV2, FGF3, FGF4, FGF19) but emphasized that these were gene-by-gene heterogeneous candidates rather than one recurrent replacement oncogene.

The residual noncanonical cohort in mapping hallmarks showed that many tumors lacked known proliferative-signaling drivers, implying that multiple weak or unassayed mechanisms jointly underlie the remaining cases.
This law was logically inferred by induction from repeated detection of many low-frequency lesion classes across driver-negative cohorts without emergence of a single dominant replacement driver.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already recognized that panel-negative LUAD can contain rare mutations, amplifications, splicing events, and fusions missed by routine testing.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is framing these lesions collectively as a distributed weak-driver regime whose biology is defined by convergent pathway sufficiency rather than by recurrence of any one substitute driver.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This law extends existing genomic observations into a systems-level rule about how residual driver-negative LUAD is organized.
references: Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [closest direct precedent]
Distinct patterns of somatic genome alterations in lung adenocarcinomas and squamous cell carcinomas [rare enriched alterations in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [oncogene-negative enrichment for rare alternative pathway lesions]

law.theory_statement: In driver-negative LUAD, direct measurement of pathway output—phosphoproteomic MAPK activity, PI3K/AKT activation, ferroptosis resistance, or lineage-factor dependency—predicts tumor biology more reliably than the specific weak lesion used to generate that output.
law.domain_scope: Applies to residual or noncanonical LUAD cohorts with phosphoproteomic, proteogenomic, RPPA, or functional signaling readouts. Especially relevant when lesions are copy-number low, noncoding, or structurally complex.
special_cases: If a strong canonical driver is present, lesion identity can still dominate output.
Bulk proteomic measures may blur tumor-cell and microenvironmental contributions in some cohorts.

supporting_evidence.text: RPPA analysis in LUAD showed that many KRAS-wild-type tumors still had significant MAPK activation, meaning pathway output was not fully captured by canonical mutation status.

In the Taiwanese proteogenomic cohort, most EGFR-wild-type cases had low MAPK signaling but the minority with high MAPK signaling were enriched for KRAS mutations, showing that pathway activity can identify biologically coherent subsets beyond mutation labels.

The oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K mouse-human convergence model classified human tumors by pERK and pAKT activation and by a mouse-derived expression signature, supporting pathway-output-defined subtyping over individual lesion identity.

KEAP1-mutant LUAD showed increased NFE2L2 phosphorylation and NRF2 signatures without RNA-level changes, demonstrating that post-transcriptional signaling output can be the critical biologic variable rather than lesion annotation alone.

EGFR-mutant and ALK-fusion tumors showed SHP2/PTPN11 and ALK phosphosite outliers respectively, illustrating a general principle that phosphosite activity can reveal actionable output states beyond DNA status.

FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD was defined by ferroptosis-resistance output and fibroblast communication rather than by a common DNA lesion, yet this output state predicted prognosis and therapy sensitivity.
This law was logically inferred from repeated discordance between genomic lesion identity and measured downstream signaling or stress-adaptation state across LUAD cohorts.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Proteogenomic studies already show that phosphosite and protein activity can diverge from DNA or RNA status in lung cancer.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is making pathway-output measurement a formal organizing principle for driver-negative LUAD, where lesion diversity is especially high and recurrence is weak.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This law is a synthesis of existing proteogenomic findings into a driver-negative-specific rule.
references: Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [MAPK output beyond canonical mutation]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [phosphosite-defined actionable biology]
Oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD model [output-defined subtype with pERK/pAKT]

law.theory_statement: When no canonical RTK/RAS/RAF driver dominates, driver-negative LUAD can be stabilized by amplification or overexpression of wild-type lineage and survival regulators, producing dependency on normal developmental circuitry rather than mutationally activated kinases.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LUADs with focal amplification, enhancer hijacking, or overexpression of wild-type regulatory genes, especially NKX2-1- or ERBB2-centered cases and related SE-SV or copy-number-driven subgroups.
special_cases: Some wild-type overexpression events may still cooperate with other private lesions.
Not all amplified genes in a region are functional drivers; some are passengers of structural complexity.

supporting_evidence.text: Focal 14q13.3 amplification identified NKX2-1 as a lineage-survival oncogene in LUAD; NKX2-1 knockdown reduced viability and colony formation in expressing lines while A549 cells lacking NKX2-1 protein were unaffected.

ERBB2 overexpression in non-CAGA LUAD arose through super-enhancer formation and structural rearrangement near HNF1beta without canonical ERBB2 mutation or focal amplification, directly demonstrating wild-type gene activation as a driver-like mechanism.

The 23-gene SE-SV outlier set, including ERBB2, EGFR, CDK4, MDM2, FRS2, CAV2, and FGF3/4/19, associated with postoperative recurrence, implying that wild-type overexpression programs can behave as driver-like events in non-CAGA LUAD.
TERT, VEGFA, CDK4/CCND1/CCNE1, and other recurrent amplifications in LUAD indicate that copy-number gain of wild-type survival or proliferative genes is a common noncanonical oncogenic mechanism.

The conceptual framework from the non-CAGA study explicitly proposed that wild-type genes may become drivers via overexpression induced by structural/epigenomic architecture, broadening the meaning of a driver event in driver-negative LUAD.

This law was logically inferred from the shared pattern that several driver-negative-enriched cohorts nominate wild-type overexpression of lineage, cell-cycle, or RTK signaling genes in the absence of classical activating mutations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Lineage-survival oncogenes and enhancer-hijacking-driven overexpression are established concepts in cancer biology, and NKX2-1 amplification is a known LUAD example.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is to treat these events as a substitution class for driver-negative LUAD broadly, not as isolated curiosities, and to unify copy-number amplification with super-enhancer/SV-driven wild-type dependencies.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The law is an integrative extension of existing lineage-survival and enhancer-hijacking concepts into the driver-negative LUAD problem space.
references: 14q13.3-amplified LUAD / NKX2-1 lineage-survival study [direct precedent]

Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [direct precedent for wild-type overexpression as driver-like event]

law.theory_statement: The observed size of the driver-negative LUAD compartment is inversely related to assay depth and modality breadth; however, the tumors that remain unresolved after deep profiling are disproportionately enriched for distributed weak drivers and pathway-output phenotypes rather than for one additional undiscovered recurrent hotspot.
law.domain_scope: Applies to cohort-level estimates of driver-negative LUAD generated by routine panels, WES, WGS, RNA-seq, fusion assays, and proteogenomic profiling.
special_cases: The law concerns population-level trends; individual tumors may still harbor strong undiscovered genomic drivers.
Different panels classify driver-negative status differently, so exact percentages vary by platform and study era.

supporting_evidence.text: In the standard-test-negative triple-negative cohort, CGP found alterations in 94% of cases and actionable events in 31%, including hidden EGFR/KRAS mutations and ROS1/RET/MET/ERBB2 alterations, showing that shallow testing inflates driver-negative classification.

In never-smoker LUAD classified as oncogene-negative by WES, additional clinical sequencing, FISH, RNA-seq, and deeper WES raised driver detection to about 81%, and lower tumor cellularity explained many initial false negatives.
WGS of RPA(-)E LUAD reclassified one-third of samples as actually RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-positive, again shrinking the residual compartment with deeper profiling.
The any-genotyping no-driver group in LCMC explicitly mixed true negatives with incompletely tested tumors, warning that crude no-driver frequencies overestimate biological driver-negativity.

Even after broad profiling, residual groups such as RPA(-)G or non-CAGA LUAD remained heterogeneous and lacked one dominant recurrent replacement lesion, supporting the second half of the law that true residuals are not simply waiting for one more hotspot discovery.
This law was logically inferred by combining direct reclassification evidence with the persistent heterogeneity of the deep-profiled residual compartment.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is well known that broader assays discover more actionable alterations and reduce the apparent driver-negative fraction.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel part is the bifurcated claim that after this predictable reclassification, the irreducible residual is specifically enriched for distributed weak-driver convergence and pathway-output phenotypes rather than a single missing recurrent event.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The first half is established; the second half is a new synthesis based on residual-cohort behavior across studies.
references: Genomic profiling of triple-negative LUAD after standard testing [direct evidence of reclassification by assay depth]
Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [direct evidence of WGS reclassification]
Never-smoker oncogene-negative LUAD by WES study [depth and cellularity effects on apparent driver-negativity]

new_predictions_likely: Across multi-institutional cohorts, the fraction labeled driver-negative will drop stepwise from routine panel to comprehensive DNA/RNA profiling, but the residual group will become more enriched for pathway-output biomarkers such as pERK/pAKT, NRF2 phosphosignaling, or fibroblast communication scores.

Tumors with weak convergent lesions (for example low-level MET/ERBB2 gain, RasGAP loss, or SE-SV overexpression) will cluster together by downstream signaling state more strongly than by lesion identity.

Residual WGS-negative tumors with ILF2 promoter mutation or complex amplicons will show disproportionate dependency on DNA-damage response or transcriptional buffering pathways compared with rare-fusion-positive controls.

A combined classifier using phosphoproteomics plus copy-number/noncoding features will identify more reproducible therapeutic groups than mutation-only classifiers in the residual driver-negative population.
new_predictions_unknown: Some residual driver-negative LUADs will require at least two weak lesions from different classes—such as RasGAP loss plus wild-type RTK overexpression or noncoding promoter mutation plus cell-cycle amplification—to maintain tumorigenicity; single lesions will fail in engineered models.

Enhancer-hijacked wild-type overexpression states and RasGAP-loss states will converge on a small set of phosphosite patterns that are targetable by the same drug combinations despite distinct upstream lesions.

A substantial subset of clinically driver-negative tumors will be classifiable by ecDNA architecture or 3D-genome rewiring rather than by sequence changes, and these tumors will show selective sensitivity to transcriptional cofactor inhibition.
Low-frequency lineage-factor amplifications such as NKX2-1 and noncoding ILF2 activation may cooperate in ways not yet recognized, producing mixed lineage-survival and genome-stability phenotypes.
negative_experiments: If residual deep-profiled driver-negative LUADs consistently reveal one high-frequency recurrent substitute oncogene in larger cohorts, the distributed weak-driver theory is weakened.
If pathway-output measurements do not outperform lesion identity for biological clustering or drug-response prediction in residual cohorts, the output-over-lesion law is weakened.
If wild-type overexpression events such as NKX2-1 amplification or ERBB2 SE-SV overexpression prove dispensable in endogenous models, the lineage-survival substitution law is weakened.
If assay-depth expansion fails to materially reduce apparent driver-negative frequency in modern cohorts, the assay-depth reclassification law is weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not explain purely state-dominant progression models in which no clear lesion convergence is needed, such as some non-genetic progression or L2S-only frameworks.
It only partially explains immune-risk and radiomics-only driver-negative classifiers that capture prognosis without directly specifying the weak-lesion architecture.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some apparently driver-negative contexts instead show strong single-event explanations, such as MET exon 14 skipping, RET/NTRK/NRG1 fusions, EGFR ECD mutations, or RIT1 hotspot mutations, which conflict with the idea that distributed weak drivers dominate all residual cases.
The NF1/RASA1/PTEN oncogene-negative mouse-human model proposes a relatively specific convergent genetic subtype rather than a broad distributed weak-driver regime.

special_cases: This theory is strongest for genomic residuals after deep profiling, not for all panel-negative tumors.
Some histologic subsets such as invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma are dominated by rare strong fusions and should not be forced into the weak-driver framework.
Pathway-convergent tumor-suppressor-loss subtypes can be viewed as a boundary case between distributed weak drivers and strong convergent genetics.

references: Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [closest direct precedent for residual heterogeneity after WGS]
Distinct patterns of somatic genome alterations in lung adenocarcinomas and squamous cell carcinomas [rare convergent lesions in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [oncogene-negative LUAD with additional alternative pathway lesions]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing studies already document that deeper profiling uncovers rare hidden drivers and that residual cohorts are heterogeneous, with many low-frequency alternative lesions.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The new contribution is to interpret this heterogeneity as a coherent weak-driver convergence regime governed by pathway-output sufficiency and lineage-survival substitution, rather than as an unstructured leftovers category.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is a synthetic reorganization of existing genomic findings into a higher-level mechanistic framework specific to driver-negative LUAD.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1861.name: Convergent Driver Substitution in Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1861.description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly driverless; they are driven by noncanonical substitutes for canonical RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes. These substitutes fall into recurrent mechanistic classes: hidden kinase fusions or rare coding mutations missed by limited assays, wild-type oncogene activation by copy-number or 3D-regulatory rewiring, and combinatorial loss of negative regulators that jointly recreate RAS/MAPK and PI3K signaling. The theory predicts that the 30% 'driver-negative' pool is a mixture of assay-limited false negatives and true noncanonical driver states rather than a single biology.
theory-1861.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Convergent Driver Substitution in Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A substantial fraction of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly driverless; they are driven by noncanonical substitutes for canonical RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes. These substitutes fall into recurrent mechanistic classes: hidden kinase fusions or rare coding mutations missed by limited assays, wild-type oncogene activation by copy-number or 3D-regulatory rewiring, and combinatorial loss of negative regulators that jointly recreate RAS/MAPK and PI3K signaling. The theory predicts that the 30% 'driver-negative' pool is a mixture of assay-limited false negatives and true noncanonical driver states rather than a single biology.

law.theory_statement: When lung adenocarcinomas are classified as driver-negative using limited hotspot panels or incomplete testing, a sizable fraction will be reclassified as driver-positive after deeper DNA/RNA/structural profiling, with the recovered events enriched for fusions, exon-skipping alterations, noncanonical receptor mutations, and low-frequency kinase-pathway lesions.
law.domain_scope: Applies to clinically or research-defined driver-negative lung adenocarcinoma cohorts initially screened by limited canonical-driver assays, especially EGFR/KRAS/ALK-centered panels or WES without high-sensitivity fusion/noncoding/CNV detection. Does not claim that all residual cases are assay failures; it applies to the reclassifiable subset.
special_cases: Reclassification is strongest when initial testing lacks RNA fusion detection, deep sequencing depth, or broad copy-number/noncoding analysis.
Some cohorts define driver negativity only relative to EGFR/ALK or a small marker set, so recovered 'new' drivers may actually be canonical events missed technically.
Even after comprehensive profiling, a residual subset remains unexplained.

supporting_evidence.text: In a Korean triple-negative LUAD cohort initially negative for EGFR/KRAS/ALK by standard testing, comprehensive genomic profiling found genomic alterations in 94% and actionable alterations in 31%, including previously missed EGFR and KRAS mutations plus ROS1, RET, MET, ERBB2, and BRAF alterations.

Among 51 standard-test-negative LUADs, 8 EGFR mutations and 8 KRAS mutations were uncovered by CGP after prior negative conventional testing; some missed EGFR/KRAS variants were outside primer coverage and some were inferred to have been below prior assay sensitivity.

In never-smoker LUADs initially called oncogene-negative by WES, 35% were negative at first pass but only 19% remained unresolved after additional RNA-seq, clinical sequencing, FISH, and deeper sequencing; lower tumor cellularity was associated with initially unresolved cases.

In the TCGA RPA-negative LUAD analysis, 118/501 tumors were initially negative for RTK/RAS/RAF pathway alterations by WES/RNA-seq, but WGS recovered pathway alterations in 33% of these apparently negative cases, including KRAS mutations, amplifications, RASA1/NF1 deletions, and NRG1 alterations.

The residual RPA-negative-by-WGS subgroup was much smaller than the initial WES-negative group, showing that a notable portion of apparent driver-negativity reflects assay limitations rather than true absence of oncogenic signaling lesions.

KIF5B-RET was discovered in clinically triple-marker-negative LUAD and then found recurrently in additional triple-negative/double-negative tumors, showing that canonical driver-negative enrichment can expose hidden recurrent kinase fusions.

NTRK1 fusions were found in tumors lacking known oncogenic alterations by standard clinical assays, with 3 of 91 lung cancers or 2 of 36 highly selected oncogene-negative LUADs harboring NTRK1 rearrangements.

In a Chinese pan-negative LUAD cohort, additional noncanonical alterations were recovered after canonical testing, including EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 extracellular/transmembrane mutations, ARAF S214C, and CD74-NRG1 fusions.

A separate pan-negative LUAD study identified multiple EGFR extracellular-domain variants in 9 of 143 pan-negative cases and functionally validated EGFR M277E as oncogenic and erlotinib-sensitive, demonstrating that some apparent driver-negative tumors harbor noncanonical forms of canonical drivers.

In 200 Korean LUADs, 26 remained without identified drivers after broad RNA-seq/exome-based analysis, but the authors explicitly suggested specimen-specific aberrations and acknowledged that RNA-seq/exome approaches could miss additional drivers.
The LCMC 'no driver' group was explicitly cautioned to mix true negatives with incompletely tested cases, further supporting that apparent driver-negativity is partly definitional and technical.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already known that broader sequencing can discover occult fusions or rare drivers in tumors initially negative by routine testing.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel synthesis is the explicit framing that much of oncogene-negative LUAD is a recoverable hidden-driver state whose composition is predictable by assay depth and modality, rather than a uniformly distinct biological class.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement integrates multiple assay-comparison studies across LUAD into a broader law about driver recovery, extending beyond any single paper's conclusion.
references: Kris et al. (2014) Using multiplexed assays of oncogenic drivers in lung cancers to select targeted drugs [broad hidden-driver precedent in LCMC-style cohorts]
Imielinski et al. (2012) Mapping the hallmarks of lung adenocarcinoma with massively parallel sequencing [recognized incomplete driver assignment in LUAD]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [direct WGS rescue of WES-negative LUAD]

law.theory_statement: True residual oncogene-negative LUADs preferentially converge on a small set of pathway-level substitutes—RAS/MAPK activation, PI3K/AKT activation, lineage-survival transcriptional support, or receptor/ligand signaling—through heterogeneous genomic mechanisms rather than one recurrent gene mutation.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LUADs that remain unresolved or only partially resolved after broad profiling, including residual RPA-negative/non-CAGA/oncogene-negative subsets and biologically similar EGFR/ALK-wild-type or never-smoker subsets. The law concerns pathway convergence, not complete mutual exclusivity or universal recurrence of specific genes.
special_cases: Different tumors may reach the same pathway state through mutation, fusion, amplification, promoter/enhancer rewiring, exon skipping, or combined tumor suppressor loss.
A substantial subset may show pathway activation at the phosphoprotein or expression level without a single identifiable DNA lesion.
Some residual tumors may instead be microenvironment-dominant or state-dominant rather than strongly signaling-addicted.

supporting_evidence.text: In oncogene-negative LUAD from TCGA, focal MET and ERBB2 amplifications plus NF1 and RIT1 mutations were enriched, and these additions increased the fraction of LUAD with somatic evidence of RTK/RAS/RAF activation from 62% to 76%.

A distinct oncogene-negative LUAD study found enrichment of rare Ras/Rho pathway regulators in the negative subset, including SOS1, RASA1, VAV1, and ARHGAP35, plus focal amplifications near FGFR1/WHSC1L1, PDGFRA/KIT/KDR, and MAPK1.

Mouse functional modeling showed that combinatorial inactivation of NF1, RASA1, and PTEN can drive oncogene-negative LUAD and create a human-relevant oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K subtype, with about 45% of human oncogene-negative tumors showing moderate-to-strong pERK and pAKT activation.

RASA1/NF1 co-inactivated NSCLC formed a distinct, canonical-driver-mutually-exclusive subset with strong trametinib sensitivity, illustrating that combined loss of pathway brakes can substitute for canonical oncogene mutation.

Residual RPA-negative-by-WGS LUAD remained heterogeneous, but recurrent lesions concentrated in tumor suppressor loss (TP53, STK11, KEAP1, SMARCA4), copy-number gains (NKX2-1, KAT6A, CCNE1, MDM2, MYC, MCL1, MYCL), noncoding ILF2 activation, and complex SVs, implying pathway and systems-level convergence rather than a missing single gene.

In non-CAGA LUAD, super-enhancer/structural-variant overlap nominated outlier overexpression of ERBB2, EGFR, CDK4, MDM2, FRS2, CAV2, and FGF3/4/19, supporting pathway convergence through wild-type gene overexpression rather than mutation.
NKX2-1 amplification defines a lineage-survival LUAD subtype in which a wild-type lineage transcription factor is the functional dependency rather than a canonical kinase mutation.
NRG1 fusions in pan-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma drive HER2/HER3 signaling, showing that ligand-driven receptor activation is another substitute route to canonical pathway activation.

Proteogenomic analysis of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD found that these tumors segregate into proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism programs rather than a single hidden oncogene, consistent with pathway-state convergence and biological heterogeneity.

In EGFR-wild-type LUAD, a ferroptosis-resistance/fibroblast-rich program centered on DDIT4, NOX4, and GCLC identified a poor-prognosis subtype, indicating that some 'driver-negative-like' tumors may converge on metabolic and microenvironmental survival circuits instead of canonical mitogenic lesions.

The MIF-HIF-1α axis in driver-gene-negative LUAD supports a glycolytic survival mechanism in AT2-like epithelial cells, suggesting that some unresolved tumors converge on metabolic reprogramming rather than on a classic receptor mutation.
