novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior studies established many individual noncanonical drivers or dependencies in subsets of LUAD, such as NF1/RIT1 lesions, NRG1 fusions, MET exon 14 skipping, or wild-type gene amplification.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the unifying principle that true oncogene-negative LUAD is better understood as pathway-substitute convergence achieved by heterogeneous mechanisms, not as a hunt for one additional recurrent gene.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative synthesis across genomics, proteomics, and functional models that goes beyond any single published mechanism while remaining anchored in observed pathway-level recurrence.
references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [oncogene-negative subset with alternative RTK/RAS/RAF lesions]
Ambrogio et al. (2023) Onc-negative RAS/PI3K mouse-human convergence study [combined tumor suppressor-loss substitute for canonical oncogenes]
Asada et al. (2024) ERBB2 super-enhancer structural abnormality study [wild-type overexpression as substitute activation]

law.theory_statement: Oncogene-negative LUAD is best decomposed into mechanistically distinct subtypes—cryptic kinase/fusion-driven, wild-type overexpression/lineage-driven, pathway-brake-loss RAS/PI3K-driven, and state/microenvironment-driven—each with different validation assays and therapeutic implications.
law.domain_scope: Applies to the clinically unresolved ~10–30% of LUAD after routine testing and to broader EGFR/ALK-wild-type or non-CAGA subsets used as proxies for driver-negative disease. It is a classification law for heterogeneity within driver-negative-like LUAD, not a claim that every tumor cleanly belongs to one category.
special_cases: Some tumors may be hybrid cases, e.g. a hidden kinase fusion plus an immune- or fibroblast-dominant state.
Subtype assignment depends on assay modality; DNA-only pipelines under-detect fusion-driven and enhancer-driven classes.
Early lesions and advanced tumors may not map identically because progression can add non-genetic state changes.

supporting_evidence.text: Cryptic kinase/fusion-driven examples include KIF5B-RET, NTRK1 rearrangements, ROS1 rearrangements, CD74-NRG1, EZR-ERBB4, TRIM24-BRAF, and MET exon 14 skipping discovered in initially driver-negative-enriched cohorts.
Wild-type overexpression/lineage-driven examples include ERBB2 SE-SV rewiring, the 23-gene outlier set, MET/ERBB2/FGFR1/MAPK1 focal amplifications, and NKX2-1 lineage-survival amplification.

Pathway-brake-loss RAS/PI3K-driven examples include onc-negative RAS/PI3K tumors driven by NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss and the smoking-associated RASA1/NF1 co-inactivated NSCLC subset sensitive to MEK inhibition.

State/microenvironment-driven examples include FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD with fibroblast-rich ferroptosis resistance, MIF-HIF-1α glycolytic driver-negative LUAD, and four proteogenomic EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker subtypes centered on proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, or metabolism rather than one hidden oncogene.

Never-smoker NS-LUAD transcriptomics identified a 'chaotic' subtype with no significantly enriched canonical driver mutations or fusions but strong EMT/CAF features and poor prognosis, reinforcing the state-driven class.

Histologic progression studies of LUAD found that progression and spatial heterogeneity can be driven primarily by non-genetic transcriptional plasticity and TME remodeling rather than acquisition of new driver mutations, supporting a non-genetic state-driven branch.
The residual complex-SV RPA-negative cohort lacked one recurrent amplified target, implying that some tumors fit a personalized structural-dosage class that resists simple gene-centric subtyping.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Several studies have proposed individual subtypes within selected driver-negative-like cohorts, especially never-smoker EGFR/ALK-wild-type LUAD, immune-risk driver-negative LUAD, and ferroptosis-related EGFR-wild-type LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is a cross-study mechanistic decomposition that unifies these disparate observations into four major classes with distinct assay requirements and translational strategies.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a higher-order synthesis across multiple cohorts and technologies rather than a previously standardized taxonomy, so it is best classified as new.
references: Kim et al. (2023) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [state-based subtype precedent]
Li et al. (2023) Ferroptosis-related gene signature for predicting prognosis in EGFR wild-type LUAD [microenvironment/metabolic subtype precedent]
Sun et al. (2022) A novel signature based on immune-related gene pairs in driver-gene-negative LUAD [immune-state subclassification precedent]

new_predictions_likely: If routine panel-negative LUADs undergo joint deep DNA sequencing, RNA fusion calling, long-read structural profiling, and phosphoproteomics, the residual unresolved fraction will fall substantially below panel-based estimates and segregate into the four mechanistic classes described here.
Tumors in the cryptic kinase/fusion-driven class will show the strongest response to matched kinase inhibition and the weakest dependence on tumor microenvironment remodeling markers.
Tumors in the wild-type overexpression/lineage-driven class will be better predicted by RNA/protein outlier expression plus structural/epigenomic architecture than by coding mutation status alone.

Tumors in the pathway-brake-loss class will show combined pERK and pAKT activation more often than fusion-driven or lineage-driven classes and will be enriched for sensitivity to SHP2/MEK/AKT combinations rather than single-agent RTK inhibitors.

Tumors in the state/microenvironment-driven class will show weaker recurrence of single DNA drivers but stronger reproducibility of stromal, ferroptosis, immune, or glycolytic programs across independent cohorts.
new_predictions_unknown: A subset of residual driver-negative LUADs will prove dependent on enhancer-promoter topology that can be therapeutically disrupted by targeted transcriptional or chromatin-loop perturbation even when kinase inhibition fails.
Some apparently state-driven driver-negative LUADs will convert into kinase-dependent states after microenvironment depletion, revealing masked oncogenic addictions.

Longitudinal sampling from preneoplasia to invasive disease will show that some driver-negative tumors begin as lineage- or metabolic-state lesions and only later acquire structural or pathway-brake-loss drivers.

Single-cell multi-omic profiling will reveal that a fraction of 'unresolved' tumors are mosaics composed of two cooperating compartments: a non-neoplastic stromal signaling source and a weakly transformed epithelial recipient clone.
negative_experiments: If comprehensive multimodal profiling of a large panel-negative LUAD cohort fails to reduce the unresolved fraction or fails to identify enrichment of hidden fusions/noncanonical lesions, the hidden-driver recovery component would be weakened.
If residual oncogene-negative tumors do not show convergence on a limited number of pathway states at the phosphoprotein or transcriptomic level, the pathway-substitution law would be weakened.
If the proposed subtype classes do not separate therapeutically or biologically in independent cohorts, the subtype decomposition law would be weakened.
If combined RAS/PI3K pathway inhibition does not preferentially affect tumors with pathway-brake-loss signatures, the pathway-brake subtype would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Some panel-negative tumors may truly lack a dominant oncogenic dependency and instead reflect broad genomic instability or passenger-heavy evolution that this theory does not fully resolve.
The theory does not fully explain geographically specific mutagenic subgroups such as SBS22a/aristolochic-acid-associated never-smoker LUAD or HRD/MSI-like rare subsets.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some studies imply that many tumors remain unexplained even after broad profiling, which could argue for substantial genuinely driver-sparse biology rather than mostly recoverable hidden drivers.

special_cases: Never-smoker cohorts are enriched for fusion, lineage, and state-driven classes, whereas smoking-associated cohorts may more often enter the pathway-brake-loss/high-TMB branch.
IMA is a special histologic compartment where ligand/fusion substitution is disproportionately common.
Early-stage and metastatic driver-negative disease may not share the same subtype proportions because progression can add state changes or genome instability.

references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [identified oncogene-negative LUAD and alternative lesions but not a unified substitute-driver taxonomy]
Ambrogio et al. (2023) Onc-negative RAS/PI3K mouse-human convergence study [demonstrates one major substitute-driver class]
Kim et al. (2023) Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [demonstrates heterogeneity without a unified general theory]
Asada et al. (2024) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities [demonstrates one wild-type overexpression class]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work has identified individual hidden drivers, wild-type overexpression mechanisms, and pathway-brake-loss subtypes in subsets of LUAD.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory combines these into a unified framework: driver-negative LUAD is a composite space of recurrent substitute-driver classes with distinct pathway logic and validation modalities.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No single cited study provides this cross-modal decomposition across hidden-driver, wild-type overexpression, pathway-brake-loss, and state/microenvironment-driven classes, making the theory a new integrative synthesis.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1862.name: Non-Genetic State Priming and Context-Dependent Driver Acquisition
theory-1862.description: A second major route to oncogene-negative LUAD is that some tumors are initiated or sustained by altered cell state, lineage plasticity, or microenvironmental programs before or instead of a dominant canonical oncogene. In this model, preneoplastic transitional epithelial states, lineage-factor loss, stromal/angiogenic remodeling, and endogenous mutagenic processes create a permissive state in which weak or noncanonical genetic lesions become sufficient. The central prediction is that a subset of driver-negative LUAD is best understood as state-primed cancer, not simply as genomically cryptic cancer.
theory-1862.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Non-Genetic State Priming and Context-Dependent Driver Acquisition
components.theory_description: A second major route to oncogene-negative LUAD is that some tumors are initiated or sustained by altered cell state, lineage plasticity, or microenvironmental programs before or instead of a dominant canonical oncogene. In this model, preneoplastic transitional epithelial states, lineage-factor loss, stromal/angiogenic remodeling, and endogenous mutagenic processes create a permissive state in which weak or noncanonical genetic lesions become sufficient. The central prediction is that a subset of driver-negative LUAD is best understood as state-primed cancer, not simply as genomically cryptic cancer.

law.theory_statement: In a subset of LUAD, especially early-stage and never-smoker disease, plastic epithelial state transitions can precede or outweigh acquisition of new canonical driver mutations, creating a tumor-permissive state that later accommodates heterogeneous weak drivers or progression programs.
law.domain_scope: Applies to early-stage LUAD, never-smoker LUAD, precursor lesions, and cohorts specifically showing non-genetic progression or transitional alveolar states. It does not claim that canonical driver mutations are absent in all such tumors, only that state change can be a primary organizing principle.
special_cases: Some state-primed tumors later acquire strong drivers; others remain dominated by lineage or microenvironment programs.
This law is most applicable to early lesions and to transcriptomic/spatial analyses, not necessarily to heavily treated metastatic disease.
Canonical driver enrichment can coexist with the state transition rather than being replaced by it.

supporting_evidence.text: A review of multi-omics LUAD progression concluded that progression and spatial heterogeneity are driven primarily by non-genetic plastic transcriptional state shifts and TME reprogramming rather than new driver mutations.

Histologic progression from lepidic to solid LUAD showed no recurrent pattern-specific genetic lesion, while expression and methylation changes tracked progression, supporting non-genetic reprogramming rather than new recurrent drivers.

KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate cells (KACs) were enriched near tumors, less differentiated, positively correlated with KRAS signatures, present before overt tumors in mouse models, and lineage-linked to tumor formation, implying a permissive transitional epithelial state between normal AT2 cells and malignant cells.

AIS3, a late adenocarcinoma-in-situ subtype, was associated with tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs, with rising endothelial/stromal scores and invasive-region endothelial enrichment, indicating that stromal and angiogenic state changes accompany pre-invasive progression.

The NS-LUAD 'chaotic' subtype had no significantly enriched canonical driver mutation or fusion but showed EMT, mixed lineage, CAF enrichment, advanced stage, and poor prognosis, implying a biology organized by cell state and microenvironment more than by one recurrent oncogene.

The p53 signaling program was activated in KACs and increased after tobacco carcinogen exposure, suggesting that transitional epithelial states carry stress-regulatory programs that may shape whether oncogenesis proceeds.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Many studies in lung and other cancers have argued that epithelial plasticity and tumor microenvironment remodeling are important for progression.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is applying this as a primary explanation for a subset of oncogene-negative LUAD, framing cell-state transition itself as the enabling lesion that can substitute for a strong canonical driver early in disease.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This builds on published non-genetic progression and KAC findings but extends them into a more explicit theory of state-primed driver-negative oncogenesis.
references: Tavernari et al. LUAD progression multi-omics study [non-genetic progression precedent]
KAC/KRT8 intermediate-cell study [state-transition precedent in LUAD initiation]
Ais3 tip-EC/mCAF progression study [microenvironmental progression precedent]

law.theory_statement: Loss or attenuation of lung lineage regulators can convert epithelial identity into alternative differentiation programs, making tumorigenesis possible through lineage-survival or lineage-switch mechanisms even without a canonical kinase driver.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LUAD states involving NKX2-1/TTF1 loss or alteration, especially mucinous, gastric-like, or lineage-reprogrammed tumors and related mouse models. It is strongest where lineage factor expression and differentiation state are experimentally measured.
special_cases: NKX2-1 can be oncogenic when amplified and tumor-suppressive when lost, depending on oncogenic context and lineage state.
Lineage loss alone may be insufficient for metastasis or poorly differentiated progression; additional hits or epigenetic changes may be required.
Effects are driver-context-specific, as shown by different outcomes in KRAS versus EGFR backgrounds.

supporting_evidence.text: NKX2-1 amplification in LUAD acts as a lineage-survival oncogene without recurrent coding mutation, and RNAi knockdown impairs viability in NKX2-1-expressing cells, showing that lineage-factor dosage itself can be oncogenic.

In KRAS-driven mouse lung tumors, reduction or loss of Nkx2-1 enabled mucinous adenocarcinoma with goblet-cell features and AP-1-associated mucin programs, whereas withdrawal of KRAS caused regression, demonstrating cooperation between lineage change and oncogenic context.

Nkx2-1-negative lung adenocarcinoma adopted a gastric differentiation program with HNF4A, CTSE, and GKN1 expression in mouse models and human NKX2-1-negative mucinous tumors, supporting lineage-switch oncogenesis.
Combined loss of Nkx2-1 and Hnf4a derepressed Hmga2, indicating that multiple differentiation restraints may need to be removed to reach an aggressive embryonal progression state.
In EGFR-driven mice, Nkx2-1 reduction decreased tumor burden and did not generate mucinous tumors, proving that lineage-loss effects are oncogenic-context-dependent rather than generic.

The KC subgroup of KRAS-mutant LUAD combines CDKN2A/B loss with low NKX2-1/TTF1 and gastrointestinal/mucinous features, showing that lineage suppression can define a coherent subtype even without a unique secondary oncogene.

TTF1-negative LUADs have fewer actionable mutations and show KEAP1-enriched, DNA-repair/cell-cycle-biased, MAPK-low biology, supporting the idea that lineage-defined tumors may use noncanonical support circuits.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: NKX2-1 biology in LUAD is established as context-dependent, including lineage-survival amplification and lineage-loss-associated mucinous differentiation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel synthesis is that lineage-regulator dysregulation can itself be a primary organizing axis for driver-negative-like LUAD, generating permissive cell identity changes that substitute for canonical kinase addiction.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement unifies previously separate NKX2-1 amplification, NKX2-1 loss, and gastric/mucinous lineage-switch findings into one permissivity principle.
references: Weir et al. NKX2-1 amplification study [lineage-survival precedent]
Maeda et al. KrasG12D;Nkx2-1 mucinous adenocarcinoma model [lineage-loss precedent]
Snyder et al. Nkx2-1-negative gastric differentiation LUAD study [lineage-switch precedent]

law.theory_statement: A subset of driver-negative or driver-poor LUADs is maintained by stromal, immune, or metabolic ecosystem circuits—especially fibroblast, endothelial, and hypoxia/glycolysis loops—that can create clinically meaningful tumor behavior independent of a dominant canonical oncogene.
law.domain_scope: Applies to driver-negative or canonical-driver-poor LUAD cohorts with transcriptomic, spatial, or single-cell evidence of strong microenvironmental structuring, including fibroblast-rich EGFR-wild-type LUAD, immune-risk driver-negative LUAD, AIS/MIA progression states, and inflammatory or immune-cold never-smoker subtypes.
special_cases: These ecosystems may coexist with hidden oncogenic drivers rather than replacing them entirely.
Microenvironmental dependence may be more visible in early-stage or untreated tumors and harder to infer in standard cell-line models.
Immune-active and immune-excluded forms both exist and should not be collapsed into one class.

supporting_evidence.text: FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD had worse survival, abundant fibroblasts, CAF-barrier-like structure, stronger fibroblast-epithelial communication, IGF-binding pathway enrichment, and preferential response of the FeSig-high model to 6-MP with reduced fibroblast infiltration.

Driver-gene-negative LUAD stratified by immune-related gene pairs showed distinct immune-cell composition, checkpoint expression, and prognosis, with high-risk tumors showing higher PD-1/PD-L1/CTLA-4 and altered immune infiltration rather than an identified alternate oncogene.

The high-risk radiomics subgroup of driver-negative LUAD was enriched for immune-related pathways, whereas the low-risk subgroup was enriched for metabolic pathways, indicating biologically distinct ecosystem states within the same genomic-negative class.

AIS3 and MIA progression states were linked to tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs, and spatial transcriptomics showed endothelial enrichment in invasive regions, implying that angiogenic/stromal ecosystems can drive progression before overt invasive carcinoma.

The MIF-HIF-1α axis in driver-gene-negative LUAD increased glycolysis and proliferation in AT2 cells, linking cytokine signaling and metabolic state to tumor maintenance in the absence of canonical drivers.

Never-smoker immune subtyping revealed an immune-cold IM-3 state with low checkpoint expression and possible CTNNB1/WNT-linked immune exclusion, showing that driver-poor biology can be organized by immune ecology rather than by a single mutation.

The PI subtype of LUAD is highly inflammatory and immune-infiltrated, while STK11-mutant immune-cold LUAD shows neutrophil-degranulation-associated immune exclusion, demonstrating that ecosystem organization strongly shapes LUAD subtype biology beyond driver mutation identity.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Immune, fibroblast, and angiogenic ecosystems are already recognized as important in lung cancer progression and treatment response.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is elevating these ecosystems from modifiers to primary sustainers of a subset of driver-negative/driver-poor LUAD, making them candidate driver-equivalent organizing principles.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative reinterpretation of multiple cohort-specific findings rather than a pre-existing formal theory in LUAD.
references: FeSig EGFR-wild-type LUAD study [fibroblast-ferroptosis ecosystem precedent]
AIS3 endothelial/CAF progression study [angiogenic ecosystem precedent]
Driver-negative immune-gene-pair study [immune ecosystem precedent]

new_predictions_likely: Single-cell multi-omic profiling of clinically driver-negative LUAD will identify transitional epithelial states or lineage-switched populations more often than in canonical EGFR- or KRAS-driven controls.

Tumors with the strongest fibroblast-rich or endothelial-rich ecosystem signatures will show weaker recurrence of single dominant oncogenes but stronger dependence on stromal communication pathways in organoid/co-culture models.
NKX2-1-low or TTF1-negative driver-poor tumors will show greater sensitivity to interventions that disrupt lineage-maintenance or stress-adaptation circuits than to standard RTK inhibitors.

Early-stage driver-negative lesions with KAC-like or AIS3-like signatures will be more likely to progress radiographically or histologically than lesions lacking those states, even when canonical driver testing is unrevealing.
new_predictions_unknown: A subset of state-primed driver-negative LUADs may be interceptable before malignant fixation by targeting transitional alveolar programs or stromal angiogenic niches rather than tumor-intrinsic kinases.

Lineage-switch tumors may derive from distinct cells of origin whose normal chromatin architecture predisposes them to specific enhancer hijacks or mucinous/gastric reprogramming under weak oncogenic pressure.
Fibroblast-rich FeSig-high tumors may become dependent on specific ligand-receptor axes that are dispensable in fusion-driven or pathway-brake-loss tumors, enabling ecosystem-selective therapy.
Some 'driver-negative' tumors may never acquire a dominant trunk driver and instead persist as cooperative ecological systems of partially transformed epithelial cells plus supportive stroma.
negative_experiments: If early driver-negative/driver-poor lesions do not show reproducible transitional epithelial states or non-genetic progression signatures in prospective sampling, the state-before-driver law would be weakened.

If disrupting stromal or metabolic ecosystem circuits in relevant co-culture or in vivo models fails to reduce growth of microenvironment-dominant tumors, the microenvironmental sustainment law would be weakened.
If lineage-factor perturbation does not alter tumor state or fitness in TTF1-low/NKX2-1-low models, the lineage-loss permissivity law would be weakened.
If canonical hidden drivers fully explain nearly all tumors with KAC-like, chaotic, or fibroblast-rich states, the claim that state can be primary rather than secondary would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not directly explain highly specific hidden-driver classes such as ARAF hotspot mutants, NTRK1 fusions, or RET fusions, which can clearly act as dominant canonical-like drivers.
It also does not directly explain residual WGS-defined structural-dosage tumors in which complex amplicons appear to be the main driver mechanism.

conflicting_evidence.text: Multiregion NSCLC studies found many known driver mutations are truncal and early, which argues that canonical genetic drivers remain primary in a large portion of lung cancer and limits overgeneralization of state-first models.

Early progression studies in never-smokers still found positive selection for EGFR and TP53 across AAH to AIS to MIA to stage I LUAD, indicating that non-genetic progression does not exclude canonical genetic selection.

special_cases: This theory is most applicable to early-stage, never-smoker, histologically plastic, or microenvironment-rich tumors and may underperform in genomically complex advanced tumors.
Canonical oncogenes and state priming can coexist; the theory does not require mutual exclusivity.
Lineage effects are highly context dependent: the same factor can be tumor promoting in one setting and tumor suppressive in another.

references: Tavernari et al. LUAD progression multi-omics analysis [non-genetic progression precedent]
KAC/KRT8 transitional alveolar cell study [preneoplastic state precedent]
Snyder et al. Nkx2-1-negative gastric differentiation study [lineage-switch precedent]
Li et al. FeSig EGFR-wild-type LUAD study [fibroblast/ferroptosis ecosystem precedent]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Non-genetic progression, lineage plasticity, and stromal ecosystem effects are all established themes in LUAD biology.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novelty lies in synthesizing these into a theory that some oncogene-negative LUADs are fundamentally state-primed or ecosystem-sustained cancers rather than merely genomically undercharacterized cancers.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The theory closely relates to several emerging frameworks in LUAD but extends them by explicitly treating state and ecosystem as primary explanatory mechanisms for driver-negative disease.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1863.name: Hidden-Driver Compression Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1863.description: A large fraction of apparently oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not biologically driverless but diagnostically compressed into one bucket by incomplete or mutation-centric assays. The missing drivers fall into recurrent classes: cryptic kinase fusions/rearrangements, noncanonical coding variants outside hotspot domains, exon-skipping/splice lesions, focal or broad copy-number activation of wild-type pathway genes, enhancer/super-enhancer rewiring, and combinatorial loss of negative regulators that converges on MAPK or PI3K signaling. The practical implication is that 'driver-negative' LUAD should be redefined as a hierarchy of residual classes after sequential DNA, RNA, copy-number, structural, and phosphoproteomic interrogation rather than as a single biological entity.
theory-1863.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Hidden-Driver Compression Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A large fraction of apparently oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not biologically driverless but diagnostically compressed into one bucket by incomplete or mutation-centric assays. The missing drivers fall into recurrent classes: cryptic kinase fusions/rearrangements, noncanonical coding variants outside hotspot domains, exon-skipping/splice lesions, focal or broad copy-number activation of wild-type pathway genes, enhancer/super-enhancer rewiring, and combinatorial loss of negative regulators that converges on MAPK or PI3K signaling. The practical implication is that 'driver-negative' LUAD should be redefined as a hierarchy of residual classes after sequential DNA, RNA, copy-number, structural, and phosphoproteomic interrogation rather than as a single biological entity.

law.theory_statement: As molecular profiling moves from limited hotspot/panel assays to combined WES/WGS, RNA-seq/fusion calling, copy-number analysis, and structural/noncoding interrogation, the apparent frequency of driver-negative LUAD falls substantially because many tumors harbor cryptic but recurrent oncogenic events that are invisible to narrower assays.
law.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma cohorts initially classified as lacking canonical actionable drivers by limited clinical panels, hotspot testing, WES alone, or RTK/RAS/RAF-focused assays; strongest in resected or metastatic human LUAD cohorts subjected to orthogonal DNA/RNA/structural profiling.
special_cases: A residual subset remains unresolved even after WGS, implying either true noncanonical biology or limits of current assays.
Low tumor purity, stromal contamination, and insufficient RNA quality can create false-negative driver assignments.
Different studies use different 'driver-negative' definitions; frequencies are therefore not directly interchangeable.

supporting_evidence.text: In a standard-test triple-negative LUAD cohort, comprehensive genomic profiling found genomic alterations in 94% of tumors and actionable alterations in 31%, including missed EGFR, KRAS, ROS1, RET, ERBB2, MET, and BRAF events, showing that many conventionally negative tumors are assay-negative rather than biology-negative.

In never-smoker LUAD classified oncogene-negative by WES, 35% were initially WES-negative but only 19% remained unresolved after RNA-seq, clinical sequencing, FISH, and deep resequencing; lower tumor cellularity was associated with initial false negativity.

Among TCGA LUAD cases lacking RTK/RAS/RAF pathway alterations by exome/RNA analysis, whole-genome sequencing reclassified 33% as actually pathway-altered, demonstrating that WES-level driver-negative status is unstable under deeper profiling.

In East Asian never-smoker LUAD, 22/202 remained pan-negative after sequential testing for EGFR, HER2, KRAS, BRAF, ALK, and ROS1, but the same study framed these unresolved tumors as a discovery pool for further whole-exome analysis, implying incompleteness of current driver capture.

A Chinese pan-negative LUAD cohort of 219 cases yielded additional noncanonical drivers after extended testing, including EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 extracellular/transmembrane mutations, ARAF mutation, and CD74-NRG1 fusions, reducing the truly unexplained fraction.

A driver-negative NTRK1 study found NTRK1 fusions in tumors that had lacked known alterations by standard assays, again converting apparently negative tumors into fusion-driven tumors once rearrangements were assayed.

KIF5B-RET discovery studies repeatedly found RET fusions in EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative or partially screened negative LUADs, showing that fusion discovery collapses part of the negative category into a targetable subtype.

The broad non-CAGA LUAD cohort shows that even after WES/RNA-seq exclusion of canonical actionable lesions, tumors can still harbor structural/epigenomic driver-like activation such as ERBB2 super-enhancer rewiring, meaning DNA hotspot negativity does not imply pathway neutrality.

The mapping-the-hallmarks LUAD study explicitly states that not every tumor had a mutated oncogene identified and that broader multi-omic studies would be required to fill the gap, directly supporting the notion that current assay spaces incompletely resolve proliferative drivers.
The LCMC 'no driver' cohort shows that even clinically operational 'no-driver' labels can partly reflect incomplete testing rather than true absence of drivers.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already known that broader sequencing reveals occult fusions, rare mutations, and amplifications in some clinically negative LUADs.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the explicit law-like claim that driver-negative LUAD is largely a diagnostic compression artifact that predictably decompresses into recurrent mechanistic classes as profiling depth increases.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This synthesizes many existing observations into a unified theory of residual-class resolution rather than a single new molecular mechanism.
references: Kris et al. (2014) Using multiplexed assays of oncogenic drivers in lung cancers to select targeted drugs [established partial resolution of clinically negative cases]
Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [identified alternative drivers in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [deep-resolution precedent]

law.theory_statement: When canonical RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes are absent, LUAD can achieve equivalent proliferative signaling by three recurrent replacement modes: structural activation of wild-type lineage or receptor genes, noncanonical coding/splicing lesions in pathway nodes, or combinatorial loss of negative regulators that jointly activate MAPK and/or PI3K signaling.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LUADs classified as oncogene-negative or pan-negative after standard canonical-driver testing; strongest for cohorts with integrated copy-number, fusion, structural, and functional follow-up.
special_cases: Not all replacement modes are equally recurrent; some are rare but highly actionable.
Some lesions may be maintenance mechanisms rather than initiating lesions.
Some broad gains or low-level amplifications may be passengers unless linked to expression or dependency.

supporting_evidence.text: NKX2-1 focal amplification defines a recurrent LUAD subgroup in which a wild-type lineage transcription factor acts as a functional dependency, demonstrating structural activation of a nonmutated lineage-survival gene as a substitute driver class.

ERBB2 overexpression in non-CAGA LUAD can arise through super-enhancer formation with structural abnormalities near HNF1β rather than through canonical ERBB2 mutation/amplification, providing a second wild-type-gene activation mechanism.

Oncogene-negative LUAD by TCGA profiling showed focal MET and ERBB2 amplifications, NF1 mutations, and RIT1 mutations enriched in the negative subset, increasing evidence of RTK/RAS/RAF activation from 62% to 76%.

Distinct-pattern analysis of oncogene-negative LUAD identified enriched rare alterations in SOS1, RASA1, VAV1, ARHGAP35, and focal gains near FGFR1/WHSC1L1, PDGFRA/KIT/KDR, and MAPK1, supporting noncanonical pathway-node replacement.
RIT1 switch-II hotspot mutations were mutually exclusive with canonical drivers and transformed cells through MAPK and PI3K signaling, establishing a noncanonical small-GTPase replacement mechanism.
MET exon 14 skipping repeatedly emerged in ostensibly driver-negative tumors across studies, demonstrating splice-based replacement of receptor mutation by altered receptor downregulation.

The onc-negative RAS/PI3K mouse and human theory showed that combined loss of NF1, RASA1, and PTEN can drive LUAD without a canonical oncogene, with human oncogene-negative tumors frequently showing dual pERK/pAKT activation and low PTEN protein even without PTEN mutation.
RASA1/NF1 co-inactivated NSCLC formed a mutually exclusive, MEK-sensitive subset lacking canonical mitogenic drivers, further validating combinatorial RasGAP loss as a replacement mode.

Residual RPA-negative LUAD after WGS remained enriched for STK11, KEAP1, TP53 loss, ILF2 promoter mutation, and complex amplicons, implying that tumor-suppressor loss and noncoding/structural dosage mechanisms continue to substitute for canonical RTK/RAS/RAF lesions.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual replacement modes—fusions, amplifications, enhancer hijacking, exon skipping, and RasGAP loss—are each known in subsets of LUAD or NSCLC.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is treating them as interchangeable pathway-replacement architectures that occupy the same ecological niche left vacant by missing canonical oncogenes.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthetic mechanistic framework built from established classes of alterations but not usually formalized as one replacement law.
references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [alternative-driver precedent]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [residual replacement mechanisms]
Vazquez et al. and related RASA1/NF1 studies [combinatorial negative-regulator loss precedent]

new_predictions_likely: If panel-defined driver-negative LUADs undergo DNA+RNA+copy-number+SV profiling in a prospective cohort, the resolved fraction should exceed that obtained by DNA-only testing, with the largest incremental gains coming from fusion/exon-skipping detection and wild-type-gene activation events.

Among residual WGS-negative tumors, phosphoproteomic pERK/pAKT activation will identify a subgroup enriched for low-PTEN protein, RasGAP loss, or noncoding/structural lesions rather than truly signaling-silent cancers.

Driver-negative tumors with broad 4q12 or focal RTK amplifications will show stronger clinical relevance when copy-number gain is paired with RNA/protein/phosphosite outlier status than when copy number alone is used.

A refined classifier separating cryptic-fusion, splice-driven, enhancer-rewired, lineage-amplified, and RasGAP/PI3K-derepressed classes will predict therapy response better than a binary driver-positive/driver-negative label.
new_predictions_unknown: Some of the currently unresolved RPA(-)G tumors will prove to be driven by recurrent noncoding lesions in regulatory regions of Ras/MAPK phosphatases or ligand genes that are only detectable with long-read or single-cell multiome methods.

A subset of apparently driver-negative LUAD may be maintained by stable post-transcriptional or protein-state activation without recurrent DNA events, producing reproducible phosphoproteomic classes that remain invisible even to WGS.

The residual unresolved class may split by cell of origin, with AT2-lineage, basal-like, and mucinous-lineage tumors each favoring different hidden-driver architectures rather than sharing one universal mechanism.
negative_experiments: If comprehensive DNA/RNA/SV/noncoding profiling in modern prospective driver-negative LUAD cohorts does not materially reduce the unresolved fraction relative to limited panels, the sequential-resolution law would be weakened.
If residual oncogene-negative tumors do not show recurrent pathway convergence at phosphoproteomic or transcriptomic levels, the convergent-pathway replacement law would be weakened.

If wild-type-gene activation events such as NKX2-1 amplification or ERBB2 SE-SV rewiring are not associated with expression/protein dependency in additional cohorts, the replacement architecture framework would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not fully explain tumors whose dominant biology appears immune, metabolic, or stromal rather than RTK/MAPK/PI3K convergent, such as MIF-HIF glycolytic driver-negative LUAD or fibroblast-linked ferroptosis states.
The theory does not directly explain non-genetic histologic progression states where no recurrent genetic lesion distinguishes lepidic-to-solid transition.

conflicting_evidence.text: A true residual class remains after WGS in RPA(-)G LUAD, and the authors explicitly could not identify a shared recurrent replacement driver, which conflicts with any overly strong claim that all negative tumors are simply missed canonical-like events.

special_cases: This theory is strongest for tumors labeled driver-negative by restricted assays; it is weaker for cohorts already comprehensively profiled with DNA, RNA, copy-number, and structural methods.
Some replacement lesions are lineage-restricted, especially NKX2-1 amplification and mucinous-fusion states.
A tumor can move between categories over time as deeper profiling reveals hidden lesions; therefore subtype assignment should be treated as provisional.

references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [alternative drivers in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [deep reclassification of residual cases]
Kris et al. (2014) Using multiplexed assays of oncogenic drivers in lung cancers to select targeted drugs [clinical assay-depth precedent]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The literature already recognizes that some clinically negative LUADs harbor rare fusions, amplifications, splicing events, or noncanonical mutations.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: What is novel is the explicit framing of driver-negative LUAD as a diagnostically compressed residual space that systematically decompresses into recurrent hidden-driver architectures with pathway convergence.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The theory is not a wholly unprecedented mechanism, but it is more integrative and law-like than prior lesion-by-lesion accounts.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1864.name: State-Driven Tumorigenesis Theory of Residual LUAD
theory-1864.description: A biologically distinct fraction of oncogene-negative or driver-incomplete LUAD is sustained not by a single dominant genomic lesion but by stable cell-state programs that become tumor-maintaining systems. These states include lineage-survival programs, stem-like/quiescence-exit programs, non-genetic histologic progression states, immune-excluded stromal states, and ferroptosis- or glycolysis-centered survival programs. In this view, the driver is the attractor state of the tumor ecosystem rather than one altered gene.
theory-1864.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: State-Driven Tumorigenesis Theory of Residual LUAD
components.theory_description: A biologically distinct fraction of oncogene-negative or driver-incomplete LUAD is sustained not by a single dominant genomic lesion but by stable cell-state programs that become tumor-maintaining systems. These states include lineage-survival programs, stem-like/quiescence-exit programs, non-genetic histologic progression states, immune-excluded stromal states, and ferroptosis- or glycolysis-centered survival programs. In this view, the driver is the attractor state of the tumor ecosystem rather than one altered gene.

law.theory_statement: When no single canonical oncogene is sufficient to explain LUAD behavior, stable transcriptional and microenvironmental states can become the dominant determinants of prognosis, progression, and therapy response, even in the absence of recurrent pattern-specific genetic lesions.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LUAD subsets defined by transcriptomic, proteogenomic, single-cell, spatial, or immune-state analyses rather than by one recurrent hotspot mutation; strongest in never-smoker, early-stage, EGFR/ALK-wild-type, or histologically heterogeneous cohorts.
special_cases: These states may coexist with canonical oncogenic mutations and modulate rather than replace genomic drivers.
Bulk transcriptomic signatures may partly reflect stromal composition rather than tumor-cell-intrinsic states.
Some state variables may be maintenance states that arise after initiation.

supporting_evidence.text: The lepidic-to-solid progression study explicitly found no recurrent mutation, copy-number event, or fusion enriched by histologic pattern and concluded that LUAD progression is driven primarily by non-genetic transcriptional and microenvironmental reprogramming.

The EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker proteogenomic cohort split into four subtypes—proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism—with distinct outcomes and dependencies despite the absence of a single shared substitute driver.

The never-smoker NS-LUAD study found a chaotic subtype with poor prognosis and no significant enrichment of canonical driver mutations or fusions, but with strong EMT, CAF, mixed-lineage, and immune-evasion features.
The ferroptosis EGFRWT study defined FeSig-high tumors by a survival-linked ferroptosis-resistant/fibroblast-rich state rather than by a canonical driver lesion.
The driver gene-negative MIF/HIF study proposed a glycolysis-centered MIF+ AT2 state in which metabolic signaling, not canonical driver mutation, best explains the aggressive phenotype.

Piano tumors in never-smoker lung cancer were genomically quiet, had long telomeres, high subclonal fractions, and few recurrent canonical drivers, leading the authors to hypothesize a stem-cell/quiescence-exit origin state.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Non-genetic plasticity, microenvironmental remodeling, and expression-defined subtypes are already recognized in LUAD and other cancers.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is elevating these observations to a general law for residual LUAD: when genomic explanation weakens, cell state becomes the dominant effective driver variable.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative synthesis of several emerging state-based studies rather than a single established theory with consensus language.
references: Tavernari et al. and related LUAD progression studies [non-genetic progression precedent]
Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker LUAD [subtype/dependency precedent]
Sherlock never-smoker NS-LUAD subtype studies [state-over-driver precedent]

law.theory_statement: Loss, amplification, or rewiring of lineage regulators can create LUAD-driving states by altering permissible differentiation programs; in these tumors, lineage identity itself becomes a dependency or a route to alternative differentiation and invasion.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LUADs with altered lineage programs, especially NKX2-1/TTF1-amplified, NKX2-1-low or -negative mucinous tumors, HNF4A-associated gastric-like states, KC subtype tumors, and related lineage-defined models.
special_cases: NKX2-1 can behave oppositely depending on oncogenic context: oncogenic dependency in some adenocarcinomas but suppressor of mucinous conversion in KRAS-driven settings.
Lineage rewiring may require cooperating events such as CDKN2A/B loss or HNF4A activation/suppression.

supporting_evidence.text: NKX2-1 focal amplification acts as a lineage-survival dependency in a subset of LUAD, with knockdown reducing viability and colony formation in NKX2-1-expressing cells.

In KRAS-driven mouse models, reduced or lost NKX2-1 permitted mucinous/gastric differentiation, AP-1 derepression, and mucinous adenocarcinoma formation, while NKX2-1 restoration suppressed these programs.

The KC subgroup of KRAS-mutant LUAD was marked by low NKX2-1, CDKN2A/B inactivation, mucinous/GI-like differentiation, and altered signaling without a single canonical co-driver explaining the full state.

TTF1-negative LUAD showed fewer actionable mutations and a distinct KEAP1/DNA-repair/cell-cycle program, implying that lineage marker loss can define a biologically coherent, therapeutically relevant state even without a canonical substitute oncogene.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Lineage-survival oncogenes and lineage plasticity are established concepts, and NKX2-1 has recognized context-dependent roles in lung adenocarcinoma.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The new element is formalizing lineage alteration as a central explanatory axis for residual LUAD states, unifying amplification-dependent lineage survival and lineage-loss-mediated alternative differentiation.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This law is a synthesis of existing lineage work into a broader rule for driver-incomplete LUAD.
references: Weir et al. and related NKX2-1 amplification work [lineage-survival precedent]
Winslow/Snyder-derived NKX2-1 mucinous reprogramming studies [lineage-loss precedent]
Skoulidis et al. KC subtype work [lineage-state integration]

new_predictions_likely: Residual driver-negative LUADs with weak genomic explanation but strong expression-state signatures will cluster more reproducibly by phosphoproteome and single-cell ecology than by exome features.

In prospective EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, subtype-specific dependencies such as CDK9, TRAF2, GRB2, and ACACA will validate in patient-derived organoids more robustly than mutation-based candidate drivers.
Tumors with high L2S, chaotic, or FeSig-high state scores will show greater plasticity under therapy and higher risk of recurrence than genomically matched but state-low tumors.
Spatial transcriptomics of unresolved driver-negative tumors will reveal that tumor-edge and stromal-interface states, not bulk mutation burden, best predict relapse.
new_predictions_unknown: Some unresolved driver-negative LUADs may be reversibly maintained by epigenetic attractors; short-course chromatin remodeling therapy could collapse the malignant state without targeting a single oncogene.
Lineage-rewired NKX2-1-low and HNF4A-high tumors may cycle between gastric-like and embryonal HMGA2-positive states under stress, creating a metastasis-prone plastic reservoir.
Piano-like stem/quiescence-exit tumors may prove especially resistant to standard cytotoxics yet vulnerable to therapies that force terminal differentiation or disrupt stem-cell niche signaling.
negative_experiments: If residual driver-negative LUADs are better stratified by genomic lesions than by expression/proteomic/spatial states in prospective datasets, the state-dominance law would be weakened.
If lineage regulator perturbation does not materially alter tumor fitness or differentiation state in patient-derived models, the lineage-constraint law would be weakened.

If recurrent-risk differences disappear after controlling for canonical driver status and stage in state-defined cohorts, the claim that cell state is an independent driver variable would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not directly explain clearly targetable cryptic-fusion or noncanonical kinase-driver tumors such as KIF5B-RET, NTRK1 fusions, or EGFR ECD mutants, where a discrete genomic lesion is probably dominant.
It also does not fully explain onc-negative RAS/PI3K tumors driven by combinatorial NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss, which appear strongly pathway-genomic rather than state-primary.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies show that a significant fraction of apparently negative tumors can still be resolved into discrete genomic drivers with deeper profiling, limiting how broadly state-primary explanations should be applied.

special_cases: State-primary explanations are most credible after reasonably exhaustive genomic and fusion profiling has already failed to identify a dominant lesion.
Some state-defined tumors are hybrids: a canonical genomic driver may initiate the tumor, while state variables determine progression, recurrence, and therapy response.
Histologic and spatial sampling matter: a single-region biopsy may underestimate the state diversity that actually drives progression.

references: Tavernari et al. (year not provided here) LUAD progression driven by non-genetic plastic transcriptional state shifts and TME reprogramming [direct conceptual precursor]
Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [state/dependency precedent]
Sherlock-Lung never-smoker subtype studies [state-based prognostic subtyping precedent]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Expression-defined, lineage-defined, and microenvironment-defined LUAD subtypes are already described in multiple cohorts.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is turning those observations into a general state-driven tumorigenesis theory for the residual LUAD fraction left unexplained by canonical oncogene discovery.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The ingredients exist in the literature, but the unifying theory that the effective driver may be the ecosystem state rather than a mutation is more synthetic and forward-looking.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1865.name: Mechanism-Compression Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1865.description: Most apparent oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not explained by a long tail of unrelated genes, but by a compressed set of recurrent driver architectures that standard workflows under-detect: kinase and ligand fusions, splice-mediated receptor stabilization, non-hotspot receptor activation, focal/complex amplification, enhancer or super-enhancer rewiring, and loss of negative regulators of RAS/PI3K/Rho/NRF2 signaling. Thus, the central scientific problem is to classify tumors by driver mechanism family and assay failure mode, then reserve a smaller residual category for genuinely pathway-state or lineage-state tumors that are not reducible to one lesion.
theory-1865.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Mechanism-Compression Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Most apparent oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not explained by a long tail of unrelated genes, but by a compressed set of recurrent driver architectures that standard workflows under-detect: kinase and ligand fusions, splice-mediated receptor stabilization, non-hotspot receptor activation, focal/complex amplification, enhancer or super-enhancer rewiring, and loss of negative regulators of RAS/PI3K/Rho/NRF2 signaling. Thus, the central scientific problem is to classify tumors by driver mechanism family and assay failure mode, then reserve a smaller residual category for genuinely pathway-state or lineage-state tumors that are not reducible to one lesion.

law.statement_name: Assay-negative status decomposes into pseudo-negative and residual-biologic classes
law.theory_statement: When lung adenocarcinomas are called driver-negative by hotspot-limited, mutation-centric, copy-number-insensitive, fusion-insensitive, splice-insensitive, or low-purity genomic workflows, a large fraction are later reclassified as driver-positive after integrated profiling; the remaining unresolved fraction is smaller and enriched for pathway-state, lineage-state, proteomic, noncoding, or combinatorial suppressor-loss mechanisms rather than a single missed canonical lesion.
law.domain_scope: Applies to primary lung adenocarcinoma cohorts, especially never-smoker, mucinous, and RTK/RAS/RAF-negative subsets, where an initial negative call was made by restricted clinical assays, incomplete WES/WGS interpretation, or incompletely integrated DNA/RNA testing and then reassessed by broader targeted DNA sequencing, RNA fusion/splice assays, WGS-SV, copy-number analysis, methylation, proteomics, or phosphoproteomics. It is most applicable to studies explicitly reporting pre- and post-expansion reclassification rates.
special_cases: Not every initially negative tumor is a technical false negative; several cohorts still retain substantial unresolved fractions after broad profiling.
The pseudo-negative fraction is expected to be highest in cohorts defined by limited hotspot panels or restricted fusion testing, and lower in already deeply profiled WGS/RNA-seq cohorts.
Low tumor purity, stromal admixture, and regional sampling can independently create false negatives even under broad assay designs.

supporting_evidence.text: Triple-negative LUADs negative by conventional EGFR/KRAS/ALK testing were strongly reclassified by hybrid-capture CGP: EGFR mutations in 8/51, KRAS in 8/51, ROS1 rearrangements, RET rearrangements, ERBB2 mutations, and MET amplification were discovered, leaving only 3/51 without detected alterations after CGP.

In never-smoker institutional LUADs initially called oncogene-negative by WES, 9/29 were later rescued by additional clinical sequencing or FISH and 2 more by deep WES identifying MET exon 14 skipping, reducing the residual unexplained institutional cohort to 16/84.

Whole-genome reanalysis of WES/RNA-defined RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD reclassified 28/85 cases: 20 had missed KRAS hotspot mutations and 8 harbored hidden pathway lesions such as EGFR amplification by BFBC, MAPK1 amplification, NF1/RASA1 deletions, ARAF amplification, NRG1 fusion, or SOS1 amplification/overexpression; 57/85 remained negative after WGS.

The transcriptome-centered LUAD study rescued initially negative tumors through MET exon 14 skipping and novel kinase fusions, but still left 26/87 tumors without canonical driver mutations after expanded workup.

The LCMC 10-driver panel still classified 267/733 fully genotyped tumors as driver-negative, and the paper explicitly states that in partially tested tumors 'no driver' can also mean incomplete testing rather than true absence of a driver.

An East Asian never-smoker LUAD cohort remained 22/202 pan-negative after sequential targeted assays for EGFR, HER2, KRAS, BRAF, ALK, and ROS1, illustrating that restricted targeted workflows leave a residual unexplained class that was explicitly proposed for further exome discovery.

A separate transcriptome-based study still retained 26/87 unexplained cases after adding fusion and exon-skipping discovery, showing that even RNA-aware rescue does not collapse all apparent negatives into missed canonical lesions.
The WGS-negative RPA(-)G subset remained large at 57/85 despite WGS rescue of 33% of WES-negative tumors, indicating a biologically residual category after major technical rescue.

Some studies explicitly identify still-unexplained residual groups even after broad workup, such as 26/87 RNA-profiled cancers without canonical drivers and broad never-smoker cohorts with heterogeneous unsolved tumors, arguing for a true residual biologic class.

Assay discordance itself is demonstrated methodologically: in NSCLC chromosomes 3 and 9, 196/448 loci were discordant between CGH and LOH, proving that a single assay class under-calls part of the alteration landscape.

The paper on somatic mutations in LUAD found 25/188 tumors with no mutations in 623 sequenced genes and noted higher stromal contamination in 16/25 of them, directly supporting a pseudo-negative component created by sample composition rather than biology alone.

The review of WES/WGS limitations explicitly states that exome-focused and single-omics workflows miss noncoding lesions and large structural variants, providing a general mechanistic explanation for pseudo-negative tumors.

This law was logically induced by combining repeated rescue fractions across panel-negative, WES-negative, and transcriptome-negative cohorts with explicit assay-failure modes including limited hotspot panels, fusion-not-assayed, splice-not-assayed, copy-number-not-interpreted, structural complexity, protein-state-only, and low tumor purity.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already well established that some driver-negative LUADs are later explained by broader testing, especially through missed fusions, splice events, and rare mutations. Multiple studies individually report rescue of EGFR, KRAS, ALK, ROS1, RET, NRG1, and MET exon 14 events.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel part is the law-like partition of apparent negatives into pseudo-negative tumors created by assay mismatch versus a residual biologic class enriched for pathway-state, lineage-state, proteomic, and noncoding mechanisms. This reframes discovery as assay-normalized mechanism attribution rather than undifferentiated rare-gene hunting.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The ingredients are existing, but the explicit two-compartment decomposition and its use as a general explanatory law across multiple negative-cohort studies is a synthesis rather than a restatement of any single paper.
references: Frampton (2015) Genomic profiling of lung adenocarcinoma patients reveals therapeutic targets and confers clinical benefit when standard molecular testing is negative [direct rescue of standard-test-negative LUAD by broader targeted sequencing]
Campbell or related WGS study (2022) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [WGS rescue of WES-negative RTK/RAS/RAF-negative tumors]
Seo (2012) The transcriptional landscape and mutational profile of lung adenocarcinoma [RNA-based rescue of novel fusions and MET exon 14 skipping with residual unexplained cases]
Kris / LCMC (2014) Using Multiplexed Assays of Oncogenic Drivers in Lung Cancers to Select Targeted Drugs [restricted-panel no-driver class and incomplete-testing caveat]

law.statement_name: Most rescued oncogene-negative LUAD collapses into a compact ontology of mechanism families
law.theory_statement: Across initially oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma, the majority of rescued drivers can be mapped to a small recurrent set of activation architectures rather than an unlimited set of gene-specific explanations: kinase-fusion dimerization, ligand-fusion ERBB activation, exon-skipping-mediated receptor stabilization, non-kinase-domain receptor structural activation, focal or complex dosage amplification, enhancer or super-enhancer rewiring, and loss of negative regulators of RAS/PI3K/Rho/NRF2/cell-cycle control. Mechanism family therefore has higher explanatory value than gene identity alone for classifying biology and predicting therapeutic logic in rare-lesion tumors.
law.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma studies that broaden driver discovery beyond canonical hotspot panels using RNA-seq, WGS structural-variant analysis, copy-number profiling, methylation, ChIP-seq, or proteogenomics, and to cross-cancer analogies when the same activation architecture recurs. It is strongest for lesions discovered after an initial negative call and weaker for well-known canonical positive tumors discovered upfront.
special_cases: Some lesions belong to more than one architecture, such as ALK fusions that are both structural kinase activators and phospho-state outliers, or NRG1 fusions that are both ligand-fusion and receptor-activation mechanisms.
Some amplicons remain unresolved at the target-gene level even when the mechanism family is clear.
This law does not claim that all residual unexplained tumors already fit one of these families; it claims that most rescued cases do.

supporting_evidence.text: Kinase-fusion dimerization recurs across ALK, RET, ROS1, MET, NTRK1, ERBB4, and BRAF fusion examples discovered or emphasized in initially negative settings: EML4-ALK, KIF5B-ALK, HMBOX1-ALK, ANKRD36B-ALK, KIF5B-RET, CCDC6-RET, KIAA1468-RET, KDELR2-ROS1, MET-CAPZA2, MET fusions, MPRIP-NTRK1, CD74-NTRK1, EZR-ERBB4, and TRIM24-BRAF.

Ligand-fusion or juxtacrine receptor activation recurs in CD74-NRG1 and SLC3A2-NRG1 and broader NRG1-fusion sets, all preserving the NRG1 EGF-like domain and driving ERBB2/ERBB3 activation, especially in mucinous adenocarcinoma.

Exon-skipping or splice-mediated receptor stabilization repeatedly explains hidden drivers via MET exon 14 skipping, discovered only after splice-aware RNA/DNA analysis in several independent cohorts.

Non-kinase-domain receptor structural activation recurs in EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 extracellular or transmembrane mutations, EGFR exon 25-26 deletion, and related EGFRvIII/de2-7 mechanistic analogs, showing that hidden activation can occur outside canonical kinase hotspots.

Focal or complex dosage amplification explains multiple hidden-driver loci, including ERBB2 amplification, NKX2-1 amplification, WHSC1L1/NSD3-driven 8p amplicons, TPX2, VEGFA, hTERT, MYC, and complex double-minute/ecDNA-like amplifications in RTK/RAS/RAF-negative tumors.

Enhancer or super-enhancer rewiring recurs in ERBB2 super-enhancer with SV, MAML2 enhancer loss/downregulation, ILF2 promoter mutation, MYC-LASE, and broader pan-cancer analogs such as IGF2 tandem-duplication enhancer hijacking, IRS4 boundary-disruption enhancer hijacking, PIK3CA promoter fusions, and WASF2-FGR promoter fusion.

Loss of negative regulators repeatedly acts as the hidden mechanism in apparently driver-negative tumors through NF1, RASA1, ARHGAP35, KEAP1, STK11, CDKN2A/B, APC, PTPRD, DACH1, MGA, and especially combinatorial Nf1/Rasa1/Pten or RASA1/NF1 states.

Proteomic or phospho-state outliers show that some biologically real driver states are defined at the protein-network level rather than DNA lesion level, including ALK Y1507 phosphosite states, ARHGEF5 Y1370/SRGAP1 suppression, PTPN11 Y62 phosphorylation, STK11-associated neutrophil degranulation, FGL1-LAG3, and RPPA-defined MAPK/mTOR activation without obvious genomic lesions.

This law was logically induced by abstracting away from gene symbols to recurrent activation architectures repeatedly observed across the evidence set and by integrating cross-cancer analogs when the same architecture recurred in different tissues.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Each mechanism class already exists in the literature individually: fusions, exon skipping, non-hotspot receptor variants, amplification, enhancer hijacking, and suppressor loss are all established phenomena. Several papers describe one specific mechanism in one tumor subset.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that these classes form a compact explanatory ontology for most rescued oncogene-negative LUAD and should replace purely gene-centric framing. The theory also emphasizes cross-cancer transportability at the level of activation architecture rather than tissue-specific gene lists.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthetic explanatory theory built from existing lesion classes, but the compression of the hidden-driver problem into a finite mechanism ontology is not directly articulated in the provided literature and is therefore broader than prior gene-by-gene accounts.
references: Shimamura (2014) RET, ROS1 and ALK fusions in lung cancer [kinase fusion architecture]
Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) Transcriptome Meta-Analysis of Lung Cancer Reveals Recurrent Aberrations in NRG1 and Hippo Pathway Genes [ligand fusions, MET skipping, pathway-fusion architecture]
Cancer Genome Atlas / Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [MET exon 14, NF1, RIT1, amplification classes]
Matsumoto (2020) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities [enhancer/SV rewiring class]
Pan-cancer enhancer hijacking analyses such as Weischenfeldt-related work [cross-cancer structural-regulatory activation motifs]

new_predictions_likely: If initially negative LUADs are prospectively annotated by assay-failure mode before re-profiling, then fusion_not_assayed and splice_event_not_assayed will dominate rescue in never-smoker and mucinous cohorts, whereas copy_number_not_interpreted and structural_complexity will dominate mixed-smoking WGS cohorts.

If rare-lesion LUADs are grouped by mechanism family rather than gene name, therapy-response prediction will outperform gene-level grouping for fusion kinases, ligand-fusion ERBB states, and exon-skipping receptor states.

If all residual WES-negative but not WGS-profiled LUADs undergo splice-aware RNA-seq plus targeted long-range SV assays, a measurable fraction will collapse into MET exon 14, cryptic kinase fusion, or non-hotspot RTK-activation families rather than entirely novel biology.

If broad profiling is focused on KRAS-negative mucinous adenocarcinoma, ligand-fusion and fusion-based architectures will account for a higher fraction of rescued cases than classical hotspot mutations.
new_predictions_unknown: If long-read sequencing plus Hi-C is applied to residual RPA(-)G tumors, a clinically meaningful subset will prove to be enhancer-rewired or ecDNA/topology-driven cancers with no necessary coding oncogene mutation.

If phosphoproteomic outlier states are used as primary discovery seeds rather than genomic negatives, additional recurrent driver families will emerge around SHP2, RhoGEFs, or ERBB phosphorylation networks in tumors lacking recurrent DNA lesions.

If cross-cancer mechanism-family transfer is valid, LUAD should contain rare promoter-fusion or enhancer-hijacking activations of PI3K/SRC/MAPK effectors analogous to PIK3CA promoter fusions or WASF2-FGR, but at frequencies below current panel detection.

If the residual unresolved class is truly biologically distinct, it will show stronger enrichment for lineage-state and microenvironmental states than for private coding drivers when matched for assay depth and tumor purity.
negative_experiments: Apply a fully integrated DNA/RNA/CN/SV/proteomic workflow to a new large negative LUAD cohort; if rescued cases do not concentrate into the proposed mechanism families, the compression claim is weakened.
Compare mechanism-family labels versus gene-level labels for predicting drug response in rare lesions; if gene labels perform equally or better across cohorts, the central ontology claim is weakened.
Remove low-purity and incomplete-testing samples from negative cohorts; if the apparent pseudo-negative component does not materially shrink, the assay-failure decomposition is weakened.
Test whether mucinous, never-smoker, and nonmucinous residual negatives actually differ by mechanism-family enrichment; if not, the theory overstates architecture-specific stratification.

unaccounted_for.text: Several recurrent point-mutated candidates such as ABCB5, CFTR, RELN, ASEF, and RNF213 remain weakly validated and are not naturally compressed into a high-confidence mechanism family with direct therapeutic logic.

Some negative-cohort studies define driver-negative subsets but do not perform enough orthogonal profiling to determine whether cases are pseudo-negative or truly residual, limiting direct adjudication of this theory.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several broad studies still retain large unresolved fractions even after RNA-seq, WGS, or integrative workup, implying that the proposed ontology is incomplete or that many residual tumors are driven by mechanisms not yet recurrently recognized.
Some recurrent amplicons remain unresolved at the target-gene level, such as 14q13.3 and certain multi-gene amplicons, which complicates clean compression into single actionable units.

special_cases: Low-purity and small-biopsy tumors can remain pseudo-negative even after broad profiling.
Some true drivers are combinatorial suppressor states rather than single lesions.
Protein-state or microenvironmental driver states may remain invisible even after near-complete genomic profiling.

references: Frampton (2015) Genomic profiling of lung adenocarcinoma patients reveals therapeutic targets and confers clinical benefit when standard molecular testing is negative [assay-rescue concept but not compressed mechanism ontology]
Li and related TCGA/WGS studies of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [expanded driver catalog and residual negative biology]
Matsumoto (2020) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities [specific regulatory-driver architecture]
Fernandez-Cuesta (2014) Transcriptome Meta-Analysis of Lung Cancer Reveals Recurrent Aberrations in NRG1 and Hippo Pathway Genes [multiple hidden-driver classes but not a unifying compression theory]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The literature already recognizes many hidden-driver classes in LUAD, including rare fusions, exon skipping, amplifications, suppressor loss, and enhancer rewiring, and it repeatedly notes assay undercalling. Existing work is mostly lesion-centric and study-specific.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is to organize these results into a compact mechanistic ontology and to treat pseudo-negative versus residual-biologic status as the key latent variable underlying oncogene-negative LUAD. This shifts both classification and validation strategy.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because the underlying components are established but the synthesis is broader, more explicit, and more operational than prior papers, the theory is best classified as somewhat related to existing work rather than entirely new or purely existing.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1866.name: Lineage-State Replacement Theory of Residual Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1866.description: After the major assay-missed driver classes are removed, the remaining oncogene-negative LUADs are not random leftovers. They segregate into lineage-state programs in which tumorigenesis is sustained by altered cell identity, chromatin state, and microenvironmental circuitry rather than by a single dominant RTK/RAS/RAF lesion. The most prominent residual programs are mucinous/gastrointestinal-like NKX2-1-low states, STK11/KEAP1-associated immune-cold or liver-like states, and alveolar intermediate/KRAS-signature precursor states.
theory-1866.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Lineage-State Replacement Theory of Residual Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: After the major assay-missed driver classes are removed, the remaining oncogene-negative LUADs are not random leftovers. They segregate into lineage-state programs in which tumorigenesis is sustained by altered cell identity, chromatin state, and microenvironmental circuitry rather than by a single dominant RTK/RAS/RAF lesion. The most prominent residual programs are mucinous/gastrointestinal-like NKX2-1-low states, STK11/KEAP1-associated immune-cold or liver-like states, and alveolar intermediate/KRAS-signature precursor states.

law.statement_name: Residual negative LUAD is enriched for lineage-program drivers rather than single-lesion oncogenes
law.theory_statement: When major missed canonical drivers are excluded, residual oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma preferentially organizes into lineage programs defined by altered master transcription-factor states and cell identity transitions, especially NKX2-1-low mucinous/gastrointestinal-like programs, gastric-like HNF4A-positive states, and alveolar intermediate precursor-like states, with these lineage programs often explaining histology and signaling better than recurrent coding lesions do.
law.domain_scope: Applies to residual or biologically unresolved lung adenocarcinoma subsets after exclusion of canonical RTK/RAS/RAF drivers, especially invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, KRAS-associated KC-like tumors, and single-cell or spatially resolved precursor-state studies. Also applies to mouse models used to infer lineage mechanisms when those mechanisms are explicitly paralleled in human mucinous or TTF1-low tumors.
special_cases: Many of these lineage programs coexist with canonical mutations such as KRAS, so the law concerns explanatory dominance of lineage state, not complete genetic independence.
Lineage-state explanations are strongest for mucinous and TTF1-low tumors and may be less explanatory for all nonmucinous residual negatives.
