theory_statements.theory_statement: In LUAD, hidden driver classes are not uniformly distributed across tumors; instead, mucinous/gastric-like, NKX2-1-low states preferentially select ligand-presentation fusions and GI-like transcriptional circuits, whereas TRU-like or NKX2-1-positive states preferentially tolerate lineage-survival amplifications or canonical kinase fusions, and structurally altered/TP53-disrupted states preferentially accumulate complex structural and suppressor-loss mechanisms.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma subtypes with resolved lineage or histologic context, especially invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, KRAS-mutant KC-like tumors, fusion-oncogene-positive TRU-like adenocarcinomas, and structurally altered former-smoker LUAD. Not intended as a deterministic law for every individual tumor; rather, it describes enrichment and selection bias across subtype-level populations.
special_cases: Lineage markers can be mixed or unstable within single tumors, especially during progression or under therapeutic pressure.
Some driver classes can appear outside their dominant lineage context, but at lower frequency or with different signaling consequences.
Mucinous histology itself is not equivalent to one molecular mechanism; KRAS, NRG1, RET, ERBB4, and BRAF fusions can all occur.

supporting_evidence.text: CD74-NRG1-positive tumors were uniformly invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas of never-smokers and women in one study, and the same enrichment was reproduced in another pan-negative cohort where both positive cases were stage I invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas.

In KRAS-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, NRG1 fusions accounted for 17.6% of cases and novel fusion drivers such as EZR-ERBB4, TRIM24-BRAF, and KIAA1468-RET were found in the same mucinous compartment, demonstrating lineage-constrained but mechanism-diverse driver selection.
Fusion-positive mucinous tumors frequently showed TTF1 negativity and HNF4A positivity, linking hidden driver choice to a gastric-like lineage program rather than to morphology alone.

NKX2-1 loss or haploinsufficiency in mouse models drove mucinous, gastric-like adenocarcinoma and de-repressed GI lineage programs, providing a mechanistic basis for why certain secretory/ligand-fusion architectures are selected in this lineage state.

The KRAS-mutant KC cluster was defined less by one recurrent co-mutation than by low TTF1/NKX2-1, GI-like differentiation, HNF4A/PDX1 activation, and frequent CDKN2A/B loss, showing that lineage program can be a stronger organizing variable than single-driver identity.

ALK-fusion tumors were enriched in the terminal respiratory unit-like subtype and miRNA cluster 5, indicating that a TRU-like lineage program is preferentially compatible with this canonical hidden fusion driver class.

KIF5B-RET and ALK fusion tumors were identified in nonmucinous adenocarcinoma or TRU-like contexts, while mucinous cribriform morphology was enriched among kinase-fusion-positive adenocarcinomas, especially ALK and ROS1, suggesting morphology/lineage-structured fusion selection rather than random distribution.

NKX2-1 amplification acts as a lineage-survival oncogene in lung adenocarcinoma, indicating that one major lineage-specific solution to tumor fitness is amplification of a normal alveolar identity factor rather than acquisition of a classical hotspot mutation.

14q13 amplification is recurrent in LUAD, but in vivo coexpression of NKX2-1 with NKX2-8 or PAX9 did not induce tumors, implying that lineage-survival amplicons are context-dependent and not reducible to simple copy-number presence.

KACs represent an alveolar intermediate state enriched near tumors and particularly linked to KRAS-mutant progression, showing that hidden driver evolution may begin in distinct pre-neoplastic lineage intermediates rather than in a uniform epithelial background.

The structurally altered LUAD subtype was enriched in former smokers, TP53 alterations, and high structural deletions/inversions, implying a different lineage/evolutionary solution to tumorigenesis than the fusion-rich or mucinous states.

In never-smoker LUAD, the low-SCNA piano subtype was characterized by sparse canonical drivers, UBA1/RET/NKX2-1-associated alterations, and stem-like/quiescent features, suggesting yet another lineage-conditioned route to hidden driver selection.

TTF1-negative LUAD was described as a clinically poor-prognosis subtype enriched for KEAP1/NRF2-associated biology and altered drug sensitivity, indicating that lineage state changes reshape both hidden driver logic and downstream therapeutic vulnerabilities.

Some non-LUAD NSCLCs that clustered transcriptionally with LUAD were 23-fold more likely to carry LUAD-favored driver mutations and often showed TTF-1 positivity or LUAD morphology, reinforcing the idea that lineage identity constrains driver choice across histologic boundaries.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: There is already substantial evidence that specific LUAD histologies and lineage markers correlate with certain drivers, such as NRG1 in mucinous tumors, ALK in TRU-like tumors, and NKX2-1 in lineage-survival programs.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the generalized law that hidden-driver discovery should be framed as lineage-conditioned driver selection: different epithelial states create different admissible solutions, so the negative pool should be stratified by lineage before searching for drivers.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a mechanistic extension of known histology-driver associations into a broader selection principle across mucinous, TRU-like, stem-like, and structurally altered LUAD states.
references: Winslow et al. (2011) Nkx2-1 represses a latent gastric differentiation program in lung adenocarcinoma [lineage-switch basis]
Maeda et al. (2012) Kras G12D and Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induce mucinous adenocarcinoma of the lung [lineage-context cooperation]
Fernandez-Cuesta et al. (2014) CD74-NRG1 Fusions in Lung Adenocarcinoma [mucinous-enriched ligand-fusion context]
Xu et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [TRU-like ALK-fusion enrichment]

theory_statements.statement_name: Therapeutic vulnerability follows lineage-conditioned hidden-driver class
theory_statements.theory_statement: Because hidden drivers are lineage-conditioned, therapeutic sensitivity in oncogene-negative LUAD is better predicted by joint driver-class plus lineage-state assignment than by lesion identity alone: mucinous/gastric-like ligand-fusion states should favor ERBB blockade, TRU-like fusion states should favor kinase-fusion targeting, suppressor-loss RAS/PI3K states should favor pathway-combination therapy, and TTF1-low/KEAP1-NRF2 or STK11/FGL1 states should favor metabolism, DNA-repair, ferroptosis, or immune-axis interventions.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to therapeutic stratification in driver-limited or residual-negative LUAD subgroups after at least basic histologic/lineage and pathway assessment. Best supported for mucinous NRG1-fusion tumors, oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K pathway-state tumors, STK11/FGL1-like immune-cold subtypes, and TTF1-low/KEAP1-associated tumors.
special_cases: Not all tumors within one lineage state share the same actionable node; further molecular partitioning is required.
Resistance mechanisms may rapidly blur these subtype boundaries under therapy.
Some predicted dependencies are supported only by preclinical or correlative data and remain clinically unproven.

supporting_evidence.text: NRG1-fusion mucinous tumors activate ERBB2/ERBB3/AKT/ERK signaling and this signaling was suppressed by lapatinib or afatinib in experimental systems, indicating ERBB dependence of the mucinous ligand-fusion state.
p-ERBB3 was strongly enriched in CD74-NRG1-positive mucinous tumors, supporting functional biomarker-guided identification of ERBB-dependent cases.

Oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K state tumors driven by NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss were sensitive to combined SHP2 and AKT inhibition in mouse and human models, supporting pathway-state therapy when no single proto-oncogene exists.
RASA1/NF1 co-loss conferred trametinib sensitivity, showing that suppressor-loss states can create targetable downstream addiction despite absence of canonical receptor drivers.
STK11/FGL1/HNF1A/CPS1 subtype-4 tumors and related cell lines showed greater sensitivity to mTOR-targeting compounds and implicated FGL1-LAG3 immune evasion as a therapeutic axis.

NENA tumors with estrogen-signaling activation linked to STK11 and GPER1-associated changes showed preclinical sensitivity to saracatinib, supporting lineage/pathway-tailored therapy beyond canonical driver targeting.

TTF1-negative LUAD was associated with reduced sensitivity to paclitaxel/pemetrexed but greater sensitivity to PARP and ATM inhibitors, indicating that lineage-state assignment can predict noncanonical therapeutic vulnerabilities.

PP tumors with STK11/KEAP1 co-alteration were proposed to have glutaminase, SCD, ferroptosis, and metabolic vulnerabilities despite lacking targetable oncogene mutations, illustrating subtype-specific therapy logic in residual-negative disease.
Low-Np63 squamous tumors formed a survivin-high subgroup with YM-155 sensitivity, showing that lineage-factor-defined states can reveal dependencies invisible to mutation-centric classification.

WHSC1L1/NSD3-versus-FGFR1 amplicon reinterpretation and SOX2-linked chromatin dependencies show that amplicon or lineage context can determine the real therapeutic target, not just the most famous gene in the locus.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Many of the individual therapeutic relationships are already known or proposed in their own subtype-specific studies.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit therapeutic law that hidden-driver treatment selection should be lineage-conditioned: identical pathway lesions may imply different dependencies in different epithelial states, and some lineage states are themselves actionable classifiers.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis across multiple subtype-specific therapeutic studies rather than a de novo mechanism, but the integrated therapeutic framework is broader than existing single-study models.
references: Fernandez-Cuesta et al. (2014) CD74-NRG1 Fusions in Lung Adenocarcinoma [ERBB-directed logic in mucinous ligand-fusion tumors]
Li et al. (2023) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [SHP2/AKT combination for oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K state]

Jiang et al. (2024) Proteogenomics of non-small cell lung cancer reveals molecular subtypes associated with specific therapeutic targets and immune evasion mechanisms [STK11/FGL1/HNF1A/CPS1 subtype vulnerabilities]

Ruan et al. (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker Lung Adenocarcinoma Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [lineage/pathway-directed saracatinib hypothesis]

new_predictions_likely: If pan-negative LUADs are first stratified by NKX2-1/TTF1, HNF4A, mucin markers, and TRU markers before genomic discovery, the yield of recurrent hidden drivers within each stratum will increase relative to unstratified discovery.

NRG1-class fusions and phospho-ERBB3 dependence will be most enriched in NKX2-1-low/HNF4A-high secretory tumors, whereas ALK/ROS1/RET-like kinase fusions will be relatively enriched in TRU-like or TTF1-positive tumors.
Suppressor-loss RAS/PI3K states will be overrepresented in tumors with reduced lineage commitment, high pERK/pAKT, and poor fit to canonical mucinous or TRU categories.
Clinical responses to noncanonical therapies in residual-negative LUAD will correlate better with combined lineage-state plus pathway-state classifiers than with individual rare lesions alone.
new_predictions_unknown: Engineering HNF4A-high/NKX2-1-low gastric-like state in lung epithelial models may increase the transformability of ligand-presentation fusions while decreasing that of certain classic kinase fusions; if true, lineage state is causally selective rather than merely correlative.
TRU-like tumors with no detectable fusion may contain cryptic lineage-survival enhancers or ecDNA involving NKX2-1-associated networks rather than receptor-level lesions.
A subset of KAC-like alveolar intermediate lesions may already be biased toward either mucinous/gastric-like or TRU-like future driver trajectories before acquisition of the major tumor driver.

Spatial lineage heterogeneity within single tumors may predict emergence of distinct resistance mechanisms, with mucinous niches evolving ligand-centered bypass and TRU-like niches evolving kinase- or amplification-centered bypass.
negative_experiments: If hidden driver classes are distributed independently of lineage markers, histology, and epithelial state, this theory is weakened.
If lineage manipulation in model systems does not alter the oncogenic output or drug sensitivity of hidden driver classes, the selection component of the theory is weakened.
If therapeutic response in residual-negative LUAD is not improved by adding lineage-state information to genomic classification, the therapeutic arm of the theory is weakened.
If mucinous, TRU-like, and structurally altered subtypes show similar frequencies of all hidden driver classes after exhaustive profiling, the theory overstates lineage conditioning.

unaccounted_for.text: Some mucinous tumors remain TTF1-positive and HNF4A-negative, such as a KIAA1468-RET case, showing that mucinous morphology does not map perfectly onto gastric-like lineage state.
Certain amplicon-driven or structural states, such as 14q13 or complex ecDNA-like events, may not yet map cleanly onto the lineage bins proposed here.

conflicting_evidence.text: Mucinous tumors can be driven by multiple different mechanisms, including KRAS, NRG1, BRAF, ERBB4, and RET-related events, which limits any overly strict one-lineage/one-driver interpretation.

special_cases: The theory describes enrichment and selection, not strict exclusivity.
Lineage state may be dynamic and can be altered by co-drivers such as CDKN2A/B loss, STK11 loss, TP53 disruption, or therapy pressure.
Morphology alone is insufficient; lineage assignment should integrate markers such as NKX2-1/TTF1, HNF4A, mucins, and phospho/proteomic state.

references: Winslow et al. (2011) Nkx2-1 represses a latent gastric differentiation program in lung adenocarcinoma [lineage-state theory related to mucinous switching]
Maeda et al. (2012) Kras G12D and Nkx2-1 haploinsufficiency induce mucinous adenocarcinoma of the lung [cooperation of lineage and oncogene]
Xu et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [TRU-like ALK fusion context]
Skoulidis et al. (2015) Co-occurring genomic alterations define major subsets of KRAS-mutant lung adenocarcinoma [lineage/state-defined KC cluster]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Specific links between lineage markers, histology, and certain driver classes are already present in the literature.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory extends those observations into a general driver-selection framework and an associated therapeutic principle for hidden-driver discovery in oncogene-negative LUAD.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because it generalizes known subtype-specific observations into a broader selection law, it is somewhat related to existing theories rather than wholly new or merely restating prior work.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1871.name: Proteomic Driver-State Theory of Truly Unresolved LUAD
theory-1871.description: A subset of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas is not primarily driven by a single recurrent DNA lesion, but by stable, selectable signaling states that are best measured at the protein and phosphoprotein level. In this view, the causal unit is a pathway state maintained by combinations of weak genomic lesions, negative-regulator loss, lineage context, secretory programs, and post-transcriptional or post-translational regulation. The key implication is that some 'driver-negative' tumors are only unresolved in DNA space; in protein-state space they are organized into recurrent, therapeutically meaningful driver-equivalent classes.
theory-1871.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Proteomic Driver-State Theory of Truly Unresolved LUAD
components.theory_description: A subset of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas is not primarily driven by a single recurrent DNA lesion, but by stable, selectable signaling states that are best measured at the protein and phosphoprotein level. In this view, the causal unit is a pathway state maintained by combinations of weak genomic lesions, negative-regulator loss, lineage context, secretory programs, and post-transcriptional or post-translational regulation. The key implication is that some 'driver-negative' tumors are only unresolved in DNA space; in protein-state space they are organized into recurrent, therapeutically meaningful driver-equivalent classes.

theory_statements.statement_name: Protein-state explanatory primacy in a subset of oncogene-negative LUAD
theory_statements.theory_statement: When canonical RTK/RAS/RAF DNA lesions are absent or insufficient to explain pathway output, recurrent and pathway-coherent protein/phosphosite outliers can serve as the nearest mechanistic explanatory unit for LUAD behavior. In these tumors, the best predictor of dependency is not mutation status alone but the presence of a stable activated phospho-network or suppressed negative-regulator protein state.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinomas, especially residual oncogene-negative or genomically under-explained tumors after broad DNA/RNA profiling, including never-smoker subsets, suppressor-loss tumors, and proteogenomically profiled cohorts. Strongest where phosphoproteomics or proteomics reveals pathway activation not recoverable from DNA-only or RNA-only assays. Not intended for tumors already fully and parsimoniously explained by a dominant canonical driver such as EGFR mutation, ALK fusion, or KRAS hotspot mutation, except insofar as those tumors illustrate the general principle that protein state can diverge from genotype.
special_cases: Not every phospho-outlier is causal; some are downstream consequences of an upstream lesion.
A protein-state signal should be treated as driver-equivalent only when it is recurrent, pathway-coherent, biologically stable, and linked to a vulnerability or functional consequence.
Bulk proteomic signals can reflect stromal or immune admixture; tumor-cell-intrinsic causality requires orthogonal validation.
Some examples arise in tumors with known genomic drivers, showing the principle of protein-state causality but not necessarily proving independence from DNA lesions.

supporting_evidence.text: ALK-fusion-positive LUADs were not only identified by RNA/WGS fusion calling but also showed a striking fusion-specific phosphosite signature, especially ALK Y1507 phosphorylation, plus downstream phosphotyrosine signaling on SND1, HDLBP, and ARHGEF5, demonstrating that a driver can be most directly observed as a phospho-state.

PTPN11/SHP2 Y62 phosphorylation was highly elevated in EGFR-mutant tumors without corresponding change in PTPN11 RNA or total protein, and ALK-fusion tumors showed SHP2 Y546/Y584 outlier phosphorylation, showing that critical pathway activation states can be invisible to DNA/RNA/total-protein abundance measurements.

ARHGEF5 Y1370 phosphorylation with reduced SRGAP1 was identified as a noncanonical signaling state in strict never-smoker LUAD, strongest in ALK-fusion cases, and explicitly described as something DNA-only testing would miss because the key event is phosphorylation-state dysregulation.

RPPA/phosphoproteomic analysis in LUAD found MAPK and mTOR pathway activation in tumors lacking an obvious genomic explanation, indicating that phospho-network output can remain uncaptured by DNA/RNA driver calling.

In oncogene-negative LUAD with activated RAS/PI3K signaling, about 45% of human tumors showed moderate to strong dual pERK/pAKT activation by IHC even when no single canonical proto-oncogene alteration was evident, supporting pathway-state-first classification.

In 13 of 20 genomic-PTEN-wildtype oncogene-negative LUADs, PTEN protein was low by IHC despite absence of genomic PTEN mutation, demonstrating a clinically relevant pathway lesion that exists at the protein/regulatory level rather than as a coding event.

KEAP1-mutant LUAD showed cases in which functional consequence was clarified only proteogenomically: some mutations did not change RNA abundance but reduced KEAP1 protein or increased NFE2L2 phosphosites, and the BTB-domain G511V case implied disruption of KEAP1-NFE2L2 binding rather than simple expression change.

The EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD cohort was not fully explained by one rescued hidden lesion; instead, broader profiling resolved tumors into pathway states involving proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism, reinforcing that for some negative cohorts the explanatory unit is a state rather than a single event.

The paper on histologic progression in LUAD concluded that progression from lepidic to solid patterns was not explained by recurrent genomic drivers but by epigenetic and transcriptional reprogramming, supporting a broader framework in which non-genetic state can dominate phenotype when genetic explanation is weak.

This law/statement was logically inferred through induction from the combined evidence that multiple studies independently report pathway activation detectable at the phosphoprotein/protein level without a matching or sufficient DNA lesion, including SHP2, ARHGEF5/SRGAP1, PTEN-low, RPPA MAPK/mTOR, and KEAP1/NRF2 examples.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Existing proteogenomic LUAD work already documents phosphosite outliers, proteomic pathway states, and mismatch between DNA lesions and protein-level signaling. Studies of SHP2 phosphorylation, ARHGEF5 phospho-activation, PTEN-low states, and RPPA-defined pathway activation all establish that protein-level states matter biologically.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is to elevate these observations into a causal classification principle for truly unresolved oncogene-negative LUAD: protein/phospho-state can be the primary explanatory driver-equivalent unit rather than merely a downstream biomarker. The theory also proposes explicit criteria—recurrence, pathway coherence, stability, and therapeutic actionability—for when this reclassification is justified.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory is built from existing proteogenomic observations but goes beyond the literature's usual biomarker framing by proposing a stronger explanatory ontology for residual driver-negative LUAD. Because it synthesizes rather than duplicates prior work, it is somewhat related to existing theory rather than wholly new or merely existing.
references: Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [direct source for ALK phospho-signatures, SHP2 phosphosite outliers, ARHGEF5/SRGAP1 state, KEAP1 phosphoproteomic consequences]

Campbell et al. (2023) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [supports protein-level PTEN loss and pathway-state explanation in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network / related LUAD proteomic studies [established genotype-to-protein mismatch and pathway-state thinking]

theory_statements.statement_name: Driver-equivalent protein states are often composite tumor-intrinsic plus microenvironmental circuits
theory_statements.theory_statement: In a subset of unresolved LUAD, the stable oncogenic state is maintained jointly by tumor-cell signaling and microenvironment-coupled secretory or immune programs. Therefore, hidden drivers can appear as composite circuits such as STK11-linked neutrophil degranulation, FGL1-HNF1A-CPS1 immune evasion, or MIF-HIF1A glycolytic reinforcement, rather than as isolated cell-autonomous mutations.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma subsets in which proteomics, phosphoproteomics, transcriptomics, or single-cell profiling identify secretory, immune, or metabolic programs that are not well reconstructed from canonical genomic drivers alone. Especially relevant to STK11-altered tumors, never-smoker cohorts, and driver-negative cohorts analyzed with proteogenomic or single-cell methods. Less applicable where microenvironmental sampling is poor or where the tumor is fully explained by a dominant cell-autonomous kinase driver.
special_cases: Some secreted or degranulation proteins may originate from infiltrating immune cells rather than tumor cells; spatial or cell-type-resolved validation is needed.
Certain programs may primarily modulate progression or therapy resistance rather than initiation.
Association with a tumor suppressor lesion such as STK11 does not prove that the downstream secretory circuit is itself sufficient for tumor maintenance.

supporting_evidence.text: STK11-mutant LUADs showed a proteomics-specific neutrophil degranulation program, with coherent overexpression of 16 degranulation proteins including CAMP, LTF, BPI, MMP8, MMP9, MPO, LCN2, ELANE, and ARG1, and the signal was explicitly absent at the RNA level because these proteins are granule-stored.

FGL1 was identified as a subtype-specific immune inhibitory ligand in NSCLC subtype 4, overexpressed in an immune-cold STK11-linked context and functionally linked to LAG3-mediated immune suppression, indicating that secreted ligand biology can be part of the disease-maintaining state.

HNF1A showed epigenetically regulated outlier expression in the same STK11-associated subtype and was linked to FGL1 and CPS1 expression, suggesting a lineage-like transcriptional module underlying the secretory immune-evasion state.

The integrated STK11-FGL1-HNF1A-CPS1 subtype 4 mechanism argued that STK11 mutation alone did not fully explain phenotype and that reduced STK11 mRNA/protein or epigenetic regulation in STK11-wildtype tumors could produce the same liver-like FGL1/CPS1 program.

In the NENA cohort, STK11 mutation, copy-number changes, GPER1-linked trans effects, and hypomethylation of LLGL2/ST14 were integrated into a broader estrogen-signaling state rather than a single-lesion explanation, further supporting composite-state drivers.

Driver-gene-negative LUAD showed a MIF-HIF-1α positive feedback axis, with MIF-high AT2-like cells showing higher glycolysis, and cell experiments showed MIF overexpression and HIF-1α activation increased lactate production, glucose uptake, LDH activity, and proliferation; ISO-1 partially reversed these effects.

The review of TTF1-negative LUAD described a poor-prognosis subtype enriched for KEAP1 mutations, NRF2 activity, DNA repair proteins, cyclin B1, and SRGN overexpression, implying a lineage-state/immune-state composite not reducible to a single driver mutation.

Proteogenomic analysis of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD identified four subgroups defined by pathway and microenvironmental states rather than a single rescued hidden lesion, including proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolic programs.

This law/statement was logically inferred through induction from the conjunction of STK11-neutrophil degranulation, FGL1/HNF1A/CPS1, NENA estrogen-signaling, and MIF-HIF1A results: in each case, the explanatory unit spans tumor-intrinsic signaling plus secretory or microenvironment-coupled programs that are incompletely captured by DNA lesion catalogues alone.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Existing studies already report STK11-associated immune-coldness, FGL1-LAG3 signaling, proteomics-only neutrophil degranulation, and MIF-HIF1A glycolytic programs in driver-negative or never-smoker LUAD contexts. These observations are individually established as biologically relevant states or vulnerabilities.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is to unify them as a general class of composite driver-maintenance circuits in unresolved LUAD, where tumor-cell signaling and microenvironment-linked secretory programs form one causal system. This reframes immune and secretory outputs from consequences into integral components of the oncogenic state in selected tumors.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Because each component observation already exists but the explicit integration into a shared causal theory is new, this classification is somewhat related to existing work. The theory extends the field from descriptive subtype biology toward a generalized mechanistic framework for residual driver-negative tumors.
references: Xu et al. or related NSCLC proteogenomic subtype studies [Subtype 4 FGL1/HNF1A/CPS1 immune-evasion state]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [STK11-associated neutrophil degranulation state]
Driver-gene-negative LUAD glycolysis study [MIF-HIF1A circuit in negative tumors]
Never-smoker EGFR/ALK-wild-type LUAD proteogenomics [subtype-level pathway-state explanation]

new_predictions_likely: In residual WGS-negative or panel-negative LUAD, unsupervised phosphoproteomic clustering will identify recurrent groups whose drug sensitivity is better predicted by phospho-network state than by mutation count, smoking status, or TMB.

Among oncogene-negative pAKT-high LUADs lacking PTEN mutation, a large fraction will show either low PTEN protein, altered PTEN-complex stoichiometry, or phosphatase-network suppression by proteomics/spatial proteomics.

FGL1-high or neutrophil-degranulation-high LUADs will show secretome profiles that cannot be reconstructed from bulk RNA abundance alone, because key proteins are granule-stored or post-transcriptionally regulated.
A subset of never-smoker oncogene-negative LUADs will segregate into MIF-high glycolytic states with stronger sensitivity to MIF/HIF1A-axis perturbation than to broad RTK inhibition.
In tumors with SHP2 Y62 or ARHGEF5 Y1370 phospho-outlier states, acute pathway inhibition will collapse downstream signaling even when no corresponding coding lesion is found in the target itself.
new_predictions_unknown: Spatial proteomics may reveal that some apparent tumor-cell phospho-driver states are stabilized by geographically restricted stromal or immune ligand niches, such that removing the niche abolishes the phospho-state without editing the tumor genome.

Longitudinal relapse specimens from initially canonical-driver-positive LUAD may convert into protein-state-dominant survival states, explaining why some relapsed tumors appear genomically 'driver-negative' despite persistent pathway addiction.

Multi-omic perturbation of ARHGEF5/SRGAP1, SHP2 phospho-states, and FGL1/HNF1A/CPS1 programs may uncover a common master stabilizer module, such as phosphatase rewiring or proteostasis circuitry, shared across otherwise different unresolved LUAD subclasses.

Some tumors classified as genomically unresolved may prove to be maintained primarily by secretory autocrine loops detectable only in plasma/tumor interstitial fluid proteomics, not in tumor DNA or bulk RNA.

Driver-equivalent protein states may define a new therapeutic taxonomy that cuts across histology and genotype, grouping STK11/FGL1, PTEN-low/pAKT-high, SHP2-high-phospho, and MIF-high tumors into intervention classes not visible from genomics alone.
negative_experiments: If residual unexplained LUADs do not form reproducible phosphoproteomic/proteomic clusters across independent cohorts and sample-preparation platforms, the theory's central claim of stable protein-state classes is weakened.

If candidate phospho-outlier nodes such as SHP2 or ARHGEF5 can be selectively perturbed without affecting viability, pathway coherence, or drug sensitivity in relevant models, their proposed driver-equivalent role is undermined.

If RNA or DNA features predict the same vulnerabilities as well as proteomic state in residual negative tumors, then the distinctive explanatory value of protein-state-first classification is reduced.

If spatial or purified-cell analyses show that neutrophil degranulation, FGL1, or related signals are purely microenvironmental contaminants with no association to tumor-cell state or treatment response, the composite-circuit version of the theory is weakened.

If PTEN-low genomic-wildtype tumors fail to show any reproducible downstream PI3K-AKT dependency or phosphatase-network abnormality, the claim that protein-level suppressor loss can act as a hidden driver-equivalent mechanism would be weakened.

unaccounted_for.text: Many oncogene-negative LUADs are explained by hidden genomic lesions rather than protein-state primacy, including noncanonical fusions, exon-skipping events, focal amplifications, and structural variants such as CD74-NRG1, EGFR ECD mutations, MET exon 14 skipping, RET fusions, and double-minute amplifications.
Some unresolved cohorts remain unexplained even after RNA-based and exome-based workup, but those studies do not show proteomic rescue, so this theory does not yet explain the full residual fraction.
Several proteomic-state observations are currently biomarker-level or pathway-modifier-level and not experimentally proven as initiating or maintenance drivers.

conflicting_evidence.text: A large fraction of tumors initially called negative are later rescued by broader DNA/RNA assays, implying that many apparent driver-negative cases reflect assay incompleteness rather than true protein-state primacy.

Some of the strongest phosphoproteomic examples arise in tumors with known canonical genomic drivers, such as EGFR-mutant or ALK-fusion tumors, so those examples can be interpreted as consequences or modifiers rather than primary explanations for unresolved tumors.

The histologic-progression study argues that non-genetic evolution can dominate phenotype, but it emphasizes epigenetic/transcriptional reprogramming more than protein-state causality specifically, partially overlapping but not directly supporting the proteomic-driver emphasis.

special_cases: This theory is strongest for the residual fraction after broad DNA/RNA fusion/splice/copy-number testing, not as a replacement for canonical genomic driver models.
It applies most directly to tumors with matched proteomic or phosphoproteomic data; without those data, the theory is difficult to operationalize.
The theory can still be useful in canonical-driver-positive tumors as an explanation of secondary dependence or resistance states, but that is not its core scope.

Lineage-state and microenvironmental programs may blur causal attribution; the theory therefore predicts mixed tumor-intrinsic and extrinsic maintenance circuits rather than purely cell-autonomous drivers.

references: Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [established phosphoproteomic outlier observations in LUAD]
Xu et al. or related NSCLC subtype proteogenomics [Subtype 4 STK11/FGL1/HNF1A/CPS1 state, immune-evasion framework]

Campbell et al. (2023) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K pathway-state explanation with PTEN protein-level loss]
Exploring the Glycolytic Mechanisms in Driver Gene-Negative LUAD (2024) [MIF-HIF1A pathway-state mechanism in driver-negative LUAD]
CPTAC and related LUAD proteogenomic studies [general genotype-proteotype decoupling and pathway-state interpretation]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The literature already shows that LUAD contains phosphosite outliers, proteomics-only immune programs, PTEN-low protein states without matching mutations, and STK11-linked secretory/immune phenotypes. Existing work therefore supports the idea that genomics does not fully determine tumor behavior.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The new element is the explicit theory that some truly unresolved oncogene-negative LUADs should be reclassified as protein-state-driven cancers, where the causal explanatory unit is a stable phospho-proteomic maintenance circuit rather than a single DNA lesion. This also proposes a practical criterion for defining such states as driver-equivalent.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because the component observations are established but the stronger causal synthesis is not standard, the theory is best classified as somewhat related to existing work. It is not wholly new in ingredients, but it is novel in explanatory emphasis, scope, and proposed therapeutic framing.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1872.name: Resolution-Stratified Driver Architecture of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1872.description: Apparent oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not one disease state. It is a superposition of at least three mechanistic strata organized by assay resolution: (1) assay-limited negatives later rescued as rare canonical or canonically targetable lesions; (2) broad-profile negatives driven by pathway-state activation, tumor-suppressor co-loss, or noncoding/structural regulatory events; and (3) residual lineage- and ecosystem-dominant tumors whose fitness is maintained by cell-state plasticity and microenvironmental circuitry. The central claim is that 'driver-negative' must be interpreted as a conditional label produced jointly by biology and ascertainment depth, and that therapeutic logic should shift from lesion-targeting to state-targeting as negative-call confidence rises.
theory-1872.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Resolution-Stratified Driver Architecture of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Apparent oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not one disease state. It is a superposition of at least three mechanistic strata organized by assay resolution: (1) assay-limited negatives later rescued as rare canonical or canonically targetable lesions; (2) broad-profile negatives driven by pathway-state activation, tumor-suppressor co-loss, or noncoding/structural regulatory events; and (3) residual lineage- and ecosystem-dominant tumors whose fitness is maintained by cell-state plasticity and microenvironmental circuitry. The central claim is that 'driver-negative' must be interpreted as a conditional label produced jointly by biology and ascertainment depth, and that therapeutic logic should shift from lesion-targeting to state-targeting as negative-call confidence rises.

theory_statements.statement_name: Assay-depth rescue law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When lung adenocarcinomas classified as oncogene-negative by narrow or incomplete testing are re-profiled with broader DNA and RNA assays, a substantial subset is reclassified as harboring rare but canonical or canonically targetable oncogenic lesions; the rescue fraction declines as the initial negative definition becomes broader, more orthogonal, and more purity-aware.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinomas initially labeled driver-negative using limited or intermediate diagnostic frameworks, especially EGFR/KRAS/ALK-only, EGFR/ALK-only, or hotspot/fusion-limited panels, and to cohorts subsequently re-profiled by broad hybrid-capture NGS, WES/WGS, RNA-seq, fusion calling, or splice-junction analysis. Strongest in resected and advanced LUAD cohorts where matched tumor DNA and RNA are both available and where purity/cellularity are measurable.
special_cases: Does not imply that all WGS plus RNA-seq-negative tumors are false negatives; a residual high-confidence biologic negative class may remain.
Rescue yield is strongly modulated by tumor purity, assay breadth, and whether RNA-based fusion and exon-skipping analyses were included.

Histology- and exposure-enriched subsets, particularly invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma and never-smoker LUAD, can have disproportionately high rescue by ligand fusion, kinase fusion, or atypical ERBB/MET events.
Some 'rescues' are not classic point mutations but copy-number, non-kinase-domain, fusion, or splice-altering lesions.

supporting_evidence.text: Broad clinical genomic profiling of EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative LUAD revealed many additional actionable alterations, including rare driver mutations, fusions, amplifications, and pathway lesions, with objective clinical responses among ROS1-rearranged patients treated on trial.

ARAF S214C was discovered in a broadly driver-negative LUAD lacking KRAS, EGFR, BRAF, ERBB2, PIK3CA hotspot mutations and ALK/ROS1/RET fusions; recurrence of RAF-family hotspot-region lesions and preclinical sensitivity supported reclassification of a hidden-driver subset.
Pan-negative LUADs were rescued by non-canonical EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, including M277E, A289D, and R324L, after initial panel testing failed to detect standard driver lesions.
Pan-negative LUADs were similarly rescued by non-kinase-domain HER2 mutations, including extracellular and transmembrane alterations such as S310Y and V659E.

Initially driver-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas were reclassified by RNA-based fusion discovery as CD74-NRG1-positive, with mechanistic evidence that the ligand fusion activates ERBB2/ERBB3-PI3K-AKT signaling.
Triple-negative LUADs lacking EGFR, KRAS, and EML4-ALK were rescued by KIF5B-RET fusion discovery through WGS and RNA-seq.
Clinically negative LUADs were rescued by NTRK1 rearrangements discovered by targeted DNA NGS plus FISH, and those fusions were shown to be transforming and drug sensitive.

MET exon 14 skipping repeatedly emerged as a hidden driver in driver-unknown or initially negative lung cancers after deeper transcriptome or DNA/RNA analysis, including direct mechanistic studies showing splice-site-driven exon 14 deletion.
S4-low or clinically panel-negative LUADs were reclassified by WGS/RNA-seq as fusion-positive or otherwise driver-positive in many cases, leaving only a smaller residual unexplained subset.

In never-smoker LUAD, low tumor cellularity was explicitly linked to false-negative WES calls; higher-depth or orthogonal testing identified EGFR, ERBB2, KRAS, ALK, ROS1, MET exon 14, and other hidden lesions in many initially negative tumors.

Whole-genome reanalysis of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD rescued 28 of 85 initially negative cases by identifying missed KRAS hotspot mutations, EGFR/MAPK1/ARAF/SOS1 amplifications, NF1/RASA1 deletions, and NRG1 events.
Pan-negative never-smoker LUAD cohorts screened stepwise for EGFR/KRAS/HER2/BRAF/ALK and then ROS1 still yielded additional ROS1 fusion-positive cases after expanded testing.

Driver-unknown tumors analyzed by RNA-seq fusion calling were reclassified into recurrent hidden-driver groups including NRG1 fusions, MET exon 14 skipping, NF1 inactivation, and Hippo-pathway fusions.
This law was logically inferred through induction from the repeated pattern that rescue yield is highest in narrow-panel-negative cohorts and lower but nonzero in WES/WGS/RNA-seq-negative cohorts.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is well established that broader sequencing can rescue missed fusions, rare mutations, exon-skipping events, and non-canonical ERBB lesions in apparently negative LUAD. Multiple studies independently document such reclassification.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the explicit law-like claim that rescue probability itself is structured by assay depth and negative-call confidence, so 'driver-negative' should first be treated as a resolution-dependent category before being treated as a biological subtype.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The component observations are existing, but the synthesis into an assay-depth law that predicts rescue yield and shifts downstream interpretation is not explicitly stated in the supplied literature.
references: Frampton et al. (2015) Genomic profiling of lung adenocarcinoma patients reveals therapeutic targets and confers clinical benefit when standard molecular testing is negative [broad-panel rescue of apparent negatives]
Nakaoku et al. (2017) Tracing Oncogene Rearrangements in the Mutational History of Lung Adenocarcinoma [WGS/RNA-seq rescue of clinically or genomically negative S4-low tumors]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [WGS rescue after WES-negative classification]
Vaishnavi et al. (2013) Oncogenic and drug sensitive NTRK1 rearrangements in lung cancer [discovery of hidden kinase fusions after standard negative testing]
Fernandez-Cuesta et al. (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusion in lung adenocarcinoma [ligand-fusion rescue of pan-negative mucinous tumors]

theory_statements.statement_name: State-dominance law in high-confidence negatives
theory_statements.theory_statement: In LUADs that remain negative after broad DNA plus RNA interrogation, malignant control is more likely to be maintained by pathway-state activation, tumor-suppressor co-loss, noncoding/cis-regulatory activation, structural-genome mechanisms, lineage plasticity, or ecosystem circuitry than by a single undiscovered high-frequency kinase oncogene.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to high-confidence oncogene-negative LUAD after broad genomic characterization, especially cohorts classified by WGS or WES plus RNA-seq and then further profiled by methylation, proteomics, phosphoproteomics, enhancer mapping, or tumor-microenvironment analysis. Strongest for residual cases unresolved after canonical rescue analysis.
special_cases: Rare intermediate-frequency coding drivers may still remain undiscovered and can contribute to a minority of residual negatives.
Some cohorts labeled negative in proteomic or transcriptomic studies use loose definitions and may still contain missed canonical oncogene-positive tumors.

The relative dominance of state-based mechanisms may differ by disease stage; early subsolid or preinvasive disease may overrepresent progression-state mechanisms compared with advanced metastatic disease.

supporting_evidence.text: Residual RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD after WGS rescue was enriched for TP53, KEAP1, STK11, and SMARCA4 alterations, high TMB, complex structural variation, and recurrent ILF2 promoter mutation/overexpression rather than a single replacement oncogene.

Oncogene-negative LUAD can be produced by combinatorial loss of pathway brakes rather than a canonical oncogene, with NF1/RASA1/PTEN co-loss creating an Onc-negative RAS/PI3K state and RASA1/NF1 co-loss defining a smoking-associated MEK-sensitive subset.

Non-CAGA LUAD implicated enhancer loss, methylation, and cis-regulatory suppression of MAML2 rather than canonical coding drivers, while non-CAGA LUAD with SE-SV overlap showed hidden regulatory activation such as ERBB2 overexpression by inversion-linked super-enhancer formation.
MYC-LASE and CESAM-style enhancer-hijacking frameworks show that tumors lacking obvious coding drivers can still be driven by noncoding regulatory architecture and 3D-genome mechanisms.

Proteogenomic LUAD studies repeatedly identified pathway-state explanations in tumors lacking currently actionable lesions, including PP subtype CDK/metabolic dependence, broad phosphoproteomic kinase states, mTOR and MAPK activity unexplained by known DNA lesions, and STK11/AMPK/FGL1 immune-evasion programs.

Never-smoker and transcriptomic driver-poor states were characterized by lineage and ecosystem logic rather than enriched canonical drivers, including piano stem-like tumors, chaotic EMT/CAF-rich tumors, and nongenetic progression dominated by plastic transcriptional state shifts and TME remodeling.

Cell-state and lineage-switch mechanisms in LUAD include KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate states, NKX2-1-negative mucinous/gastric reprogramming, and Foxa1/2 chromatin retargeting after lineage-factor loss, demonstrating tumor fitness maintenance without reliance on a new canonical oncogene.

Metabolic and ecosystem states are repeatedly proposed as organizing mechanisms in negative or quasi-negative LUAD, including MIF-HIF-1alpha glycolysis, ferroptosis-resistant fibroblast-rich EGFR-WT states, metabolism-high NSLA, THBS2-positive CAF progression states, and angiogenesis-high immune-excluded states.

Hallmark mapping explicitly showed that sustaining proliferative signaling remained genetically unexplained in a large fraction of LUAD and motivated epigenetic/RNA deregulation as an additional explanatory class.

This law was logically inferred through abduction from the convergence of genomic, proteomic, enhancer, and microenvironment studies showing diverse noncanonical mechanisms but no recurring single replacement kinase driver in the residual high-confidence negative set.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work already supports tumor-suppressor co-loss, epigenetic deregulation, proteomic state dependence, lineage plasticity, and microenvironmental subtypes in LUAD. Each mechanism class is individually represented in the literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit prioritization rule: once canonical lesion rescue is exhausted, the explanatory weight shifts toward state-based, regulatory, and ecosystem mechanisms rather than toward a single undiscovered common kinase lesion.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is an integrative synthesis of existing observations rather than a single published model, but the ordering principle for interpreting high-confidence negatives appears new.
references: Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [residual negatives enriched for suppressor, noncoding, and structural mechanisms]
CPTAC/APOLLO LUAD proteogenomic studies (2020-2024) [pathway-state and subtype dependence in tumors lacking actionable lesions]

Sun et al. (2024) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in LUAD without clinically actionable genetic alterations [regulatory-driver model]
Lee et al. (2023) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [state-based hidden-biology model]
Marjanovic et al. / nongenetic evolution literature [lineage and state-shift explanation for progression without new drivers]

theory_statements.statement_name: Subtype partition law
theory_statements.theory_statement: Biologic driver-negative LUAD is better explained by recurrent mechanistic families than by the umbrella negative label itself; the most reproducible families are hidden canonical lesion, dual-pathway suppressor-loss, noncoding/cis-regulatory activation, lineage-plasticity state, metabolic-dependency state, and microenvironment-dominant ecosystem state.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to the full spectrum of oncogene-negative or no-known-actionable-driver LUAD after harmonizing negative-call definitions and separating assay-limited negatives from broad multi-omic negatives. Intended as a unifying taxonomy across genomic, proteomic, transcriptomic, and microenvironment-focused studies rather than as a strict replacement for existing histologic categories.
special_cases: A single tumor can belong to more than one family, for example STK11-loss plus immune-cold plus metabolic rewiring, or regulatory activation plus canonical ERBB dependence.

Some families are enriched in specific contexts, such as ligand/kinase fusions in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, estrogen signaling in EGFR/ALK-negative never-smokers, or cholesterol/ER-stress programs in subsolid nodules.
Technical false-negative cases should be separated before assigning a biologic family.

supporting_evidence.text: Hidden canonical lesion family: rare coding oncogenes, kinase fusions, ligand fusions, and splicing events explain many apparent negatives, including ARAF/RAF1, EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 non-kinase mutations, KIF5B-RET, NTRK1 rearrangements, ROS1 fusions, RIT1, NRG1 fusions, BCAR4/ERBB3 fusions, and MET exon 14 skipping.

Dual-pathway suppressor-loss family: oncogene-negative LUAD can be driven by NF1/RASA1/PTEN combinatorial loss or by RASA1/NF1 co-loss, producing MAPK- and PI3K-activated states with SHP2/AKT/MEK vulnerabilities.

Noncoding/cis-regulatory activation family: ERBB2 super-enhancer inversion, MYC-LASE amplification, MAML2 enhancer silencing, CESAM-style enhancer hijacking, and related 3D-genome mechanisms show that a driver-negative phenotype can be converted into a regulatory-driver phenotype without a coding oncogene mutation.

Lineage-plasticity family: piano stem-like tumors, chaotic EMT/CAF-rich tumors, KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate states, NKX2-1-negative mucinous/gastric programs, and histologic continuum studies all support non-genetic or differentiation-state control of malignancy.

Metabolic-dependency family: metabolism-high NSLA, MIF-HIF-1alpha glycolysis-high driver-negative LUAD, ferroptosis-resistant fibroblast-linked EGFR-WT states, cholesterol/PCSK9 early LUAD states, and ER-stress-linked progression states identify metabolism and proteostasis as organizing axes.

Microenvironment-dominant ecosystem family: immune-high NSLA, angiogenesis-high NSLA, never-smoker immune subtypes, THBS2-positive CAF progression states, and fibroblast-linked ferroptosis signatures identify tumors in which ecosystem circuitry dominates prognosis and therapeutic logic.

This partition was logically inferred through induction from repeated recurrence of the same mechanism classes across independent genomic, proteogenomic, and lineage/ecosystem studies, despite different cohort definitions of 'negative'.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual mechanism families such as hidden fusions, suppressor co-loss, epigenetic drivers, metabolic states, and immune/ecosystem states are all already represented in the literature. Several papers hint that apparent driver-negative LUAD is heterogeneous.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the explicit six-family causal taxonomy proposed as a replacement for the undifferentiated 'negative' label and as a bridge between genomic and state-based explanations.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The family members are not individually new, but the partition itself is a novel synthetic theory of organization supported across otherwise disconnected literatures.
references: Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [supports residual-negative genomic heterogeneity but not full taxonomy]
CPTAC/APOLLO LUAD proteogenomics [supports pathway-state and subtype arms of taxonomy]
Lee et al. (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [supports state-based family]
Fernandez-Cuesta et al. (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusion in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [supports hidden-canonical-lesion family]
Marjanovic et al. / nongenetic evolution studies [supports lineage/ecosystem family]

new_predictions_likely: If a historical 'driver-negative' LUAD cohort is reannotated with a standardized negative-call confidence score, narrow-panel-negative tumors will be enriched for rescued canonical lesions, whereas WGS plus RNA-seq-negative tumors will be enriched for suppressor-loss, regulatory, lineage, and ecosystem families.

If RNA fusion calling and splice-junction analysis are added systematically to historical pan-negative mucinous and never-smoker LUAD cohorts, the largest incremental diagnostic gains will occur for NRG1-family, ROS1/RET/NTRK-family, and MET exon 14 events.

If high-confidence broad-negative LUADs are jointly clustered by phosphoproteomics, methylation, enhancer activity, and TME composition, they will separate more reproducibly by mechanism family than by the umbrella 'driver-negative' label.
If therapy is assigned by mechanism family rather than by umbrella negative status, within-group pharmacologic response variance will be lower than in the undifferentiated driver-negative population.
new_predictions_unknown: If truly lesion-minimal driver-negative LUAD exists as a stable class, deep single-cell phylogenomics plus long-read genome/transcriptome sequencing will reveal convergent lineage-state attractors without recurrent trunk oncogene lesions; failure to find such tumors would imply that almost all negatives are lesion-explainable or assay-limited.

If residual WGS plus RNA-seq-negative LUAD is heavily driven by noncoding architecture, combined long-read sequencing, Hi-C, and H3K27ac profiling will identify a new class of low-copy enhancer-juxtaposition events that account for a large fraction of currently unexplained tumors.

If some ecosystem-dominant negatives are maintained by tumor-extrinsic loops rather than tumor-intrinsic lesions, organotypic co-culture perturbation of CAF/endothelial/immune interactions will collapse tumor fitness in models that show weak dependency in tumor-cell monoculture.

If coding-driver discovery is nearing saturation specifically in high-confidence negatives, future pan-cancer significance analyses restricted to coding mutations will plateau in explanatory power while enhancer, structural, and phosphoproteomic analyses continue to reduce the unexplained fraction.
negative_experiments: Assemble only high-confidence WGS plus RNA-seq-negative LUADs and test whether hidden canonical lesions still dominate after orthogonal validation. If they do, the theory overstates the shift toward state/regulatory biology.

Apply the six-family taxonomy prospectively to an external broad-negative cohort. If tumors fail to partition reproducibly or show no family-specific molecular coherence, the subtype partition law is weakened.
Quantify rescue probability as a function of assay breadth across harmonized cohorts. If rescue yield does not decrease with broader initial testing, the assay-depth rescue law is false.

Test whether family-based therapy matching improves biomarker coherence or response consistency relative to umbrella 'driver-negative' assignment. If not, the taxonomy may be biologically interesting but clinically non-predictive.

unaccounted_for.text: Pan-cancer mutation-discovery studies still suggest that intermediate-frequency coding cancer genes remain underdiscovered, so the theory may underweight the future contribution of rare coding oncogenes to residual negatives.

Several proteomic or transcriptomic subtype papers use loose or implicit definitions of negativity, so some state-based evidence may derive from mixed cohorts rather than rigorously broad-negative tumors.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some studies imply that many apparently negative tumors are eventually rescued by deeper testing, suggesting that true biologic driver-negative disease may be rarer than a broad multi-family architecture might imply.
Pan-cancer saturation analyses argue that many coding drivers remain undiscovered, which could mean residual negatives are still more coding-driver-rich than this theory assumes.

special_cases: Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma is a special case in which ligand and kinase fusions are overrepresented and can dominate apparent negative biology.
Never-smoker LUAD is a special case in which fusion, estrogen-signaling, lineage, and immune/ecosystem mechanisms are especially informative.
Subsolid-nodule and preinvasive disease may obey progression-state mechanisms not directly comparable to advanced broad-negative LUAD.
Smoking-associated oncogene-negative subsets with dual RasGAP loss may behave differently from never-smoker broad-negative states.

references: Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [closest broad-negative genomic framework, but not a full resolution-stratified theory]
Nakaoku et al. (2017) Tracing Oncogene Rearrangements in the Mutational History of Lung Adenocarcinoma [hidden-driver rescue logic, but not an assay-depth architecture]

Lee et al. (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [state-based hidden biology in a restricted negative cohort]
Fernandez-Cuesta et al. (2014) CD74-NRG1 fusion in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma [hidden ligand-fusion rescue model]
Marjanovic et al. / Nongenetic evolution literature [lineage and ecosystem explanations, but not unified with rescue and regulatory strata]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The literature already contains separate theories or observations for missed canonical drivers, suppressor co-loss, noncoding regulatory activation, phosphoproteomic pathway states, and lineage/ecosystem explanations in LUAD. Several studies also acknowledge heterogeneity within apparent driver-negative disease.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: What is novel is the explicit assembly of these observations into one resolution-stratified theory that predicts how mechanism prevalence changes with assay depth and that replaces the umbrella driver-negative label with a mechanistic partition.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No supplied paper states this theory in this integrated form. It is a new synthesis grounded in existing evidence but organized around a new causal architecture.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1873.name: Network-Replacement Theory of Oncogenic Control in Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1873.description: In driver-negative LUAD, oncogenic control is often achieved not by one dominant activated receptor or kinase, but by replacement of the missing canonical trunk driver with an equivalent control module at another biological layer. Three major replacement modules recur: (1) pathway-equivalence modules, in which suppressor loss recreates RAS/MAPK and PI3K signaling without a canonical oncogene; (2) regulatory-equivalence modules, in which enhancer hijacking, super-enhancer formation, methylation, or structural variation recreate oncogene output without coding activation; and (3) ecosystem-equivalence modules, in which immune exclusion, angiogenesis, fibroblast states, or lineage transitions stabilize malignant fitness. The theory predicts that driver-negative LUAD should be classified by the layer at which proliferative and survival control is restored.
theory-1873.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Network-Replacement Theory of Oncogenic Control in Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: In driver-negative LUAD, oncogenic control is often achieved not by one dominant activated receptor or kinase, but by replacement of the missing canonical trunk driver with an equivalent control module at another biological layer. Three major replacement modules recur: (1) pathway-equivalence modules, in which suppressor loss recreates RAS/MAPK and PI3K signaling without a canonical oncogene; (2) regulatory-equivalence modules, in which enhancer hijacking, super-enhancer formation, methylation, or structural variation recreate oncogene output without coding activation; and (3) ecosystem-equivalence modules, in which immune exclusion, angiogenesis, fibroblast states, or lineage transitions stabilize malignant fitness. The theory predicts that driver-negative LUAD should be classified by the layer at which proliferative and survival control is restored.

theory_statements.statement_name: Pathway-equivalence law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When canonical RTK/RAS/RAF driver lesions are absent, LUAD can recreate equivalent mitogenic output through combinatorial loss of negative regulators or rare pathway-member alterations, producing a driver-negative state that is biochemically RAS/MAPK- and/or PI3K/AKT-active.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to oncogene-negative LUAD and related NSCLC subsets in which broad profiling excludes major canonical activating lesions but reveals suppressor loss or rare pathway-member abnormalities. Strongest for cohorts with direct pathway readouts by IHC, RPPA, phosphoproteomics, or functional perturbation.
special_cases: Some pathway-equivalent tumors are driven mainly by dual RAS plus PI3K activation, whereas others may be MAPK-dominant or mTOR-dominant.
Certain pathway states may be exposure-linked, such as RASA1/NF1 co-loss in smoking-associated disease or STK11-linked estrogen signaling in EGFR/ALK-negative never-smokers.
A minority of tumors may show pathway activation without yet-identified causal lesions.

supporting_evidence.text: NF1/RASA1/PTEN co-inactivation efficiently initiates oncogene-negative LUAD in vivo and creates an explicit Onc-negative RAS/PI3K subtype with pERK and pAKT activation plus SHP2/AKT inhibitor sensitivity.

RASA1 and NF1 co-loss defines a smoking-associated NSCLC subset mutually exclusive with canonical drivers and sensitive to MEK inhibition, demonstrating that dual RasGAP loss can substitute for a canonical oncogenic driver.

Oncogene-negative LUAD in TCGA was enriched for NF1, RIT1, RASA1, SOS1, VAV1, ARHGAP35, ERBB2 amplification, and MET alterations, indicating that hidden pathway-member lesions recur specifically in the negative set.
RIT1 mutations in oncogene-negative LUAD activate MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT signaling and transform cells, providing a rare pathway-member replacement driver.

MAPK and mTOR pathway activity often exceeds what can be explained by known DNA lesions, with KRAS-wild-type tumors still showing high MAPK activation and multiple mTOR states linked only partly to PIK3CA, STK11, AMPK, or AKT features.

Proteogenomic LUAD studies repeatedly nominate pathway-state vulnerabilities downstream of tumor suppressor loss, including PP subtype CDK/glycolysis states, STK11-KEAP1 metabolic-redox programs, and STK11-AMPK-HNF1A-FGL1 mTOR-linked immune evasion.
This law was logically inferred through induction from studies showing that negative tumors repeatedly converge on the same downstream pathways even when the causal lesions differ.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already known that suppressor loss and rare pathway-member mutations can activate RAS/MAPK or PI3K signaling in LUAD and NSCLC. Functional proof exists for several such mechanisms.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel point is the equivalence framing: these lesions are treated not as miscellaneous exceptions but as a coherent replacement module that restores the same control function usually provided by canonical RTK/RAS/RAF drivers.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The component evidence is established, but the theory that these lesions form a pathway-equivalence class that should replace genotype-centric negative labeling is a new synthesis.
references: Sanchez-Vega et al. pan-cancer pathway analyses [pathway convergence exists but not framed as LUAD driver-replacement theory]
Lu et al. / oncogene-negative RAS-PI3K LUAD studies [strong mechanistic precursors for pathway-equivalence logic]
Nilsson et al. RASA1/NF1 co-loss in NSCLC [specific suppressor-loss replacement module]

theory_statements.statement_name: Regulatory-equivalence law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When coding oncogene activation is absent, LUAD can restore oncogenic output through cis-regulatory or 3D-genome rewiring that elevates oncogene or pathway-node expression; in such tumors, the functional driver is the regulatory architecture rather than a coding mutation.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to no-known-actionable-driver or non-CAGA LUAD studied with WGS, enhancer mapping, methylation profiling, Hi-C, long-read sequencing, or integrated cis-structural analysis. Best supported in tumors with outlier expression linked to structural variants or altered enhancer states.
special_cases: Regulatory events can activate canonical oncogenes such as ERBB2 or MYC without requiring coding mutation or high-level gene-body amplification.
Some regulatory events are silencing rather than activating, as in MAML2-associated suppressive programs, but still function as driver-replacement mechanisms by rewiring lineage or signaling output.
Standard short-read clinical testing may miss many of these events.

supporting_evidence.text: Non-CAGA LUAD can overexpress ERBB2 through super-enhancer formation plus structural rearrangement, including an inversion near HNF1B; CRISPR modeling increased HER2 expression and PDXs showed sensitivity to HER2-directed agents.

MYC-LASE is a lung adenocarcinoma-specific super-enhancer amplicon that physically contacts the MYC promoter; CRISPRi or deletion of the e3 constituent enhancer lowers MYC expression and suppresses anchorage-independent and clonogenic growth.

MAML2-centered non-CAGA LUAD shows ablated H3K27ac enhancer activity, increased methylation, and reduced MAML2 expression linked to Notch/Wnt-related downstream programs, indicating a regulatory rather than coding driver architecture.

CESAM identifies hidden cis-regulatory drivers via breakpoint-expression coupling and validates enhancer-hijacking logic in cancer, providing a mechanistic framework transferable to driver-negative LUAD even when examples in the study are outside LUAD.

Review-level evidence on structural variation and the 3D genome argues that tissue-specific enhancer context, ecDNA, and neo-TAD logic are plausible hidden-driver mechanisms when canonical coding lesions are absent.

This law was logically inferred through abduction from recurring observations that outlier oncogene expression and tumor fitness can be causally linked to enhancer or structural architecture in tumors without coding activation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Enhancer hijacking, super-enhancer amplification, and cis-regulatory activation are already recognized in cancer and are specifically documented for ERBB2 and MYC in LUAD-related studies.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The new contribution is the equivalence principle: regulatory architecture is treated as a functionally interchangeable substitute for coding oncogene activation within driver-negative LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a higher-level synthesis built from existing regulatory-driver observations, but the explicit replacement logic in the driver-negative setting is novel.
references: Sun et al. ERBB2 super-enhancer inversion in non-CAGA LUAD [direct precursor for regulatory-driver logic]
Hnisz-derived super-enhancer amplification literature including MYC-LASE [noncoding oncogene activation in LUAD]
CESAM enhancer-hijacking framework [general hidden cis-driver discovery mechanism]

theory_statements.statement_name: Ecosystem-equivalence law
theory_statements.theory_statement: A subset of driver-negative LUAD sustains malignant progression through ecosystem modules in which stromal, vascular, immune, or lineage-transition programs functionally replace a dominant tumor-cell oncogene by stabilizing survival, invasion, or immune escape.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to transcriptomic, proteogenomic, single-cell, and spatially resolved studies of LUAD and never-smoker LUAD where canonical drivers are absent, sparse, or non-explanatory relative to phenotype. Strongest for cohorts explicitly showing CAF, endothelial, fibroblast, or immune-state dominance.
special_cases: These ecosystem modules can coexist with weak or secondary tumor-intrinsic genomic lesions.
Some modules are progression-specific and may be strongest in early lesions or subsolid nodules rather than established advanced carcinomas.
Immune-hot and immune-cold ecosystem modules can both occur in driver-poor disease, but imply opposite therapeutic logic.

supporting_evidence.text: Angiogenesis-high EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD is enriched for angiogenic ligands, TGF-beta/cell-migration signaling, CAFs, immune exclusion, and poor prognosis, indicating a stromal-vascular ecosystem state rather than a hidden single mutation.

Immune-high EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD is defined by TCR signaling, TLS-like features, B/T-cell infiltration, and checkpoint expression, showing that an ecosystem state can dominate prognosis and candidate therapy selection.
FeSig-high EGFR-WT LUAD links ferroptosis resistance to fibroblast-rich microenvironments, stronger fibroblast-tumor communication, IGF-related signaling, and in vivo sensitivity to 6-mercaptopurine.
THBS2-positive CAFs and COL6A3-positive fibroblast-associated states were identified as progression-linked stromal modules in LUAD-related disease, supporting fibroblast-dominant ecosystem control.
The chaotic never-smoker LUAD subtype is characterized by EMT, CAF enrichment, mixed-lineage features, and lack of enriched canonical drivers, supporting an ecosystem-plus-lineage replacement mode.

Early LUAD progression in never-smokers and in SSN-associated disease was linked to angiogenesis, matrix CAF programs, ER stress, and cholesterol/PCSK9 biology, indicating that progression can be sustained by ecosystem and proteostasis modules without requiring a newly acquired canonical oncogene.

This law was logically inferred through induction from repeated observations that microenvironmental states predict prognosis and therapeutic hypotheses even when canonical driver assignment is absent or non-explanatory.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Immune, stromal, angiogenic, and fibroblast-associated states are already recognized as important in LUAD and never-smoker LUAD. Several papers propose these as clinically meaningful subtypes.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the claim that these ecosystem states are not merely modifiers but can act as oncogenic-control replacements in a subset of driver-negative LUAD, functionally taking over the role usually played by a dominant tumor-cell driver.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The underlying subtype observations exist, but the ecosystem-equivalence framing is a new mechanistic synthesis tailored to driver-negative LUAD.
references: Kim et al. EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD subtypes [immune, angiogenic, and metabolic ecosystem states]
Ferroptosis/fibroblast EGFR-WT LUAD work [fibroblast-linked therapeutic state]
Spatial and single-cell LUAD progression studies [ecosystem remodeling without new drivers]

new_predictions_likely: If broad-negative LUADs are grouped by replacement layer rather than by histology alone, pathway-equivalence tumors will be enriched for pERK/pAKT or equivalent signaling activity, regulatory-equivalence tumors for enhancer/SV-linked outlier expression, and ecosystem-equivalence tumors for strong stromal or immune-state signatures.

If driver-negative LUAD organoids are grown with and without matched stromal or immune components, ecosystem-equivalence tumors will lose a larger fraction of their fitness in co-culture perturbation experiments than pathway-equivalence tumors.

If long-read sequencing and enhancer profiling are added to broad-negative tumors with outlier ERBB2, MYC, or MAML2-axis expression, a high fraction will show structural or cis-regulatory abnormalities rather than unexplained RNA overexpression.

If suppressor-loss pathway-equivalence tumors are treated by signaling-node combinations (for example SHP2 plus AKT or MEK plus PI3K) rather than monotherapy, synthetic efficacy will be greater than in canonical single-oncogene-driven tumors.
new_predictions_unknown: If ecosystem-equivalence states truly replace dominant oncogenes in some tumors, removing specific stromal or immune niches in vivo will produce regressions comparable to direct kinase inhibition in canonical driver-positive LUAD.

If regulatory-equivalence is common, a substantial fraction of broad-negative LUAD will prove to be addicted to enhancer transcriptional machinery, making BET/CDK7/Mediator-axis blockade unusually effective in selected negative tumors.

If replacement layers are evolutionarily ordered, some tumors will transition from regulatory-equivalence or pathway-equivalence to ecosystem-equivalence during progression or therapy pressure, and longitudinal sampling will show corresponding shifts in dependency.

If lineage-plasticity is itself a replacement layer, forced restoration of lineage-fidelity regulators such as NKX2-1- or alveolar-state programs will reduce fitness in selected driver-negative subtypes even without directly targeting proliferation pathways.
negative_experiments: Profile a broad-negative cohort for phosphoproteomics, enhancer architecture, and spatial TME states. If most tumors cannot be assigned to any replacement layer or show no coherence within layers, the theory is weakened.

Test whether regulatory-equivalence tumors actually depend on the nominated regulatory element or architecture. If enhancer/SV-linked outlier expression is not functionally required, the regulatory-equivalence law fails.
