Disrupt stromal or immune modules in ecosystem-dominant models. If tumor fitness is unchanged, the claim that ecosystem modules can replace missing dominant oncogenes is undermined.

Directly compare therapy response between pathway-equivalence and canonical oncogene-positive models under matched pathway blockade. If response logic is not convergent, the equivalence framing may be too strong.

unaccounted_for.text: Some papers describing driver-negative or broad-negative LUAD remain purely descriptive and do not establish whether the observed subtype states are causal replacements or merely correlates.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some apparent negatives are later rescued by hidden canonical lesions, which could mean that replacement-layer biology is overrepresented only because true false negatives were not fully removed.

special_cases: Pathway-equivalence is especially plausible in smoking-associated dual RasGAP-loss disease.
Regulatory-equivalence is especially plausible in non-CAGA LUAD with outlier oncogene expression but absent coding drivers.
Ecosystem-equivalence is especially plausible in angiogenesis-high, immune-high, fibroblast-rich, or early progression SSN contexts.
Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma often exits this framework because many cases are better explained by hidden canonical ligand or kinase fusions.

references: Lu et al. oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD studies [supports pathway-equivalence arm only]
Sun et al. ERBB2 super-enhancer inversion in non-CAGA LUAD [supports regulatory-equivalence arm only]
Kim et al. EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD subtypes [supports ecosystem-equivalence arm only]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: There are existing mechanistic papers for each replacement layer separately: suppressor-loss signaling, regulatory enhancer hijacking, and stromal/immune ecosystem states. However, they are not unified as alternative layers that restore oncogenic control when canonical drivers are absent.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The theory's novelty is the replacement-layer concept itself: driver-negative LUAD is explained by where oncogenic control has been reconstituted—signaling, regulation, or ecosystem—rather than by whether a canonical oncogene is present.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This layered replacement model is not explicitly present in the supplied literature and therefore represents a new integrative theory built from existing component mechanisms.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1874.name: Convergent State Attractor Theory of True Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1874.description: A substantial fraction of apparently driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not unified by one missing gene class but by convergence onto a limited set of stable malignant states. In these tumors, multiple upstream routes—including rare coding lesions, tumor-suppressor co-loss, noncoding regulatory rearrangements, epigenetic silencing, or no currently detectable lesion—can converge on recurrent downstream attractors: a RAS/PI3K phospho-signaling attractor, a lineage-plasticity attractor, a metabolic persistence attractor, and an ecosystem-coupled angiogenic/immune/CAF attractor. Within these attractors, signaling state and epithelial–stromal organization become more predictive of prognosis and therapeutic vulnerability than the initiating lesion itself. This theory is intended for the residual population remaining after broad genomic driver testing, while explicitly acknowledging that many 'negative' tumors are later rescued by deeper sequencing.
theory-1874.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Convergent State Attractor Theory of True Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A substantial fraction of apparently driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not unified by one missing gene class but by convergence onto a limited set of stable malignant states. In these tumors, multiple upstream routes—including rare coding lesions, tumor-suppressor co-loss, noncoding regulatory rearrangements, epigenetic silencing, or no currently detectable lesion—can converge on recurrent downstream attractors: a RAS/PI3K phospho-signaling attractor, a lineage-plasticity attractor, a metabolic persistence attractor, and an ecosystem-coupled angiogenic/immune/CAF attractor. Within these attractors, signaling state and epithelial–stromal organization become more predictive of prognosis and therapeutic vulnerability than the initiating lesion itself. This theory is intended for the residual population remaining after broad genomic driver testing, while explicitly acknowledging that many 'negative' tumors are later rescued by deeper sequencing.

theory_statements.statement_name: Convergent phospho-state law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In LUAD that lacks an obvious canonical actionable oncogene, distinct upstream lesions or even currently unresolved lesions can converge onto recurring active phospho-signaling states—especially MAPK and PI3K/mTOR—and these downstream states can be more predictive of tumor behavior and therapeutic vulnerability than genotype labels alone.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma analyzed with phosphoproteomics, RPPA, or strong pathway-state inference, especially oncogene-negative/no-known-actionable-driver cohorts, EGFR-wild-type tumors with unexplained signaling, and tumor-suppressor-driven LUAD models. It does not claim that all driver-negative LUAD is phospho-state driven, nor that phospho-state supersedes genotype in canonical kinase-addicted tumors where the genomic driver is already sufficient and therapeutically dominant.
special_cases: Canonical driver-positive tumors can share the same phospho-state as driver-negative tumors; this law concerns convergence, not exclusivity.
Some pathway-active tumors are later reclassified by deeper sequencing as harboring rare canonical drivers, so the law is strongest for high-confidence residual negatives after broad testing.
Expression-only cohorts support the law weakly; direct phosphoproteomic or RPPA evidence is the strongest support.
MAPK and mTOR states need not be coupled in every tumor; some tumors show one dominant active axis.

supporting_evidence.text: In East Asian never-smoker LUAD, coordinated phosphoproteomics showed EGFR-pY1197 correlated with MAP2K2-pT394 and MAPK3-pT198/pT202, and tumors ranked by MAPK-signaling score included EGFR-wild-type tumors with high MAPK activity and EGFR-mutant/TP53-mutant tumors with low MAPK activity, demonstrating that pathway activity is not determined by genotype alone.

RPPA analysis in TCGA-like LUAD showed substantial phospho-MAPK activity in KRAS-wild-type tumors and concluded that multiple mechanisms and additional undetected alterations likely exist beyond known mutations.

RPPA also showed substantial mTOR pathway activation, with distinct mTOR states linked to PIK3CA mutation, STK11/AMPK loss, or basal-low state, indicating that pathway activity can be organized into recurrent states only partly explained by known genomic lesions.

Mouse and human oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD defined by NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss showed direct pERK and pAKT positivity, RNA-signature evidence of RAS and PI3K activation, and pathway-linked therapeutic response to SHP2 and AKT inhibition, establishing that a state-defined subtype can exist without canonical oncogene activation.

Combinatorial NF1/RASA1/PTEN inactivation was shown to create a potent oncogene-negative LUAD state with MAPK/PI3K signaling consequences, supporting the idea that different genomic routes can converge on the same downstream signaling regime.

In broad proteogenomic LUAD studies, tumors lacking targetable oncogene mutations were repeatedly interpreted through proteome/phosphoproteome-defined pathway states rather than through one recurrent hidden gene, including PP-like proliferative/CDK states, EGFR-wild-type MAPK states, and kinase-substrate enrichment signatures.

The APOLLO/CPTAC-style LUAD expression-subtype framework identified PP tumors as lacking targetable oncogene mutations in the majority of cases while still showing strong phosphoproteomic evidence of CDK and MAP2K7 activity, illustrating that actionable pathway state can substitute for canonical oncogene dependence.

The proliferation-high and metabolism-high proteogenomic subgroups in EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD were defined primarily by pathway-state activation rather than one hidden lesion, again indicating convergence on recurrent signaling-output phenotypes.

Proteogenomic work in non-smoking East Asian LUAD argued that constitutive MAPK signaling can exist even in EGFR-wild-type tumors and may be only partly accounted for by KRAS mutation status, directly motivating a pathway-state interpretation of some apparently driver-negative tumors.

This law was induced by combining the direct phosphoproteomic evidence of genotype–state mismatch with the oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K mouse-human subtype and the PP/NSLA proteogenomic subtype data into a single convergence principle.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Proteogenomic LUAD studies already show that MAPK and PI3K/mTOR activity can diverge from simple mutation labels, and oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD has been described as a pathway-defined subtype. State-versus-genotype mismatch is therefore not new by itself.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel component is the explicit claim that these recurrent phospho-signaling states function as attractors organizing true residual driver-negative LUAD across multiple upstream lesion classes and unresolved cases, making downstream state a unifying explanatory unit rather than a secondary correlate.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory integrates several related existing observations into a stronger organizing law about residual driver-negative disease. Because the attractor framing is new but the component findings are established, the best classification is somewhat-related-to-existing.
references: Huang (2021) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [closest existing pathway-defined oncogene-negative subtype, Onc-negative^RAS/PI3K^]
Gillette/CPTAC-type LUAD proteogenomic studies (2020–2023) [existing evidence for genotype–phospho-state mismatch and phosphoproteomic vulnerabilities in LUAD]
East Asian non-smoking LUAD proteogenomics (2024) [existing evidence that MAPK activity can be high in EGFR-wild-type tumors]
Huang (1948)/Kauffman-style attractor concepts [general systems-biology attractor precedent, not LUAD-specific]

theory_statements.statement_name: Lineage-ecosystem coupling law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In driver-poor LUAD, unstable epithelial lineage states and local ecosystem programs co-stabilize one another: mesenchymal, alveolar-intermediate, mucinous/gastric, or stem-like epithelial states preferentially recruit CAF-, angiogenic-, or immune architectures, and those stromal/ecosystem states in turn preserve the malignant epithelial state even when no dominant canonical oncogene is evident.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to transcriptomic, single-cell, spatial, and proteogenomic studies of driver-poor or non-oncogene-dominated LUAD, especially never-smoker LUAD, early progression states, histologic transition states, and subtypes defined by EMT/CAF/angiogenesis/immune programs. It is less applicable to tumors that are fully explained by a single strong kinase driver with minimal state plasticity.
special_cases: Some lineage states coexist with canonical drivers such as KRAS mutation or NKX2-1 loss; the law addresses stabilization of state, not absence of any genomic lesion.
Immune-high states may be favorable rather than aggressive; the coupling claim concerns co-stabilization, not uniformly poor prognosis.
Certain ecosystem programs are inferred from bulk deconvolution and need spatial or single-cell confirmation for strongest interpretation.
The law is strongest where epithelial-state data and stromal/ecosystem data are measured together.

supporting_evidence.text: The 'chaotic' transcriptomic subtype of never-smoker LUAD had no significant enrichment of known driver mutations or fusions, yet showed strong EMT/cell-movement programs, high CDH2 and TWIST1, mixed lineage fidelity, increased CAF proportions, advanced stage, and poor prognosis.

The 60-gene NS-LUAD signature recapitulated the transcriptomic subtypes including the driver-poor chaotic class, supporting that lineage/plasticity states are coherent, clinically meaningful structures even when driver information is absent.

The piano subtype in never-smoker lung cancer had low SCNA burden, low TMB, long telomeres, high intratumor heterogeneity, high development score, and basal-cell/stem-like features; the authors proposed quiescence exit and lineage plasticity, potentially involving ARID1A/NOTCH1, as a mechanism for tumors lacking clear canonical drivers.

KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate cells (KACs) were positioned between alveolar cells and tumor cells by scRNA-seq and spatial analysis, were enriched in tumor-adjacent and premalignant regions, associated with poor survival, and showed less differentiated, plastic transitional identity; this supports an epithelial-state bridge to malignancy.

The review citation on non-genetic evolution in LUAD explicitly argued that spatial heterogeneity and progression are driven primarily by plastic transcriptional state shifts and TME reprogramming rather than new driver mutations.

Histologic progression from lepidic to solid LUAD was reported to follow a continuum driven primarily by non-genetic reprogramming of cancer-cell identity plus microenvironment remodeling, with no reproducible pattern-specific recurrent genomic drivers explaining the transition.

NKX2-1-negative mucinous/gastric-like LUAD was driven by lineage-state reprogramming in mouse and human studies, where loss of pulmonary fate enabled a gastric differentiation program and altered Foxa1/2 occupancy, showing that lineage circuitry itself can be an organizing driver-like force.

The angiogenesis-high subgroup of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD was defined by CAF-associated features, TGF-beta/cell migration programs, angiogenic ligand expression, immune exclusion, and poor prognosis, indicating a stable epithelial–stromal ecosystem state rather than one hidden mutation.

The immune-high subgroup in the same cohort was marked by T/B-cell and TLS features, cytokine/chemokine signaling, and high checkpoint expression, demonstrating that ecosystem organization is itself a subtype-defining axis in driver-poor disease.

THBS2-positive CAFs were highlighted as a progression-linked stromal biomarker associated with suppressed antitumor immunity and poor response, reinforcing that stromal state can act as a major organizing component of LUAD behavior.

FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD linked fibroblast-rich microenvironment, ferroptosis resistance, and poor prognosis, suggesting that fibroblast ecosystem state and epithelial metabolic state are coupled rather than independent.

The CLDN2-positive AT2/COL6A3-positive fibroblast subtype in multiple primary lung cancers showed explicit epithelial–fibroblast signaling and aggregation, providing an additional lung adenocarcinoma-related example of co-organized epithelial and stromal identity.

This law was inferred by integrating lineage-plasticity studies (chaotic, piano, KAC, NKX2-1-low, histologic progression) with ecosystem-state studies (angiogenesis-high, immune-high, THBS2+ CAF, FeSig-high), yielding a coupled-state rather than parallel-feature interpretation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Lineage plasticity, non-genetic progression, CAF biology, angiogenic ecosystems, and immune-state subtypes are all established in LUAD and related lung cancers. Several of the cited studies already argue for state-based rather than mutation-based explanations in specific settings.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel component is the explicit coupling law: epithelial plasticity and stromal/ecosystem architecture are proposed to be mutually stabilizing attractor components in residual driver-negative LUAD, rather than separate descriptive layers.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a genuinely integrative reinterpretation across multiple strands of existing work. Because no provided paper states the coupling principle this explicitly for driver-negative LUAD, but many provide its ingredients, the classification is new.
references: Marjanovic (2023/2024) Nongenetic evolution drives lung adenocarcinoma spatial heterogeneity and progression [closest direct precedent for non-genetic progression]
KAC/alveolar intermediate cell atlas studies [closest precedent for transitional lineage-state model]
Proteogenomic characterization of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD [closest precedent for immune/angiogenic/metabolic ecosystem subtypes]
Nkx2-1 lineage plasticity studies in mucinous LUAD [closest precedent for lineage-circuit-driven subtype identity]

theory_statements.statement_name: Metabolic persistence law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When canonical oncogene addiction is weak, absent, or therapeutically unresolved, LUAD persistence increasingly depends on stress-adaptive metabolic programs—glycolysis, cholesterol handling, oxidative phosphorylation, ferroptosis resistance, and ER-stress buffering—and these metabolic programs are conditioned jointly by epithelial lineage state and by stromal ecosystem context.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to driver-negative, driver-poor, EGFR-wild-type, never-smoker, and early-stage/subsolid LUAD contexts in which metabolic or stress-response programs were identified by transcriptomics, proteomics, phosphoproteomics, or functional perturbation. It is not intended to claim that metabolism replaces oncogenic signaling in every LUAD, only that it becomes preferentially organizing in residual negative disease.
special_cases: Different metabolic states imply different interventions; glycolysis-high, OXPHOS-high, ferroptosis-resistant, and ER-stress-buffered tumors are not therapeutically equivalent.
Some metabolic programs are strongest in early lesions or pre-invasive disease and may not dominate advanced LUAD.
Certain metabolic states coexist with canonical drivers, especially STK11/KEAP1- or KRAS-associated backgrounds.
PCSK9/cholesterol findings in early lesions are mechanistically important but therapeutically paradoxical in parts of the dataset.

supporting_evidence.text: Driver gene-negative LUAD showed strong glycolysis enrichment by bulk and single-cell analysis, with MIF-high AT2 cells having higher glycolysis scores and a proposed MIF–HIF-1alpha positive feedback loop; MIF overexpression and HIF-1alpha stabilization increased glucose uptake, lactate, LDH activity, and proliferation, while ISO-1 reversed these effects.

The metabolism-high subgroup of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD showed enrichment for oxidative phosphorylation, lipid metabolism, carbon metabolism, ACAT1, ACACA, and CERS4, indicating a coherent metabolic-dependency subtype independent of one hidden driver gene.

FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD linked fibroblast-rich ecosystem state to ferroptosis resistance and identified DDIT4/NOX4/GCLC as a ferroptosis-inhibitory signature; knockdown increased RSL3-induced ferroptosis and 6-mercaptopurine showed preclinical activity in vivo.

Early LUAD/subsolid-nodule proteogenomics identified cholesterol metabolism as a preneoplastic hallmark in AIS and used PCSK9 perturbation to show functional impact on transformation and organoid growth, implicating cholesterol handling in tumor initiation.

The same SSN studies identified sustained ER stress and unfolded protein response as a hallmark of progression from AIS to IAC, with higher PERK, ATF4, and phospho-eIF2S1 in rapidly progressing lesions and preclinical suppression of growth by PDI inhibition or Pdia3 knockdown.

FGFR3 S408 phosphosite activation in early lesions activated PI3K/AKT/mTOR without change in total FGFR3 abundance, demonstrating that metabolic-survival signaling can be controlled at the phosphosite level rather than by classic genomic activation.

Broad proteogenomic LUAD studies repeatedly nominated metabolic rewiring as the main actionable layer in tumors without clear genomic targets, especially the PP subtype with glycolysis/glutaminolysis programs and the environment/metabolism-high S-I-like states.

The STK11-KEAP1 axis in PP-like LUAD was explicitly linked to metabolic rewiring, oxidative-stress adaptation, and ferroptosis protection/SCD1 dependence, supporting the idea that metabolic persistence becomes central when canonical targetable drivers are absent.

This law was induced by integrating glycolysis-high, OXPHOS-high, ferroptosis-resistant, cholesterol-handling, and ER-stress progression studies into one persistence framework specific to driver-poor disease.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Metabolic rewiring, ferroptosis resistance, cholesterol metabolism, and ER-stress adaptation are established hallmarks of cancer and each has been separately implicated in LUAD subsets. Multiple provided studies already nominate one of these states as therapeutically relevant.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that these disparate findings form a general law of driver-negative LUAD persistence: as canonical oncogene addiction weakens, metabolic and stress-adaptive states become the main organizing substrate of malignancy.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The component observations are established, but their synthesis into a specific persistence principle for residual driver-negative LUAD goes beyond the individual papers. This makes the theory new rather than merely restating one metabolic subtype paper.
references: Driver gene-negative LUAD MIF-HIF-1alpha study [closest direct glycolysis-specific precedent]
FeSig EGFR-wild-type LUAD study [closest ferroptosis/microenvironment precedent]
SSN proteogenomic progression studies [closest cholesterol and ER-stress progression precedent]
General Warburg/ferroptosis/UPR literature [broad background but not this driver-negative synthesis]

new_predictions_likely: If high-confidence residual driver-negative LUADs are jointly profiled by DNA/RNA/phosphoproteomics/spatial transcriptomics, most tumors will cluster more strongly by phospho-state plus stromal architecture than by residual mutational burden alone.

If paired epithelial-only organoids and fibroblast/endothelial co-cultures are generated from the same driver-negative LUAD, angiogenic/CAF-linked tumors will lose part of their state identity in epithelial-only culture and recover it in co-culture.

If driver-negative LUADs are stratified by glycolysis-high versus OXPHOS-high versus ER-stress-high states, each class will show distinct ex vivo drug sensitivity patterns that are more reproducible than sensitivity stratified only by broad-negative genomic status.

If residual driver-negative early lesions are followed longitudinally, increases in ER-stress and stromal-coupling markers will precede invasive progression more often than acquisition of a new canonical RTK/RAS/RAF driver.
new_predictions_unknown: If attractor states are real dynamical basins, then CRISPR perturbation of different upstream lesions in unrelated driver-negative models should collapse onto the same single-cell manifold if and only if they belong to the same phospho-lineage-ecosystem attractor class.

If lineage-ecosystem coupling is obligatory in a subset of true negatives, some tumors will be poorly transplantable as epithelial-only xenografts but robustly sustained in alveolar niche, vascularized, or humanized stromal systems.

If metabolic persistence is a defining principle of residual negatives, plasma metabolite/secretome profiles may classify driver-negative LUAD subtypes more accurately than ctDNA mutation panels in recurrence monitoring.
If recurrence preserves attractor identity better than genotype, post-therapy relapses will often keep the same phospho-lineage-ecosystem state despite acquiring new genomic lesions.
negative_experiments: Test whether phospho-state plus spatial ecosystem variables outperform genotype in predicting ex vivo drug response among high-confidence residual driver-negative LUAD. If genotype consistently dominates, the attractor framing is weakened.
Disrupt CAF/endothelial support in ecosystem-dominant driver-negative models. If epithelial state and tumor fitness remain unchanged, the lineage-ecosystem coupling law is undermined.
Normalize glycolysis, ferroptosis resistance, or ER-stress buffering in metabolic-state negatives. If tumors remain viable without compensatory rewiring, metabolic persistence is not central.

Perform longitudinal multi-region single-cell profiling of residual negative lesions. If progression is usually explained by later emergence of canonical actionable drivers rather than state transitions, the theory overstates true attractor-driven disease.

unaccounted_for.text: Many subtype papers provide only correlative evidence; immune-high, angiogenesis-high, and some ecosystem states are not functionally proven to be causal in tumor maintenance.

Several broad-negative cohorts may still contain technical false negatives or incompletely screened canonical drivers, making it difficult to cleanly isolate the true residual state-driven population.
Some theories in the evidence point toward rare gene discovery rather than state convergence, especially RAF-family mutants, RIT1, NTRK1, NRG1 fusions, MET exon 14 skipping, and KIF5B-RET.

conflicting_evidence.text: Multiple studies show that many apparently driver-negative tumors are later rescued by rare canonical or near-canonical genomic lesions after deeper testing, arguing that the true state-dominant population may be much smaller than initial panel-negative estimates.

Some strong pathway-state evidence comes from canonical driver-positive tumors rather than true negatives, so transfer of the same organizing logic to residual driver-negative disease remains partly inferential.

special_cases: Onc-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD is a special case where the state-attractor idea already has unusually strong causal support from genetics, pathway activation, and drug response.
Subsolid-nodule/AIS–MIA progression may represent a pre-invasive attractor landscape distinct from advanced metastatic residual-negative LUAD.
Mucinous/NKX2-1-low tumors may combine lineage-attractor logic with hidden fusion biology rather than fitting a pure state-only mechanism.

Never-smoker cohorts may be especially enriched for lineage/ecosystem attractors, whereas smoking-associated negatives may be more enriched for tumor-suppressor co-loss and genomic-instability attractors.

references: Huang (2021) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [closest existing pathway-state subtype, not a whole attractor theory]
Marjanovic (2023/2024) Nongenetic evolution drives lung adenocarcinoma spatial heterogeneity and progression [closest lineage-state/non-genetic progression background]
East Asian and CPTAC/APOLLO proteogenomic LUAD studies (2020–2024) [closest phospho-state and subtype background]
KAC/alveolar intermediate cell atlas studies (2023–2024) [closest lineage-transition background for early LUAD]
General systems-biology attractor theory from Kauffman/Huang [conceptual precedent, not specific to driver-negative LUAD]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work already supports pathway-state mismatch with genotype, non-genetic progression, lineage plasticity, stromal ecosystem subtypes, and metabolic persistence in separate LUAD contexts. One strong example, oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K LUAD, already functions as a pathway-defined disease model.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel step is unifying these findings into one theory that true residual driver-negative LUAD is organized by a limited number of stable phospho-lineage-ecosystem-metabolic attractors that can be entered through multiple lesion classes or non-genetic reprogramming.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No provided paper offers this full attractor-level synthesis for residual driver-negative LUAD. The theory is therefore new, though it is built directly on multiple related and partially overlapping existing frameworks.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1875.name: Hidden Lesion Hierarchy Theory of Apparently Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1875.description: Most clinically 'driver-negative' LUAD is not biologically driverless but reflects a hierarchy of hidden lesion classes missed by routine testing. The hierarchy is ordered by detectability: first, rare canonical coding drivers and overlooked domain variants; second, hidden fusions and exon-skipping events; third, focal amplifications and complex structural variants; fourth, noncoding enhancer-hijacking or super-enhancer mechanisms; and finally, a smaller residual class dominated by tumor-suppressor co-loss or pathway-state biology. This theory explains why broader DNA/RNA/structural/proteogenomic assays steadily shrink the negative pool and why residual negatives are enriched for noncoding regulation, chromosomal instability, and state-based dependencies.
theory-1875.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Hidden Lesion Hierarchy Theory of Apparently Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Most clinically 'driver-negative' LUAD is not biologically driverless but reflects a hierarchy of hidden lesion classes missed by routine testing. The hierarchy is ordered by detectability: first, rare canonical coding drivers and overlooked domain variants; second, hidden fusions and exon-skipping events; third, focal amplifications and complex structural variants; fourth, noncoding enhancer-hijacking or super-enhancer mechanisms; and finally, a smaller residual class dominated by tumor-suppressor co-loss or pathway-state biology. This theory explains why broader DNA/RNA/structural/proteogenomic assays steadily shrink the negative pool and why residual negatives are enriched for noncoding regulation, chromosomal instability, and state-based dependencies.

theory_statements.statement_name: Assay-depth rescue law
theory_statements.theory_statement: As molecular interrogation expands from hotspot panels to broad DNA/RNA/structural/noncoding analyses, the size of the 'driver-negative' LUAD pool monotonically decreases because many negatives are actually hidden-driver positives in less commonly assayed lesion classes.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to clinically or research-defined driver-negative LUAD cohorts where sequential testing depth can be compared, including EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative, RTK/RAS/RAF-negative, pan-negative mucinous LUAD, and never-smoker cohorts. It is most accurate in cohorts initially defined by limited panels and less dramatic in cohorts already assayed by WGS plus RNA-seq.
special_cases: The rescue rate depends strongly on the initial panel; broad WGS/RNA-defined negatives will show smaller rescue fractions than hotspot-panel negatives.
Some cohorts confound true biological negativity with low purity or technical false negatives.
Not every residual case is rescued even after WGS/RNA-seq; the law concerns majority trend, not completeness.

supporting_evidence.text: Comprehensive genomic profiling of EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative LUAD reclassified many apparently triple-negative tumors by finding additional actionable mutations, fusions, amplifications, and deletions, with clinical benefit especially for ROS1-rearranged patients.

In never-smoker LUAD, many WES-defined oncogene-negative tumors were later reassigned after deeper or orthogonal testing, including ALK and ROS1 fusions, EGFR/ERBB2/KRAS mutations, MET exon 14 skipping, and rare EGFR and AKT1 lesions; low tumor cellularity contributed to false negatives.

Transcriptome sequencing of initial EGFR/KRAS/EML4-ALK-negative LUAD found additional EGFR point mutations, novel kinase fusions, and MET exon 14 skipping, demonstrating that initial negativity was often incomplete.

In S4-low and other broad-negative LADC cohorts, many apparently negative tumors were resolved by WGS/RNA-seq as fusion-oncogene or canonical-driver positive, leaving only a small residual set without known major oncogene alterations.

Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD rescued 28 of 85 initially negative cases by identifying KRAS hotspot mutations, amplifications of EGFR/MAPK1/ARAF/SOS1, NF1/RASA1 deletions, and NRG1 fusion/mutation events.

Pan-negative Chinese LUAD cases were later rescued by deeper testing that identified EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 extracellular/transmembrane mutations, ARAF mutation, and CD74-NRG1 fusions.

KRAS-negative/pan-negative invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas were reclassified by RNA-seq/RT-PCR/FISH as harboring NRG1, ERBB4, BRAF, and RET fusions, showing that histology-enriched negative cohorts can be strongly rescueable.
NTRK1 rearrangements were discovered only after broader targeted DNA/FISH testing in clinically negative LUAD, showing that rare but actionable fusion drivers can reside beyond standard panels.
Rare RAF-family mutants (ARAF S214C and RAF1 S257/S259) were found in otherwise driver-negative LUAD after broad WGS/RNA analysis, with functional and therapeutic relevance.

This law is induced directly from the repeated observation across multiple studies that deeper testing converts many negatives into positives, while the residual pool becomes smaller and biologically different.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already well established that broader sequencing can rescue hidden drivers in NSCLC and LUAD, especially rare mutations, fusions, and exon-skipping events. Multiple cited studies explicitly make that point.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the explicit hierarchical ordering of hidden lesion classes by detectability and the claim that assay depth shrinks the negative pool in a predictable sequence, leaving a biologically distinct residual class enriched for noncoding/state-based mechanisms.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The underlying rescue phenomenon is existing; the hierarchical framework that organizes what kinds of lesions are discovered at successive assay depths is a new synthesis. Thus the theory is somewhat-related-to-existing.
references: Janne/Sequist-style multiplex genotyping studies and Foundation-style CGP papers [existing rescue-of-hidden-drivers paradigm]
Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [closest direct precedent for rescue after deeper testing]
NRG1/KIF5B-RET/NTRK1 hidden-fusion discovery papers [existing examples of deeper-assay rescue]

theory_statements.statement_name: Hidden lesion hierarchy law
theory_statements.theory_statement: The residual 'driver-negative' pool is structured by lesion class rather than by one missing pathway: rare coding oncogenes and noncanonical domain mutations are usually found first, hidden fusions and exon-skipping events second, focal amplifications/complex SVs third, noncoding enhancer-hijacking events fourth, and only then does a smaller residual class remain that is dominated by tumor-suppressor co-loss or unresolved pathway state.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LUAD cohorts with sufficiently broad lesion detection across coding mutations, fusions, splice events, CNAs/SVs, and noncoding structural/regulatory mechanisms. It is less reliable in studies that examine only one lesion class.
special_cases: Some lesions can belong to multiple classes, such as ligand fusions or amplifications with enhancer architecture.
The hierarchy is about practical discovery order and residual frequency, not strict biological chronology in tumor evolution.
Certain histologic subsets, especially invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, are enriched for fusion/lipid-ligand classes earlier in the hierarchy.

supporting_evidence.text: Rare coding oncogenes beyond standard hotspots were found in pan-negative LUAD, including EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, EGFR M277E, HER2 non-kinase domain mutations, ARAF S214C, RAF1 regulatory mutants, RIT1 mutations, and rare JAK2-like candidate events.

Fusion and splice-event classes repeatedly explained negative cohorts, including KIF5B-RET, RET fusions, ROS1 fusions, ALK fusions, NTRK1 fusions, NRG1 fusions, BCAR4 fusions, ERBB4/BRAF fusions in IMA, and MET exon 14 skipping.

Copy-number and structural classes explained additional negatives, including focal ERBB2 and MET amplifications in oncogene-negative subsets, recurrent MYC and hTERT amplifications, NKX2-1 and WHSC1L1 amplicons, and complex structural events identified only by WGS.

Noncoding enhancer-hijacking/regulatory mechanisms were demonstrated in clinically non-actionable LUAD, especially ERBB2 super-enhancer formation with inversion-driven overexpression, and more generally MYC-LASE and CESAM-style cis-regulatory discovery show how coding-negative tumors can still be driver-positive at the 3D-genome level.

Residual RTK/RAS/RAF-negative tumors that remained negative even after WGS were enriched for TP53, KEAP1, SMARCA4, STK11 loss, ILF2 promoter mutations, high TMB, and complex SVs, indicating that the deepest residual class is dominated by tumor-suppressor loss, instability, and noncoding/state mechanisms rather than one recurrent oncogene.

The Hallmarks-mapping paper independently argued that many tumors still lack genetically assigned proliferative signaling and proposed epigenetic/RNA deregulation as a missing explanatory category, consistent with the late-hierarchy residual class.

This hierarchy law is logically induced from the ordering of discovery across the cited studies: coding rescue first, then fusions/splice, then copy-number/SV, then regulatory 3D-genome events, leaving a residual state-based class.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Each lesion class already exists in the literature as a hidden-driver mechanism in LUAD or lung cancer more broadly: rare coding variants, fusions, exon skipping, amplifications, and enhancer hijacking are all established individually.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is the ordering of these lesion classes into a practical hierarchy of residual driver-negative explanation, turning many isolated discovery papers into one theory of negative-pool decomposition.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is not an existing named theory, but a synthesis of several known lesion classes into one explanatory hierarchy. The best classification is new.
references: Clinical CGP and hidden fusion discovery studies in NSCLC [existing lesion classes, not the hierarchy]
Pan-cancer enhancer hijacking/CESAM papers [existing noncoding hidden-driver mechanisms]
Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [closest direct evidence for late residual class]

theory_statements.statement_name: Residual negative law
theory_statements.theory_statement: After exhaustive coding, fusion, splice, and structural interrogation, the remaining true driver-negative LUADs are enriched for tumor-suppressor co-loss, chromosomal instability, epigenetic or allele-specific dysregulation, and proteomic pathway-state dependence rather than for undiscovered high-frequency canonical oncogenes.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to high-confidence residual negatives after broad WGS/RNA-seq/structural analysis, not to panel-negative cohorts before rescue. It is most relevant to RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD, piano/chaotic-like never-smoker disease, and non-CAGA cohorts after multi-omic analysis.
special_cases: A minority of residual negatives may still harbor undiscovered rare coding or regulatory drivers not yet detectable.
Some residual cohorts are still contaminated by technical false negatives due to purity or coverage issues.
This law concerns enrichment, not absolute exclusivity; residual negatives can still contain rare private oncogenic events.

supporting_evidence.text: Residual WGS-negative RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD was characterized by high TP53 mutation frequency, KEAP1 and SMARCA4 enrichment, high TMB, complex structural variation, focal deletions in STK11/KEAP1/TP53, and recurrent ILF2 promoter mutations associated with higher expression and SV burden.

Piano tumors in never-smokers rarely harbored known recurrent drivers and instead showed low SCNAs, low TMB, long telomeres, high heterogeneity, and stemness/quiescence-exit features, suggesting a residual class not dominated by canonical oncogenes.

The chaotic NS-LUAD subtype lacked enrichment of known drivers yet showed EMT, mixed-lineage features, and CAF-rich ecology, indicating that some residual negatives are better explained by state programs than by one missing kinase mutation.

CN-independent allele-specific expression in LUAD was associated with hypomethylation and mutations in chromatin modifiers such as SETD2, KDM5C, and KMT2B, supporting an epigenomic-dysfunction layer in residual unexplained tumors.

The hallmarks study proposed epigenetic and RNA deregulation as a missing explanatory hallmark for tumors not accounted for by canonical driver/hallmark mapping, explicitly nominating recurrent alterations in RBM10, U2AF1, ARID1A, SETD2, BRD3, and SMARCA4.

Non-CAGA LUAD was explained by MAML2 enhancer loss and hypermethylation with broad transcriptomic consequences, showing that some residual negatives are hidden regulatory/epigenetic diseases rather than unrecognized hotspot-mutation diseases.

Oncogene-negative LUAD models and reviews repeatedly highlighted tumor-suppressor co-loss (NF1/RASA1/PTEN, RASA1/NF1, STK11/KEAP1, CDKN2A/RB1-related states) as a plausible central mechanism in residual negative disease.

This law is induced by noting that once broad lesion rescue is exhausted, the recurring explanatory motifs are instability, epigenetic dysregulation, tumor-suppressor loss, and pathway-state dependence, not one dominant new oncogene family.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Residual negative cohorts enriched for TP53, KEAP1, SMARCA4, STK11, chromatin regulators, and pathway-state biology are already described in several studies, and epigenetic/RNA deregulation as a missing explanation has been proposed.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that these residual features are not miscellaneous leftovers but define the terminal stage of hidden-driver decomposition after coding/fusion/structural rescue, explaining why exhaustive sequencing increasingly yields state-based and suppressor-loss models rather than a new canonical oncogene set.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The ingredients are present in existing studies, but the framing of these residual features as the endpoint of a lesion hierarchy is new. This supports a somewhat-related-to-existing classification.
references: Whole-genome characterization of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD [closest direct residual-negative dataset]
Hallmarks mapping of LUAD and epigenetic/RNA deregulation proposal [closest conceptual precedent for residual unexplained class]
TRACERx genomic–transcriptomic evolution/ASE papers [closest precedent for epigenetic residual mechanism]

new_predictions_likely: If a clinically panel-negative LUAD cohort is re-profiled sequentially by broad DNA panel, RNA fusion/splice detection, WGS/SV calling, and noncoding enhancer mapping, the rescued cases will appear in the predicted order: rare coding lesions first, then fusions/splice events, then CNA/SV events, then regulatory events.

If residual negatives after WGS/RNA-seq are compared with panel-negative but not deeply profiled tumors, the residual group will show higher rates of tumor-suppressor loss, chromatin dysregulation, complex SVs, and pathway-state dependence and lower rates of rescued kinase fusions.
If invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma negatives are analyzed separately, their rescue fraction by RNA fusion discovery will exceed that of unselected broad-negative LUAD.
If low-purity negative tumors are re-biopsied or sequenced at higher depth, a meaningful fraction will move upward in the hierarchy from 'residual negative' into coding/fusion/CNA classes.
new_predictions_unknown: If the hierarchy is biologically real and not only technical, each successive residual tier will have a distinct evolutionary architecture—coding-rescued tumors will be trunk-driver dominated, whereas final residuals will show more parallel evolution, ASE, and epigenetic diversity.

If enhancer-hijacking is a major late-tier mechanism in residual negatives, prospective H3K27ac/Hi-C/long-read profiling of high-confidence residual-negative LUAD could reveal a clinically actionable rate of regulatory oncogene activation comparable to rare fusion frequencies.

If the final residual class is truly suppressor/state dominated, ctDNA mutation assays may systematically underperform compared with phosphoproteomics or expression-state assays in therapy selection for that tier.

If the lesion hierarchy reflects therapy evolution as well as diagnosis, resistant recurrences after targeted therapy may 'descend' the hierarchy, shifting from canonical oncogene dependence toward suppressor-loss/regulatory/state-based disease.
negative_experiments: Prospectively apply full multi-layer profiling to a broad panel-negative LUAD cohort. If the negative pool does not shrink materially with increasing assay depth, the theory is weakened.

Test whether residual negatives after broad WGS/RNA are actually enriched for suppressor-loss/epigenetic/state features. If they instead reveal many undiscovered recurrent canonical oncogenes, the terminal-law component is wrong.

Measure whether rescue events appear in the predicted hierarchy order across cohorts. If noncoding enhancer events are discovered as frequently and as early as coding lesions in standard negatives, the hierarchy needs revision.
Control rigorously for purity and coverage. If most residual-negative explanations disappear after technical correction alone, the hierarchy overstates biological residual disease.

unaccounted_for.text: Some studies define negative status very narrowly, such as EGFR-only or EGFR/ALK-only exclusion, making them hard to place cleanly within a universal hierarchy.

A few candidate mechanisms remain weakly supported and may not fit stably into the hierarchy, such as JAK2 V617F, some outlier-expression events without functional validation, and broad immune-only prognostic signatures.

conflicting_evidence.text: The Cancer5000 and pan-cancer saturation papers argue that many intermediate-frequency coding cancer genes remain undiscovered, which leaves open the possibility that residual negatives still hide a substantial coding-driver component rather than being mostly suppressor/state driven.
Some broad-negative cohorts remain unresolved even after sizable sequencing efforts, and the papers do not always show that state/suppressor explanations are sufficient for every residual case.

special_cases: Invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma is a special case with unusually high enrichment for hidden ligand and kinase fusions.
Never-smoker panel-negative LUAD is a special case where low purity and rare fusion events make technical and biological explanations especially intertwined.
RTK/RAS/RAF-negative cohorts after WGS are the best substrate for studying the final residual tier because they have already traversed much of the hierarchy.

references: Ross/Foundation-style comprehensive genomic profiling papers [existing concept that deeper testing rescues hidden drivers]
Whole-genome characterization of LUAD lacking RTK/RAS/RAF alterations [closest precedent for residual-negative decomposition]
Pan-cancer enhancer hijacking/CESAM papers [existing noncoding hidden-driver precedent]
Hallmarks mapping of LUAD [existing argument for unresolved epigenetic/RNA hallmarks]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Hidden-driver rescue by broader testing is already established, and individual lesion classes such as rare coding variants, fusions, exon skipping, amplifications, and enhancer hijacking are all known. Residual negatives enriched for suppressor-loss and chromatin dysregulation have also been described.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel step is combining these findings into one ordered hidden-lesion hierarchy that predicts how the negative pool shrinks with assay depth and what kind of biology remains after each layer of rescue.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No provided study presents this hierarchy explicitly. Because it integrates several existing discovery paradigms into a single explanatory structure for apparently driver-negative LUAD, it is best classified as new.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1876.name: Substitute-Driver Convergence Theory for Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1876.description: A substantial subset of bona fide oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas is not driverless but is driven by convergence onto core signaling output states through noncanonical architectures. In one branch, combinatorial loss of negative regulators substitutes for a canonical oncogene by jointly activating RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling; in another branch, rare hidden lesions such as NF1/RASA1 loss, RIT1, SOS1, RASA1, VAV1, MET exon 14 skipping, focal MET/ERBB2 amplification, and related RTK/RAS/RAF-axis alterations produce the same functional output. The governing principle is that pathway-state sufficiency, not lesion identity, is the operative driver unit.
theory-1876.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Substitute-Driver Convergence Theory for Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A substantial subset of bona fide oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinomas is not driverless but is driven by convergence onto core signaling output states through noncanonical architectures. In one branch, combinatorial loss of negative regulators substitutes for a canonical oncogene by jointly activating RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling; in another branch, rare hidden lesions such as NF1/RASA1 loss, RIT1, SOS1, RASA1, VAV1, MET exon 14 skipping, focal MET/ERBB2 amplification, and related RTK/RAS/RAF-axis alterations produce the same functional output. The governing principle is that pathway-state sufficiency, not lesion identity, is the operative driver unit.

theory_statements.statement_name: Suppressor-convergence substitute-driver law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In lung adenocarcinomas lacking known canonical proto-oncogene activation, concurrent inactivation of multiple negative regulators spanning the RAS and PI3K pathways can be sufficient to initiate and sustain malignant growth, with NF1 plus RASA1 plus PTEN loss constituting a validated substitute-driver configuration and RASA1 plus NF1 co-loss representing a human clinical analogue.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to oncogene-negative or broad-no-known-actionable-driver lung adenocarcinoma in which canonical activating RTK/RAS/RAF lesions are absent or insufficient to explain tumor behavior. Strongest support is in autochthonous mouse LUAD models and human NSCLC/LUAD genomic subsets with concurrent loss of RasGAP and PI3K-suppressor function. Does not claim that all driver-negative LUAD uses this mechanism, only that it defines a major mechanistically coherent subclass.
special_cases: The exact suppressor set need not always be NF1, RASA1, and PTEN; human tumors may realize the same state through partial genomic loss, epigenetic silencing, low protein abundance, or related pathway-brake lesions.
Double-loss states can be tumorigenic but are generally weaker than triple-loss states in the mouse models.
Background genotype modifies penetrance and fitness landscape accessibility, especially TP53, KEAP1, and LKB1 status.
Some human tumors with dual pERK/pAKT activity may instead be explained by hidden rare oncogenes or splice/fusion events rather than suppressor-loss convergence.

supporting_evidence.text: Combinatorial inactivation of NF1, RASA1, and PTEN efficiently initiated oncogene-negative LUAD and defined an oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K subtype with dual pathway activation.

The same work showed pERK and pAKT positivity in mouse tumors, and human oncogene-negative LUAD showed a substantial fraction with moderate-to-strong dual pERK/pAKT activation, supporting pathway-state convergence rather than lesion-specific dependence.

The fitness-landscape study demonstrated that all mutational paths toward Nf1/Rasa1/Pten triple loss were fitness-increasing, indicating that suppressor-loss substitute-driver evolution is unusually accessible in oncogene-negative LUAD.

Concurrent RASA1 and NF1 loss defined a distinct smoking-associated human NSCLC subset lacking other major mitogenic drivers and showing RAS-pathway dependence, providing a clinically observed human branch of the same general substitute-driver logic.

Oncogene-negative LUAD in TCGA/GENIE was explicitly framed as a clinically important subset in which pathway activation often arose through tumor suppressor inactivation rather than canonical oncogene mutation, with >60% of cases showing alterations in either RAS or PI3K pathway components and 22% in both.

The review and cohort analyses of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD showed that truly negative tumors were enriched for tumor suppressor loss, including NF1 and RASA1 deletions and low PTEN/STK11/KEAP1 states, indicating that suppressor-loss architectures are overrepresented once canonical oncogenes are absent.
NF1 and RASA1 were each enriched in oncogene-negative LUAD, and NF1 loss was repeatedly highlighted as a hidden route to RTK/RAS/RAF pathway activation in cases lacking canonical activating lesions.

This law was logically generalized by induction from the validated NF1/RASA1/PTEN model and the human RASA1/NF1 co-loss subset: if multiple independent negative regulators of the same signaling outputs are recurrently lost in driver-negative LUAD, then pathway-state sufficiency is the conserved driver principle.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Direct evidence already exists that NF1/RASA1/PTEN co-loss can initiate oncogene-negative LUAD in vivo, and that RASA1/NF1 co-loss defines a human MEK-sensitive NSCLC subset. The notion that tumor suppressor co-loss can functionally replace canonical oncogene activation is therefore grounded in prior work.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the explicit abstraction of these findings into a unifying substitute-driver law for a broad subclass of oncogene-negative LUAD, treating dual-pathway output convergence as the fundamental driver unit that links mouse triple-loss, human RasGAP co-loss, and residual RTK/RAS/RAF-negative tumors enriched for suppressor loss.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement is closely related to existing work because its core causal backbone is already experimentally demonstrated; the novelty lies in synthesis and scope refinement, not in the underlying primary observation.
references: Huang (2021) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [direct precursor for oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K subtype driven by NF1/RASA1/PTEN co-loss]
Borst (2024) Fully accessible fitness landscape of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma [direct precursor showing smooth accessibility of the NF1/RASA1/PTEN substitute-driver state]

Hayes (2021) RASA1 and NF1 are preferentially co-mutated and define a distinct genetic subset of smoking-associated non-small cell lung carcinomas sensitive to MEK inhibition [human RasGAP co-loss analogue]

Campbell/TCGA-linked analyses summarized in Distinct patterns of somatic genome alterations in lung adenocarcinomas and squamous cell carcinomas [oncogene-negative LUAD enriched for NF1/RASA1/SOS1/VAV1/RIT1-like pathway lesions]

theory_statements.statement_name: Dual-pathway therapy law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When oncogene-negative LUAD is maintained by convergent activation of both RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT outputs rather than by a single canonical oncogene, combined blockade of an upstream RAS-input node and a PI3K/AKT-output node should outperform monotherapy and should be more robust to lesion heterogeneity than lesion-matched single-agent therapy.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to oncogene-negative LUAD with validated or strongly inferred dual-pathway activation, especially dual pERK/pAKT-positive tumors or tumors with suppressor-loss architectures affecting both branches. Strongest evidence is preclinical in mouse models, allografts, and human cell systems. It should not be extrapolated to driver-negative tumors whose main biology is microenvironmental, lineage-plastic, angiogenic, or immune-dominant without biochemical evidence of dual pathway activation.
special_cases: Tumors with dominant activation of only one branch may respond differently and may not require dual blockade.
The optimal vertical pair may vary: SHP2 plus AKT, MEK plus PI3K, or other RAS/PI3K combinations could outperform one another depending on genotype and pathway topology.
Clinical translation may be constrained by toxicity even if preclinical efficacy is superior.
Canonical single-driver tumors rescued by deeper sequencing may still be better treated with lesion-matched monotherapy.

supporting_evidence.text: In the oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K mouse and cell models, SHP2 inhibition with RMC-4550 plus AKT inhibition with capivasertib outperformed single agents, reducing tumor burden and synergistically suppressing growth.

The same oncogene-negative substitute-driver work argued that these tumors are vulnerable to pharmacologic inhibition of both pathway branches, and the combination was more effective than monotherapy in autochthonous tumors, cell lines, and allografts.

In the human RASA1/NF1 co-loss subset, trametinib was active, and combination with PI3K inhibition or EGFR-pathway inhibition further improved suppression, supporting the general principle that dual-pathway substitute drivers require combination targeting.

The broad therapeutic review for RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD proposed SHP2, SOS1, MEK, ERK, PI3K, AKT, and mTOR as rational combinatorial targets precisely because pathway-state activation can persist without a single dominant oncogene lesion.

RPPA and phosphoproteomic analyses in oncogene-negative or pathway-unexplained LUAD showed substantial MAPK and mTOR activation not fully explained by known DNA lesions, implying that pathway-state-guided rather than lesion-only therapy is necessary in this domain.

This law was logically inferred from the dual-activation substitute-driver law above and the preclinical superiority of SHP2+AKT and MEK+PI3K combinations in suppressor-loss contexts: if two signaling branches are jointly required for tumor fitness, then suppressing only one should leave residual fitness through the other branch.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Combination inhibition of RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling in suppressor-loss lung cancer models is already demonstrated, and SHP2/AKT or MEK/PI3K combinations have direct preclinical support in the cited systems.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit promotion of combination therapy as a subtype-defining therapeutic law for oncogene-negative substitute-driver LUAD, detached from any one specific genotype and grounded instead in pathway-output state.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is somewhat related to existing work: the experimental treatment observations are established, but the generalized therapeutic rule for the driver-negative subclass is a synthetic extension.
references: Huang (2021) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [direct SHP2+AKT evidence in oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K subtype]

Hayes (2021) RASA1 and NF1 are preferentially co-mutated and define a distinct genetic subset of smoking-associated non-small cell lung carcinomas sensitive to MEK inhibition [MEK plus PI3K/EGFR combination support in human analogue]

Borst (2024) Fully accessible fitness landscape of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma [supports robustness and accessibility of the substitute-driver state, strengthening the rationale for pathway-state-guided therapy]

new_predictions_likely: If broad-negative LUADs are prospectively selected by concurrent moderate-to-strong pERK and pAKT staining, they will be enriched for combinations of NF1, RASA1, PTEN, STK11, KEAP1, or related pathway-brake deficiencies even when hotspot-driver sequencing is negative.

If PTEN, NF1, and RASA1 protein abundance and promoter methylation are added to DNA-based testing in broad-negative LUAD, the fraction assignable to a substitute-driver class will increase beyond WES/WGS-only classification.

If patient-derived organoids from oncogene-negative LUAD are stratified by dual-pathway activation, SHP2+AKT or MEK+PI3K combination therapy will outperform monotherapy mainly in the dual-high group and not in dual-low controls.

If multiregion sampling is performed in substitute-driver tumors, branch-specific upstream lesions will vary but the tumors will recurrently converge on preserved dual RAS/PI3K pathway activation states.
new_predictions_unknown: If some residual WGS-negative LUADs are truly substitute-driver tumors, a subset will show purely epigenetic silencing of NF1/RASA1/PTEN-axis components with no coding or copy-number lesion but the same dual-pathway drug sensitivity as genomic triple-loss models.

Single-cell phosphoproteomics of broad-negative LUAD may reveal that substitute-driver tumors contain parallel subclones using different upstream lesions yet occupying the same dual-pathway signaling attractor state, implying state-level rather than genotype-level clonality.

In early-stage never-smoker driver-negative LUAD, rare lineage-plastic states such as chaotic/piano tumors may transition into substitute-driver states late in progression by accumulating suppressor-loss combinations rather than acquiring a canonical kinase driver.

A clinically useful 'substitute-driver score' integrating pERK, pAKT, PTEN/NF1/RASA1 protein loss, and structural suppressor lesions may predict benefit from dual-pathway inhibition better than any single genotype.
negative_experiments: Identify a large cohort of rigorously broad-negative LUAD with strong dual pERK/pAKT activation, intact NF1/RASA1/PTEN-axis regulation at DNA, RNA, protein, and methylation levels, and no sensitivity to dual-pathway inhibition; this would substantially weaken the substitute-driver interpretation.

Show in validated NF1/RASA1/PTEN or RASA1/NF1 substitute-driver models that single-agent therapy consistently matches combination therapy in suppressing tumor growth and pathway output; this would directly challenge the dual-pathway therapy law.

Demonstrate that tumors with NF1/RASA1/PTEN triple loss no longer depend on RAS/MAPK or PI3K/AKT signaling after establishment, despite preserved genotype; this would undermine the claim that pathway-state convergence is the operative driver unit.

Prospectively show that canonical rare-driver rescue events account for nearly all dual pERK/pAKT-positive broad-negative tumors, leaving no meaningful suppressor-loss subclass; this would collapse the theory into a pure hidden-oncogene model.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not explain microenvironment-dominant driver-negative states such as immune-high, angiogenesis-high, or metabolism-high EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, where no single suppressor-loss substitute driver is demonstrated.

It does not explain lineage-plastic or stem-like driver-poor states such as piano never-smoker LUAD or the chaotic NS-LUAD subtype, where the leading hypotheses are quiescence exit, lineage infidelity, EMT, and CAF-rich ecosystem remodeling rather than dual RAS/PI3K convergence.
It does not capture metabolic driver-negative mechanisms such as the MIF-HIF-1α glycolysis axis or ferroptosis/fibroblast-associated EGFR-wild-type states.
It does not explain noncoding regulatory hidden drivers such as ERBB2 super-enhancer/structural-variant activation or MYC-LASE enhancer amplification.

conflicting_evidence.text: A large fraction of apparent driver-negative LUAD is rescued by rare canonical or quasi-canonical lesions on deeper testing, including ROS1, RET, NTRK1, EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, HER2 non-kinase mutations, ARAF/RAF1 mutations, BCAR4 fusions, and MET exon 14 skipping, implying that suppressor-loss substitute drivers are only one subset of the problem.

Some driver-negative cohorts are dominated by pathway-state or ecosystem-state explanations without strong evidence for suppressor-loss convergence, including estrogen-signaling NENA disease, proteomic PP/PI-like states, and immune/radiomics-defined negative cohorts.

special_cases: Smoking-associated RASA1/NF1 co-loss may be a human-specific branch of the general substitute-driver mechanism and may be more MAPK-dominant than NF1/RASA1/PTEN triple-loss disease.
PTEN deficiency should be assessed at DNA, methylation, protein, and phospho-state levels because genomic annotation alone undercalls pathway loss.
TP53, KEAP1, and LKB1 reshape the fitness landscape and may alter which dual-pathway combination is therapeutically optimal.
This theory is intended for rigorously broad-negative LUAD after reasonable rescue efforts for rare canonical drivers; it should not be applied prematurely after minimal hotspot-panel testing.

references: Huang (2021) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [direct precursor; oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K subtype]
Borst (2024) Fully accessible fitness landscape of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma [direct precursor; accessibility of NF1/RASA1/PTEN state]
Hayes (2021) RASA1 and NF1 are preferentially co-mutated and define a distinct genetic subset of smoking-associated non-small cell lung carcinomas sensitive to MEK inhibition [human co-loss analogue]

Campbell/TCGA-style oncogene-negative LUAD analyses summarized in Distinct patterns of somatic genome alterations in lung adenocarcinomas and squamous cell carcinomas [related enrichment of NF1, RASA1, SOS1, VAV1, RIT1 in oncogene-negative LUAD]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work already demonstrates NF1/RASA1/PTEN-driven oncogene-negative LUAD in vivo, a smooth accessibility landscape for that genotype, and a human RASA1/NF1 co-loss subset with MEK sensitivity. The idea that tumor suppressor co-loss can replace a canonical oncogene is therefore already established in important examples.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The new contribution is the explicit synthesis of these findings into a broader substitute-driver convergence theory for a mechanistically defined subclass of driver-negative LUAD, with pathway-state sufficiency and dual-branch therapy as the central abstractions.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because the core causal mechanisms are already directly shown in prior studies, this theory is not new in its foundation; its novelty lies in unifying several lines of evidence into a subtype-level conceptual framework for the broader driver-negative LUAD problem.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1877.name: Ecosystem-to-Pathway Driver Transfer Theory
theory-1877.description: Many driver-negative LUADs are not explained by hidden mutations alone because the effective driver resides in a stable tumor ecosystem state that can substitute for a classical oncogene. In this framework, tumors are maintained by self-reinforcing loops between tumor cell state and microenvironmental programs: immune-active/TLS states, angiogenesis-CAF exclusion states, fibroblast-ferroptosis-resistance states, estrogen-signaling never-smoker states, and ER-stress or glycolysis programs. The clinically relevant subtype unit is therefore an ecosystem program with a downstream signaling dependency, not necessarily a discrete DNA lesion.
theory-1877.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Ecosystem-to-Pathway Driver Transfer Theory
components.theory_description: Many driver-negative LUADs are not explained by hidden mutations alone because the effective driver resides in a stable tumor ecosystem state that can substitute for a classical oncogene. In this framework, tumors are maintained by self-reinforcing loops between tumor cell state and microenvironmental programs: immune-active/TLS states, angiogenesis-CAF exclusion states, fibroblast-ferroptosis-resistance states, estrogen-signaling never-smoker states, and ER-stress or glycolysis programs. The clinically relevant subtype unit is therefore an ecosystem program with a downstream signaling dependency, not necessarily a discrete DNA lesion.

theory_statements.statement_name: Microenvironmental substitute-driver law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In a substantial fraction of oncogene-negative LUAD, stable microenvironmental programs can function as substitute drivers by sustaining tumor fitness, immune escape, and progression despite absence of a dominant canonical oncogene; the main recurrent ecosystem archetypes are immune-high/TLS, angiogenesis-CAF exclusion, fibroblast-linked ferroptosis resistance, and lineage-coupled estrogen signaling states.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to driver-poor or clinically non-actionable LUAD cohorts defined by EGFR/ALK-wild-type, EGFR-wild-type, broad clinical-panel negative, or proteogenomic state-based classifications, especially never-smoker and early-stage disease where transcriptomic/proteomic ecosystem signals are strong. Does not claim these states are mutually exclusive or purely extrinsic; many are mixed intrinsic-extrinsic programs.
special_cases: Immune-high states may improve prognosis rather than worsen it, but still act as dominant biology shaping therapy response.
Angiogenesis-high and fibroblast-rich states can coexist with TP53/KRAS/STK11/KEAP1 lesions, so ecosystem state may be downstream of genomics rather than fully independent.
Never-smoker estrogen-signaling states are only formally established in EGFR/ALK-negative cohorts and should not be generalized to all driver-negative LUAD.
Some apparent ecosystem states may actually be secondary to undiscovered hidden drivers in a minority of cases.

supporting_evidence.text: EGFR- and ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD segregated into immune-high, angiogenesis-high, metabolism-high, and proliferation-high subgroups, indicating that driver-negative biology is often organized by stable ecosystem or pathway states rather than a single lesion.

The immune-high subgroup showed high T- and B-cell infiltration, TLS features, checkpoint receptor/ligand expression, and the best prognosis, demonstrating that an ecosystem program can define a clinically coherent driver-negative subtype.

The angiogenesis-high subgroup showed CAFs, MDSCs, TGF-β/cell-migration signaling, LGALS3/FGF2/CXCL12/PDGFB expression, immune exclusion, and poor prognosis, supporting an angiogenic stromal substitute-driver state.

EGFR-wild-type LUAD with high ferroptosis-related signature showed fibroblast-enriched microenvironment, enhanced fibroblast-tumor communication, IGF-binding pathway activity, worse survival, and preclinical vulnerability linked to 6-mercaptopurine and ferroptosis biology.

Never-smoker LUAD without EGFR or ALK alterations showed elevated estrogen signaling at RNA and protein levels, associated with STK11 mutation, chromosome 14/21 loss, LLGL2/ST14 hypomethylation, and GPER1 CNA/trans effects, supporting a pathway ecosystem rather than a single classical hidden oncogene.

Driver gene-negative LUAD cohorts defined by broad clinical panels separated mainly by immune-context differences, including checkpoint expression and immune infiltration, without discovery of a dominant genomic driver, again supporting an ecosystem-state framing.

Single-cell and spatial analyses in LUAD and related early disease identified epithelial-stromal states such as CLDN2+ AT2/COL6A3+ fibroblast interaction and THBS2+ CAF programs that predict progression or poor immunotherapy response, consistent with ecosystem-level tumor maintenance.

This law was inferred by induction across multiple independently defined negative or non-actionable LUAD cohorts: when no single hidden lesion dominates, reproducible microenvironmental programs recur and stratify prognosis and therapy more strongly than lesion identity.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual ecosystem states such as immune-hot, angiogenic CAF-rich, ferroptosis-related, and estrogen-signaling never-smoker LUAD have already been reported in separate studies. Microenvironmental stratification of LUAD is therefore established at the component level.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the synthesis that these states are not merely modifiers of oncogene-defined disease but form a substitute-driver class for many driver-negative LUADs, where the operative biological unit is a self-stabilizing ecosystem program.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory is somewhat related to existing work because its ingredients are known, but the claim that ecosystem programs should be elevated to the same conceptual status as hidden genomic drivers in driver-negative LUAD is a stronger, integrative reformulation.
references: Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [direct source for immune/angiogenic/metabolic/proliferative ecosystem subtypes]
Ferroptosis-related gene signature for predicting prognosis and identifying potential therapeutic drug in EGFR wild-type lung adenocarcinoma [fibroblast-ferroptosis-resistance state]
Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker Lung Adenocarcinoma Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [estrogen-signaling pathway ecosystem]
Harnessing multi-omics approaches to decipher tumor evolution and improve diagnosis and therapy in lung cancer [stromal and spatial ecosystem examples]

theory_statements.statement_name: Ecosystem-state therapeutic law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When oncogene-negative LUAD is organized by an ecosystem state, therapeutic sensitivity is better predicted by state-specific ligands, stromal cell types, checkpoint context, or metabolic crosstalk than by canonical genomic driver status alone; therefore, treatment should be assigned by ecosystem archetype rather than by residual negative-call category.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to driver-negative or no-known-actionable-driver LUAD in which transcriptomic, proteomic, phosphoproteomic, or spatial profiling identifies a dominant ecosystem state. Best supported in preclinical or retrospective settings for immune-high, angiogenesis-high, estrogen-signaling-high, ferroptosis-high/fibroblast-rich, and ER-stress-high disease. Not yet established as a standard prospective treatment algorithm.
special_cases: Some ecosystem markers are prognostic but not yet predictive in prospective trials.
A single tumor may contain more than one ecosystem state, requiring regional or single-cell profiling.
State-targeted therapy may need to be combined with lesion-targeted therapy in tumors rescued by deeper sequencing.
Different biomarkers may be needed for early-stage versus metastatic disease.

supporting_evidence.text: The immune-high EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker subgroup showed potential combination immunotherapy targets including CCL5, CXCL13, CD27, TRAF2, and LPAR6-associated vulnerabilities, even though no single actionable oncogene defined the subgroup.

The angiogenesis-high subgroup suggested candidate interventions against LGALS3/galectin-3, CXCL12/SDF-1, and FGF2, indicating that stromal-ligand context can nominate therapy in driver-negative disease.
The metabolism-high subgroup nominated ACACA/mtFAS and PRISM-matched compounds such as clorsulon, supporting metabolically assigned treatment rather than genomic-driver assignment.
