The estrogen-signaling NENA state prioritized saracatinib and showed greater in vitro sensitivity in STK11-mutant and STK11/ERBB2-mutant models, implying that pathway-state context can guide therapy in EGFR/ALK-negative never-smoker LUAD.

The fibroblast/ferroptosis EGFR-wild-type state nominated 6-mercaptopurine and linked fibroblast-rich communication signatures to ferroptosis resistance, with knockdown and xenograft data supporting state-linked therapy.

ER-stress-high early LUAD/SSN states were vulnerable to PDI inhibition with CCF642 and to Pdia3 knockdown in PDX, PTC, allograft, and knockdown models, showing that a pathway-state can itself be the therapeutic entry point.

Transcriptomic driver-negative cohorts repeatedly linked high-risk states to immune or metabolic programs and suggested differential immunotherapy or chemotherapy benefit even without discovery of a hidden genomic driver.

This law was logically inferred from the repeated observation that distinct ecosystem states in negative LUAD each nominate different targets, whereas the negative-call label alone does not predict therapy.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: State-specific therapeutic hypotheses already exist for several driver-negative or no-actionable-driver LUAD subgroups, including immunologic, angiogenic, metabolic, estrogen-signaling, and ER-stress contexts.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that these should be treated as a unified therapeutic law for ecosystem-driven negative LUAD: treatment assignment should pivot from 'what mutation is missing?' to 'what ecosystem loop is dominant?'.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is somewhat related to existing work because it synthesizes multiple biomarker-oriented studies into a coherent therapeutic principle rather than introducing an entirely new mechanism.
references: Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [state-specific target nomination]
Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker Lung Adenocarcinoma Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [saracatinib state assignment]
Ferroptosis-related gene signature for predicting prognosis and identifying potential therapeutic drug in EGFR wild-type lung adenocarcinoma [fibroblast-ferroptosis therapy linkage]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals tumorigenesis and progression of lung cancer manifested as subsolid nodules [ER-stress/PDI therapeutic entry point]

new_predictions_likely: If driver-negative LUADs are profiled jointly by spatial transcriptomics and plasma cytokine/ligand assays, ecosystem archetypes will be classifiable using a small circulating panel such as CXCL13, CCL5, LGALS3, CXCL12, FGL1, and MIF.

If therapy-naive broad-negative LUADs are assigned to immune-high, angiogenesis-high, estrogen-signaling-high, fibroblast-ferroptosis-high, or ER-stress-high states, response patterns will segregate by state-specific biomarkers better than by broad negative-call status alone.

If serial biopsies are taken during progression of driver-negative LUAD, many tumors will switch between ecosystem archetypes without acquiring a new canonical oncogene, especially from immune-high or steady states toward angiogenesis/CAF or stress-adaptive states.

If fibroblast-rich ferroptosis-high tumors are cocultured with matched CAFs, ferroptosis resistance will increase and become reversible by disrupting predicted ligand-receptor programs such as IGF-related or fibroblast signaling circuits.
new_predictions_unknown: A pan-LUAD 'ecosystem driver score' combining TLS density, CAF state, angiogenic ligand abundance, ferroptosis state, estrogen-signaling score, and ER-stress markers may outperform mutation-based stratification even in some nominally driver-positive tumors.
Some chaotic or piano tumors may prove to be metastable ecosystem states that can be therapeutically reprogrammed into immune-responsive states without targeting any hidden genomic lesion.

Microbiome or airway colonization patterns, not yet measured in these datasets, may map selectively onto angiogenesis-high or immune-high driver-negative LUAD and act as upstream stabilizers of those ecosystem states.
Ecosystem archetypes may be detectable by radiomics plus blood biomarkers alone, allowing noninvasive assignment of therapy in negative LUAD without repeat tissue biopsy.
negative_experiments: Prospectively show that state-specific biomarkers such as CAF abundance, estrogen signaling, TLS markers, ferroptosis/fibroblast signatures, or ER-stress markers do not improve therapy prediction over the undifferentiated negative-call label; this would undermine the theory.

Demonstrate that most ecosystem-classified driver-negative tumors are reclassified into single canonical hidden drivers after exhaustive sequencing and lose their state-specific therapeutic behavior; this would argue the ecosystem is secondary rather than substitute-driving.

Show in matched models that disrupting the dominant ecosystem loop leaves tumor fitness unchanged despite marker suppression; this would challenge the claim that the ecosystem state is functionally driver-like.
Find that spatially distinct ecosystem states within a tumor do not correspond to distinct outcome or drug-response behavior; this would weaken the subtype utility of the theory.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not explain rare lesion-centric hidden drivers with strong direct causality, such as NTRK1, RET, ROS1, ALK, KIF5B-RET, EGFR extracellular-domain mutations, MET exon 14 skipping, or CD74-NRG1 fusions.

It does not directly explain suppressor-loss substitute-driver tumors centered on NF1/RASA1/PTEN or RASA1/NF1 co-loss, where the dominant biology is dual RAS/PI3K signaling rather than a stromal ecosystem.
It does not account for noncoding enhancer-hijacking drivers such as ERBB2 super-enhancer activation or MYC-LASE, which are lesion-defined rather than ecosystem-defined.

conflicting_evidence.text: Multiple studies show that many apparent driver-negative tumors are only technically negative and can be rescued by deeper molecular testing, implying that ecosystem states cannot explain the entire negative-LUAD fraction.
Some driver-negative-focused studies mainly found rare coding or fusion events rather than ecosystem states, such as RIT1, ARAF/RAF1, BCAR4 fusions, and Hippo-pathway fusions.

special_cases: An ecosystem state can be tumor-intrinsic-extrinsic mixed; for example, estrogen signaling or ferroptosis resistance often requires both tumor genotype and stromal reinforcement.
Early-stage disease may be especially ecosystem-governed, whereas late metastatic disease may increasingly incorporate hidden lesion rescue or suppressor-loss substitute drivers.
The same tumor may move between ecosystem states over time, so classification should be repeated longitudinally when feasible.

references: Proteogenomic characterization identifies clinical subgroups in EGFR and ALK wild-type never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma [immune/angiogenic/metabolic/proliferative state taxonomy]
Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker Lung Adenocarcinoma Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [estrogen-signaling state in negative LUAD]
Ferroptosis-related gene signature for predicting prognosis and identifying potential therapeutic drug in EGFR wild-type lung adenocarcinoma [fibroblast-ferroptosis ecosystem state]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals tumorigenesis and progression of lung cancer manifested as subsolid nodules [ER-stress and cholesterol pathway-state progression model]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The component ecosystem states and their associated biomarkers or vulnerabilities are already reported in several independent negative or non-actionable LUAD cohorts. Microenvironmental and pathway-state heterogeneity in LUAD is therefore established.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit claim that these recurrent ecosystem programs should be considered substitute-driver architectures for driver-negative LUAD, and that treatment should be assigned by ecosystem archetype rather than by residual absence of a mutation.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is built from existing state-specific studies but recombines them into a broader mechanistic and therapeutic framework for driver-negative LUAD, making it more than a simple restatement but less than a wholly new mechanism.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1878.name: Convergent Pathway-State Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
theory-1878.description: A large fraction of apparently oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not truly driverless; instead, these tumors converge on a limited set of activated signaling states through noncanonical mechanisms that are often missed by hotspot-centric genomics. The dominant recurrent states are dual RAS/PI3K activation via tumor-suppressor co-loss, ERBB activation via ligand presentation/fusion, and proteomic MAPK/mTOR activation that can exceed what is explained by canonical DNA lesions. Thus, the biologically relevant unit is often a pathway-activated state, not a single mutated oncogene.
theory-1878.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Convergent Pathway-State Theory of Oncogene-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: A large fraction of apparently oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma is not truly driverless; instead, these tumors converge on a limited set of activated signaling states through noncanonical mechanisms that are often missed by hotspot-centric genomics. The dominant recurrent states are dual RAS/PI3K activation via tumor-suppressor co-loss, ERBB activation via ligand presentation/fusion, and proteomic MAPK/mTOR activation that can exceed what is explained by canonical DNA lesions. Thus, the biologically relevant unit is often a pathway-activated state, not a single mutated oncogene.

theory_statements.statement_name: Convergent-pathway substitution law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In LUAD lacking canonical RTK/RAS/RAF oncogene lesions, tumorigenesis can be sustained by combinations of non-oncogene alterations that recreate the same downstream signaling outputs, especially RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR activation.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinomas classified as oncogene-negative or RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative by broad genomic assays, and to related preclinical models of oncogene-negative LUAD. Best supported in tumors lacking known activating EGFR/KRAS/BRAF/ALK/ROS1/RET/MET-class lesions at initial testing, then profiled by WES/WGS/RNA-seq and/or proteogenomics.
special_cases: A substantial subset of initially negative tumors are later rescued by deeper sequencing as rare canonical or near-canonical drivers; this law applies most strongly to the residual negative set after broad testing.
The exact substituting mechanism differs across tumors: tumor-suppressor co-loss, noncanonical rare coding mutations, exon skipping, focal amplification, or ligand-driven signaling.
Not all negative tumors converge on the same pathway; some are dominated by immune, metabolic, or lineage-state programs instead.

supporting_evidence.text: Oncogene-negative LUAD was shown to contain an explicit Onc-negative RAS/PI3K subtype, with pERK and pAKT positivity in human tumors and in mouse models driven by combinatorial Nf1/Rasa1/Pten loss, indicating that canonical downstream pathways can be recreated without canonical oncogene activation.

Concurrent RASA1 and NF1 loss defined a smoking-associated NSCLC subset lacking other known mitogenic drivers and produced strong RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT activation with MEK-inhibitor sensitivity, showing that co-loss of RasGAPs can substitute for classical driver oncogenes.

In TCGA-style oncogene-negative LUAD, hidden RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway lesions were enriched, including MET and ERBB2 focal amplification, NF1 loss, and RIT1 mutation, implying that many negative tumors remain pathway-driven despite lacking the standard activating event set.

Proteomic RPPA analyses showed significant MAPK activation in many KRAS-wild-type tumors and significant mTOR activation across tumors not fully explained by known genomic alterations, directly supporting the idea that pathway state outruns mutation annotation.

In East Asian never-smoker LUAD, phosphoproteomics showed heterogeneous MAPK activity across EGFR-wild-type and EGFR-mutant tumors, including high-MAPK EGFR-wild-type cases and low-MAPK EGFR-mutant/TP53-mutant cases, demonstrating that genotype alone incompletely specifies pathway activity.

Whole-genome reanalysis of RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway-negative LUAD rescued many initially negative tumors by discovering KRAS hotspot mutations, ARAF/EGFR/MAPK1/SOS1 amplifications, NF1/RASA1 deletions, and NRG1 fusions, while the remaining negative tumors still showed recurrent tumor-suppressor loss and structural complexity.

The review of RPA-negative LUAD explicitly concludes that some WES-negative tumors are later found to harbor RTK/RAS/RAF-pathway lesions by WGS, while the residual group likely depends on noncanonical drivers, downstream amplifications, or epigenetic pathway activation.

Distinct patterns study of oncogene-negative LUAD found enrichment of rare pathway members such as SOS1, RASA1, VAV1, ARHGAP35, and NF1, implying that pathway membership rather than canonical gene identity is the key organizing principle of some negative tumors.

RIT1 mutations occurred exclusively in the oncogene-negative subset and experimentally activated MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT signaling, providing a direct example of a rare coding substitute for canonical RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes.
MET exon 14 skipping, identified predominantly in driver-unknown tumors, is another example of a hidden pathway-restoring lesion that substitutes for missing canonical RTK activation.

This statement is additionally supported by induction/abduction across the above findings: multiple orthogonal lesion classes in negative LUAD repeatedly converge on the same signaling outputs (RAS/MAPK, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, ERBB), implying that pathway state is a better explanatory variable than lesion class alone.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is established that some 'driver-negative' LUADs are later explained by rare drivers, and that RAS/MAPK or PI3K signaling can be activated by NF1, MET exon 14, RIT1, or other alterations.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is to formalize these disparate findings as a general substitution law: oncogene-negative LUAD is often a pathway-state disease in which distinct lesion classes functionally replace canonical oncogenes by converging on a small number of downstream signaling attractors.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis and abstraction across multiple known mechanisms rather than a wholly unprecedented biological claim; the integrative pathway-state framing is broader than any single cited paper.
references: Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Therapeutic Vulnerabilities in Lung Adenocarcinoma [proteomic pathway-state view of LUAD signaling beyond genotype]
Sanchez-Vega et al. (2018) Oncogenic Signaling Pathways in The Cancer Genome Atlas [general pathway-convergence framework in cancer]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [RPA-negative LUAD as direct precursor concept]
Nam et al. (2022) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [Onc-negative RAS/PI3K subtype foundation]

theory_statements.statement_name: Proteomic primacy law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In oncogene-negative LUAD, phosphoproteomic and protein-state readouts are often more faithful indicators of the operative driver than DNA genotype alone, because actionable signaling may arise from noncanonical lesions, regulatory rearrangements, or combined suppressor loss that are incompletely captured by standard driver panels.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LUAD cohorts with proteomic or phosphoproteomic data and to clinically negative tumors evaluated by deeper multi-omics. Especially relevant in EGFR/ALK-negative never-smoker LUAD, RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD, and tumors lacking currently actionable drivers on standard testing.
special_cases: This law is strongest for pathway-state therapeutics and weaker for tumors truly driven by a single highly penetrant canonical kinase lesion.
It does not imply DNA is uninformative; rather, it implies DNA alone is often insufficient in the negative subset.
Some pathway states remain correlative until functionally perturbed.

supporting_evidence.text: RPPA data showed MAPK and mTOR activity in many tumors that could not be fully accounted for by known genomic alterations, directly motivating proteomic interpretation of apparently driver-negative disease.
In East Asian never-smoker LUAD, a co-phosphorylation MAPK signature stratified tumors more precisely than EGFR genotype alone, with EGFR-wild-type high-MAPK tumors and EGFR-mutant low-MAPK tumors.

The LSCC CDK4/6 biomarker study showed that downstream RB phosphorylation predicted inhibitor response better than mutation status alone, providing a cross-lung-cancer principle that pathway-state biomarkers can outperform genotype-only biomarkers.

In LSCC, EGFR pathway activity correlated with ligand abundance rather than EGFR amplification alone, reinforcing that pathway activity can be driven by protein-context variables not captured by DNA alteration status.

Proteogenomic subtype analyses of LUAD identified PP, PI, and TRU states with distinct kinase activities and therapeutic hypotheses, and explicitly noted that many LUADs without actionable genomic drivers still possess proteome-defined signaling dependencies.

In EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD, four proteogenomic subtypes (P, I, A, M) were defined by pathway state, prognosis, and candidate therapies rather than by a single hidden mutation, showing practical subclassification of clinically negative disease through proteogenomics.

The STK11-AMPK-HNF1A-FGL1/CPS1 axis in proteome subtype 4 illustrates a driver-relevant state discovered primarily at protein level, with genotype alone insufficient because some STK11-wild-type tumors also showed low STK11 expression and downstream program activation.

SHP2/PTPN11 and SOS1 phosphosite outliers in LUAD reveal actionable signaling nodes downstream of EGFR, ALK, and KRAS that are more visible in phosphoproteomics than in RNA or total protein abundance.

ARHGEF5 phosphosite activation in non-smoking LUAD represents a hidden pathway state inferred at the phosphosite level rather than from a canonical coding lesion, supporting the broader principle that phosphoproteomics reveals non-genomic drivers.

This law is further supported by induction from multiple studies showing that clinically negative LUAD can be partitioned into reproducible transcriptomic/proteomic states with therapeutic implications even when no single genomic driver is identified.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Proteogenomic studies already show that signaling activity is not perfectly predicted by genotype in lung cancer and other tumors.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is to cast this specifically as a law of oncogene-negative LUAD: in the negative subset, proteomic state is often the nearest measurable causal representation of the true driver mechanism.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The components are established, but the explicit prioritization of proteomic state as the principal explanatory layer for driver-negative LUAD is a synthetic extension.
references: Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Therapeutic Vulnerabilities in Lung Adenocarcinoma [direct proteogenomic foundation]
CPTAC lung studies [broader evidence that phosphoproteomics refines genotype-driven interpretation]
Satpathy et al. and related phosphoproteomic precision oncology work [general pathway-state interpretation framework]

new_predictions_likely: If broad driver-negative LUAD cohorts are stratified by phosphoproteomic signatures rather than DNA status, at least three reproducible actionable classes will emerge: dual RAS/PI3K-active, ERBB-ligand-active, and proliferation/CDK-active tumors.
If pERK, pAKT, pRB, and pERBB3 IHC panels are applied to clinically negative LUAD, these markers will predict deeper hidden-driver classes better than hotspot mutation panels alone.

If residual RPA-negative tumors are subjected to long-read WGS plus phosphoproteomics, a substantial subset will be reassigned to noncanonical structural, splicing, or suppressor-loss pathway classes rather than remaining biologically unclassified.
new_predictions_unknown: If pathway-state classification is used prospectively in oncogene-negative LUAD, treatment matching by phosphoproteomic state may outperform treatment matching by expanded DNA panel alone in response rate and progression-free survival.
A residual subset of phospho-MAPK-high, genomically negative LUAD may prove to be driven by allele-specific expression or 3D-genome rewiring of pathway regulators rather than coding events.

Some clinically negative LUADs may alternate between pathway states over time, such that serial phosphoproteomics will reveal state-switching under therapy even without acquisition of new canonical driver mutations.
negative_experiments: Show in a large oncogene-negative LUAD cohort that phosphoproteomic pathway activity is fully explained by known genomic alterations and adds no predictive value beyond DNA; this would undermine the proteomic primacy law.
Demonstrate that combined suppressor-loss models of oncogene-negative LUAD fail to activate the predicted downstream pathways or fail to phenocopy human negative tumors.
Find that deeper long-read/WGS analyses do not reduce the unclassified fraction and that residual tumors do not cluster by pathway state; this would argue against convergence.

unaccounted_for.text: Purely immune-stratified driver-negative prognostic groups can be described without identifying a hidden oncogenic pathway, suggesting some negative tumors may be dominated by ecosystem state rather than tumor-intrinsic signaling convergence.

Some lineage-plastic or evolution-dominated tumors, such as piano never-smoker cancers or histologic progression continua, may be governed more by cell-state dynamics than by the pathway substitution model.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several studies emphasize that many apparently negative tumors are ultimately rescued as rare canonical drivers rather than representing stable pathway-state-driven biology, which limits how broadly this theory applies to all negative calls.

special_cases: Best applied after broad DNA/RNA testing, not after minimal clinical hotspot testing.
More likely to hold in residual negative tumors than in all tumors initially labeled negative.
May coexist with strong microenvironmental and lineage-state effects rather than replacing them.

references: Nam et al. (2022) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [Onc-negative RAS/PI3K subtype foundation]
Gillette et al. (2020) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Therapeutic Vulnerabilities in Lung Adenocarcinoma [pathway-state interpretation beyond genotype]
Carrot-Zhang et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [residual negative LUAD and rescue logic]
Sanchez-Vega et al. (2018) Oncogenic Signaling Pathways in The Cancer Genome Atlas [general pathway-convergence paradigm]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work establishes rare hidden drivers, suppressor-loss-driven RAS/PI3K activation, and proteogenomic mismatch between genotype and signaling state.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory unifies those observations into a single general account of oncogene-negative LUAD as convergent pathway-state disease, with proteomic state treated as the central explanatory layer.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: It is not a de novo biological mechanism, but it is a novel synthesis with stronger predictive structure than the individual precursor observations.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1879.name: State-Space Taxonomy Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
theory-1879.description: Driver-negative LUAD is not one disease but a mixture of at least four biologically distinct state classes: pathway-driven proliferative tumors, stromal-angiogenic tumors, immune-structured tumors, and lineage/metabolic-plastic tumors. These classes recur across transcriptomic, proteomic, and single-cell studies under different naming schemes, implying that negative LUAD can be organized by latent state-space rather than by absence of a mutation. This taxonomy predicts subtype-specific validation strategies and therapies.
theory-1879.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: State-Space Taxonomy Theory of Driver-Negative LUAD
components.theory_description: Driver-negative LUAD is not one disease but a mixture of at least four biologically distinct state classes: pathway-driven proliferative tumors, stromal-angiogenic tumors, immune-structured tumors, and lineage/metabolic-plastic tumors. These classes recur across transcriptomic, proteomic, and single-cell studies under different naming schemes, implying that negative LUAD can be organized by latent state-space rather than by absence of a mutation. This taxonomy predicts subtype-specific validation strategies and therapies.

theory_statements.statement_name: Four-state decomposition law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When canonical driver lesions are absent or clinically unavailable, LUAD can be decomposed into recurrent latent states centered on proliferation, angiogenic/stromal organization, immune organization, and lineage/metabolic plasticity, and these states recur across independent multi-omic classification systems.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to oncogene-negative, EGFR/ALK-wild-type, or no-known-actionable-driver LUAD cohorts analyzed by transcriptomics, proteomics, phosphoproteomics, or single-cell/spatial methods. Most relevant in never-smoker LUAD and in clinically driver-negative subsets, but extends to broader LUAD where actionable drivers are absent or not explanatory.
special_cases: Not all studies recover exactly four groups; some collapse or split one state into adjacent subtypes.
A single tumor may show hybrid features or transition between states over time.
Histology-specific subsets such as invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma may overlay rather than replace this state-space.

supporting_evidence.text: EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD separated into four proteogenomic classes: proliferation-high, immune-high, angiogenesis-high, and metabolism-high, directly matching the proposed four-state structure.

Never-smoker LUAD transcriptomics defined steady, proliferative, and chaotic states, where steady maps to lineage-stable, proliferative maps to growth-driven, and chaotic maps to mesenchymal/CAF-rich aggressive biology, capturing three major axes of the same state-space.

Integrated LUAD expression/proteomic subtyping repeatedly recovered proliferative/proximal proliferative or PP states, inflammatory/immune states, and terminal respiratory unit or metabolically differentiated states, indicating cross-study recurrence of the same latent structure under different names.
Consensus multi-omics clustering produced CS1-CS4, where CS3 and CS4 were proliferative/aggressive, CS2 was immune-hot, and CS1 was more metabolic/less immune, again reproducing the same axes.

Additional multi-omics studies identified C1/C2 or iC1/iC2 LUAD classes organized around proliferative/immune-cold versus immune-active states, showing that the state-space is robust even in reduced-dimensional solutions.

Single-cell and early-lesion studies add a lineage/metabolic axis not fully visible in bulk genomics, including KRT8+ alveolar intermediate states, MIF+AT2 glycolytic states, AIS cholesterol biology, ER-stress progression states, and fibroblast-linked ferroptosis-resistant EGFR-wild-type states.

This decomposition is further supported by induction from multiple independently named subtype systems that repeatedly align along the same latent axes despite using different omics layers and cohorts.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Many studies already report subtype structures in LUAD, including proliferative, immune, metabolic, and stromal/angiogenic states.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is to interpret these as one shared state-space taxonomy specifically for driver-negative LUAD, allowing integration across transcriptomic, proteomic, and single-cell naming systems.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a high-level synthesis that unifies multiple existing subtype frameworks into a single latent taxonomy focused on the driver-negative problem.
references: Hwang et al. and related EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD proteogenomic subtype studies [closest direct precedent]
CPTAC/APOLLO LUAD subtype papers [recurrent proliferative/immune/metabolic axes]
single-cell early LUAD state atlases [lineage-plasticity and metabolic axes]

theory_statements.statement_name: Therapeutic orthogonality law
theory_statements.theory_statement: The major latent states of driver-negative LUAD imply orthogonal therapeutic vulnerabilities: proliferative states favor cell-cycle/replication-stress targeting, angiogenic-stromal states favor stromal-vascular disruption, immune-structured states favor checkpoint or immune-context therapies, and lineage/metabolic states favor metabolism, ferroptosis, or cell-state-directed interventions.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to driver-negative-like LUAD state classes defined by multi-omics or single-cell profiling, especially when no canonical targeted therapy is available. Best supported by preclinical or inferential evidence rather than prospective trials.
special_cases: Vulnerabilities are often inferential or preclinical; clinical validation remains limited.
Hybrid tumors may require combinations rather than single-state-matched therapy.
Some therapies may act on the microenvironment rather than tumor cells directly.

supporting_evidence.text: The proliferation-high never-smoker subtype showed candidate vulnerabilities involving CDK2, CDK5, PLKs, ATR, E2F1, and CDK9 dependency, linking the proliferative state to cell-cycle-directed therapy logic.
The angiogenesis-high subtype showed poor prognosis with CAFs, immune exclusion, and candidate targets including LGALS3, CXCL12/SDF-1, and FGF2, matching a stromal-vascular therapeutic axis.

The immune-high subtype showed checkpoint-rich immune activation with candidate therapeutic directions involving CCL5, CXCL13, CD27, TRAF2, and LPAR-linked immunomodulation, matching an immune-context treatment axis.

The metabolism-high subtype showed oxidative phosphorylation/lipid-metabolism enrichment and candidate metabolic liabilities including ACACA/mtFAS and CERS4-related programs, matching a metabolic-targeting axis.

FeSig-high EGFR-wild-type LUAD linked fibroblast-rich ferroptosis-resistant state to 6-mercaptopurine sensitivity and knockdown-validated ferroptosis genes, supporting the metabolic/microenvironment branch of the theory.

Driver-negative glycolysis-high LUAD identified a MIF-HIF-1alpha axis whose inhibition reversed glucose uptake, lactate production, LDH activity, and proliferation in AT2 cells, supporting a lineage-metabolic vulnerability class.

Early SSN progression studies identified cholesterol/PCSK9 biology and ER-stress/PDI vulnerability, including preclinical activity of CCF642 and Pdia3 knockdown, supporting a state-based intervention strategy outside canonical oncogene targeting.

Proteome subtype 4 with STK11-AMPK-HNF1A-FGL1/CPS1 signaling suggested mTOR plus LAG3/FGL1-targeted combination logic, showing that hidden state classes can yield orthogonal, non-oncogene treatment hypotheses.
Onc-negative RAS/PI3K tumors responded to SHP2 and AKT inhibition, showing that a pathway-defined proliferative state can be therapeutically attacked even without a canonical oncogene.

The review of RPA-negative LUAD therapy explicitly maps driver-negative biology to distinct pathway vulnerabilities including DDR, EZH2, TGF-beta, NRF2/KEAP1, SHP2/SOS1, and immunotherapy combinations, reinforcing the idea of orthogonal treatment logic by state.

This law is also supported by induction: once negative LUAD is treated as a state-space rather than a mutation bucket, each state repeatedly nominates a different therapeutic axis across independent studies.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual subtype papers already propose state-matched therapies in proliferative, immune, angiogenic, or metabolic LUAD groups.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is to state that these vulnerabilities are orthogonal consequences of a common driver-negative state taxonomy, implying a general treatment-selection framework for negative LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The treatment ideas exist piecemeal; the integrated orthogonality principle across state classes is a new synthesis.
references: Proteogenomic subtype studies in EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD [direct source of orthogonal vulnerabilities]
FeSig and glycolysis-axis studies [metabolic branch]
Onc-negative RAS/PI3K studies [pathway branch]

new_predictions_likely: If a clinically driver-negative LUAD cohort is jointly profiled by RNA-seq, proteomics, and spatial imaging, most tumors will map reproducibly onto one of four latent states rather than remaining biologically unstructured.

Therapeutic response ex vivo will correlate more strongly with state class than with negative-call gene list: proliferative tumors with CDK/ATR sensitivity, angiogenic tumors with stromal/vascular sensitivity, immune-high tumors with checkpoint-context signatures, and metabolic/lineage tumors with ferroptosis or glycolysis sensitivity.
Independent subtype systems from different populations will align by latent axes even if they use different labels.
new_predictions_unknown: A small but clinically important fifth state may emerge from microbiome- or secretome-dominated driver-negative LUAD, orthogonal to the four-state taxonomy.
Longitudinal sampling may show deterministic transitions from lineage/metabolic states to stromal-angiogenic or proliferative states during progression or therapy resistance.
State-matched combination therapies may outperform mutation-guided standard-of-care in a prospective basket trial of driver-negative LUAD.
negative_experiments: Show that multi-omic subtypes in driver-negative LUAD are not reproducible across cohorts and collapse after batch correction; this would undermine the state-space theory.
Demonstrate that proposed state-matched vulnerabilities do not segregate by subtype in organoid or ex vivo drug testing.
Find that most clinically negative LUADs are ultimately just hidden rare canonical drivers with no residual recurrent state structure.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not specifically explain single-gene rescued subsets such as ARAF, EGFR extracellular-domain mutants, NTRK1 fusions, or KIF5B-RET, which behave more like rare canonical driver classes than state classes.

conflicting_evidence.text: Several 'driver-negative' cohorts are strongly affected by assay incompleteness or low tumor purity, meaning some apparent state classes may partly reflect incomplete rescue rather than true noncanonical biology.

special_cases: The taxonomy is intended for residual negative disease after reasonable broad testing, not for minimal-panel negatives.
The same tumor may occupy a hybrid state, especially at progression.
Some states are more visible in single-cell or proteomic data than in DNA-centric cohorts.

references: Hwang et al. proteogenomic subtypes in EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD [closest direct taxonomy precursor]
Gillette et al. proteogenomic LUAD subtype studies [PP/PI/TRU framework]
recent single-cell LUAD state atlases [lineage-plasticity branch]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Multiple LUAD subtype systems already exist and individually identify proliferative, immune, stromal, and metabolic programs.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory unifies them into a state-space taxonomy explicitly for driver-negative LUAD and derives an orthogonal therapeutic-selection principle from that taxonomy.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: It is best viewed as a cross-study synthesis with translational implications rather than a wholly new biological mechanism.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1880.name: Residual oncogene-negative LUAD is a pathway-output disease, not a pathway-absence disease
theory-1880.description: The main hidden structure within 'driver-negative' lung adenocarcinoma is not simply whether a DNA driver was found, but whether the tumor has already reinstated canonical oncogenic signaling output through noncanonical routes. Many apparent driver-negative tumors are actually covert RTK/RAS/RAF-MAPK and/or PI3K-AKT tumors whose activation is achieved by rare coding variants, exon-skipping, fusions, negative-regulator loss, amplification, enhancer hijacking, or protein-state activation without a canonical hotspot lesion. After stricter exclusion of false negatives, the remaining residual class is predicted to split into at least two biologically distinct branches: an Onc-negative RAS/PI3K branch with pathway output already high, and a noncanonical-state branch whose main liabilities are lineage, immune, metabolic, or epigenetic.
theory-1880.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Residual oncogene-negative LUAD is a pathway-output disease, not a pathway-absence disease
components.theory_description: The main hidden structure within 'driver-negative' lung adenocarcinoma is not simply whether a DNA driver was found, but whether the tumor has already reinstated canonical oncogenic signaling output through noncanonical routes. Many apparent driver-negative tumors are actually covert RTK/RAS/RAF-MAPK and/or PI3K-AKT tumors whose activation is achieved by rare coding variants, exon-skipping, fusions, negative-regulator loss, amplification, enhancer hijacking, or protein-state activation without a canonical hotspot lesion. After stricter exclusion of false negatives, the remaining residual class is predicted to split into at least two biologically distinct branches: an Onc-negative RAS/PI3K branch with pathway output already high, and a noncanonical-state branch whose main liabilities are lineage, immune, metabolic, or epigenetic.

theory_statements.statement_name: Ascertainment-compression law
theory_statements.theory_statement: The apparent frequency of oncogene-negative LUAD decreases monotonically as assay breadth expands from limited hotspot/fusion panels to RNA-based splice/fusion discovery to WES/WGS with copy-number, structural, noncoding, and protein-state profiling, because a substantial fraction of 'negative' tumors are assay-limited false negatives rather than a single coherent biological class.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to human lung adenocarcinoma cohorts classified as driver-negative, pan-negative, non-CAGA, RPA-negative, or EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative using non-uniform clinical or research assays. Most applicable when comparing panel-negative, RNA-seq-assisted, WES-negative, and WGS/proteogenomic-negative definitions across resected or advanced LUAD cohorts, especially never-smoker-enriched cohorts where uncommon fusions and splice alterations are frequent.
special_cases: Less informative in cohorts already profiled by high-quality WGS plus RNA-seq plus broad CNV/SV/noncoding analyses, because the residual class there is closer to true biological negativity.
The apparent decrease can be attenuated by low tumor purity, limited tissue, FFPE degradation, low-VAF subclonality, or incomplete capture of phosphoprotein-only activation states.
Some residual tumors remain unexplained even after WGS, so the law concerns shrinking frequency, not elimination, of the negative class.

supporting_evidence.text: Targeted clinical negativity often collapses after broader sequencing: a 33-year-old never-smoker LUAD judged EGFR/KRAS/EML4-ALK-negative by clinical testing was found by WGS/RNA-seq to harbor KIF5B-RET.

Small replication cohorts of triple-negative or double-negative primary lung adenocarcinomas also yielded KIF5B-RET after deeper transcript analysis, showing that clinically negative status can be a prelude to rescue of a canonical kinase driver.
In East Asian never-smoker LUAD, tumors initially negative for EGFR/HER2/KRAS/BRAF/ALK could be rescued by later ROS1 fusion testing; ROS1 fusions were found only after extending the assay set.

Pan-negative LUADs defined after standard driver testing contained hidden EGFR extracellular-domain mutants; among 143 panel-negative tumors, additional EGFR ECD sequencing identified M277E and other ECD mutations.

RNA-seq rescue occurred even within conventionally screened tumors: six tumors negative by conventional EGFR screening were found by RNA-seq to harbor EGFR point mutations, while additional tumors carried novel kinase fusions or MET exon 14 skipping.

Broad hybrid-capture NGS in triple-negative LUAD revealed actionable alterations in 31% of cases and recovered EGFR and KRAS mutations missed by conventional assays, plus ROS1/RET fusions, MET amplification, ERBB2 mutation, and BRAF mutation.

Within RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD, WGS rescued occult canonical pathway lesions in about one-third of WES-negative tumors, including hidden KRAS mutations, ARAF/EGFR/MAPK1/SOS1 amplifications, RASA1/NF1 deletions, and NRG1 fusions/mutations.

The WGS-negative RPA(-)G study explicitly attributes a fraction of WES-negative status to low purity and technical detection failure, showing that assay depth and completeness materially alter the apparent size of the negative class.

Driver-negative/driver-unknown lung cancers in RNA meta-analysis were partially rescued by recurrent NRG1 fusions/outlier expression, Hippo fusions, NF1 truncating fusions, and MET exon 14 skipping, jointly explaining about 16% of the driver-unknown population.

NTRK1 rearrangements were identified in LUAD tumors considered negative by standard clinical assays, showing that even highly actionable kinase fusions can hide beneath standard negative designations.

The non-CAGA LUAD work notes that integrated profiling rescued an occult ERBB2 activation mechanism through structural rearrangement and super-enhancer formation despite absence of canonical actionable ERBB2 alterations in the initial screening logic.

The review literature explicitly generalizes this rescue phenomenon: broad hybrid-capture sequencing can identify actionable alterations in tumors otherwise negative by earlier genomic testing approaches.

Driver-negative labels based only on EGFR/ALK or EGFR/KRAS/ALK exclusion are especially unstable because subsequent profiling identifies ERBB2 exon 20 insertion, KRAS mutations, RET/ROS1/NRG1/NTRK1 rearrangements, and noncanonical EGFR lesions in such cohorts.

This law is also supported negatively: cohorts with only targeted-panel negativity still retain sizable unexplained groups, but those studies themselves note that broader exome/genome sequencing is needed to identify hidden drivers.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already established in multiple studies that broader profiling can rescue occult drivers in panel-negative LUAD, including rare fusions, splice events, rare EGFR variants, and structural/regulatory alterations.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit law-like framing that assay breadth itself is a first-order determinant of the measured driver-negative fraction, and that 'driver-negative LUAD' should be interpreted hierarchically by confidence of exclusion before any biological subtype theory is applied.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This statement is a synthesis of many existing rescue observations into a general organizing principle for the field, rather than a newly discovered molecular mechanism.
references: Campbell (2024) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [explicit WES-to-WGS false-negative rescue; closest prior formulation of assay-limited negativity]
Takeuchi (2012) RET fusion discovery in clinically negative LUAD [classic rescue of hidden fusion after limited testing]
Sun et al. broad hybrid-capture sequencing of standard-testing-negative LUAD [direct assay-expansion rescue concept referenced in the evidence]
Shibata (2024) ERBB2 super-enhancer mechanism in non-CAGA LUAD [shows that integrated structural/regulatory profiling rescues actionable biology missed by canonical DNA logic]

theory_statements.statement_name: Canonical-output convergence law
theory_statements.theory_statement: When a driver-negative LUAD shows high MAPK and/or PI3K-AKT pathway output, the most likely hidden causal architecture is not a wholly novel downstream biology but a noncanonical route back into canonical oncogenic signaling, such as RasGAP loss, PTEN loss, rare RAF-family mutation, splice-driven MET activation, fusion-driven RTK/ligand signaling, enhancer hijacking, amplification, or phosphoprotein activation without a canonical hotspot lesion.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to strict or moderate-confidence driver-negative human LUADs and oncogene-negative mouse LUAD models with direct or inferred evidence of elevated pERK, pAKT, phospho-S6, phosphoproteomic MAPK/PI3K signatures, or functional sensitivity to SHP2/AKT/MEK/RTK blockade. Most applicable to resected LUAD, never-smoker LUAD, and non-CAGA/RPA-negative cohorts after at least moderate exclusion of canonical EGFR/KRAS/ALK/ROS1/RET/BRAF/MET/ERBB2 lesions.
special_cases: Immune-high or lineage-plastic tumors may show attenuated bulk phospho-signals despite focal signaling in malignant subclones.
Protein-state activation can be transient, ligand-driven, or microenvironmentally sustained rather than fixed by a stable genomic lesion.
Some pathway-high tumors may still harbor truly novel effectors, but current evidence suggests convergence on canonical output is common.

supporting_evidence.text: The strongest direct evidence is the oncogene-negative mouse/human RAS-PI3K model: combined loss of NF1, RASA1, and PTEN drove oncogene-negative LUAD in vivo, produced high pERK and pAKT, and defined a human Onc-negative RAS/PI3K subtype with pathway-targetable vulnerabilities.

A related human NSCLC subset with concurrent RASA1 and NF1 loss lacked canonical EGFR/KRAS/ALK/RET/ROS1/ERBB2/BRAF/MET exon 14 drivers and instead converged on MAPK/PI3K output with strong trametinib sensitivity, supporting RasGAP-loss convergence onto canonical signaling.

Rare coding driver substitutes also converge on canonical signaling: RIT1 mutations in oncogene-negative LUAD activated MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT, transformed cells and xenografts, and responded to PI3K/MEK blockade.

Rare RAF-family hotspot mutations in otherwise driver-poor LUAD likewise converged on MAPK activation and sorafenib/trametinib sensitivity, including ARAF S214C and paralogous ARAF/RAF1 CR2 hotspot lesions.

Splice-driven MET activation is another direct convergence route: MET exon 14 skipping occurred in canonical-driver-negative tumors, was recurrently found in driver-unknown cohorts, and in functional studies sustained ligand-dependent MET/MAPK signaling by preventing CBL-mediated downregulation.

Fusion-driven RTK activation repeatedly rescued negative tumors into canonical pathway biology, including KIF5B-RET, ROS1 fusions, NTRK1 fusions, NTRK2 fusions, novel RTK fusions, and ALK/RET/ROS1-type fusions in broader cohorts.

Ligand-driven fusion biology also converges on canonical RTK output: CD74-NRG1 and related NRG1 fusions in pan-negative mucinous LUAD activated ERBB2/ERBB3 and PI3K-AKT signaling, with phospho-ERBB3 enrichment and functional dependence on the NRG1-ERBB axis.

Structural/regulatory rewiring can restore canonical receptor signaling without canonical mutations: ERBB2 super-enhancer/rearrangement in non-CAGA LUAD caused HER2 overexpression and sensitivity to poziotinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan.

EGFR pathway output can also be restored without canonical kinase-domain mutation: EGFR amplification occurred independently of EGFR mutation and correlated with higher EGFR expression; EGFR extracellular-domain mutation M277E transformed cells and xenografts and responded to erlotinib/cetuximab; EGFR exon 25/26 structural deletion was oncogenic and erlotinib-sensitive.

Outlier kinase expression without canonical rearrangement suggests an additional convergence route at the protein or transcript abundance level: ROS1 and FGFR3 outlier-expression cases occurred almost exclusively in driver-unknown samples despite absent FISH-detectable rearrangement.

Proteogenomics independently supports the same law: EGFR-mutant LUAD showed strong SHP2 Y62 phosphorylation without RNA/protein abundance change, demonstrating that signaling-state activation can reside at the phosphoprotein layer rather than the DNA hotspot layer alone.

In early-stage East Asian LUAD, a small EGFR-wild-type subset with high MAPK phosphoproteomic activity was mostly explained by KRAS mutations, reinforcing the principle that when pathway output is high, hidden or alternate canonical activators are often nearby.

The TCGA/combined LUAD oncogene-negative analysis reached the same conclusion at the DNA level: MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, NF1 mutation, RIT1 mutation, and MET exon 14 skipping were specifically enriched in tumors lacking canonical RTK/RAS/RAF drivers, expanding targetable lesions within the same pathway logic rather than replacing it.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Many individual studies already show that rare coding, structural, splice, or suppressor-loss lesions in ostensibly negative LUAD feed MAPK or PI3K signaling.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel element is the unifying rule that high MAPK/PI3K output in a driver-negative LUAD should be treated as a convergence signature of covert canonical signaling reinstatement, regardless of whether the lesion is coding, structural, noncoding, ligand-based, or proteomic.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a cross-layer synthesis rather than a new single mechanism; it reorganizes disparate findings into a practical explanatory law for pathway-high driver-negative tumors.
references: Campbell (2024) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [strongest existing support for suppressor-loss convergence on RAS/PI3K output]
The Cancer Genome Atlas LUAD integrated profiling [shows MET/ERBB2/NF1/RIT1 as noncanonical entries into the same pathway space]
Shibata (2024) ERBB2 super-enhancer mechanism [structural-regulatory convergence on HER2 signaling]
Drilon/Doebele-era fusion studies on RET/ROS1/NRG1/NTRK/MET exon 14 [canonical examples of covert pathway reinstatement]

theory_statements.statement_name: Residual noncanonical-state law
theory_statements.theory_statement: After exhaustive exclusion of covert canonical signaling drivers, the remaining true residual oncogene-negative LUADs are more likely to be maintained by state variables at chromatin, lineage, immune, or metabolic layers than by a single undiscovered recurrent kinase gene.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to the strict residual class of human LUAD after broad genomic and transcriptomic exclusion of canonical RTK/RAS/RAF and major fusion/splice/amplification lesions, especially cohorts with WGS, RNA-seq, methylation, copy-number, structural, or proteogenomic profiling. This law is not intended for low-confidence panel-negative cohorts.
special_cases: Some residual tumors may still harbor undiscovered rare coding drivers below current cohort power, particularly intermediate-frequency genes or low-VAF subclones.
Residual tumors can still contain downstream oncogene amplifications or tumor suppressor losses that partially overlap with canonical pathway biology.
Bulk-omics residual states may mix tumor-intrinsic and microenvironment-derived signals.

supporting_evidence.text: The clearest WGS-based residual class, RPA(-)G LUAD, lacked a single recurrent replacement oncogene and instead showed enrichment for TP53, KEAP1, SMARCA4, STK11 loss, ILF2 promoter mutation, complex structural variation, and downstream amplifications such as MYC, MCL1, MYCL, MDM2, CCNE1, KAT6A, and NKX2-1.

That WGS study explicitly concluded that no unique recurrent genetic lesion compensated for absence of RTK/RAS/RAF alterations, supporting heterogeneity of residual biology rather than substitution by one new kinase driver.

Non-CAGA LUAD likewise emphasized regulatory and epigenetic dysregulation: most MAML2-locus alterations were noncoding, associated with suppressed H3K27ac enhancer activity, reduced MAML2 expression, and methylation-linked repression, without direct proof of a replacement kinase driver.

In the same non-CAGA setting, ERBB2 super-enhancer/rearrangement cases were explicitly a small rescued subset, implying that the broader residual cohort cannot be explained mainly by recurrent covert ERBB2-like lesions.

EGFR/ALK-negative never-smoker LUAD (NENA) was organized not by a dominant hidden oncogene but by an estrogen-signaling/SRC/GPER1 state associated with STK11 mutation, chr14/chr21 deletion, and LLGL2/ST14 hypomethylation.

A separate driver-gene-negative LUAD cohort was organized around glycolytic state rather than a hidden kinase lesion, with MIF-high AT2-like malignant cells and a proposed MIF-HIF-1α axis defining glycolysis-dependent biology.

Proteogenomic subtype work in EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD also resolved the negative cohort into proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism states rather than into one replacement oncogene class.

The APOLLO/CPTAC expression-proteomic frameworks similarly identify PP and PI states, where many tumors lack targetable oncogene mutations yet are defined by metabolic rewiring, immune activation/exclusion, or tumor suppressor context rather than a single novel kinase lesion.

Piano/driver-poor never-smoker LUADs are characterized by low SCNA, low TMB, long telomeres, high subclonality, stemness/development signatures, and scarcity of known recurrent drivers, supporting a stemness/quiescence or lineage-state explanation for at least part of the residual class.

Steady never-smoker LUAD is another driver-sparse state-like subtype characterized by high alveolar lineage fidelity, low proliferation, low immune suppression, and better prognosis rather than a specific replacement oncogene.

Early LUAD progression studies argue that histologic progression from lepidic to solid is often driven by non-genetic transcriptional and microenvironmental reprogramming rather than acquisition of new recurrent genetic drivers, reinforcing the plausibility of state-maintained residual disease.

Single-cell and spatial studies identify KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate cells (KACs), angiogenic tip endothelial cells, and matrix CAF states as progression-relevant programs in LUAD, suggesting that some residual driver-negative tumors may be sustained by cell-state ecology rather than canonical oncogene dominance.

SETD2/KDM5C/KMT2B-associated CN-independent ASE and broader epigenetic-modifier-linked allele-specific expression provide a mechanistic route by which chromatin dysregulation can generate transcriptomic driver states without new copy-number or canonical kinase lesions.

Residual classes are also captured by prognosis-only frameworks based on immune or transcriptomic states, such as IRGP signatures and second-level non-CAGA clustering, further implying that downstream state architecture remains informative even when recurrent genomic drivers are not.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: State-based explanations for subsets of LUAD already exist in methylation, immune, proteomic, lineage, and metabolic studies, and WGS work has shown that some residual RTK/RAS/RAF-negative tumors remain heterogeneous after deeper profiling.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that these state variables should be treated as the expected dominant explanation of the strict residual class specifically after covert-canonical and false-negative cases are removed, rather than being viewed as secondary annotations on top of a mainly undiscovered-kinase model.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: No single cited study explicitly formulates this residual-class hierarchy; the statement is therefore a synthetic, higher-order theory built by integrating WGS-negative, non-CAGA, proteogenomic, and single-cell evidence.
references: Campbell (2024) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [shows residual heterogeneity after deep exclusion]
Shibata (2024) ERBB2 super-enhancer mechanism in non-CAGA LUAD [demonstrates that rescued structural drivers are minority events within a broader residual cohort]
Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD without EGFR or ALK Alterations [state-defined negative cohort]
Exploring the Glycolytic Mechanisms in Driver Gene-Negative LUAD [metabolic-state-defined negative cohort]
CHUV multi-region LUAD plasticity study [non-genetic progression logic relevant to residual biology]

new_predictions_likely: If a prospectively collected LUAD cohort is profiled in stages (panel, RNA fusion/splice, WES, WGS/SV/noncoding, phosphoproteomics), the apparent driver-negative fraction will shrink at each stage, with the steepest drop occurring when RNA splice/fusion discovery and WGS/SV/noncoding analysis are added.

If strict residual driver-negative LUADs are stratified by pERK/pAKT or phosphoproteomic MAPK/PI3K output, the pathway-high group will be enriched for negative-regulator lesions (NF1, RASA1, PTEN, STK11/KEAP1-related pathway release), rare RAF/RIT1 lesions, RTK/ligand fusions, exon-skipping events, or regulatory overexpression mechanisms.

If ERBB2/EGFR/ROS1/FGFR3 outlier-expression cases lacking canonical lesions undergo long-read WGS plus Hi-C/H3K27ac profiling, a nontrivial fraction will show enhancer hijacking, local SV rewiring, or focal regulatory gains rather than true expression-only phenomena.

If a strict residual cohort is separated after removal of all covert-canonical cases, the remaining tumors will cluster more strongly by metabolic, immune, chromatin, or lineage state than by recurrent kinase-gene mutation.
new_predictions_unknown: If residual pathway-low strict oncogene-negative LUADs are sampled longitudinally from premalignant lesions, some will initiate as lineage-state or injury-state tumors and only later acquire MAPK/PI3K-high maintenance states, implying sequential transition from state-driven initiation to covert-canonical maintenance.

If phosphoproteomic pathway-output classifiers are applied prospectively to strict driver-negative LUAD, they may outperform genomic labels in predicting treatment response, potentially redefining precision therapy around pathway state rather than lesion class.

If airway microbiome or chronic epithelial injury contributes to the state-driven residual branch, spatial microbial signatures or epithelial injury programs will co-segregate with KAC-like, piano-like, or estrogen-signaling states independently of canonical kinase output.

If some residual tumors are maintained by ligand ecology rather than stable intrinsic lesions, matched co-culture with fibroblasts/macrophages will restore phospho-RTK activity and drug sensitivity patterns not seen in tumor-only culture.
negative_experiments: Profile a rigorously WGS/RNA-negative LUAD cohort with phosphoproteomics and test whether most pathway-high tumors still map to noncanonical canonical-signaling mechanisms. If many pathway-high tumors remain unexplained after broad genomic, structural, regulatory, and protein-state analysis, the canonical-output convergence law is weakened.

Prospectively compare panel-negative, RNA-augmented, WES-negative, and WGS-negative LUAD classification on identical specimens with controlled purity. If assay breadth does not reduce the apparent negative fraction, the ascertainment-compression law would fail.

In a strict residual cohort, test whether lineage/immune/metabolic/chromatin states are actually enriched relative to rescued false-negative cases. If no such enrichment appears, the residual noncanonical-state law is undermined.

Functionally test SHP2/AKT/MEK/RTK dependence in pathway-high strict residual tumors. If they are broadly insensitive despite high canonical phospho-output, the theory overstates the relationship between output convergence and hidden canonical addiction.

unaccounted_for.text: Very rare coding candidates such as POU4F2, ZKSCAN1, SOS1, ARHGAP35, ARAF/RAF1 hotspots, and SIK2 duplication may represent bona fide additional drivers that do not fit cleanly into either the covert-canonical branch or the state-driven branch on current evidence.

Some recurrence-risk biomarker studies based on IRGPs, circulating tumor cells, S-MMR scores, or recurrence-associated RNA-protein discordance may track downstream behavior rather than core driver biology and are therefore only partially addressed by this theory.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some panel-negative cohorts remain fairly large even after broad targeted testing and do not yet reveal a clear rescued mechanism, leaving open the possibility that a substantial fraction of negatives are not simply covert-canonical or state-defined under current assays.

Driver-poor stemness-like subtypes such as piano or steady are not consistently accompanied by direct phosphoproteomic evidence of low canonical output, so the boundary between covert-canonical and residual-state disease may blur in bulk tumors.

Some studies identify additional rare coding drivers in ostensibly negative tumors, implying that the residual class may still contain more low-frequency gene-defined subtypes than this theory foregrounds.

special_cases: In mucinous adenocarcinoma, ligand-fusion biology (for example NRG1) and lineage-plasticity biology can co-occur, so canonical-output convergence and lineage-state explanations may both be valid in the same tumor.
In EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker cohorts, hormone-like or estrogen/SRC programs may dominate even when no obvious RTK lesion is present, making pathway state more informative than lesion class.
In low-purity, FFPE-limited, or multi-regionally heterogeneous tumors, apparent pathway absence may reflect sampling or detection bias rather than genuine biological absence.
This theory is intended for LUAD and should not be naively generalized to squamous-only or neuroendocrine lung cancers, where the driver landscape and lineage logic differ.

references: Campbell (2024) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [establishes one major covert-canonical branch via suppressor loss but not the full hierarchy]

Campbell (2024) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [shows false-negative rescue and residual heterogeneity, but not the pathway-output/state bifurcation]

Shibata (2024) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in LUAD without clinically actionable genetic alterations [supports structural-regulatory rescue branch]
Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [supports state-driven residual branch]
Exploring the Glycolytic Mechanisms in Driver Gene-Negative LUAD [supports metabolic residual branch]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work recognizes hidden rare drivers, tumor suppressor cooperation, and state-based negative cohorts in selected LUAD studies.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory integrates them into a hierarchical framework: first correct for assay-limited false negativity, then split the strict residual class into covert-canonical output disease versus true state-driven residual disease.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No cited paper explicitly formulates this two-stage explanatory architecture with driver-negativity confidence as the primary stratifier and canonical-output state as the secondary splitter, so the whole-theory integration is best classified as new.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1881.name: Driver-negative LUAD is a state-space mosaic of four recurrent ecological modes
theory-1881.description: True driver-negative lung adenocarcinoma is better understood as a mosaic of recurring system states than as a single leftover category. Across the evidence, most unexplained tumors fall predominantly into one or more of four ecological modes: covert canonical signaling, lineage/quiescence-plasticity, immune ecology, and metabolic-redox stress. Different studies recover different fractions of these modes because negativity definitions, assay depth, and tissue context differ. Rare gene events matter mainly insofar as they seed or stabilize one of these modes.
theory-1881.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Driver-negative LUAD is a state-space mosaic of four recurrent ecological modes
components.theory_description: True driver-negative lung adenocarcinoma is better understood as a mosaic of recurring system states than as a single leftover category. Across the evidence, most unexplained tumors fall predominantly into one or more of four ecological modes: covert canonical signaling, lineage/quiescence-plasticity, immune ecology, and metabolic-redox stress. Different studies recover different fractions of these modes because negativity definitions, assay depth, and tissue context differ. Rare gene events matter mainly insofar as they seed or stabilize one of these modes.

theory_statements.statement_name: Four-mode partition law
theory_statements.theory_statement: Driver-negative or driver-poor LUADs recurrently organize into four dominant systems-level modes: (1) covert canonical signaling, where hidden lesions or suppressor losses reconstitute RTK/RAS/RAF or PI3K output; (2) lineage/quiescence-plasticity, where lineage identity, stem-like quiescence, or epithelial-state transitions dominate; (3) immune ecology, where prognosis and treatment are governed primarily by immune infiltration, exclusion, or checkpoint state; and (4) metabolic-redox stress, where glycolysis, ferroptosis resistance, NRF2/stress adaptation, or hormone-SRC metabolic wiring dominate. Different cohorts enrich different modes because assay layer and cohort ascertainment determine which state variables are visible.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinoma cohorts called driver-negative, oncogene-negative, non-CAGA, RTK/RAS/RAF-negative, EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker, or driver-poor by partial or comprehensive profiling. Most accurate in studies with multi-omic or orthogonal validation; also applies as an interpretive framework to adjacent driver-poor LUAD-like states in never-smoker cohorts and histology-linked subgroups such as mucinous adenocarcinoma.
special_cases: A single tumor can occupy multiple modes simultaneously, especially advanced tumors, mixed-histology tumors, or spatially heterogeneous tumors.
Mode assignment can be distorted when negativity is defined by narrow panels; in those settings, some apparent 'modes' may actually be false-negative covert canonical cases.
Some proliferative copy-number amplicon states (for example TPX2, WHSC1L1, 20q11, 8p12) may cut across the four-mode structure as an orthogonal mitotic/chromosomal branch.
The framework is intended for LUAD and LUAD-like tumors; lung squamous and neuroendocrine comparators are informative analogies but not part of the core target domain.

supporting_evidence.text: Large residual fractions remain unexplained after canonical driver testing in multiple LUAD cohorts, but the residuals differ substantially by assay breadth: 22/202 East Asian never-smoker LUADs were pan-negative after EGFR/HER2/KRAS/BRAF/ALK/ROS1 testing; 267/733 LCMC tumors had no driver on a 10-gene multiplex panel; 189/626 single-center LUADs were driver-gene negative on an eight-gene panel; and >20% of EAGLE LUAD lacked nonsynonymous mutations in known LUAD genes. This argues against a single homogeneous residual class.

A stricter RTK/RAS/RAF-negative framework still leaves substantial residual disease, but deeper profiling rescues only part of it: 118/501 TCGA LUAD were initially RTK/RAS/RAF-negative by WES/RNA-seq, and after WGS 57 remained RPA(-)G while 28/85 were reclassified as occult pathway-positive. This indicates both assay-failure cases and a true residual biology.

Covert canonical signaling mode is supported by multiple occult or noncanonical pathway-restoring lesions in apparently negative tumors: MET exon 14 skipping in canonical-driver-negative LUAD, KIF5B-RET and ROS1 fusions emerging from EGFR/KRAS/ALK-negative or pan-negative cases, NTRK1 rearrangements discovered in standard-testing-negative LUAD, EGFR extracellular-domain M277E in a pan-negative case, ERBB2 enhancer-hijacking/super-enhancer overexpression in non-CAGA LUAD, RTK/RAS/RAF rescue by WGS in WES-negative tumors, and combinatorial NF1/RASA1/PTEN loss or RASA1/NF1 co-loss restoring RAS/PI3K signaling without a canonical oncogene.

Lineage/quiescence-plasticity mode is supported by multiple studies where biology is governed less by a discrete hidden kinase lesion and more by cell-state identity: piano LCINS/LUAD is low-SCNA, long-telomere, stemness-like and driver-poor; steady never-smoker LUAD is alveolar, low-proliferative and driver-sparse; NKX2-1-amplified LUAD behaves as a lineage-survival state; NKX2-1-loss mucinous/gastric programs and NKX2-1-low mucinous tumors adopt alternative lineage identities; KAC intermediate cells mark an early plastic state linked to KRAS programs; and lepidic-to-solid progression is driven mainly by non-genetic reprogramming and microenvironment remodeling rather than recurrent new drivers.

Immune-ecology mode is supported by multiple driver-negative or adjacent LUAD frameworks in which prognosis and therapy predictions are primarily immune-state dependent rather than oncogene dependent: PI subtype is IFNγ/STAT1/PD-L1/CTLA4 high; driver-negative IRGP and immune-pair cohorts split tumors into checkpoint-high versus chemo-sensitive states; METCor and iCluster subtypes separate checkpoint-high dysfunctional versus less suppressed tumors; HLA-A loss is proposed as an immune-evasion lesion; and spatial progression studies show transitions from immune desert to inflamed to excluded/suppressive states.

Metabolic-redox stress mode is supported by several distinct driver-negative or noncanonical cohorts: MIF-HIF-1α glycolytic driver-negative LUAD; PP and STK11/KEAP1-rich proliferative metabolic LUAD; Subtype 4 FGL1/CPS1/HNF1A-STK11 rewiring; KL LKB1/KEAP1 stress adaptation and HSP90 sensitivity; KEAP1/NRF2 phosphoproteomic activation; ferroptosis-risk and ferroptosis-resistant subtypes; APOBEC-high female EGFR-WT states; pollution- and aristolochic-signature never-smoker states; and NENA estrogen/SRC/GPER1-associated noncanonical signaling.

Proteogenomic and phosphoproteomic studies repeatedly show that genome-only negativity is not equivalent to pathway inactivity: EGFR-mutant tumors show SHP2 phosphorylation without RNA/protein increase; EGFR-WT tumors can still show high MAPK phosphosignaling; FGFR3 S408 phospho-activation can activate PI3K-AKT-mTOR without FGFR3 abundance change; and ROS1/FGFR3 outlier expression can occur without detectable rearrangement. This supports state-based categorization rather than DNA-only labels.

Several studies explicitly suggest that driver negativity is partly a technical construct and partly a biological one: broader NGS rescued actionable alterations in standard-testing-negative LUAD; transcriptome sequencing rescued hidden EGFR mutations, MET exon 14 skipping, and kinase fusions; WGS rescued 33% of WES-negative RTK/RAS/RAF tumors; and multiregion sampling showed that a single biopsy misses candidate driver events in many NSCLCs.
This four-mode partition is an inductive synthesis from the above recurring cross-paper patterns rather than an explicit prior literature taxonomy.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work already defines many partial subtype systems: hidden canonical driver rescue, immune-high versus immune-cold expression classes, stem-like or lineage-plastic states, and metabolic/redox rewiring states. Several papers also separately argue that residual LUAD is heterogeneous rather than one bucket.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is to unify these disparate residual-biology observations into four recurrent ecological modes and to treat rare genes mainly as state-entry lesions rather than as the primary ontology of driver-negative LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: No cited paper explicitly proposes a four-mode ecological taxonomy spanning covert signaling, lineage plasticity, immune ecology, and metabolic-redox stress for driver-negative LUAD. The theory is therefore synthetic and new, though strongly grounded in related subtype literature.
references: Campbell (2023) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [covert-canonical oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K state]
Sherlock-Lung consortium (2024) The mutagenic forces shaping the genomic landscape of lung cancer in never smokers [piano/mezzo-forte/forte state system, related to lineage-quiescence mode]
Gillette (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [proteogenomic state logic and genome-protein discordance relevant to mode framework]

NENA proteogenomic study (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker Lung Adenocarcinoma Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [metabolic-redox/hormone-SRC branch]
CHUV multiregion study (2024) histopathology-guided multiregion LUAD progression [lineage-plasticity and immune-ecology transition branch]

theory_statements.statement_name: Mode-specific therapeutic law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In driver-negative LUAD, treatment sensitivity follows dominant ecological mode more reliably than residual-gene naming alone: covert canonical signaling predicts benefit from pathway-restoring kinase-axis therapy (for example RTK, SHP2, MEK, AKT, HER2, TRK, RET, MET); immune-ecology predicts checkpoint or immune-modulating strategies; metabolic-redox mode predicts sensitivity to ferroptosis, redox, glutamine, SRC, HSP90, or cell-cycle-metabolic combinations; and lineage/quiescence-plasticity predicts benefit from interception or state-targeting approaches such as anti-angiogenic, stromal, developmental, or lineage-biomarker-guided therapy.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to driver-negative or driver-poor LUADs after mode assignment by integrated DNA/RNA/protein/phosphoprotein/spatial data, and to translational models such as organoids, PDXs, and genetically engineered mice that recapitulate the same mode. Most reliable when pathway activity is measured functionally rather than inferred only from DNA.
special_cases: Tumors with overlapping modes may require combination therapy across modes rather than a single mode-matched drug.
Gene-specific sensitivity can supersede mode assignment when a strong validated hidden canonical driver is present, such as RET, ROS1, NTRK1, MET exon 14, or EGFR M277E.
Immune predictions can be confounded by purity and stromal composition in bulk assays.
