supporting_evidence.text: Covert canonical mode already has direct mechanism-matched therapy evidence: oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K tumors respond to SHP2 plus AKT inhibition; RASA1/NF1 co-loss is trametinib-sensitive; EGFR M277E is sensitive to erlotinib and cetuximab; NTRK1 fusions are sensitive to TRKA inhibitors; KIF5B-RET is sensitive to RET-active multikinase inhibitors in Ba/F3 models; and ERBB2 super-enhancer tumors respond to poziotinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan but not afatinib.

Immune-ecology mode has treatment-stratifying evidence: PI tumors show high PD-L1, CTLA4, IFNγ signaling and are proposed as checkpoint-sensitive; driver-negative immune-pair/IRGP high-risk tumors show higher checkpoint expression and distinct chemo-vs-immunotherapy predictions; METCorC1/iC2/CNVCorC1 have high checkpoint and high TIDE consistent with likely resistance; and KP-like inflamed contexts are more checkpoint-responsive than KL-like cold contexts.

Metabolic-redox mode has multiple candidate liabilities: saracatinib is prioritized and active in NENA/SRC-estrogen-signaling models; KL/LKB1-deficient tumors show HSP90 sensitivity; Subtype 4 FGL1/CPS1-high cells are more sensitive to docetaxel and mTOR inhibitors; PP and STK11/KEAP1-rich states are linked to glutaminase, SCD, and ferroptosis-related strategies; and MIF pathway inhibition (ISO-1) suppresses glycolytic effects in driver-negative LUAD models.

Lineage/quiescence-plasticity mode points to non-classical intervention logic: AIS3/tip EC/mCAF studies nominate anti-angiogenic or stromal interception, L2S scores stratify aggressive pattern progression, KAC-rich precursor-like organoids respond to KRAS(G12D) inhibition before overt advanced disease, and SSN A1/A2 progression states are linked to ER-stress-targeting strategies such as PDI inhibition.

The same genomic label can imply different therapies depending on state context: STK11 links to immune-excluded KL/PP disease, to FGL1/CPS1 Subtype 4 mTOR addiction, and to NENA estrogen/SRC signaling, so therapy should not be assigned by STK11 alone.

This law is partially inferred by synthesis: because mode-defining readouts repeatedly outperform simple mutation labels for prognosis, immune state, and drug associations, dominant mode should be a stronger therapeutic variable than gene identity alone in the driver-negative residual class.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Many individual studies propose subtype-specific or lesion-specific therapies, such as SHP2/AKT for oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K tumors, saracatinib for NENA, or checkpoint therapy for immune-high LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel claim is that all major driver-negative LUAD therapies can be organized and prospectively assigned by ecological mode, and that this should outperform residual-gene naming in the unexplained fraction.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a theory-level therapeutic unification rather than a standard existing framework. It is therefore new, though closely built from multiple existing lesion- and subtype-specific treatment observations.
references: Campbell (2023) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [SHP2/AKT branch]
RASA1/NF1 study (2018) RASA1 and NF1 are preferentially co-mutated and define a distinct genetic subset of smoking-associated NSCLC sensitive to MEK inhibition [covert-canonical branch]
NENA proteogenomic study (2024) estrogen signaling/SRC in EGFR-ALK-negative NSLA [metabolic-redox/hormone branch]
APOLLO/CPTAC subtype studies [immune and proliferative state-linked treatment implications]
Driver-negative glycolysis study (2024) MIF-HIF-1α axis [metabolic branch]

theory_statements.statement_name: Lineage-gates-driver law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In driver-negative LUAD, lineage state and epithelial cell identity constrain which hidden mechanisms are oncogenically accessible: mucinous or NKX2-1-low states preferentially admit ligand-fusion or gastric-program mechanisms; stem-like or quiescent states preferentially admit low-burden driver-poor evolution; and terminal-respiratory-unit or EGFR-compatible states preferentially admit covert receptor-kinase signaling lesions.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to LUAD subsets with strong lineage-state resolution, including mucinous/invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, NKX2-1-high TRU-like tumors, piano/steady-like stemness states, and KAC/lepidic precursor-like states. Best supported in never-smoker, low-smoking-signature, and histology-resolved cohorts.
special_cases: Lineage state can evolve over time, so the accessible hidden-driver class is dynamic rather than fixed.
Some canonical lesions such as KRAS or TP53 can later override lineage constraints and produce mixed or dedifferentiated states.
Lineage-compatible covert canonical lesions can coexist with apparently driver-poor states, so lineage bias is probabilistic rather than absolute.

supporting_evidence.text: Mucinous and NKX2-1-low states are specifically linked to ligand-fusion or alternative lineage mechanisms: CD74-NRG1 and related NRG1 fusions are enriched in invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma and never-smoking women; KRAS-negative IMAs harbor CD74-NRG1, SLC3A2-NRG1, EZR-ERBB4, TRIM24-BRAF, and KIAA1468-RET; NKX2-1 loss drives gastric/mucinous programs and latent gastric differentiation; and mucinous LUADs often lack NKX2-1 while expressing gastric markers.

Stem-like or quiescent states are linked to driver poverty rather than classic oncogene addiction: piano tumors are low-SCNA, low-TMB, long-telomere and rare for recurrent drivers; driver-poor piano-LUAD is hypothesized to arise from quiescent adult stem-like cells; steady tumors are alveolar/TRU-like, low-proliferative and driver-sparse; and ARID1A/NOTCH1 are proposed as quiescence-exit lesions in this context.

TRU-like or terminal respiratory-unit states are closely aligned with covert receptor signaling: TRU is enriched for EGFR mutations and receptor signaling; ALK fusion tumors fall into TRU/C4-like proteomic space and often lack other key drivers; EGFR phosphoproteomic clusters show strong downstream receptor signaling despite limited RNA concordance; and NKX2-1 amplification creates a lineage-survival subtype in LUAD.

Early epithelial intermediate states prefigure later hidden-driver accessibility: KACs are intermediate AT2-to-tumor cells enriched near tumors and associated with KRAS programs; lepidic-to-solid and multi-region progression studies show pattern evolution without recurrent pattern-specific genetic lesions; and AIS3/tip EC/mCAF studies indicate that state transition precedes invasive biology.
This lineage-gating law is logically inferred from the repeated co-alignment of hidden mechanism class with histology/lineage state across mucinous, TRU-like, stem-like, and precursor-state studies.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Histology-specific driver enrichment and lineage dependence are already well known in LUAD, especially for mucinous NRG1 fusion disease, TRU/EGFR disease, and NKX2-1-dependent states.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the explicit statement that lineage acts as a gate on which hidden-driver classes can be selected in driver-negative LUAD, integrating mucinous, TRU-like, stem-like, and precursor-state evidence into one law.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: No cited study formulates this as a generalized gating law across the residual driver-negative landscape. The idea is therefore new, though strongly related to established lineage-dependence and histology-specific driver literature.
references: Mucinous adenocarcinoma NRG1 studies [histology-specific hidden driver access]
NKX2-1 lineage studies [lineage plasticity and lineage-survival dependence]
Sherlock-Lung piano/steady analyses [stem-like driver-poor access]
APOLLO/CPTAC TRU analyses [TRU-compatible receptor signaling states]

new_predictions_likely: If a prospective strict driver-negative LUAD cohort is profiled with WGS, RNA-seq, phosphoproteomics, methylation, and spatial transcriptomics, most tumors will cluster more reproducibly by the four ecological modes than by any residual-gene list.

If tumors are assigned to the covert canonical mode using functional pathway readouts, those tumors will show higher response rates to SHP2/MEK/AKT/RTK-matched therapies than tumors assigned only by partial DNA negativity labels.

If mucinous or NKX2-1-low driver-negative LUADs are systematically tested with broad fusion and enhancer-hijack assays, they will be enriched for ligand-fusion, ERBB-family, or lineage-rewired mechanisms relative to non-mucinous driver-negative LUAD.
If piano/steady-like driver-poor tumors are profiled for telomere length, subclonality, and stemness markers, they will segregate from covert-canonical tumors despite overlapping panel negativity.

If driver-negative LUAD organoids are stratified by immune-ecology versus metabolic-redox mode, checkpoint-modulating interventions will preferentially affect the former while ferroptosis/redox/SRC/glutamine interventions will preferentially affect the latter.
new_predictions_unknown: If the four-mode taxonomy is complete, single-cell multi-omics may reveal that apparently distinct residual entities such as NENA, piano, and PP are not separate diseases but stable attractor states connected by rare transition routes.

If lineage truly gates hidden-driver access, forced reprogramming of LUAD organoids from TRU-like to mucinous/NKX2-1-low states will alter which occult fusions or enhancer-rewired events are transforming.

If metabolic-redox and immune modes are mechanistically coupled through ferroptosis and antigen presentation, pharmacologic ferroptosis induction in STK11/KEAP1-rich driver-negative tumors may convert part of the immune-cold class into checkpoint-responsive disease.

If quiescence-derived piano tumors arise years before radiographic disease, matched normal-appearing airway/alveolar tissue from such patients will contain expanded long-telomere, stem-like epithelial fields with partial driver-poor transcriptional programs.
negative_experiments: Profile a large, prospectively accrued strict driver-negative LUAD cohort across institutions. If the four proposed modes do not emerge reproducibly or do not meaningfully outperform a simpler continuum model, the theory is weakened.
Test whether mode assignment predicts drug response better than mutation-panel negativity, smoking status, TMB, or histology alone. If it does not, the mode framework may be overfit or nonessential.

Evaluate whether mucinous/NKX2-1-low, TRU-like, and piano/steady-like tumors are actually enriched for their predicted hidden-driver classes. Failure of enrichment would falsify the lineage-gates-driver law.
Perturb representative lesions from each mode in matched organoid/PDX systems. If mode-matched vulnerabilities are not preferentially observed, the mode-specific therapeutic law would be undermined.

unaccounted_for.text: A subset of amplification-centered mitotic candidates such as TPX2, WHSC1L1, 20q11.2, and unresolved 22q11 amplicons do not map cleanly into one of the four proposed modes and may represent an orthogonal proliferative-chromosomal branch.
Some partial-testing cohorts may be dominated by technical false negatives rather than true residual biology, making mode assignment uncertain without deeper profiling.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some driver-sparse or favorable-prognosis lineage states are not free of canonical drivers: steady never-smoker tumors can still show EGFR enrichment, showing that lineage state and covert canonical signaling are not mutually exclusive.

EGFR-WT high-MAPK tumors in the Taiwan cohort were mostly explained by KRAS, indicating that some phosphoproteomic 'noncanonical' states collapse back into covert canonical signaling on deeper inspection.

special_cases: The four modes are ecological abstractions, not mutually exclusive genomic bins.
The same gene can mark different modes in different contexts, especially STK11 and KEAP1.
Partial-testing cohorts should be interpreted as lower-confidence members of the framework until occult canonical drivers are excluded by broader profiling.

references: Campbell (2023) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [related covert-canonical oncogene-negative subtype, not full four-mode taxonomy]
Sherlock-Lung consortium (2024) The mutagenic forces shaping the genomic landscape of lung cancer in never smokers [related piano/mezzo-forte/forte taxonomy, not residual LUAD ecological mode theory]
Gillette (2020) Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [related proteogenomic subtype framework, not driver-negative-specific ecological taxonomy]

NENA proteogenomic study (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker Lung Adenocarcinoma Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [related specialized noncanonical mode]
CHUV multiregion progression study (2024) [related nongenetic progression/immune-plasticity model]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The literature already contains several partial subtype systems and many single-axis explanations for unexplained LUAD, including hidden canonical drivers, immune states, stem-like lineages, and metabolic rewiring.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the explicit synthesis of these partial systems into a four-mode ecological taxonomy for driver-negative LUAD, with rare genes treated as entry lesions into recurrent system states.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: No provided source proposes this unified residual-state ontology. The theory is therefore new, though it is built by integrating multiple somewhat-related subtype and mechanism frameworks.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1882.name: Pathway-reinstatement without canonical oncogenes
theory-1882.description: A large fraction of ostensibly driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly pathway-negative. Instead, they achieve the same proliferative outputs usually produced by RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes through less obvious mechanisms: combinatorial loss of negative regulators of RAS and PI3K signaling, rare coding mutations outside standard hotspot panels, exon-skipping events, cryptic fusions, focal amplifications, and occasionally protein-level activation uncoupled from obvious DNA lesions. The central implication is that 'driver-negative' LUAD is often a failure of driver representation, not absence of oncogenic circuitry.
theory-1882.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Pathway-reinstatement without canonical oncogenes
components.theory_description: A large fraction of ostensibly driver-negative lung adenocarcinomas are not truly pathway-negative. Instead, they achieve the same proliferative outputs usually produced by RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes through less obvious mechanisms: combinatorial loss of negative regulators of RAS and PI3K signaling, rare coding mutations outside standard hotspot panels, exon-skipping events, cryptic fusions, focal amplifications, and occasionally protein-level activation uncoupled from obvious DNA lesions. The central implication is that 'driver-negative' LUAD is often a failure of driver representation, not absence of oncogenic circuitry.

theory_statements.statement_name: Hidden-pathway-reactivation law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In lung adenocarcinomas classified as lacking canonical RTK/RAS/RAF oncogenes, recurrent hidden lesions disproportionately converge on reactivation of MAPK and/or PI3K-AKT signaling, so true biological driver-negativity is rarer than panel-negative status.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to primary and metastatic LUADs initially classified as oncogene-negative, pan-negative, non-CAGA, or RTK/RAS/RAF-negative by targeted genotyping, WES, or incomplete clinical assays; strongest where deeper WGS, RNA-seq, fusion calling, exon-skipping analysis, or phospho/protein assays were subsequently performed.
special_cases: A residual subset remains unexplained even after WGS/RNA-seq and may use noncanonical lineage, immune, metabolic, or epigenetic mechanisms rather than direct RTK/RAS/RAF reactivation.

Some tumors called driver-negative by narrow clinical testing become canonical-driver-positive after broader profiling; this law concerns the whole class of such reclassifications and pathway-convergent rescues.
Protein-level activation can occur without a matching coding lesion, so genomic negativity does not imply signaling inactivity.

supporting_evidence.text: In comprehensive LUAD profiling, 38% of tumors were initially classified as oncogene-negative for known activating RTK/RAS/RAF events, but this same analysis identified MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, NF1 mutation, RIT1 mutation, and MET exon 14 skipping as recurrent alternative lesions in this subset, explicitly expanding targetable pathway alterations.

In WGS refinement of RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD, 28 of 85 cases initially negative by WES/RNA-seq were rescued as pathway-altered by WGS, including hidden KRAS mutations, EGFR/ARAF/MAPK1/SOS1 amplifications, NF1 and RASA1 deletions, and NRG1 fusions/mutations, demonstrating that a large fraction of apparent driver-negative cases are assay-limited false negatives.

Pan-negative clinical cohorts repeatedly yielded occult canonical or near-canonical drivers upon broader interrogation: EGFR extracellular-domain M277E in 1 of 143 pan-negative LUADs, ROS1 fusions in 2 of 202 East Asian never-smoker pan-negative cases, CD74-NRG1 in pan-negative never-smoker IMAs, and KIF5B-RET in triple-negative or double-negative lung adenocarcinomas.

RNA-seq of canonical-driver-negative LUAD uncovered multiple nonstandard kinase alterations including novel tyrosine kinase fusions and MET exon 14 skipping, and even rescued EGFR mutations missed by conventional screening.

Integrated transcriptomic meta-analysis of driver-unknown lung cancers showed that recurrent NRG1 fusions/outlier expression, Hippo pathway fusions, NF1 truncating events, and MET exon 14 skipping together explained about 16% of driver-unknown tumors, indicating that alternative recurrent lesions are common within the apparently negative pool.

Rare coding alterations in canonical signaling nodes can define true hidden-driver subsets within oncogene-negative LUAD, including RIT1 switch II mutations in 5 of 87 oncogene-negative TCGA tumors and recurrent RAF-family hotspot mutations in tumors lacking known major oncogenes.

Proteogenomic data show that pathway output can be present even when canonical DNA drivers are absent or incomplete: EGFR-mutant tumors had strong SHP2 Y62 phosphorylation without RNA or protein abundance change, and EGFR-wild-type tumors with high MAPK signaling existed in phosphoproteomic analysis, mostly explained by KRAS but showing that signaling-state assays can reveal biology missed by mutation labels.

The review literature explicitly argues that many LUADs lacking actionable mutations are rescued by deeper profiling and that broader genomic and proteogenomic assays are needed because standard testing misses actionable alterations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: It is already established that some panel-negative LUADs harbor occult fusions, amplifications, exon-skipping events, or rare coding mutations. The general idea of deeper sequencing rescuing hidden drivers is therefore known.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel synthesis is that these disparate findings are not isolated anecdotes but expressions of a higher-order law: most clinically important 'driver-negative' LUADs are better understood as pathway-reinstated tumors whose lesions are distributed across under-profiled molecular layers rather than absent altogether.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This theory is built from existing observations but integrates them into a broader explanatory framework that unifies WGS rescue, RNA-based rescue, and phosphoproteomic rescue under one pathway-convergence principle.
references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [found alternative oncogenic lesions within initially oncogene-negative LUAD]
Niu et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [WGS rescue of occult canonical pathway drivers]
Imielinski et al. (2012) Comprehensive transcriptome sequencing of lung adenocarcinoma [RNA-seq rescue of hidden EGFR, kinase fusions, and MET exon 14 skipping]
Takeuchi et al. (2012) KIF5B-RET fusion in lung adenocarcinoma [classic hidden-fusion rescue of triple-negative disease]
Zhang et al. (2023) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by super-enhancer formation in non-CAGA LUAD [noncanonical structural/epigenetic rescue of a targetable RTK state]

theory_statements.statement_name: Suppressor-convergence law
theory_statements.theory_statement: A major biologic subclass of true oncogene-negative LUAD is driven by combinatorial loss of negative regulators of RAS and PI3K signaling rather than by a single activated proto-oncogene; in this subclass, dual-pathway output is the driver-state and the genotype is distributed across multiple tumor-suppressor lesions.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to oncogene-negative LUADs lacking known proto-oncogene alterations but showing pERK/pAKT activation, especially tumors with NF1, RASA1, PTEN, STK11, KEAP1, or related suppressor loss; strongest in mouse models and human tumors validated by pathway IHC or pathway signatures.
special_cases: Single suppressor loss is often insufficient; the law concerns combinatorial or functionally convergent loss.
Some suppressor-defined tumors remain only moderately dependent on downstream inhibitors unless the full combinatorial state is present.
This law explains a substantial subtype, not all residual driver-negative tumors.

supporting_evidence.text: Autochthonous oncogene-negative LUAD modeling identified combined inactivation of Nf1, Rasa1, and Pten as the strongest tumor-initiating combination; triple loss was nearly as potent as KRASG12D, all mutational routes to the triple state were accessible, and the triple-mutant genotype had the highest relative fitness.

Human oncogene-negative LUADs validated this pathway-state model: over 60% had alterations in either RAS or PI3K pathways, 22% had alterations in both, about 45% of oncogene-negative tumors showed moderate-to-strong pERK and pAKT, and many PTEN-protein-low tumors lacked PTEN genomic mutation, implying convergent non-genomic suppressor loss as well.

RASA1 and NF1 co-inactivation defines a distinct smoking-associated NSCLC subset that is mutually exclusive with major canonical EGFR/KRAS/ALK/RET/ROS1/BRAF/METex14/ERBB2 drivers and strongly sensitive to MEK inhibition, illustrating a human suppressor-convergence route to MAPK addiction.

RPA(-)G and noncanonical LUAD analyses repeatedly find enrichment of suppressor loss rather than activated RTKs: TP53, KEAP1, STK11, SMARCA4, NF1, and RASA1 deletions or mutations are common in the WGS-confirmed negative subset.

STK11/LKB1 loss can create pathway-defined states even when sequence-level assignment is incomplete: STK11-wild-type KL tumors often still showed low LKB1 protein/mRNA and STK11 copy loss, implying functionally suppressor-negative states beyond simple mutation calls.

KEAP1-pathway loss in EGFR-driven LUAD demonstrates that suppressor alterations can dominate therapeutic phenotype even in oncogene-positive tumors, supporting the broader principle that suppressor-defined signaling states are biologically primary and can outcompete canonical oncogene labels.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Combinatorial suppressor loss in oncogene-negative LUAD, especially NF1/RASA1/PTEN, is already directly supported by recent mouse and human work. KRAS co-mutation subtype literature also established suppressor-defined states such as KL and KC.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel step is elevating these findings into a general law of oncogene-negative LUAD: the driver unit is often a pathway-state generated by distributed suppressor lesions rather than a single proto-oncogene lesion.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is closely related to existing experimental findings but adds a broader explanatory abstraction that unifies several suppressor-loss observations across cohorts and model systems.
references: Foggetti et al. (2024) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [direct basis for NF1/RASA1/PTEN convergence]
Sale et al. (2023) Fully accessible fitness landscape of oncogene-negative lung adenocarcinoma [fitness-law support for triple suppressor convergence]
Hayes et al. (2018) RASA1 and NF1 are preferentially co-mutated and define a distinct genetic subset of smoking-associated NSCLC sensitive to MEK inhibition [human co-loss subset]
Skoulidis et al. (2015) STK11/LKB1 and KEAP1 co-mutation subtypes of KRAS-mutant LUAD [pathway-state primacy over single-gene labels]

new_predictions_likely: If all panel-negative LUADs are profiled by tumor-normal WGS plus stranded RNA-seq plus phosphoproteomics, the fraction remaining unexplained by either canonical pathway lesions or suppressor-convergent pathway activation will fall substantially below the current clinical 'driver-negative' label.

If pERK/pAKT IHC and PTEN/NF1/RASA1 protein assessment are added prospectively to oncogene-negative LUAD workups, a clinically useful RAS/PI3K-active subgroup will emerge that is enriched for response to SHP2 plus AKT or MEK combinations.

If narrow clinical panels are expanded to include EGFR extracellular domain, RAF-family CR2 hotspots, NTRK fusions, MET exon 14 splice mutations, and noncanonical fusions, a measurable portion of currently untreated driver-negative patients will become targetable.

If copy-number, promoter, and protein analyses are integrated, more tumors with apparent 'wild-type' PTEN, KEAP1, STK11, or EGFR will be reclassified as pathway-activated through noncoding, dosage, or protein-level mechanisms.
new_predictions_unknown: A clinically important subgroup of WGS-negative but phospho-MAPK/phospho-AKT-high LUAD may prove to be driven by recurrent phosphatase-proteolysis circuits or RNA-editing states that are invisible to current DNA-centric profiling.

Some oncogene-negative LUADs may depend on transient ligand-driven signaling loops that create episodic RTK addiction without stable genomic lesions, making time-resolved phosphoproteomics superior to static sequencing for driver detection.

A fraction of residual unexplained tumors may be driven by microbiome- or injury-induced paracrine signaling that phenocopies RTK activation and could be therapeutically disrupted without directly targeting tumor genome lesions.

Integrated single-cell phosphoproteogenomics may reveal that several seemingly distinct 'driver-negative' classes collapse into a few conserved signaling attractor states, irrespective of the exact upstream lesion.
negative_experiments: Profile a large, rigorously panel-negative LUAD cohort with WGS, RNA-seq, copy-number, and phosphoproteomics; if most cases still show neither hidden pathway lesions nor RAS/PI3K output, the pathway-reinstatement theory is weakened.

Test NF1/RASA1/PTEN- or RASA1/NF1-deficient patient-derived models with SHP2/MEK/AKT combinations; if these suppressor-convergent tumors do not preferentially depend on these pathways, the suppressor-convergence law is weakened.

Prospectively compare standard clinical driver testing with expanded multi-omic testing in newly diagnosed 'driver-negative' LUAD; if expanded profiling rarely rescues targetable or pathway-convergent biology, the theory is weakened.

Assess whether pERK/pAKT-high oncogene-negative LUADs are actually enriched for suppressor loss compared with pERK/pAKT-low oncogene-negative tumors; lack of enrichment would weaken the convergence claim.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not fully explain driver-negative subsets centered on estrogen signaling, developmental quiescence, or immune-state programs that are not obviously MAPK/PI3K driven.
It also does not by itself explain pattern progression driven mainly by non-genetic state change rather than acquisition of pathway lesions.

conflicting_evidence.text: WGS-confirmed RPA(-)G LUAD explicitly lacked any single recurrent compensatory canonical lesion, and the study concluded that no unique recurrent genomic event explained all remaining negatives.
Piano/steady-like and KAC-related studies imply that some tumors may begin from lineage or quiescence programs rather than obvious pathway-reactivation lesions.

special_cases: A tumor can be truly noncanonical even after complete genomic rescue; this theory predicts major shrinkage of the driver-negative category, not its elimination.
Suppression of PTEN, NF1, STK11, or KEAP1 can be genomic, epigenetic, structural, or protein-level; the theory is about functional loss, not only sequence mutation.
Some hidden drivers are still canonical pathway lesions but in unusual forms (ECD mutations, exon skipping, enhancer hijacking), while others are suppressor-defined pathway states.

references: Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network (2014) Comprehensive molecular profiling of lung adenocarcinoma [alternative drivers in oncogene-negative LUAD]
Niu et al. (2023) Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [WGS rescue framework]
Foggetti et al. (2024) Combinatorial inactivation of tumor suppressors efficiently initiates lung adenocarcinoma with therapeutic vulnerabilities [oncogene-negative RAS/PI3K pathway-state model]
Hayes et al. (2018) RASA1 and NF1 are preferentially co-mutated and define a distinct genetic subset of smoking-associated NSCLC sensitive to MEK inhibition [suppressor-convergent human subset]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: The literature already contains separate explanations for hidden fusions, exon-skipping events, rare coding mutations, and combinatorial suppressor loss in driver-negative LUAD.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: This theory unifies those findings into a general model in which panel-negative LUAD is mainly a distributed pathway-reinstatement problem spanning DNA, RNA, copy-number, and protein layers.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: The component mechanisms are established, but the integrated framing of them as one dominant explanation for residual driver-negative LUAD is a new synthesis rather than a direct pre-existing theory.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1883.name: State-first oncogenesis in residual driver-negative LUAD
theory-1883.description: After hidden canonical and suppressor-convergent cases are removed, a residual class of lung adenocarcinomas appears to be driven less by a single dominant genomic driver and more by cell-state dysregulation. These tumors occupy developmental, lineage-plastic, metabolic, immune, or hormone-linked attractor states: quiescent/stem-like piano disease, KAC-rich precursor programs, NKX2-1-loss mucinous reprogramming, MIF-HIF-1α glycolytic states, and estrogen-SRC/GPER1 signaling in EGFR/ALK-negative never-smoker disease. In this class, progression follows state transitions and microenvironmental remodeling more than recurrent acquisition of new truncal oncogenes.
theory-1883.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: State-first oncogenesis in residual driver-negative LUAD
components.theory_description: After hidden canonical and suppressor-convergent cases are removed, a residual class of lung adenocarcinomas appears to be driven less by a single dominant genomic driver and more by cell-state dysregulation. These tumors occupy developmental, lineage-plastic, metabolic, immune, or hormone-linked attractor states: quiescent/stem-like piano disease, KAC-rich precursor programs, NKX2-1-loss mucinous reprogramming, MIF-HIF-1α glycolytic states, and estrogen-SRC/GPER1 signaling in EGFR/ALK-negative never-smoker disease. In this class, progression follows state transitions and microenvironmental remodeling more than recurrent acquisition of new truncal oncogenes.

theory_statements.statement_name: State-dominance law
theory_statements.theory_statement: A residual subset of true driver-negative LUAD is better explained by stable but plastic epithelial state programs than by recurrent dominant genomic lesions; these tumors cluster by lineage identity, developmental score, immune architecture, or metabolic program, and their biology is more visible in RNA/protein/spatial data than in hotspot mutation catalogs.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to residual LUADs after exclusion of canonical hidden drivers and overt suppressor-convergent RAS/PI3K states; strongest in never-smoker or low-SCNA/TMB tumors, early-stage multiregion disease, mucinous lineage-shift tumors, and transcriptomic/proteogenomic subtypes defined without a dominant oncogene.
special_cases: State-dominant tumors can still harbor occasional canonical drivers, especially EGFR, KRAS, or RET; the law concerns the principal organizing biology, not absolute absence of mutations.
Bulk expression signatures can be confounded by stromal or immune admixture, so single-cell or spatial validation is important.
This law is least applicable when clear pathway-high lesions or targetable fusions are already present.

supporting_evidence.text: Piano LCINS is a low-TMB, low-SCNA, low-SV, long-telomere, high-subclonality subtype with sparse known recurrent drivers and elevated developmental/stemness scores, explicitly motivating a quiescent adult stem-like origin hypothesis for tumors lacking a dominant known driver.

The steady never-smoker LUAD subtype shows low proliferation, high alveolar AT2 identity, fewer driver alterations, favorable prognosis, low immune-evasion scores, and TRU-like lineage fidelity, consistent with a driver-sparse developmental state rather than strong oncogenic pathway addiction.

KRT8-positive alveolar intermediate cells (KACs) are less differentiated transitional epithelial states enriched near tumors and through premalignant progression, associated with poor outcome, and proposed as an intermediate state in transformation from alveolar epithelium to LUAD.

NENA (EGFR/ALK-negative never-smoker LUAD) is characterized not by a single hidden canonical driver but by elevated estrogen signaling linked to STK11 mutation, GPER1 CNA/trans effects, LLGL2/ST14 hypomethylation, and SRC overexpression, indicating a hormone-linked state mechanism.

Driver-gene-negative LUAD enriched for glycolysis identified a MIF-positive AT2 cell state with higher glycolysis and malignancy, and the study proposed a MIF-HIF-1α positive-feedback loop as the key organizing mechanism rather than a hidden canonical oncogene.

Immune-defined driver-negative cohorts and non-driver molecular classes stratify strongly by cell-state composition and checkpoint context rather than genomic drivers alone, including 5-IRGP high-risk versus low-risk groups and PI/immune-high subtypes.

Proteogenomic EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD is heterogeneous into proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism groups, showing that clinically driver-negative cohorts resolve into state-defined subtypes rather than a single hidden-driver class.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Individual studies already describe piano stemness, KAC transitional states, NENA estrogen signaling, glycolytic MIF-HIF-1α biology, and immune-defined subtypes in driver-negative or noncanonical LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novelty is the unifying claim that these are manifestations of a common residual class of state-first oncogenesis, where cell-state architecture rather than a dominant genomic lesion is the primary explanatory unit.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a synthesis across several adjacent but previously separate subtype literatures; the component observations are known, but their merger into one residual-driver-negative framework is new.
references: Sherlock-Lung investigators (2024) The mutagenic forces shaping the genomic landscape of lung cancer in never smokers [piano quiescence/stemness concept]
Wang et al. (2024) An atlas of epithelial cell states and plasticity in lung adenocarcinoma [KAC transitional-state model]
Li et al. (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [NENA hormone-state model]
Driver-gene-negative glycolysis study (2024) Exploring the Glycolytic Mechanisms in Driver Gene-Negative LUAD [MIF-HIF-1α state model]

theory_statements.statement_name: Non-genetic progression law
theory_statements.theory_statement: Within state-dominant driver-negative LUAD, histologic progression is governed primarily by shifts in epithelial identity and spatial ecology—angiogenic endothelial states, fibroblast states, immune exclusion or inflaming, and lineage reprogramming—rather than by recurrent acquisition of pattern-specific new genomic drivers.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to early-stage, multiregion, pattern-heterogeneous LUAD and lineage-switch tumors, especially lepidic-to-solid progression, AIS/MIA transition, and NKX2-1-low mucinous reprogramming; strongest where spatial transcriptomics, methylation, and multiregion sequencing are available.
special_cases: Later canonical lesions can still emerge and accelerate progression, but they are not required to explain the main histologic transitions covered by this law.
This law concerns progression within established tumor fields and may not describe the very first initiating event.
It is most applicable to early-stage disease and may be partly obscured in heavily evolved metastatic tumors.

supporting_evidence.text: Multiregion histology-guided LUAD showed that drivers were predominantly clonal and not pattern-specific, while progression from lepidic to solid correlated with transcriptomic, methylation, and immune-remodeling changes; the authors explicitly treated pattern progression as not attributable to distinct region-specific canonical genomic drivers.

In a TCGA pattern-annotated LUAD subset, no recurrent genetic lesion was enriched in any histologic pattern, whereas gene-expression and methylation changes robustly tracked the lepidic-to-solid continuum, arguing against a simple pattern-specific driver-mutation model.

AIS3 was defined as a late AIS subtype enriched for tip endothelial cells and matrix CAFs, and spatial validation in MIA supported angiogenesis and stromal remodeling as features of the transition toward invasion.

The review-level synthesis of LUAD progression states concluded that lepidic-to-solid evolution is primarily nongenetic, involving epithelial plasticity and TME remodeling rather than new driver mutations.

NKX2-1 loss models and human NKX2-1-negative mucinous adenocarcinomas show that lineage-factor disruption can reprogram lung tumors toward a gastric/mucinous identity with major phenotypic consequences without requiring a new canonical RTK driver.

The lepidic-to-solid (L2S) signature reproduced progression and prognosis across large cohorts, supporting the idea that a transcriptomic state gradient captures progression better than a pattern-specific genomic lesion.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Non-genetic progression, lineage plasticity, and spatial microenvironmental remodeling in early LUAD are already described in multiregion and mucinous-lineage studies.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is to place those observations specifically into a theory of progression for residual driver-negative/state-dominant LUAD, making state transition the principal progression mechanism of that subtype family.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: This is a new integrative framing of several existing progression results, not a wholly unprecedented biological claim.
references: CHUV multiregion LUAD study (2024) [non-genetic pattern progression]
L2S and pattern-annotated TCGA LUAD analyses (2024) [state-gradient progression]
Maeda et al. and related NKX2-1 lineage studies [mucinous lineage switch]

new_predictions_likely: If residual WGS-negative or non-CAGA LUADs are profiled by single-cell multiome and spatial transcriptomics, they will segregate into a limited number of recurrent state programs—quiescent/alveolar, glycolytic AT2-like, hormone-SRC/GPER1, angiogenic-stromal, and lineage-switched mucinous—despite weak recurrence at the DNA-driver level.

If early lesions from piano- or KAC-rich lungs are followed longitudinally, the first changes predicting progression will be shifts in epithelial-state composition, tip endothelial cells, and matrix CAFs rather than recurrent acquisition of new hotspot kinase mutations.

If organoids from state-dominant driver-negative LUAD are perturbed, differentiation-state manipulation, hypoxia/hormone modulation, or stromal coculture changes will often alter growth more than single-agent RTK inhibition.

If NENA and glycolytic driver-negative LUAD are treated with pathway-matched but noncanonical agents, SRC inhibition and MIF/HIF-1α axis perturbation will show stronger subtype selectivity than broad anti-EGFR strategies.
new_predictions_unknown: A subset of residual driver-negative LUAD may be initiated by cyclical injury-repair programs in alveolar intermediates, so temporally patterned injury or pollution biomarkers could predict future emergence of state-first tumors years before diagnosis.

Differentiation therapy that restores alveolar identity or NKX2-1-like chromatin occupancy may suppress progression of lineage-switched driver-negative LUAD even without producing immediate tumor-cell death.

Some state-dominant tumors may depend on extracellular-vesicle-mediated communication from transitional epithelial cells or fibroblasts, making circulating vesicle cargo a stronger early biomarker than ctDNA.

Hormone-linked never-smoker LUAD and glycolytic AT2-like driver-negative LUAD may share a deeper common dependency on stress-adaptation nodes such as SRC, HIF-1α, or unfolded-protein-response wiring despite very different transcriptomic appearances.
negative_experiments: In rigorously profiled residual driver-negative LUAD, test whether single-cell and spatial state programs outperform genomic lesions in predicting prognosis, histology, and treatment response; if not, the state-first theory is weakened.

Longitudinally sample premalignant and early invasive lesions to test whether progression is preceded by transcriptomic/spatial state transitions rather than recurrent new drivers; failure of this prediction would weaken the non-genetic progression law.

Experimentally perturb NKX2-1 restoration, MIF inhibition, SRC inhibition, or differentiation-state regulators in subtype-matched models; if these interventions do not selectively affect the proposed state-defined tumors, the theory is weakened.

Compare residual driver-negative tumors with canonical-driver tumors for adjacent normal-tissue enrichment of KAC or developmental states; if enrichment is not selective, the quiescent/state-origin claim loses specificity.

unaccounted_for.text: This theory does not explain clearly driver-rescued cases with strong targetable RTK activation such as ERBB2 enhancer hijacking, NTRK1 fusions, RET fusions, or MET exon 14 skipping, which are better handled by hidden-driver theories.
It also does not primarily account for dual-pathway suppressor-convergent oncogene-negative tumors with overt pERK/pAKT activation and SHP2/AKT/MEK vulnerabilities.

conflicting_evidence.text: Some state-defined tumors still contain canonical drivers, including EGFR enrichment in steady/TRU-like disease and occasional RET or KRAS lesions in piano-like tumors, so state biology and canonical driver positivity can coexist.
The MIF-HIF-1α and estrogen-SRC models remain partly associative and not fully causally proven, which weakens any claim that state programs alone are sufficient drivers.

special_cases: Mucinous/NKX2-1-low tumors may begin as lineage-state tumors but later acquire KRAS or ligand-fusion dependence; state-first and hidden-driver mechanisms can occur sequentially in the same cancer.
KAC-like programs are not exclusive to driver-negative disease and can also be strong in KRAS-mutant tumors.
Some immune or metabolic subtypes may reflect both tumor-intrinsic state and microenvironmental composition, so subtype assignment should ideally use tumor-cell-resolved data.

references: Sherlock-Lung consortium (2024) The mutagenic forces shaping the genomic landscape of lung cancer in never smokers [piano quiescent/stemness hypothesis]
Wang et al. (2024) An atlas of epithelial cell states and plasticity in lung adenocarcinoma [KAC precursor/intermediate-state model]
CHUV multiregion LUAD study (2024) [non-genetic lepidic-to-solid progression]
Li et al. (2024) Proteogenomic Characterization Reveals Estrogen Signaling as a Target for Never-Smoker LUAD Patients without EGFR or ALK Alterations [state-based hormone-signaling subtype]
Driver-negative glycolysis study (2024) [MIF-HIF-1α metabolic-state subtype]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Several papers already describe quiescent/stem-like, lineage-plastic, glycolytic, immune, and hormone-linked LUAD subgroups, especially in never-smoker or early-stage disease.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novelty is treating these not as isolated curiosities but as one coherent residual-driver-negative family in which cell state is the primary organizing principle of origin and progression.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: This theory is grounded in existing subtype observations but is novel in its cross-study integration and explicit positioning as the main explanation for the residual class left after hidden-driver rescue.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.

theory-1884.name: Enhancer-jacked receptor activation subtype
theory-1884.description: A specific subclass of oncogene-negative or non-CAGA lung adenocarcinoma is driven not by canonical kinase-domain mutations or classic fusion oncogenes, but by cis-regulatory rewiring that creates pathologic overexpression of wild-type receptor tyrosine kinases or receptor-proximal signaling nodes. The central mechanism is enhancer-jacking: structural variants, focal super-enhancer amplification, promoter/noncoding mutation, or boundary disruption reposition or amplify lineage-active regulatory elements, yielding outlier ERBB2, EGFR, ROS1, RET-pathway, FRS2, CAV2, or related expression. In this subtype, genomic silence at the usual coding-driver loci can coexist with strong protein abundance and pathway output, so the biologically relevant lesion sits in chromatin architecture rather than in the receptor coding sequence.
theory-1884.theory_query: Build theories about what drives oncogene negative lung adenocinomas. About 30 percent of lung adenos do not demonstrate readily identifiable drivers in RTK/RAS/RAF pathway genes. Is it rare genes we are missing? Are we missing something at the proteomic level? Is it a ligand? Different pathway? Microbiome? Different biology? What are some novel hypotheses and how can we validate these? Are there parallels in other cancers? Can we categorize driver negative cancers into different subtypes? Will there be therapeutic implications for this, based on the following results.

components.theory_name: Enhancer-jacked receptor activation subtype
components.theory_description: A specific subclass of oncogene-negative or non-CAGA lung adenocarcinoma is driven not by canonical kinase-domain mutations or classic fusion oncogenes, but by cis-regulatory rewiring that creates pathologic overexpression of wild-type receptor tyrosine kinases or receptor-proximal signaling nodes. The central mechanism is enhancer-jacking: structural variants, focal super-enhancer amplification, promoter/noncoding mutation, or boundary disruption reposition or amplify lineage-active regulatory elements, yielding outlier ERBB2, EGFR, ROS1, RET-pathway, FRS2, CAV2, or related expression. In this subtype, genomic silence at the usual coding-driver loci can coexist with strong protein abundance and pathway output, so the biologically relevant lesion sits in chromatin architecture rather than in the receptor coding sequence.

theory_statements.statement_name: Regulatory-overexpression driver law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In driver-negative or non-CAGA lung adenocarcinoma, recurrent outlier expression of an RTK or receptor-proximal signaling gene in the absence of a canonical activating coding lesion is evidence for a regulatory-driver mechanism, most commonly enhancer hijacking, super-enhancer formation/amplification, promoter mutation, or other local cis-regulatory rewiring.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to lung adenocarcinomas classified as non-CAGA, oncogene-negative, RTK/RAS/RAF-negative, or driver-unknown after broad but still imperfect conventional genomic testing, especially tumors with RNA/protein outlier expression of ERBB2, EGFR, ROS1, FGFR3, RET, FRS2, CAV2, or related signaling genes. Best supported when structural, chromatin, methylation, or enhancer-linked data are available; not intended for tumors already explained by canonical activating kinase mutations or recurrent in-frame fusions.
special_cases: RNA outlier expression alone is insufficient because it can reflect lineage state, stromal contamination, or generalized transcriptional dysregulation rather than a causal driver event.
Some apparent regulatory-driver cases are mixed-mechanism lesions with both moderate copy gain and enhancer rewiring, rather than pure enhancer hijacking.
A subset of outlier-expression tumors may later be reclassified as hidden classical amplifications or cryptic fusions if deeper WGS/long-read or RNA assays are performed.
Ligand-fusion tumors such as CD74-NRG1 are adjacent but not identical to this law because the causal lesion is a membrane-tethered ligand rather than receptor overexpression itself.

supporting_evidence.text: In non-CAGA LUAD, ERBB2 overexpression was linked to structural rearrangement plus super-enhancer formation despite absence of usual actionable ERBB2 alterations by initial WES/RNA screening logic, establishing a direct example of a regulatory-driver route in driver-negative disease.

Hi-C and long-read sequencing showed the rearranged ERBB2 locus moved to within ~125 kb of HNF1β from ~1.9 Mb in reference sequence, directly supporting cis-regulatory rewiring rather than a coding mutation mechanism.

CRISPR-induced double-strand breaks at both ERBB2 and HNF1β loci increased HER2 expression whereas single-break controls did not, functionally supporting structural enhancer-jacking as the cause of overexpression.

In non-CAGA LUAD integrated analysis, ERBB2, EGFR, CDK4, and MDM2 were highlighted as outlier genes associated with super-enhancer to gene links and overlap with structural variants, indicating that ERBB2 is not an isolated example.

FRS2 was among the top-ranked SE-to-gene candidates in non-CAGA LUAD and was proposed as a plausible alternative driver because of known oncogenic signaling capacity in other cancers, supporting extension of the mechanism to receptor-adjacent signaling nodes.
CAV2 was also nominated from the same non-CAGA SE-SV/outlier framework, again implying a broader class of noncanonical expression-driven candidate drivers beyond a single receptor gene.

FGF3, FGF4, and FGF19 emerged among outlier-expression gene groups associated with worse recurrence risk in the non-CAGA setting, consistent with enhancer-linked ligand/receptor-axis dysregulation as a clinically meaningful event class.

A separate non-CAGA LUAD study implicated predominantly noncoding alterations at the MAML2 locus with reduced enhancer H3K27ac, reduced expression, and altered methylation, showing that driver-negative LUAD can indeed be explained by recurrent cis-regulatory lesions even when the target is not an RTK.

ILF2 promoter mutations in RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD occurred in 6 of 57 cases, all within ATAC peaks and conserved promoter regions, and were associated with increased ILF2 expression after controlling for copy number and purity, directly demonstrating a promoter-mutation regulatory driver candidate in a strict negative subset.

ROS1 and FGFR3 outlier-expression tumors were almost exclusively driver-unknown and lacked detectable rearrangement by FISH in tested cases, indicating that some kinase-high tumors are activated by mechanisms other than classic fusions.

RET overexpression occurred in a TCGA LUAD subset without resolved mechanism, and the original RET paper explicitly suggested that overexpression might arise from regulatory mutation, amplification, or alternate RET fusion partners, supporting the existence of noncanonical RET activation states beyond standard fusion calls.

Within the driver-poor piano subtype, RET fusions occurred only in that quiet/stem-like group, showing that sparse-driver tumors can still harbor nonstandard or restricted receptor-axis activation and that lineage context matters for rare RTK activation routes.

EGFR amplification independent of kinase-domain mutation status was documented in NSCLC, with amplification-positive/mutation-negative samples showing elevated EGFR expression, showing that receptor-pathway activation can occur in the absence of canonical coding mutation.

A broad hybrid-capture sequencing strategy in clinically negative LUADs was cited as rescuing actionable alterations missed by prior testing, supporting the broader principle that apparent driver-negativity can reflect assay blindness to noncanonical or structurally complex activation mechanisms.

Cross-cancer epithelial examples show that focal noncoding super-enhancer amplification can directly upregulate oncogenic targets, including MYC-LASE in LUAD, MYC-ECSE in UCEC, USP12-CCSE in CRC, PARD6B-LCSE in LIHC, and KLF5-HNSE in HNSC, supporting a transferable epithelial cancer mechanism rather than a one-off LUAD anomaly.

MYC-LASE in LUAD itself provides direct lineage-matched precedent: a lung-adenocarcinoma-specific noncoding super-enhancer 3' to MYC is focally amplified, physically contacts the MYC promoter, raises MYC expression, and is functionally required through CRISPRi/deletion experiments.

This law is logically induced from ERBB2 enhancer-hijacking in non-CAGA LUAD, ILF2 promoter mutation in strict RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUAD, MAML2 noncoding dysregulation in non-CAGA LUAD, and cross-cancer super-enhancer amplification examples, which jointly imply that unexplained LUADs are enriched for cis-regulatory rather than coding drivers.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Enhancer hijacking, super-enhancer amplification, and promoter-driven oncogene dysregulation are established across hematologic and some epithelial cancers, and ERBB2 enhancer-jacking in non-CAGA LUAD has already been directly demonstrated. Individual LUAD examples also exist for MAML2 and ILF2 noncoding dysregulation.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: What is novel is the unification of these observations into a coherent driver-negative LUAD subtype centered on receptor or receptor-adjacent overexpression, rather than treating each lesion as an isolated exception. The theory also explicitly proposes that RTK-high/FISH-negative tumors are often regulatory-driver cases rather than merely unexplained noise.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Because core ingredients already exist in the literature but the subtype framing and extension to a broader receptor-overexpression class are not explicitly formulated in the supplied studies, this statement is best classified as somewhat related to existing work rather than fully new or fully established.
references: Shibata (year not provided) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [direct LUAD prototype of enhancer-jacked receptor activation]
The pan-cancer super-enhancer amplification study containing MYC-LASE/MYC-ECSE/KLF5-HNSE/USP12-CCSE/PARD6B-LCSE (year not provided) [broad epithelial precedent for noncoding amplification drivers]

Multi-omics and clustering analyses reveal the mechanisms underlying unmet needs for patients with lung adenocarcinoma and identify potential therapeutic targets [MAML2 noncoding dysregulation in non-CAGA LUAD, related non-RTK regulatory-driver example]
Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [ILF2 promoter mutation in strict negative LUAD, related promoter-driver example]

theory_statements.statement_name: Protein-state discriminator law
theory_statements.theory_statement: In regulatory-overexpression LUADs, receptor abundance and downstream phospho-output are more reliable indicators of oncogenic dependence than canonical mutation-era activation markers, because regulatory rewiring often activates signaling without the textbook phosphosite or coding-lesion pattern expected for mutant receptors.
theory_statements.domain_scope: Applies to receptor-overexpressing, coding-silent or structurally rewired LUADs and adjacent NSCLC comparators in which signaling activation is suspected to arise from enhancer hijacking, noncanonical amplification, or protein-state rewiring rather than a classic kinase-domain mutation. Most relevant when RNA/protein/phosphoproteomic assays are available.
special_cases: Some overexpressed receptors may be biologically inert unless ligand, dimerization partner, or cooperating structural context is present.
Canonical phosphosite markers can still be informative in some rewired tumors, so this law is comparative rather than absolute.
Bulk phosphoproteomics can be confounded by purity and cell-type mixture, particularly in immune-rich tumors.

supporting_evidence.text: In ERBB2 enhancer-jacked non-CAGA LUAD, HER2 transcript and protein overexpression were robust, but pY1248 did not significantly increase, and the authors explicitly concluded that signaling may proceed by a nonstandard route not captured by the usual HER2 activation marker.

Despite absent pY1248 increase, ERBB2-overexpressing PDXs were sensitive to poziotinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan while afatinib was ineffective, demonstrating that standard phosphosite logic can mis-rank therapeutic relevance in rewired receptor tumors.

EGFR-mutant LUADs in CPTAC showed highly significant SHP2 Y62 phosphorylation without RNA or total-protein change, showing that clinically relevant signaling output can be visible in phosphoproteomics even when upstream genomic or abundance markers are incomplete.

ALK fusion-positive LUADs displayed outlier ALK mRNA, outlier ALK protein, and especially strong ALK Y1507 phosphorylation; phosphosite-specific IHC distinguished ALK-fusion tumors from ROS1/RET-fusion tumors and NATs, showing the value of protein-state diagnostics over simple fusion-presence logic alone.

EGFR wild-type tumors with elevated MAPK signaling were identifiable by phosphoproteomics, and 3 of 4 were KRAS mutant, indicating that pathway state can reveal functionally coherent subsets even when the tested receptor itself is wild-type.

FGFR3 S408 phosphorylation in early-stage SSN LUAD increased from NAT to AIS/MIA/IAC without detectable FGFR3 protein abundance change, and phosphomimetic S408D increased PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling and proliferation, directly showing that phospho-state can outperform total abundance or coding-genotype in identifying an active axis.

ROS1 outlier-expression tumors lacked rearrangement by FISH in tested cases, leaving the mechanism unresolved and implying that conventional DNA/FISH assays alone are insufficient for identifying the relevant active state.

RET-high transcriptomic subsets were observed without resolved genomic cause, and the original RET discovery papers noted possible alternative mechanisms besides known fusions, again implying that pathway-state assessment may be required where canonical genomic tests are negative.

Proteogenomic studies of EGFR/ALK-wild-type never-smoker LUAD emphasized pathway-level subgrouping by proliferation, immune, angiogenesis, and metabolism rather than single hidden coding drivers, supporting the broader claim that protein/pathway state can be more actionable than mutation status alone in negative cohorts.

This law is induced by combining the ERBB2 noncanonical activation-marker evidence with phosphoproteomic observations in EGFR-mutant, ALK-fusion, EGFR-wild-type high-MAPK, and FGFR3-phosphorylated LUADs, which jointly show that oncogenic output can be decoupled from canonical mutation-era marker expectations.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Proteogenomic discordance between DNA/RNA and pathway activation is established in lung adenocarcinoma, and noncanonical phosphosite behavior has already been reported for ERBB2, EGFR/SHP2, ALK, and FGFR3-related contexts. The value of phosphoproteomics in uncovering hidden signaling states is therefore not new in itself.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The novel contribution is the subtype-specific rule that regulatory-overexpression tumors should be diagnosed and triaged primarily by receptor abundance plus downstream phospho-output, not by canonical hotspot mutations or textbook receptor phosphosites alone. This reframes several scattered findings into a practical diagnostic principle for driver-negative LUAD.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: The ingredients are clearly grounded in existing proteogenomic studies, but the explicit statement that regulatory-driver LUAD requires a different biomarker hierarchy is an integrative extrapolation rather than a pre-existing named rule.
references: Proteogenomic characterization reveals therapeutic vulnerabilities in lung adenocarcinoma [SHP2 Y62, ALK Y1507, genome-protein discordance]

Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [HER2 overexpression with atypical phosphosite behavior]
Proteogenomic characterization reveals tumorigenesis and progression of lung cancer manifested as subsolid nodules [FGFR3 phosphosite activation without abundance change]

new_predictions_likely: If strict non-CAGA or RTK/RAS/RAF-negative LUADs with ERBB2, EGFR, ROS1, FGFR3, RET, FRS2, or CAV2 outlier expression are analyzed by long-read WGS plus H3K27ac profiling and chromosome-conformation assays, a substantial fraction will show local enhancer relocation, boundary disruption, focal noncoding amplification, or promoter-region mutation near the outlier gene.

If organoids or PDXs from receptor-outlier, coding-silent LUADs are subjected to CRISPRi against the dominant H3K27ac-marked enhancer rather than receptor coding knockout alone, receptor RNA/protein and clonogenic growth will decrease in parallel.

If clinically driver-negative LUADs are stratified by receptor abundance plus downstream phosphoproteomics rather than hotspot sequencing alone, drug response to receptor-axis therapy will be predicted more accurately than by standard mutation/FISH workflows.

If ROS1-high/FGFR3-high/RET-high FISH-negative tumors are reassessed with long-read sequencing or optical mapping, some will resolve into cryptic local SVs or noncanonical transcript architectures missed by standard FISH.
new_predictions_unknown: Some enhancer-jacked receptor tumors will prove more dependent on transcriptional coactivators or chromatin readers than on the receptor kinase itself, such that BET, CDK7, or EP300-directed therapy may outperform matched kinase inhibitors.

A subset of receptor-outlier negative LUADs may be maintained by a hybrid mechanism in which enhancer rewiring primes receptor abundance while microenvironmental ligand supply provides the dominant activation pulse.

Regulatory-driver LUAD may exhibit unusually high intratumoral spatial heterogeneity of receptor expression, creating mixed response to kinase therapy that could be normalized by enhancer suppression or chromatin therapy.

There may be a lung-lineage-specific enhancer grammar, perhaps anchored by NKX2-1/HNF1β/other lineage factors, that predicts which wild-type RTKs are most likely to become noncanonically activated in driver-negative LUAD.
negative_experiments: Take a series of receptor-outlier, coding-negative LUADs and perform long-read WGS plus H3K27ac/Hi-C. If most show no local regulatory rewiring and no recurrent noncoding lesions, the core subtype claim weakens substantially.

In receptor-outlier models, suppress the candidate enhancer with CRISPRi and separately suppress the receptor coding region. If neither manipulation reduces growth or pathway output, the outlier expression is likely epiphenomenal rather than driver-like.

Compare prediction of targeted-therapy response using canonical mutation/FISH assays versus receptor abundance plus phosphoproteomics in regulatory-outlier models. If standard mutation-era biomarkers outperform protein-state measures, the protein-state discriminator law is wrong.

Reassess receptor-outlier negative LUADs using dense copy-number methods. If most cases are actually straightforward high-level genomic amplifications rather than cis-regulatory rewiring, enhancer-jacking is being over-invoked.

Test whether receptor-outlier cases with unresolved mechanism are fully explained by lineage-state markers alone. If expression outliers disappear after cell-state deconvolution and tumor-cell purification, the theory has over-attributed them to regulatory drivers.

unaccounted_for.text: The theory does not explain the major class of driver-negative LUADs driven by combinatorial loss of NF1, RASA1, and PTEN with direct RAS/PI3K activation rather than receptor overexpression.
The theory does not explain metabolic driver-negative subtypes centered on MIF-HIF-1α glycolytic rewiring or broader glycolysis-related prognostic programs.
The theory does not explain estrogen-signaling-driven NENA tumors, where SRC/GPER1/STK11-linked pathway activation is proposed without a primary receptor-enhancer overexpression mechanism.

The theory only partially covers ligand-driven fusion subtypes such as CD74-NRG1 and other NRG1 rearrangements, where the causal lesion is a ligand-fusion mechanism rather than receptor overexpression per se.
The theory does not explain lineage-plasticity and non-genetic progression models such as KACs, lepidic-to-solid progression, NKX2-1-loss mucinous conversion, or piano stemness/quiescence states.

conflicting_evidence.text: ROS1 and FGFR3 outlier-expression cases remain mechanistically unresolved and were supported only by RNA/FISH-negative evidence, so they currently fit the theory only as hypotheses, not demonstrated regulatory-driver events.

RET-high expression in TCGA LUAD and RET restriction to piano imply noncanonical activation but do not prove enhancer hijacking or cis-regulatory rewiring, so the specific mechanism remains inferential.

Some outlier genes nominated in non-CAGA LUAD, including FRS2 and CAV2, were not functionally validated in LUAD, so the theory may overgeneralize from ERBB2 to genes that are only associative candidates.

In several cohorts, deeper sequencing rescued hidden classical coding or fusion drivers, implying that some apparent receptor-outlier negative tumors may simply be incompletely genotyped rather than true regulatory-driver tumors.

special_cases: ERBB2 cases with both moderate copy gain and enhancer rewiring should be considered hybrid copy-number/regulatory drivers rather than pure enhancer-jacked lesions.
CD74-NRG1 and related NRG1 fusions are adjacent to this subtype but are better treated as ligand-fusion tumors rather than receptor-overexpression tumors.
MYC-LASE-like lesions are strong proof-of-principle for regulatory oncogenesis in LUAD but sit outside the receptor-centric core of this subtype.

In immune-rich or low-purity samples, protein-state or expression outliers must be interpreted with tumor-cell-resolved assays because stromal admixture can create false RTK-high or pathway-high impressions.

references: Shibata (year not provided) Mechanism of ERBB2 gene overexpression by the formation of super-enhancer with genomic structural abnormalities in lung adenocarcinoma without clinically actionable genetic alterations [direct prototype of enhancer-jacked receptor activation in non-CAGA LUAD]
Pan-cancer super-enhancer amplification study containing MYC-LASE/MYC-ECSE/KLF5-HNSE/USP12-CCSE/PARD6B-LCSE (year not provided) [related epithelial regulatory-driver framework]
Whole-genome characterization of lung adenocarcinomas lacking alterations in the RTK/RAS/RAF pathway [related strict-negative noncoding/promoter-driver discovery including ILF2]

Multi-omics and clustering analyses reveal the mechanisms underlying unmet needs for patients with lung adenocarcinoma and identify potential therapeutic targets [related MAML2 noncoding dysregulation in non-CAGA LUAD]
existing_theory.what_already_exists: Existing work already establishes that noncoding lesions, super-enhancer gain, and structural rewiring can activate oncogenic transcription in both LUAD and other epithelial cancers. ERBB2 enhancer-hijacking in non-CAGA LUAD is the clearest direct precedent.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: The novel aspect is the proposal that these events define a coherent, receptor-overexpression subclass of driver-negative LUAD with a distinct diagnostic logic: seek cis-regulatory rewiring and prioritize protein-state readouts over hotspot mutation logic. This reframes several disconnected findings into a single subtype theory.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Because the mechanism itself is not new but the subtype-level synthesis, extension across multiple receptor/proximal targets, and explicit diagnostic rule are not directly stated in the source studies, the theory is best judged somewhat related to existing work.

components.mission_statement: I want to understand the drivers of oncogene negative lung adenocarcinomas, so that I can make impactful contributions to research in this area, and potentially help identify new therapeutic targets for this type of cancer.
